👋 Introductions & Front Matter
Introductions


        Autumn is coming soon here on the East Coast, and as the leaves begin to turn colors, I just know that we’re all going to be sitting down for some more tabletop roleplaying games, right? And what could be more fun as the days grow shorter than blowing up baddies with fire, ice, and everything nice? Yes, that’s right—it’s high time that we reviewed the kineticist class, first introduced in Paizo’s Occult Adventures sourcebook and widely considered one of the more complicated classes that Pathfinder 1e ever produced. Paizo is on record as saying that the kineticist occupies the mechanical space that the warlock occupies in D&D 5e, sans the meddling-with-forces-best-left-alone flavor, and that description really does fit the class: it’s got blasts pouring out of every pore, both single-target and area-of-effect; it’s got at-will abilities that you get to keep using throughout the day; and most importantly, it’s got a mechanic for making you want to draw more and more power even as you edge closer to your own destruction.


        Keen readers may notice that this guide took me much longer than my guides normally take me—at this point in my development, a good class guide usually takes about 4 months. The delay is largely because I changed my mind about what would be included in the guide partway through, from Paizo-official first-party content only (the original design mission) to first-party content and curated third-party content. There will be more to say about that choice in the next section, but for now I’ll state with utmost confidence that adding third-party content only enhances the game that your players (and hopefully your monsters, just to keep things interesting!) will be playing. Some paring down of power is always necessary with third-party content, but I’ve done my best to guide you through with careful edits and revisions where necessary. All that’s left is to wish you bon voyage as you disembark across the Dreamlands to sink your teeth into this most excellent of classes!


With best regards for a good game,
Chris (/u/Allerseelen)
________________
KIN 100: A Word About 3PP


Whether deserved or not, third-party content (i.e., content released by publishers other than Paizo) for Pathfinder 1e gets a bad reputation. GMs get burned one too many times by players bringing in overpowered content that wasn’t vetted by Paizo’s editing team, and they just throw up their hands and say, “No third-party! Never, never, never!” And, look, as a GM myself, I hate it when players break the balance of the game, whether through first- or third-party content.
Here’s the thing, though: Pathfinder 1e, at the time of this guide’s writing, is a dead system. It’s gone the way of Latin and Old English; the world has moved on. If you ever wanted more archetypes, feats, items, etc. for your favorite 1e classes, there’s no cavalry that’s on its way—Paizo has stopped making content for the system. This becomes a particular problem for the occult classes (psychic, mesmerist, medium, spiritualist, kineticist, and occultist), which were introduced in the Occult Adventures book toward the end of Pathfinder 1e’s publication. As late introductions to the game, they never really received all that much love from Paizo: they tend to have fewer archetypes than core classes based solely on how long those classes had been around, and they have only a few feats or wondrous items appropriate for them. Fortunately, not everyone gave up on the occult classes, and the kineticist in particular received a lot of love from Purple Duck Games, who underwrote N. Jolly’s excellent Kineticists of Porphyra series, and Legendary Games, who are famous for their top-to-bottom rewrites of classes (Legendary Kineticists) that fall behind in the power curve. I believe that players and GMs are not getting the complete kineticist experience without Kineticists of Porphyra I-IV and Legendary Kineticists I-II.
Why not? Well, first of all, they’re pretty well-balanced. N. Jolly (author of Kineticists of Porphyra) was one of the original prolific authors of Pathfinder 1e guides, and his prose and formatting style taught me a lot about how to write guides myself. He’s an experienced third-party author, and he gets that the point of third-party content is not to give players unlimited power to sneak past a GM, but to introduce new options that make the class more complex and nuanced. Ditto for Legendary Games, whose rewrites of struggling classes like the rogue, monk, and ranger have become my new baselines for those classes. Second, more than anything else, kineticists lack versatility in what they can do, and too often get relegated to the role of a blaster; the introduction of third-party content changes that and allows them to fill many more roles than they previously could. Third, kineticists are simply lacking a lot of content. To whit, there are very few feats or wondrous items that make sense for the class. Wouldn’t you rather have something to do with all those feats and all that gold? Fourth, this stuff is just plain cool! I’ve often found that third-party publishers can take chances on wackier ideas than first-party publishers can, and when those ideas pay out, they tend to pay out big. Want to rip your own entrails out and then use them as an aetherically charged whip against enemies? Well, that wacky idea is possible through third-party content.
All that being said, I understand if your GM doesn’t want to play with third-party content. Once bitten, twice shy, and all that. Throughout this guide and its appendices, third-party content will be notated in italics where possible, or with the [3PP] tag where not possible. Any items, feats, or wild talents that need to be curbed because of power creep are notated in black italics, and I’ll give suggested fixes where I can to improve entities that are simply too powerful for normal games. I’m not going to reference which specific splatbook each of the wild talents, feats, wondrous items, etc. comes from—all of the content that I’m referencing is publicly available at the Library of Metzofitz, so you can go there for an easy-to-navigate third-party kineticist experience.
💯 Rating System
The Rating System


Throughout this guide, you’ll see feats, spells, class features, etc. rated using a color coding system that was first introduced by Treantmonk and since copied in many other guides. Everyone tends to use their own colors, though, so here’s what we’ll be working with:


Purple ratings are singular, the one ability at a given level or for a given playstyle that is cream of the crop. The apex, the zenith, the pinnacle: purple options are the royalty, the nobility. You will pick purple abilities, and damn the torpedoes.


Blue abilities are supercharged goodness: options that define the way a class is played, or that otherwise provide some exponential leap in effectiveness. Try your utmost to pick up these options.


Green abilities are almost universally useful, a good pick for nearly any build. If they’re not gamebreakingly powerful, they’re definitely holding up the line at a solid B+.


Yellow abilities are our C standard, neither good nor bad. A yellow rating can also denote situational abilities that might be useful to some playstyles but not others.


Red abilities are the traps. Stay away from these.
🏋️ Kineticist Advancement


🔥 Class Features
KIN 101: Class Chassis


Kineticists are a bizarre mix of unorthodox striker and unorthodox spellcaster, and in fact Paizo (or Jason Buhlman, at least) is on record as saying that the kineticist is Paizo’s answer to D&D’s warlock class—mechanically, at least, because the witch got most of the flavor. At any rate, it’s not difficult to see why many players have trouble wrapping their heads around the kineticist: it’s a complex class that suffers from a few unusual design choices (or perhaps layout choices is more accurate) on Paizo’s part. Never fear. We’ll break things down step by step after a bit of housekeeping.




The Chassis


d8 Hit Die: A d8 is standard for striker classes, as is ¾ BAB progression. In practice, kineticists will have an absolute boatload of HP because of their Constitution focus. Quite a bit of that will get eaten up by burn, of course, but we haven’t gotten there yet. You’ll always be able to increase your HP with the Toughness feat or any type of favored class bonus, so this will never be that huge a problem.


¾ BAB Progression: Again, standard for striker classes. For the most part, kineticists using energy blasts won’t have to worry much at all about BAB, as they target touch AC with their blasts; those using physical blasts, however, will need to invest more in Dexterity in order to be able to hit heavily armored targets, especially as they have no weapon enhancement bonuses to lean on. Kineticists do specialize in single, devastating hits made at high BAB, rather than iterative attacks, though, so things will never get too dire for you even if you are specializing in physical blasts.


Skills: Oof. That is...sparse. 4 + Intelligence skill ranks per level isn’t terrible, and you’ll get some additional class skills depending on your chosen element, but even so, dang. Slim pickings. Your choices of skills are reviewed below; class skills are italicized.


* Perception: Surprising no one, Perception clocks in as the kineticist’s most valuable skill. It determines participation in surprise rounds, it determines whether you spot traps, it determines your ability to notice danger approaching or buried loot...you’ll max this every level, with no questions asked.
* Stealth: Dexterity focus, light armor, class skill: all signs point to yes for a good Stealth score. You’ll never regret being able to move around quietly as any Pathfinder character—it’s one of those skills that you don’t appreciate until you’re clanking around as some heavy armor-wearing fighter.
* Use Magic Device: Sure, why not? UMD is a useful skill for just about any class, especially once you can hit a +19 reliably. You’re no occultist, so you won’t be slinging around scrolls and wands regularly, but you never know when you’ll want to use a cool magic item you found. If you want to focus on UMD, I’d recommend the Pragmatic Activator trait in order to key UMD off of Intelligence, rather than Charisma; there are a few abilities that give kineticists familiars, too, which is another route to UMD dominance.
* Acrobatics and Escape Artist: Your BAB will never be so high that you can reliably beat grapples, and your AC will never be so high that you can reliably avoid attacks of opportunity. Instead, investment in these two skills, Acrobatics in particular, will be an excellent use of your skill ranks. The kineticist is predominantly a ranged class; it needs to be able to scoot out of threatened range when needed.
* Fly: No surprise that aerokineticists will want to invest full ranks in Fly almost from the get-go, as will anyone who’s planning to get the Wings of Air wild talent through their expanded element. It’s always a good skill, since pretty much everyone is flying by the double digits. Fly checks are fairly easy to beat, though, so don’t go hog wild here.
* Disable Device: Your Dexterity score won’t be terrible, and most kineticists will be wearing light armor; it’s not crazy that you might want to pick a lock or two. Probably goes up to green for aetherkineticists who are planning to use the Telekinetic Finesse wild talent to pick locks, disable traps, and steal stuff from afar.
* Heal: Heal is always a good skill to have a few ranks in. If nothing else, you can stabilize a colleague or treat poison or disease when needed. I’d dump a few ranks in here, especially early in the game. Kineticists can also make decent non-magical healers with the Healer’s Hands + Incredible Healer feat combo. Something to think about if you ever want something more to do with your feats.
* Intimidate: Intimidate on a Constitution-based pseudo-caster? Huh? What’s that all about? For most people, Intimidate will be useless. It doesn’t do much on its own, after all: in social settings you need to be worried about people retaliating against you, and in combat demoralization is pretty weak if you’re using Intimidate on single targets. Fortunately, kineticists do not have to use Intimidate on single targets. The Kinetic Awe utility wild talent is quite good, costing 0 burn and allowing you to demoralize an entire crowd of baddies with only a standard action. With the Intimidate Unchained Skill Unlock, Violent Display, Disheartening Display, and Shatter Defenses, kineticists can add some much-needed debuffing utility to their line-up. A high Charisma score really doesn’t get kineticists much, so I would maybe suggest the Bruising Intellect trait and a higher Intelligence score if you want to pursue this path.
* Knowledge (Arcana, Dungeoneering, Local, Nature, Planes, Religion): These Knowledge skills are the ones that get used for monster identification, which is generally the most important function of a Knowledge skill. You have neither the skill ranks nor the class skills to invest in most of these. If your element offers one like Knowledge (Nature), put ranks in whenever you can; otherwise, let sleeping skills lie.
* Spellcraft: Kineticists can craft magical items through the Kinetic Crafter feat or the Kinetic Crafting utility wild talent, so it’s not wildly out of the question that you would want to craft things. The feat is, by all accounts, quite bad, but the wild talent helps you out with your Spellcraft checks by letting you ignore more spell prerequisites every few levels. Something to consider in long-running campaigns.
* Appraise: Oddly enough, Appraise is one of the best Background Skills because of its ability to detect magical items without detect magic. Unless you’re playing with the Background Skills ruleset, though, there’s no earthly reason why you would take this.
* Bluff, Diplomacy, Disguise, Perform, Sense Motive: No class skills, no particular attribute support. Let someone else be the party face. Your job is to bring the boom.
* Climb, Swim: Some elements are capable of some form of flight, and with expanded element neither Climb nor Swim will be super relevant in the long term. If you’re a hydrokineticist or geokineticist, by all means, add a rank to get your class skill bonus, just don’t do much more than that.
* Craft: Mundane crafting is an exceptionally poor choice for the kineticist.
* Handle Animal, Ride: Barring the cinderlands adept archetype, kineticists have zero reasons to take ranks in Handle Animal or Ride.
* Knowledge (Engineering, Geography, History, Nobility): They’re Background Skills for a reason: they’re almost never used. Even aetherkineticists with Aether Architect are better served by using Craft to construct their cities in the clouds.
* Linguistics: As with Appraise, probably the most useful Background Skill out there. Invest if you’re using that system, and don’t if you’re not.
* Profession: Nah. I mean, okay, but nah. It’s fun flavor, especially with Background Skills, but no mechanical powerhouse.
* Sleight of Hand: Of very niche use for aetherkineticists, Sleight of Hand is downright garbage for everyone else.
* Survival: Let the ranger take care of this. It ain’t gonna be you.


Armor Proficiencies: Light armor is ultimately what you would have wanted anyway—kineticists are almost always ranged or Weapon Finesse combatants, ranged combat and Weapon Finesse require a high Dexterity score, and there’s no reason to wear medium or heavy armor if you have a high Dexterity score. QED.


Weapon Proficiencies: Simple weapons only, but what do you care? You don’t need a weapon. You are the weapon.


Good Fortitude and Reflex save, Bad Will Save: Let’s start with the good news: between your general focus on Dexterity/Constitution and your strong save progression, you should basically never fail a Fortitude or Reflex save. And now the bad news: your Will save sucks, your Wisdom score isn’t likely to be high, and Will is by far the worst save to be bad at. You’re going to have to consider Iron Will and Improved Iron Will very strongly, and I’d recommend leaving your Wisdom score no lower than 12 at point buy. A Wisdom headband also wouldn’t be a bad option, unless you’re blowing all your money on a dual Dexterity/Constitution belt. Consider picking up the Elemental Whispers utility talent and selecting a familiar that grants a Will save bonus, too—every little bit helps.


Attribute Allocation: Constitution > Dexterity > Intelligence, Wisdom > Strength, Charisma will do it for nearly all kineticists. Constitution determines your save DCs, concentration check modifier, HP, Fortitude save, blast damage, and way more, and should be given top priority. Dexterity, meanwhile, determines your AC, Reflex saves, initiative, form infusion save DCs, skill checks, and more. Intelligence and Wisdom are somewhat negotiable; I’d prefer to keep Wisdom around 12, and Intelligence at 12 certainly doesn’t hurt if you can swing it. Strength and Charisma are more or less useless unless you’re running a Kinetic Blade build, in which case you’ll want to go with the kinetic knight archetype, wear heavy armor, and build for Constitution > Strength > Dexterity, Intelligence, Wisdom > Charisma.
________________
Class Features


        I used to just rate class features as I went along, but I’ve since discovered that I tend to make a lot of mistakes when I don’t read the rules text closely enough to be aware of every nuance. And for you, the reader, well, you’re going to have to take my word for it that there’s no embarrassment quite as acute as being called out for not knowing your own class features at the table. Additionally, I should mention that kineticist is widely considered to be one of the most poorly worded classes in all of Pathfinder. I’m therefore going to do a tl;dr section at the beginning of some class features that tries to sum up what the feature is about before we get into the nitty-gritty. All set? Let’s go.


        (1) Elemental Focus (Su): Every kineticist chooses an element that they want to master—think Avatar: The Last Airbender, but please, God, not the live-action Avatar. Each element will be covered in way more depth down in KIN 251: Elements and Wild Talents, so never fret. Your element is the most important way to determine what kind of role your kineticist is going to play, with elements like air providing great mobility, aether providing great utility and shenanigan potential, and earth providing great tanking and melee capabilities. It’s worthwhile to know now that you’re also not locked into one element if you don’t want to be: your expanded element class feature, which comes along at 7th level, will open up the playing field to different elements. Until 7th, though, you’re stuck with what you picked, so pick wisely.


At 1st level, a kineticist chooses one primary element on which to focus. This element determines how she accesses the raw power of the Ethereal Plane, and grants her access to specific wild talents (see below) and additional class skills. She can select aether (telekinesis), air (aerokinesis), earth (geokinesis), fire (pyrokinesis), or water (hydrokinesis). She gains her selected element’s basic utility wild talent (basic telekinesis, basic aerokinesis, basic geokinesis, basic pyrokinesis, or basic hydrokinesis; see page 23) as a bonus wild talent. See Elements on page 14 for the specific abilities granted by each element.


Not a lot to know here, rules-wise. You’ll get two class skills added onto your list, your basic kinesis utility talent for free, and the potential to select more wild talents off of either your element’s list or the universal list.


(1) Wild Talents: Here’s where things start to get a little linguistically and mechanically confusing in the kineticist. So soon? So soon. I think I would have just called these “kineticist talents,” which is right in line with rogue talents, investigator talents, slayer talents, etc. Calling them “wild talents” just throws me off somehow, and it doesn’t help that simple blasts, composite blasts, defense talents, form infusions, and substance infusions are also wild talents. You know what? I’m just going to make a table and put it below the tl;dr. 
Tl;dr: there are six kinds of wild talents: simple blasts, composite blasts, defense talents, form infusions, substance infusions, and utility talents. You can split these into four general categories: blasting (simple blasts and composite blasts), keeping you alive (defense talents), changing your blast (form infusions and substance infusions), and using spell-like abilities (utility talents). Kinetic blasts, as you’ve no doubt guessed, blow stuff up. Form infusions change the shape of your blasts, and substance infusions typically alter your blast’s damage or add on debuffs. Utility talents are, in most respects, just like spells: they’re supernatural and spell-like abilities that progress just as a sorcerer or oracle’s spells progress—you get 1st-level abilities at 1st level, 2nd-level abilities at 4th level, 3rd-level abilities at 6th level, etc., and buy them with the utility talent slots you get at every even level. Each of the six types of wild talents has its own save DC calculations, ways of mitigating burn, etc., so I’m going to try my best to summarize them in a table below.

And now for the rules text to make sure we don’t miss anything:


A kineticist can use wild talents—magical abilities similar to spells but drawn from the kineticist’s innate psychic talent and usable at will. Wild talents are typically spell-like abilities (though some are supernatural abilities), and take a standard action to use unless otherwise noted. A wild talent always has the elemental descriptor or descriptors (aether, air, earth, fire, or water) matching its Element entry. A wild talent that can be used with any of several elements gains the appropriate elemental descriptor when used with an element. For example, the wall wild talent gains the earth descriptor when used by a geokineticist.


        This is pretty much what I was saying earlier: wild talents are usually spell-like abilities that function nearly identically to spells. As a reminder, spell-like abilities have no somatic, verbal, or material components, but do target spell resistance unless the spell they imitate doesn’t target spell resistance; they also require concentration checks to use as normal, which in this case will use your kineticist level + your Constitution modifier. We’ll get into why concentration is important for kineticists in a little bit, but suffice it for now to say that it is. The last item to note is that all of your wild talents can be used at will—but only so long as you have enough HP left to acquire more burn. Composite blasts, form infusions, and substance infusions can all have their burn cost mitigated as you move on in level, but utility talents never can: if you’re running at maximum burn and want to use a utility talent that costs 1 burn, you’re up Schitt’s Creek without a paddle.


Every wild talent has an effective spell level. A kineticist can always select 1st-level wild talents, but she can select a wild talent of a higher level only if her kineticist level is at least double the wild talent’s effective spell level. Kinetic blast and defense wild talents are always considered to have an effective spell level equal to 1/2 the kineticist’s class level (to a maximum effective spell level of 9th at kineticist level 18th).


        This is why kineticists need to worry about concentration. Everytime you use a wild talent, you have to worry about two questions: first, is the talent I’m using a ranged attack like a simple or composite blast? If so, you’re going to provoke an attack of opportunity for making a ranged attack if any enemies are in range. Second, is the talent I’m using a spell-like ability? If so, you’re going to have to make a concentration check to cast defensively in order to avoid taking an attack of opportunity. (Ranged attacks will provoke anyway, and since you can’t provoke twice for the same action, no need to worry about casting defensively for kinetic blasts. Where concentration comes into play is when you want to use utility wild talents in combat while you’re in threatened range.) Just as low-level spells are always the easiest ones for casters to pull off when they’re under fire, so too are your lowest-level wild talents easier to pull off when you’re under fire. The concentration check to cast defensively is 15 + twice the spell level; if you’re casting a 1st-level wild talent, that’ll probably be pretty easy. If you’re casting a 9th-level wild talent, well, that gets harder. Note also that kinetic blasts, infusions, and defense talents are always at your maximum level. This is good and bad: on the one hand, a higher level means higher, scaling save DCs for your form and substance infusions; on the other, a higher level means higher, scaling concentration checks to cast defensively. You won’t just be at 15 + 2 by 20th level—you’ll be at 15 + 18 for literally every blast or defense talent you try to pull off in threatened range. Unless you have an extraordinarily compelling reason, any kineticist without the Kinetic Blade/Whip infusions should have some means of staying at range from a fight (spider climb, flight, extreme range, etc.), the Combat Casting feat, or (ideally) both. Most rounds, kineticists will get one attack. You’ve got to make it count.


Unless otherwise noted, the DC for a saving throw against a wild talent is equal to 10 + the wild talent’s effective spell level + the kineticist’s Constitution modifier. The kineticist uses her Constitution modifier on all concentration checks for wild talents.


        I’ve already noted this in the table above, but it’s all important. Your Constitution modifier is what determines your concentration checks and nearly all your save DCs, except for form infusions. Constitution is also what will keep you from going unconscious after taking only 1 or 2 points of burn. You must put as much as you can into Constitution.


In addition to the wild talents she gains from her other class features, at 2nd level and every 2 levels thereafter, a kineticist selects a new utility wild talent from the list of options available to her. A kineticist can select only universal wild talents or those that match her element (see Elemental Focus above). At 6th, 10th, and 16th levels, a kineticist can replace one of her utility wild talents with another wild talent of the same level or lower. She can’t replace a wild talent that she used to qualify for another of her wild talents.


Much like an oracle’s mystery determines which revelations are available to them, a kineticist’s element determines which wild talents are available to them. No matching element, no talent. You can switch out your utility talents, but it takes a while to get there, so just try to pick good stuff right off the bat.


(1) Burn (Ex): Burn is a kineticist’s greatest love and greatest hate. It fuels your powers, but also makes you more vulnerable. The mark of a skilled kineticist player lies almost entirely in how they manage their burn throughout an adventuring day. Tl;dr: burn is the cost of casting all those cool blasts and spell-like abilities we covered in the previous section. Every time you use a talent and are unable to bring its burn cost down to 0, you take 1 point of nonlethal damage per character level and per point of burn that cannot be healed by any means except a full night’s rest. Yikes. Class features like elemental overflow and elemental defense have better and better effects the more burn you’re carrying around, but the more burn you’re carrying around, the closer you put yourself to being OHKO’ed by monsters. Truly, with great power comes great risk. Wise kineticists will learn how to use their gather power, supercharge, infusion specialization, composite specialization, and 0-burn class features to extend their longevity throughout the day.


At 1st level, a kineticist can overexert herself to channel more power than normal, pushing past the limit of what is safe for her body by accepting burn. Some of her wild talents allow her to accept burn in exchange for a greater effect, while others require her to accept a certain amount of burn to use that talent at all. For each point of burn she accepts, a kineticist takes 1 point of nonlethal damage per character level. This damage can’t be healed by any means other than getting a full night’s rest, which removes all burn and associated nonlethal damage. Nonlethal damage from burn can’t be reduced or redirected, and a kineticist incapable of taking nonlethal damage can’t accept burn. A kineticist can accept only 1 point of burn per round. This limit rises to 2 points of burn at 6th level, and rises by 1 additional point every 3 levels thereafter. A kineticist can’t choose to accept burn if it would put her total number of points of burn higher than 3 + her Constitution modifier (though she can be forced to accept more burn from a source outside her control). A kineticist who has accepted burn never benefits from abilities that allow her to ignore or alter the effects she receives from nonlethal damage.


One of the reasons I love the Kinetic Healer talent is because it introduces your team to the concept of burn: there are tradeoffs associated with your powers, and if those tradeoffs can’t be managed and mitigated, you’ll need a full night’s rest before noon on an adventuring day. At any rate, kineticists need to know how nonlethal damage works: it scales up as you take damage, and if your HP ever falls below the current amount of nonlethal damage you’ve taken, you fall unconscious—you’re not dying, but you’re out of the fight. If it helps, you can think of each point of burn as 2 points of Constitution drain: you’re losing potential HP right off the top that can’t be healed until the drain/burn is gone. And don’t think you can weasel your way around this restriction, either—if you’re immune to nonlethal damage, you’re immune to burn. There’s no such thing as a free nova. The last thing to note is that burn has per-round limits about how many points you can take on board, as well as a maximum burn cap that only scales with your Constitution modifier. Remember how I said Constitution was your most important ability score? This is yet another reason why. You might not get to 8 burn every day as a 1st-level character with 20 Constitution, but you sure as shootin’ want that option.


To wax a bit philosophical, burn isn’t really good or bad. There are advantages to carrying around burn, and there are advantages to carrying around no burn. What you really want are options: options to take a lot of burn at once, and options to negate the burn of high-cost abilities. The former is accomplished only through levels—by the time you hit 18th level, you’ll be able to accept up to 6 points of burn in a single turn. That’s a ridiculous amount of nonlethal damage, but who knows? You might need that option one day. The latter is accomplished in myriad ways, mostly class features: gather power reduces blast and infusion costs, supercharge further reduces those costs, infusion and composite specialization reduce the cost of infusions and composite blasts, and certain favored class bonuses (lookin’ at you, gathlain) can also reduce the cost of a kineticist’s abilities. Ideally, you should make sure that you have the option to use your workhorse blasts and infusions with no burn cost. Utility talents and big nova rounds will always suck a little more out of you than you’d like, so you have to keep the basic combat applications of your blasts and infusions within reach and free at all times.


(1) Kinetic Blast (Sp): This is what you came to the class for, no? You wanted to be able to shoot people with bolts of crackling elemental energy at will. Welp, that’s what kinetic blasts are for! You came to the right place. Kinetic blasts have a limited range (30 ft.) and always use a standard action, barring any metakinesis. They’re generally treated as magical ranged weapons, except they can’t be enchanted and can’t be used with many of the feats you’d normally take with ranged weapons (Rapid Shot, e.g.). There’s a further delineation between physical blasts and energy blasts: physical blasts generally deal more damage and don’t target spell resistance, but target regular AC; energy blasts generally deal less damage and do target spell resistance, but target touch AC. Whichever type of blast you start the game with, you’ll probably want to branch out when you hit your 7th-level expanded element—more varied damage typing is never a bad thing, and you’ll want the ability to target both touch AC and regular AC as needed.


At 1st level, a kineticist gains a kinetic blast wild talent of her choice. This kinetic blast must be a simple blast that matches her element. As a standard action, the kineticist can unleash a kinetic blast at a single target up to a range of 30 feet. She must have at least one hand free to aim the blast (or one prehensile appendage, if she doesn’t have hands). All damage from a kinetic blast is treated as magic for the purpose of bypassing damage reduction. Kinetic blasts count as a type of weapon for the purpose of feats such as Weapon Focus. The kineticist is never considered to be wielding or gripping the kinetic blast (regardless of effects from form infusions; see Infusion on page 12), and she can’t use Vital Strike feats with kinetic blasts. Even the weakest kinetic blast involves a sizable mass of elemental matter or energy, so kinetic blasts always deal full damage to swarms of any size (though only area blasts deal extra damage to swarms). A readied kinetic blast can be used to counterspell any spell of equal or lower level that shares its descriptor. A kinetic blast that deals energy damage of any type (including force) has the corresponding descriptor. The various kinetic blasts, as well as additional rules for simple blasts, are described on pages 15–16.


Even though you’re only required to have one hand free in order to fire off a kinetic blast, you need both hands free in order to gather power, so most kineticists will be walking around with nothing wielded except the elements. Feats like Weapon Focus, Deadly Aim, Point-Blank Shot, Precise Shot, Weapon Finesse, and Power Attack are all valid candidates for your kinetic blasts, but not Rapid Shot or Vital Strike. It is what it is.


(1) Gather Power (Su): Did I mention that there’s very little that’s subtle about the kineticist? This “extremely loud, visible display” of elemental energy will make you a prime target for any monster with two eyes in its head (and quite a few with no eyes in their heads at all) but it’s an absolutely necessary component to the kineticist. Why? It reduces the burn cost of your blasts, including metakinesis. You’re generally asking for trouble if you gather power with a full-round or full-round + move action (that’s a great way to get hit, fail your concentration check, and immediately take a massive amount of burn) so my recommendation would be to stick with gathering power as a move action. 5-foot step, gather power, blast, rinse, repeat. It ain’t subtle, but we already covered what kineticists think of subtlety, no?


If she has both hands free (or all of her prehensile appendages free, for unusual kineticists), a kineticist can gather energy or elemental matter as a move action. Gathering power creates an extremely loud, visible display in a 20-foot radius centered on the kineticist, as the energy or matter swirls around her. Gathering power in this way allows the kineticist to reduce the total burn cost of a blast wild talent she uses in the same round by 1 point. The kineticist can instead gather power for 1 full round in order to reduce the total burn cost of a blast wild talent used on her next turn by 2 points (to a minimum of 0 points). If she does so, she can also gather power as a move action during her next turn to reduce the burn cost by a total of 3 points. If the kineticist takes damage during or after gathering power and before using the kinetic blast that releases it, she must succeed at a concentration check (DC = 10 + damage taken + effective spell level of her kinetic blast) or lose the energy in a wild surge that forces her to accept a number of points of burn equal to the number of points by which her gathered power would have reduced the burn cost. This ability can never reduce the burn cost of a wild talent below 0 points.


Some key points from the rules text: you need both hands free to gather power, even though you need only one free to shoot your blast; gather power only applies to simple blasts or composite blasts (and metakinesis, although that’s not explicitly stated here); bad things happen if someone causes you to take damage while you’re gathering power. This, by the way, GMs, is how you destroy a kineticist: give a monster Step Up (Following Step or Step Up and Strike are optional, but nice to have), get them next to the kineticist, ready an action to attack if they gather power (Vital Strike is preferable so you can amp up the concentration check DC), then just follow their 5-foot step. If they try to gather power, you’ll hit them with your readied action and probably cause them to take a bunch of burn; if they try to blast you, they’ll provoke an attack of opportunity for firing a ranged weapon in melee; if they try to move away without Acrobatics, they’ll provoke an attack of opportunity for moving out of a threatened square; if they withdraw, they’re not a threat that round anyway. Players, this is why you invest in Acrobatics: you need every means possible to escape melee aggressors.


(3) Infusion (Su): As I mentioned previously, infusions are split up into two categories, substance infusions and form infusions. Mechanically, they function more or less identically, with the exception that the save DCs for form infusions are Dexterity-based and the save DCs for substance infusions are Constitution-based. Not sure why Paizo felt that little tidbit was necessary, but them’s the breaks. Anyway, infusions are by far the most costly items on a kineticist’s plate: if you chose a 4-burn form infusion and a 4-burn substance infusion for your blast, you’d have 8 burn in total on top of whatever blast and metakinesis shenanigans you had going on. In practice, of course, you can’t take 8 burn in a single turn, even at 20th level, and with your infusion specialization class feature coming up in a few levels, you’ll gradually knock down the cost of using infusions. But these are the bread and butter for your blasts: the blasts themselves and the metakinesis that you use on those blasts will only ever affect damage and damage type; infusions affect how the blasts are shaped, how many targets they can hit, whether there are any debuffs or interesting effects associated with them, etc. You don’t have to use an infusion of either type on a blast, of course, but once you get infusion specialization, you’re essentially leaving extra effects on the table if you don’t—so you may as well. The cost of infusions can be reduced through gather power and supercharge, per this FAQ.


At 1st level, a kineticist gains an infusion wild talent from the list of options available based on her elemental focus. She gains additional infusions at 3rd, 5th, 9th, 11th, 13th, 17th, and 19th levels. By using infusions along with her kinetic blasts, a kineticist can alter her kinetic blasts to suit her needs. Infusions come in two types, each of which changes a kinetic blast differently: a substance infusion causes an additional effect, while a form infusion causes the kinetic blast to manifest in a different way. Each infusion can alter only certain kinds of kinetic blasts, which are listed in its Associated Blasts entry. Each time the kineticist uses one of her kinetic blast wild talents, she can apply up to one associated form infusion and up to one associated substance infusion. Some infusions change the action required to activate a kinetic blast or entirely transform the kinetic blast’s normal effects. The burn cost listed in each infusion’s Burn entry is added to the burn cost of the kinetic blast the infusion modifies.


Only one form infusion and one substance infusion can be applied to a given kinetic blast, and only certain types of infusions can be used on certain types of blasts. Your choice of element will determine a lot about your menu of available infusions, as will the specific simple blast type you chose when you picked up your element. Don’t worry about this now—we’ll get into it in much greater detail in the Elements and Wild Talents section further down. Take note that you can acquire extra infusions (along with utility talents) using the Extra Wild Talent feat, although your effective kineticist level suffers.


The DC for a save against an infusion is based on the associated kinetic blast’s effective spell level, not the level of the infusion. The DCs for form infusions are calculated using the kineticist’s Dexterity modifier instead of her Constitution modifier. When a kineticist modifies a kinetic blast with a form infusion and a substance infusion that both require saving throws, each target first attempts a saving throw against the form infusion. If a target succeeds and a successful save negates the infusion’s effects, the entire kinetic blast is negated; otherwise, the target then attempts a saving throw against the substance infusion. If a kineticist’s form and substance infusions both alter the kinetic blast’s damage, apply the substance infusion’s alteration first.


It’s important to remember that your infusion save DCs scale with your kinetic blast level. If you’re a 1st-level kineticist, the DC will be 11 + Dexterity or Constitution; if you’re 20th-level, 19 + Dexterity or Constitution. If you’re worried about the clause negating form infusions entirely, don’t: form infusions typically have either no save or a Reflex save for half damage, although substance infusions tend to run the gamut. The worst-case scenario of a save negating your entire blast’s effects sounds scary, but there are generally very few form infusions that allow a save to negate all of the effects. Don’t sweat it too much.


At 5th, 11th, and 17th levels, a kineticist can replace one of her infusions with another infusion of the same effective spell level or lower. She can’t replace an infusion that she used to qualify for another of her wild talents.


No, sorry, you can’t swap out Extended Range for Extreme Range and leave it there. No prerequisite swapping. You know the rules.


        (2) Elemental Defense (Su): Unlike utility talents, infusions, and kinetic blasts, each element has only one defense talent, and they get it automatically at 2nd level. These are generally scaling, burn-free abilities that can be activated and deactivated as immediate actions, which makes them perfect for responding to sudden threats that you didn’t expect (as long as you’re not flat-footed). Nearly all defense talents also offer improved effects if you voluntarily take 1 or more points of burn. These are neither kinetic blasts (hence, unaffected by gather power or supercharge) nor infusions (hence, unaffected by infusion specialization) so there’s no real way to mitigate this burn—you just have to take it. Some elements tend to have quite powerful defense talents, earth being a particular standout, so consider the Expanded Defense utility talent at 8th level if you’d like to pick up your expanded element’s defense talent. Taking as much burn as you can for your elemental defense at the start of the day is a completely legitimate (and often recommended) tactic for improving both your defenses (via elemental defense) and offenses (via elemental overflow) quickly.


        (3) Elemental Overflow (Ex): Elemental overflow is where the kineticist starts playing with fire—literally. You remember I said that burn was both your bosom-est friend and bitterest enemy, right? When I introduced it, it probably seemed like just an enemy, pouring huge amounts of nonlethal damage into your PC with no return on investment. Well, elemental overflow is that return on investment. For every point of burn you’re carrying around in a day, you get a +1 bonus to hit and a +2 bonus to damage on all your blasts (to a maximum of your level divided by 3); beyond a certain point, you also start gaining bonuses to physical attributes, chances to negate crits and sneak attack damage, and more. Basically, the hotter you keep the engine running, the more dangerous you are to enemies—but also to yourself. It’s a cool mechanic that encourages you to keep walking a dangerous line with the level of burn you carry around.


At 3rd level, a kineticist’s body surges with energy from her chosen element whenever she accepts burn, causing her to glow with a nimbus of fire, weep water from her pores, or experience some other thematic effect. In addition, she receives a bonus on her attack rolls with kinetic blasts equal to the total number of points of burn she currently has, to a maximum bonus of +1 for every 3 kineticist levels she possesses. She also receives a bonus on damage rolls with her kinetic blast equal to double the bonus on attack rolls. The kineticist can suppress the visual effects of elemental overflow by concentrating for 1 full round, but doing so suppresses all of this ability’s other benefits, as well. The next time the kineticist uses any wild talent, the visual effects and benefits return instantly.


        If my math is right, this should equate to +6 to hit and +12 to damage for carrying 6 burn at 18th level. Not bad, especially when you consider that all that damage will multiply on a crit. It’s not easy to hide, though, which is another reason why kineticists don’t make great party faces—as soon as you use any wild talent, you’re instantly surrounded with that nimbus of fire again, and everybody knows that they’re not talking to Josephine Schmo. Have I mentioned yet that very little about the kineticist is subtle?


As a kineticist’s body becomes more and more suffused with her element, she begins to gain more powerful benefits. Starting at 6th level, whenever she has at least 3 points of burn, the kineticist gains a +2 size bonus to two physical ability scores of her choice. She also gains a chance to ignore the effects of a critical hit or sneak attack equal to 5% × her current number of points of burn. At 11th level, whenever the kineticist has at least 5 points of burn, these bonuses increase to a +4 size bonus to one physical ability score of her choice and a +2 size bonus to each of her other two physical ability scores. At 16th level, whenever the kineticist has at least 7 points of burn, these bonuses increase to a +6 size bonus to one physical ability score of her choice, a +4 size bonus to a second physical ability score of her choice, and a +2 size bonus to the remaining physical ability score.


        Mmmm, tasty attribute bonuses. To some extent, these bonuses actually help you “buy back” some of the burn you take on in order to attain them: a +6 size bonus to Constitution (that’s almost always what your highest bonus of any type should go toward) will give you the equivalent of 3 extra points of burn in addition to boosting your damage, save DCs, Fortitude save, HP, etc. Always apply your secondary bonus to Dexterity unless you’re playing a Kinetic Blade/Whip build, and even then only if you haven’t gone the Weapon Finesse route. Strength should come last in almost every scenario. No word on whether the bonuses to avoid sneak attack or crit damage stack with effects like light fortification armor—check with your GM to see whether they think that’s reasonable, but I would think they don’t stack.


        (5) Infusion Specialization (Ex): Infusions are the costliest items on your menu, so it’s cool to be able to reduce their cost. By the absolute end of your career, you’ll be able to use a 3-burn form infusion and a 3-burn substance infusion without taking on any additional burn, all through this class feature alone. I don’t think it would have particularly broken the bank for Paizo to have created a ⅙ infusion specialization favored class bonus, but maybe I’m biased in favor of the players. At any rate, Extended Range or Kinetic Blade will probably be what most 5th-level players use infusion specialization on—what follows will depend on your elements, blasts, and selected infusions.


        (7) Metakinesis (Su): Metakinesis slaps metamagic feats onto your kinetic blasts. Bigger, faster, stronger: metakinesis is what takes your kinetic blast’s damage from linear to exponential. You’ll only be able to empower your kinetic blasts at first, which boosts the damage of your blast by 50% over whatever you rolled, but you can eventually maximize, quicken, or even double up your blasts, unloading huge amounts of damage in exchange for huge amounts of burn. Two salient points: first, your gather power class feature does affect metakinesis, which is why the gathlain favored class bonus is so monstrously overpowered. With supercharge, a move action to gather power, and two reductions in burn from 12 levels of gathlain favored class bonus, gathlains can fire off empowered maximized simple blasts or empowered composite blasts at no burn cost, all day long. Crazytown bananapants. Second, the maximized option that opens up to you at 9th level is actually a cleverly disguised trap if you use it in isolation. Empower is going to add 50% to whatever damage you roll, including the level-dependent static damage attached to each of your simple and composite blasts (per this FAQ); 150% of 3.5 average damage per d6 leaves you with 5.25 damage per d6, plus an additional 0.5 from the static damage. Maximize, meanwhile, treats all your damage dice as if you had rolled 6 on a d6 for double the burn cost. 5.75 vs. 6 is definitely not worth that extra cost. Once you can reliably empower + maximize, however, you’ve got a ballgame and can do some truly disgusting damage on single shots. For a more in-depth mathematical analysis of how empower and maximize shake out, check out this RPG StackExchange thread. 


At 5th level, a kineticist gains the ability to alter her kinetic blasts as if with metamagic feats by accepting burn. By accepting 1 point of burn, she can empower her kinetic blast (as if using Empower Spell). At 9th level, by accepting 2 points of burn, she can maximize her kinetic blast as if using Maximize Spell. At 13th level, by accepting 3 points of burn, she can quicken her kinetic blast as if using Quicken Spell. At 17th level, by accepting 4 points of burn, the kineticist can use her kinetic blast twice with the same standard action, or swift action if she also uses metakinesis to quicken the blast. When she uses a double kinetic blast, all modifications, such as metakinesis and infusions, apply to both of the blasts, but the kineticist needs to pay the burn cost only once.


        The quicken benefit you get at 13th level allows you to attack twice in a single round, which at first appears to be just a straight 100% increase on your DPR. Keep in mind, however, that gather power and supercharge only allow you to reduce the burn cost of one of these shots. You can try to offload a big blast right after you gather power, of course, but most of that burn reduction is going to go toward mitigating the quicken metakinesis itself, rather than improving your damage with empower, maximize, or a composite blast. There’s no such thing as a free lunch. Without gather power for your swift-action blast, you’re probably going to have to stick with a simple blast, which won’t deal nearly as much damage. All these problems get fixed at 17th level, when you can let loose two blasts for the price of one. (Well, the price of one, plus 4 points of burn.) If this is all starting to make your eyes cross a bit, don’t worry: we’ll wrap up with a nice table at the end to summarize your path through all this madness.


        (6) Internal Buffer (Su): Internal buffer is basically a “burn layaway” program. You take some of the burn that you have today, sock it away in a savings account (that’s your internal buffer), and then have it available to spend at a later date when you really need to tap some backup power. Once you rest, the burn goes away, but the internal buffer remains, allowing you to temporarily exceed your per-round burn limit and really turn a baddie into paste on the wall.


At 6th level, a kineticist’s study of her body and the elemental forces that course through it allow her to form an internal buffer to store extra energy. The buffer starts empty and doesn’t replenish each day, but the kineticist can accept 1 point of burn to add 1 point to the buffer as a full-round action, to a maximum of 1 point total. This maximum increases to 2 points at 11th level and to 3 points at 16th level. Once the kineticist adds points to her buffer, they remain indefinitely until she spends them. When she would otherwise accept burn, a kineticist can spend 1 point from her buffer to avoid accepting 1 point of burn. She cannot spend more than 1 point from her buffer in this way for a single wild talent. Points spent from the internal buffer don’t activate elemental overflow or add to its effects. Similarly, this buffer can be used to exceed the limit on the number of points of burn the kineticist can accept in a single turn.


        If you can, you should always be running around with a fully stocked internal buffer. Think of it like an emergency fund in your savings account: you don’t want to use it, but when the turd hits the turbine, you might have to. Better in those moments to have the backup available. Because you can’t ever mitigate more than 1 point of burn with internal buffer, it’s great for items that have fixed, low costs. If you’re a 12th-level gathlain who wants to let loose an empowered, maximized composite blast, you can reduce a 5-burn action by 4 with gather power and your internal buffer can handle the rest. If you took the Kinetic Invocation feat and want to cast a spell but don’t necessarily want to spend burn on it, the internal buffer is one of the few mechanisms that can reduce the cost of a utility wild talent. (Yes, I know you’re not actually reducing the cost, but you’re not feeling the effects of the burn you still had to take.) It’s nice to have one more layer of burn management available.


        (7) Expanded Element (Su): Ah, I love expanded element! It’s like giving an oracle a second mystery, or a sorcerer a second bloodline—lots of potential. Let’s run over the list of things you get with your expanded element: you get 1) a second simple blast, 2) any and all composite blasts whose prerequisites you meet at 7th level, and 3) your “basic ____kinesis” talent. You do not get any extra class skills (absent the Elemental Knowledge feat) or your new element’s defensive talent (absent the Expanded Defense talent).
        The question of whether to go deep into your initial element or go broad into other elements has deep implications for both the mechanics of your class and what role you’ll be able to play. An aetherkineticist who doesn’t then go on to a different element, for example, will have a hard time dealing damage effectively, while a pyrokineticist might need to expand into a different element in order to be able to beat the elemental resistances and fire immunities that are common at high levels. There are cases to be made for both single-element kineticists and multi-element kineticists, so it all comes down to your build priorities and role within the party.


        (11) Supercharge (Su): Anything that mitigates burn gets a blue. Period. Gathlain are once again laughing all the way to the bank with their favored class bonus, which basically gives them the equivalent of supercharge once every 6 levels, but for everyone else, this is like getting an oxygen tank midway through a marathon.


        (16) Composite Specialization (Su): Composite blasts always cost 2 burn, so reducing that number to 1 is most welcome. Pity it comes online so late.


        (19) Metakinetic Master (Su): Yet another burn reduction ability that comes online far too late. Shame.


        (20) Omnikinesis (Su): I’ll give this to omnikinesis: it makes it really easy to blow past elemental resistances, immunities, and DR. If I haven’t helped you figure that out by 20th level, though, I think I’ve done my job poorly. As far as changing your wild talents, sure, it’s decent, but I think you’re likely to have your best choices taken by 20th level. You used to not have any option about omnikinesis, but with the release of Chronicle of Legends, there are actually some much better options available...


Alternate Capstones: Chronicle of Legends released alternate capstones for all characters who didn’t trade away their capstone through an archetype. Some of these are probably a bit weaker than omnikinesis, but several are considerably stronger. Here are your options:


* Perfect Body, Flawless Mind (Ex): It’s just so hard to beat massive attribute bonuses, isn’t it? An extra 8 points of Constitution give you 4 extra points of burn, 80 extra HP, a +4 to your Fortitude save, more kinetic blast damage, better concentration checks, more spell resistance penetration, easier access to elemental overflow bonuses, higher save DCs from your substance infusions and utility talents...it’s hard to overstate the benefits of huge Constitution bonuses. Dexterity is obviously a choice as well, and you can feel free to partition those bonuses however you like, but it’s definitely a secondary stat for kineticists. To put this into monetary perspective, a tome that grants a +5 innate bonus to one attribute score costs 137,500 gp. Scaling that up to a +8, you’re getting roughly 220,000 gp worth of benefits here that stack with stat tomes. I mean, you’re basically a god at this point, so the issue is moot, but still. Wow.
* The Boss (Ex): The boss is great from a roleplaying perspective, and makes a fitting sunset for any PC, taking over your own kingdom, arcane academy, etc., but it’s not combat-functional, per se.
* With This Sword (Ex): With This Sword is weaker for kineticists because they don’t rely on conventional weaponry to deal damage, but even so, I suppose you could improve a suit of armor or something. A flat 100,000 gp is plenty good, but again, not even close to Perfect Body, Flawless Mind. Paizo really broke the mold with that one.
* Unbridled Power (Su): Unbridled power? More like unwarmed porridge. This is a +7 damage bonus for one energy blast or a +9 damage bonus for one physical blast that still doesn’t let you beat energy immunities. Blech. For comparison, a kineticist’s diadem will give you +2d6 damage for 50,000 gp, less than a quarter of what Perfect Body, Flawless Mind is worth. Nope nope nope.
* Won’t Stay Dead (Ex): With gajillions of HP and a monumentally high Fortitude save, kineticists probably won’t be in immediate danger of death very often, and it’s not like resurrection magic is uncommon at this level. Pass.
________________
Burn Reduction


        We’ve already covered the fact that kineticists want the option to reduce burn as much as possible, so here we’ll sum up all the class features that can do so, along with estimates of how much burn the optimal damage at each level will cost you. You’re going to notice a few points as we go forward:


   1. Quicken metakinesis is often not the most effective use of your burn. Quicken is monumentally expensive, given that you’re also going to have to spend burn on a composite blast if you want to deal any damage at all; you’re often left using a simple blast after quicken, which gets beaten easily by the empower + maximize combo applied to a composite blast.
   2. Gathlain freaking rock. I know, who would have guessed that these little leafy goobers would be the dominant class for kineticists? Removing 3 additional burn each and every time you gather power leaves you with a -5 at 18th level. That’s like a free empower + maximize on every single blast! Every. Single. Blast. They start to pull ahead at 7th level when they can use composite blasts instead of empowered simple blasts, and the lead only grows from there. By the time you get to 19th level, gathlain can do literally double the damage of normal kineticists. 270 damage per turn with no burn is crazy, crazy good, and they can burst up as high as 555+ damage if they take 6 burn in a single turn. I would strongly advise that GMs ban the gathlain favored class bonus from their games. This single change will help normalize power greatly across races.
   3. Life is pretty slow for kineticists before 5th level. Kineticists’ damage potential grows exponentially as they add on more infusion specializations, metakinesis, and composite blasts, but you really don’t have any of those tools available at early levels.

Sustainable DPR & Peak DPR


        Sustainable DPR is the amount of damage that a kineticist can deal without taking any burn at all. This will mean simple blasts with the occasional gather power + infusion combo at early levels, and progressing gradually into composite blasts and metakinesis as elements like supercharge and composite specialization come online in later levels. Peak DPR, meanwhile, is the amount of damage that a kineticist can deal if they push themselves to the absolute limit of their per-round burn. Although this number scales up to 6 burn by the end of the game, you’ll never get to the point where you can fire off two doubled, empowered, maximized blasts in one turn. Can’t be done—although gathlain can get pretty close. This chart is intended to be element-agnostic, so everyone (regardless of infusions or utility talents) can play along; it ignores Constitution modifiers and elemental overflow being applied to damage and assumes that everyone is using physical blasts. Damage for energy blasts will be lower, obviously, but will also hit more reliably in the endgame.
