To Expand or Not to Expand?


        At 7th level, every kineticist needs to decide whether to use their expanded element class feature to either move into a different element than the one they started play with or double down on the element they initially chose. Since you won’t get the chance to make another choice until 15th level, this is a really big choice that usually comes down to the tradeoff between versatility (expanding) and power (not expanding). Let’s argue in favor of the pros and cons of each approach below.


Expansion: Pros


   * A whole new set of utility talents. In my mind, this is the main reason to expand your element. Utility talents, when properly cherrypicked, can be incredibly powerful—even when you’re selecting lower-level talents using feats like Extra Wild Talent. Grabbing things like Wings of Air, Telekinetic Invisibility, etc. can be a massive shift in power that you don’t want to ignore.
   * More variable damage or blast typing. More variable damage typing and blast types (physical or energy) means more tools for dealing with energy resistance, energy immunity, spell resistance, and DR. You want that! Kineticists don’t make as many attacks as other classes, so failures to beat AC, DR, energy resistances, or spell resistance feel correspondingly more devastating. It’s that feeling of “Great, I just wasted my entire turn.” Expanding your element rewards smart, tactical play instead of brute force solutions, and that’s to be commended.
   * The option for another defensive talent. If you took Expanded Defense, you’ll get your expanded element’s defensive talent; this, depending on your choice of expanded element, can be a huge deal. Earth, with DR/adamantine? Huge deal. Fire, with a few points of fire damage dealt out to melee attackers? Much smaller deal.


Expansion: Cons


   * No 7th-level infusion. 7th level is when a lot of AoE options begin to open up for kineticists, but if you expand, you don’t get an infusion at 7th. This can be a big deal if you’re trying to move away from your role as a single-target striker and into more...explosive varieties of blasting.
   * Limited infusion options for composite blasts, and sometimes limited utility talents. The price that most dual-element composite blasts pay is that they don’t get access to a bunch of the coolest and most iconic utility talents or infusions that an element has to offer. Metal Blast (Earth + Earth) and Blue Flame Blast (Fire + Fire) are great examples of this precept: Metal Blast gets Rare-Metal Infusion, Clockwork Heart, and Spark of Innovation; Blue Flame Blast gets Detonation, Explosion, and Unraveling Infusion. In many cases, you simply can’t get those infusions or utility talents without focusing on a single element, and in general the stronger the infusions or utility talents available to the single-element builds, the more you’ll want to consider not expanding. Elements that offer basically zero return on investment (lookin’ at you, aether) should almost never be single-element builds.
   * Tighter builds. With more potential utility talents and infusions comes more constraints on your feat, infusion, and utility talent slots. Sticking with an element you already know, while it can cause you to stagnate in the long run, also lets you breathe a little easier without feeling like you’re bursting at the seams with all the new abilities you want to get your grubby little mitts on.
________________


Physical or Energy Blasts?


        An enduring question of novice kineticist players is, “Which is better, physical blasts or energy blasts?” The rather maddening answer is that neither of them is better—or, rather, that both of them can be good depending on the circumstances. Most kineticists would be well-served by having access to both types of blasts, but we can get into the specifics below.


Physical: The Argument


        The thing that new kineticist players tend to notice first is that physical blasts deal a lot more damage than energy blasts, which make them the weapon of choice for blowing away single minions or lieutenants before moving on to the boss. Most composite blasts are physical blasts, as well, which makes it likely that your highest damage dealer at 7th level will be a physical blast. Physical blasts have to hit normal AC and are subject to DR, however, which can be a serious problem in higher levels when both high AC numbers and exotic types of DR become prevalent—remember, your blasts count as magic weapons for the purposes of bypassing DR, but can’t do anything about DR/cold iron, adamantine, alignment, etc. unless you have some very specific infusions on board. Slightly less obvious is that physical blasts tend to do a lot less area of effect (AoE) damage. Often AoE form infusions will grant energy blasts full damage with a Reflex save (commonly) for half, while physical blasts will get half damage with a Reflex save for quarter. Not altogether great. Unlike their cousins the energy blasts, however, physical blasts are not subject to energy resistances, energy immunities, or spell resistance, which can be incredibly troublesome at high levels.


Energy: The Argument


        Energy blasts deal less damage than physical blasts, but that’s actually a bit misleading to say. Although it’s true that energy blasts deal less damage when they hit, they will hit much more reliably, as they target touch AC instead of regular AC. In high-level play against massive, lumbering monsters with abysmal Dexterity scores, targeting touch AC (as any Gunslinger player will tell you) is basically a guaranteed hit, making attacks like Fire Blast or Blue Flame Blast incredibly consistent performers in the damage department; the generally improved AoE damage that energy blasts do also helps keep them relevant as destroyers of multiple weaker opponents on a crowded battlefield. If energy blasts have one weakness in high-level play, though, it’s that enemies tend to be either resistant or immune to the energy damage that they deal: outsiders in particular are famous for their resistances and immunities, and if you don’t have the right energy damage type prepared in your blasts, you’re not going to perform well against them. Dedicated energy blasters such as the fire element have infusions and utility talents to help mitigate energy resistances and immunities, but other elements with energy blasts (water, air, etc.) generally do not, making it necessary for you to think about how you will contribute in encounters where your energy damage is useless or suboptimal. Lastly, energy blasts are subject to spell resistance, which becomes a going concern around the entry into double-digit levels (9th-11th) and stays concerning for the rest of the game. Spell Penetration and Greater Spell Penetration are must-have feats for dedicated energy blasters, as these feats allow them to bypass spell resistance more readily.


The Takeaway


        Ultimately, every kineticist needs to be prepared for times when their typical blasting routine doesn’t work for them. Whether due to potent DR or SR, high AC, strong resistances or energy immunities, etc., there will come a time when your blasts simply fail you. Have multiple back-up plans! Do you have buffs or debuffs available from your infusions or utility talents? Can you shape the battlefield with control spells? Can you pull a melee weapon and act as a flanking partner or aid another spammer? Can you use wondrous or mundane items, wands, or scrolls to contribute to the team? Think about what types of enemies are most likely to resist your go-to tactics and move on from there.
🔥 Class Features (3PP)
KIN 102: Legendary Kineticist
Class Chassis


[3PP] LKineticist Class Features


        The legendary kineticist (link in the title above) changes quite a bit about the class, in (mostly) good ways; changing how burn and utility talent save DCs work are particularly welcome additions to the class. We’re just going to go down the line of class features in the legendary kineticist to see how things have changed.


(1) Class Chassis: Your HD, BAB, class skills, save progressions, and attribute allocations will all remain the same for a legendary kineticist, but you gain access to medium armor. It’s a small fix, but I think it’s a lovely one. If you’re trying to build a tankier melee kineticist who dumped Dexterity (say, a geokineticist who makes extensive use of Kinetic Blade) then your only real option is to take the kinetic knight archetype and stomp around in heavy armor. Medium armor being given to the class by default lets you emphasizes Dexterity a little less if that’s your game, and makes it easier to build a non-kinetic knight who’s still pretty beefy on defense. Beefense.


        (1) Elemental Focus (Su): No changes from the base class.


        (1) Wild Talents: Two salient changes here. First, the language about only kinetic blast and defense talents having an effective spell level equal to half the kineticist’s level is removed and replaced with the more generic “wild talents.” This means that your utility talent save DCs now scale with your level! Put in perspective, this is probably too powerful for most games, but it’s worth noting that increasing the effective spell level of utility talents means that their concentration check DCs also scale, similar to the way that kineticists’ concentration check DCs scale for blast, defense, and infusion wild talents. Is it balanced? Still no, but it’s at least leaning closer to balance. Second, legendary kineticists get one more utility talent replacement than base kineticists—6th/10th/16th vs. 6th/10th/14th/18th. Not game-breaking, but a nice little perk.


        (1) Burn (Ex): This is a major change to the way the kineticist does business. For every point of burn a legendary kineticist has on board, they don’t take nonlethal damage—they take a stacking -1 penalty to all Strength- and Dexterity-based skill and ability checks (except initiative). Losing the nonlethal damage associated with burn completely overhauls the class. Penalties to Strength- and Dexterity-based skill and ability checks can be bad, of course, but they’re not nearly as bad as nonlethal damage, so legendary kineticists will probably be significantly more liberal about when they use burn—and how much they use. Kineticists tend to have the “high floor, low ceiling” complex where they’re quite good in the early game when spell resources are limited, but then fall off in usefulness as their lack of versatility begins to take its toll in the later game. Between the changes in burn and the addition of battle burn, legendary kineticists get to raise that ceiling significantly. Still, these changes to burn are likely too powerful (again) for most GMs to consider allowing.


        (1) Kinetic Blast (Sp): No changes from the base class.


        (1) Gather Power (Su): The legendary kineticist is altered so that you only need one hand free to gather power. An interesting buff, and easily missed if you’re not doing a very close reading of the rules texts, but one that opens up kineticists to use shields, hold wands, keep a light melee weapon in hand for attacks of opportunity, etc. Quite nice.


        (1) Infusion (Su): Two salient changes. First, legendary kineticists get two more infusions than base kineticists. Since the legendary chassis changes your expanded element levels to 6th and 14th, rather than 7th and 15th, you now just get an infusion at every odd level; legendary kineticists who choose not to branch out into a different element still receive a bonus infusion as usual, so the alterations to this class feature don’t change the calculus of “Should I expand or not expand?” Second, legendary kineticists get to swap out their infusions more frequently, from 5th/11th/17th in the base chassis to 4th/8th/12th/16th/20th in the legendary chassis. Both of these are excellent changes for the kineticist. You’re still limited by burn (both how much you can take in a single turn and how much you can take in a day) but you’ll have more variety to work with. Much needed to improve the kineticist’s versatility on the battlefield.


(2) Elemental Defense (Su): No changes from the base class. 


(3) Elemental Overflow (Ex): No changes from the base class, which is good, because elemental overflow didn’t need to be more powerful than it already was.


(4) Battle Burn (Su): An amazing class feature for the legendary kineticist, and one that the base kineticist certainly doesn’t have. Battle burn is essentially consequence-free burn: you don’t take any penalties when you accept battle burn and it goes away after five minutes of rest, but in exchange you can’t use any infusion or utility talent that lasts more than five minutes, and you can’t take more than 1 point of battle burn per round. Again, fabulous! Battle burn is perfect for short-duration buffing utility talents, battlefield control utility talents, costly infusions that are a once-per-encounter type of deal, and more, and just gives kineticists the option of making more interesting choices in combat other than “five-foot step, gather power, blast, repeat.” Boring for both players and GMs.


(5) Infusion Specialization (Ex): No changes from the base class.


(5) Metakinesis (Su): Here’s another design choice that’s so freaking obvious and so brilliant. So you know how I said that maximize was rarely the right choice for your metakinesis, since empower almost always is more cost-effective for increasing damage? Well, the legendary kineticist just gets rid of maximize and gives you persistent instead. With this change, kineticists can get much, much better at debuffing! Persistent Spell is a favorite of debuffers and blasters for its ability to make enemies roll successful saves over again, taking the lower result, and it does excellent work here by forcing enemies to reroll their saves against substance and form infusions. Now there’s actually a meaningful opportunity cost to your blast and infusion choices: do you want to put a bunch of debuffing infusions and Persistent Spell on a simple blast to trash a bunch of enemies’ saves or lay down status conditions? Do you want to stick with empower and double on a composite blast to blow away a single target with massive damage? Diversifying your metakinesis options is fantastic for making legendary kineticists actually use their brains during combat, rather than just giving them the hammer of massive damage and forcing them to solve every problem by blowing stuff up.


(6/14) Expanded Element (Su): Expanded element gets pushed up one level in the legendary kineticist, essentially trading places with internal buffer. This seems like a reasonable trade to me. Only two differences, one big and one little: the big difference is that legendary kineticists aren’t treated as though they were four levels lower in their expanded element when determining which wild talents they can get. Again, such a minor change, with such big results! Removing that limitation frees kineticists up to build more flexibly, and no longer have to plan their way through leveling quite as much. If you want to play an earth/void build, you don’t have to worry about which to pick at 1st level—barring any considerations about the defense talent, you can simply pick whichever you like and know that 6th level will still allow you to pick your expanded element’s 3rd-level wild talents. The little difference is that legendary kineticists who choose to deepen their knowledge of their primary element can only pick an infusion as a bonus wild talent, not a utility wild talent. It’s okay! Not a huge loss.


(7) Internal Buffer (Su): The legendary kineticist’s internal buffer gets pushed back a level and starts with 1 point of available burn in it every day, instead of the base kineticist’s 0, but is otherwise unchanged. Remember that you can’t use internal buffer in conjunction with battle burn.


(11) Supercharge (Su): The biggest change about supercharge in the legendary kineticist chassis is that you can now gather power as a swift action to reduce the burn cost of a blast/infusion wild talent by 1. This, of course, is ridiculous in the hands of gathlain, and I’d recommend once again that any GM ban gathlain kineticists; for normal kineticists, however, it’s just one more bit of versatility added to the action economy. In particular, it allows the void, fire, and aether elements to use their pseudo-flight utility talents as a move action while still gathering power as a swift action, which is a big benefit for them.


(16) Composite Specialization (Su): No changes from the base class.


(19) Metakinetic Master (Su): No changes from the base class, except that persistent replaces maximize in your metakinesis lineup.


(20) Omnikinesis (Su): You can’t use omnikinesis with battle burn, but otherwise the class feature is unchanged.




[3PP] LKineticist Summary


        Below are the salient changes that the legendary kineticist makes over the base class. If you’re a GM who’s getting intimidated by the amount of high ratings I’ve given those abilities and is reconsidering whether to allow players to use this alternate class, please know that almost all of the ratings are based around abilities that increase versatility, rather than power. If you’re worried about your monsters getting splattered by huge blasts, the legendary kineticist actually makes that less likely by giving players viable alternatives to blasting, like battlefield control, buffing, debuffing, and tanking. Give it a try in a few one-shots and see how it works out for you! Most importantly, play one and see whether you like the feel of it. I know I like it a lot more than the base kineticist.


   * Gains medium armor. Good for hybrid Strength / Dexterity builds, and makes the kinetic knight archetype less necessary for melee kineticists.
   * Scales utility talent save DCs with kineticist level, rather than utility talent level. Keeps low-level utility talents relevant forever, but also increases your concentration check DCs to use them while in threatened range.
   * Note for GMs: In addition to the burn changes, vetoing scaling save DCs for utility talents is also a good idea. This busts the power curve for what are primarily at-will abilities, and that ain’t good. For some elements this won’t be a big deal, since they don’t have many save-based utility talents; for other elements like mind, this will be a massive power boost.
   * Changes burn from 1 point of nonlethal damage per level to a -1 penalty to all Strength- and Dexterity-based skill and ability checks (except initiative) per level. Fundamentally changes how risky, creative, and aggressive kineticist players will be willing to become.
   * Note for GMs: If you’re worried about the power of the legendary kineticist, this is the feature to change. Battle burn and the changes to internal buffer/supercharge should allow kineticists to manage burn better, and many GMs may find that penalties to skill and ability checks aren’t enough of an incentive to get their players to manage burn carefully—which is, after all, the point of the class feature. Leaving burn as scaling nonlethal damage also solves the problem of what to do about kineticists suddenly having hundreds of HP more than other classes. This is the only feature that smacks of third-party power creep to me.
   * Allows gather power to be performed with one hand. Lets kineticists play around with simple melee weapons, wands or potions, etc.
   * Gains two more infusions, and two more chances to swap out infusions. Allows for more variety in blast shaping or substance infusion rider effects.
   * Introduces battle burn. Incentivizes short-term buffs, battlefield control spells, etc. to be used more proactively in combat, and helps kineticists not flame out midway through an adventuring day.
   * Eliminates the Maximize Spell metakinesis and replaces it with the Persistent Spell metakinesis. Helps kineticists focus more on debuffing and AoE damage with substance and form infusions, instead of blasting 24/7.
   * Note for GMs: Realistically, maximize metakinesis and persistent metakinesis are good for two different things, and it’s likely that some kineticist builds will prefer one or the other. Giving players a choice is actually a good decision. A pyrokineticist may want damage, damage, damage, but other utility-focused elements like void or aether may realize they’re going to do crappy damage anyway, and opt for debuffing.
   * Moves expanded element up a level and eliminates the penalty to your effective kineticist level in your expanded element. Gives you more variety when choosing infusions and utility talents, ultimately making more well-rounded characters.
   * Moves internal buffer back a level and starts your day with 1 point of burn already in the buffer. A little freebie; ultimately not game-breaking.
   * Allows supercharge to be performed as a swift action to remove 1 point of burn from a blast’s cost. Unlocks more of the action economy for kineticists, who are traditionally very dependent on move and standard actions but use swift actions for almost nothing except the occasional Quicken Spell metakinesis.
🥷 Roles
KIN 151: Kineticist Roles


        In many of my recent guides, I’ve started going into more depth on what types of combat and social roles classes can expect to play. Kineticists are a bit of an odd duck when it comes to roles, especially if you’re playing with Paizo-official material only: there are some minor things that you can do with buffing, debuffing, control, recon, mobility, etc., but it’s the addition of third-party content that really supercharges many of these roles and gives the different elements a feeling of being distinct from one another.


________________






Combat Roles


Ranged DPS


        When people think about the kineticist class, ranged DPS is the role that they think of: kineticists who set themselves up at the back of a giant fight, gather power, and then let loose with incredibly destructive single-target or area of effect blasts. Ranged DPS is, in fact, the role that most kineticists excel at, and few builds will have to invest heavily in order to become great at it—it’s baked in, in many cases. The ability to damage foes at range plays out a bit differently for kineticists than for other full-BAB archers and gunslingers, though, primarily because of burn mechanics and gather power.


   * ¾-BAB, rather than full-BAB. The fact that kineticists are a ¾-BAB class has some pretty big implications. First off, it slows your access to ranged combat feats, many of which are gated behind BAB requirements. Second, depending on your blast type (physical or energy), it’s going to make it a lot harder to hit enemies. Yes, ranged kineticists almost always attack at their highest BAB without any iteratives, and yes, they do get elemental overflow bonuses, but this still leaves physical blasters underperforming full-BAB archers in most scenarios; it especially hurts in early levels, when you may be dealing with cover bonuses and penalties for shooting into melee range that will make it basically impossible for you to hit. Energy blasters will hit more reliably (well, at least until spell resistance becomes prevalent) but deal less damage. I believe I’ve seen some math that shows that the two blast types more or less even out in the long run.
   * Peak DPS is heavily dependent on gathering power. Kineticists can wind up for some huge, huge damage numbers, certainly, but that nova capacity is offset by the fact that you can only take so much burn in a day (3 + your Constitution modifier) and in a round (varies depending on level). What you’re going to be looking for in most cases is sustainable DPS, which will typically involve gathering power and using some form of metakinesis to boost your damage while relying on infusion specialization to reduce the cost of whatever infusions you’re adding to your kinetic blast. If you can’t reliably gather power, your DPS will suffer. You absolutely must have the ability to get out of foes’ threatened ranges, because this is a huge impediment to gathering power. Grab the Mobile Gathering feat, pump your Acrobatics skill, invest in items that let you move through threatened range without provoking attacks of opportunity—do whatever you have to, but make sure that you can gather power, as gathering power determines your maximum sustainable DPS.
   * More focus on DR, energy resistances, and energy immunities. Ranged kineticists will basically never use their iterative attacks, opting instead to shoot off one high-powered kinetic blast per round (or two, with your quicken or double metakineses). There’s an odd tension here: on the one hand, you have to worry about DR and energy resistances less, because those defenses penalize one big hit a lot less severely than multiple smaller hits. On the other hand, without physical weapons that can be enchanted, kineticists don’t particularly have the tools to deal with DR, energy resistances, and energy immunities—and they can be nasty. Earth gives you Rare-Metal Infusion to get around DR/adamantine, cold iron, or alchemical silver, but no one else can bypass DR/metal, and while fire can help scorch away some amount of fire resistance with Searing Flame, you’re still up a creek without a paddle if you meet a fire-immune enemy. Mind similarly suffers against foes with immunity to mind-affecting effects, even with Reifying Infusion and Piercing Thoughts. There’s really nothing to be done about this problem except to be aware that kineticists often have trouble against enemies with exotic forms of DR or energy resistances/immunities, and focus on rounding yourself out with more options from the control, buffing, debuffing, or summoning roles to continue contributing to the team even when you can’t blow people up effectively.


        The other important determinant of how good you are at the Ranged DPS role is what your element is! This should come as no surprise to anyone, of course, but it does bear mentioning that some elements are simply better at damage than others. Fire is the classic “all damage, all the time” element among the Paizo-official elements that were released during 1e’s lifetime, but others can also do good work in that department. My rankings are below.

________________






Melee DPS and Tanking


        In first-party, Paizo-official kineticist content, there’s no special path you need to follow to become good at the melee DPS role. Kinetic blasts count as light weapons, so most kineticists interested in melee combat will take Weapon Finesse as early as they can, grab the Kinetic Blade, Kinetic Whip, Blade Rush, Blade Whirlwind, and Whip Hurricane infusions, and then simply get into melee range and mash. Likewise, there’s no real secret to becoming a good tank as a kineticist: you’re already likely to have incredibly good Dexterity and Constitution scores, which is more than half the battle, so all you need to do then is ensure that you’ve got a good defensive talent or two (earth’s Flesh of Stone and water’s Shroud of Water are classics) and you’re likely to be significantly more durable than just about any other d8 HD class. Unfortunately, Paizo only wrote two archetypes that help the kineticist class focus on melee—the kinetic knight and elemental annihilator—and both come with considerable drawbacks. Given the constraints of AC, attack rolls, and point buy, these two archetypes are the only real ways to turn a vanilla kineticist into a Strength-based melee tank.


        If we expand our horizons into third-party content, the picture gets quite a bit rosier. First, third-party content expands the number of melee-focused archetypes significantly. You’ll find that the kinetic duelist, telekinetic bladeshifter, kinetic lancer, and dragon pact kineticist offer many more flexible takes on this role. Second, third-party content introduces a few new ways to genuinely tank: you can get difficult terrain auras (Distorted Area), damage auras (Chilling Void), debuffing auras that force enemies to focus on you (Magnetic Pull), and more, all of which help lock enemies down, force them to address you as the chief threat, or prevent them from effectively attacking your allies. This is one area in which Pathfinder truly struggles, so having these options open is lovely. Finally, third-party content makes some huge inroads on empowering natural attack builds, particularly in the Kineticists of Porphyra books, which create a huge number of natural attack utility talents for the viscera, wood, and earth elements. Turning kineticists into natural attackers feels unorthodox at first, given that kinetic blasts are the kineticist’s usual schtick, but it actually benefits the class in the end: kineticists don’t really have the BAB or attack bonuses to regularly hit with their iterative attacks, so using natural attacks (which typically use the character’s full BAB or have a maximum penalty of BAB - 5) is actually helpful in a lot of instances. The Prototype build was my attempt to port over a good old “ball of natural attacks” summoner eidolon build to the kineticist.

Casting Roles


Buffing


        Classically, buffing is a popular hobby for spellcasters because buffing improves the odds of your team winning a fight without ever relying on failed saves, attack rolls, or pesky spell resistance checks. Kineticists are actually more limited in this role than you might think. Utility wild talents offer utility, right? And shouldn’t that include some team buffing? Well, not really. Kineticists, much like inquisitors, warpriests, and other striker classes, have to reserve most of their buffs for themselves. In the case of kineticists, it’s not even a choice most of the time: many of your more powerful buffing utility talents simply can’t affect other people. Some elements, however, do fairly well for themselves in this arena, notably air, mind, sound, and time. Other elements might have only one or two classic buffing elements, such as the Kinetic Healer talent or something similar. If your chosen element has access to party buffs, I highly recommend that you plan to pick them up, as it’s not always possible for kineticists to fulfill their primary DPS roles effectively. Air and time can use Celerity, mind can use Mesmerizing Suggestion or Improved Hivemind, sound can use Inspiration Amp, and so on.

________________






Control


        “Control” in Pathfinder usually refers to battlefield control, the process of creating walls, pits, difficult terrain, sightline obstructions like fogs or mists, etc. Battlefield control is effective because control spells and spell-like abilities rarely require failed saves in order to work, and can multiply the effectiveness of your team by making it impossible for enemies to retaliate while you buff up, pick off weaker enemies, or steadily waste their resources. Although you would expect the kineticist class to have lots of tools in its pockets for battlefield control, the first-party kineticist actually doesn’t: you’ve got isolated wild talents like Slick, Kinetic Cover, etc., but these abilities are typically quite a bit weaker than comparable spells and often require sustained action input in order to work. All this changes if your GM is willing to add the third-party content reviewed in this guide. Each element gets at least one or two new, solid options for battlefield control, and many elements get a lot more than just one or two. Some existing wild talents (e.g., Kinetic Cover) also get meaningful upgrades that help take useless abilities into useful territory. Adding these abilities onto the kineticist class can only make it better, as there will be fights in which your ranged or melee DPS roles just fall flat thanks to concealment, exotic DR, energy resistances or immunities, etc. When your main options fail, control and buffing should be the first roles you fall back on, as they often work irrespective of enemy defenses. So go ahead, use third-party content. Tell your GMs Allerseelen sent you.

________________






Debuffing


        The debuffing role in tabletop RPGs focuses on making enemies less threatening, either by lowering their defenses, lowering their offenses, hindering their mobility, or performing some other function that prevents foes from operating at full capacity. Under normal circumstances, ¾-BAB spellcasters don’t do a lot of enemy-targeted debuffing—or at least it’s not the optimal move for them to make. Because ¾-BAB spellcasters only ever get spells up to 6th level, often need to balance physical stats with mental stats, and tend to focus more on combat feats rather than spellcasting feats, ¾-BAB d8 HD classes rarely have save DCs that are on par with full casters. Here’s the secret about kineticists, though: they only look like ¾-BAB spellcasters. In reality, they’re full casters! Sure, their casting is a lot more limited in scope than a sorcerer’s or a wizard’s, but in terms of save DCs, they’re likely to be right on track. The first point in your favor is the way that substance and form infusion save DCs scale—that is, with your kineticist level, rather than the level of the spell. Every time you reach a new even kineticist level, the save DC of all your infusions goes up by 1. Try getting that deal on a casting class! The second point in your favor is that the stats you’re using to determine your save DCs are also the stats you’re using for attack rolls, damage rolls, AC, HP, Fortitude and Reflex saves, initiative, etc. Kineticists have a mandate to pay attention basically solely to Dexterity and Constitution, and this single-attribute dependent approach pays off in their favor. The third point is that it’s actually fairly easy to get boosts to your infusion save DCs through feat investment. Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast) is a legitimate and entirely reasonable feat choice, as are the Elemental Focus feats for some of the energy blasts available to kineticists; later on in your kineticist career, elemental overflow will start granting you size bonuses to physical attributes (read: Constitution and Dexterity) that will further increase your save DCs.


Now, if you’re trying to debuff using utility talents, that’s a tougher row to hoe; utility talent save DCs, unlike the save DCs for infusions, remain static throughout the levels, and there’s no Heighten Spell mechanism to improve their effective spell level. Some third-party content tries to alter this, notably the legendary kineticist class and the Adaptive Utility feat, but I’ve made arguments elsewhere that utility talent levels are assigned for a reason, and part of that reasoning has to do with how powerful at-will spell-like abilities might become if they had infinitely scaling save DCs. GMs, if you’d like to turn Adaptive Utility into a feat that permits the save DC of one utility talent to scale with kineticist level, that likely won’t break the power curve; giving out free scaling to every utility talent, however, is a recipe for trouble.

________________






Summoning


        In the traditional sense, summoning means conjuring allies from your environment or from extraplanar locations to aid you in a fight: reanimated undead, summoned or called outsiders, animated objects or plants, illusions, etc. Summoning never stops being relevant for the simple reason that more “bodies on the field” gives you an action advantage, an HP advantage, an attack and damage advantage, a physical space and threatened area advantage, and more. For the purposes of this guide, I’m also going to consider elements such as mind—which focuses on using charm and compulsion effects to secure the willing (or unwilling) cooperation of other creatures—as a type of summoning, since you’re still using your actions and resources to ensure that you have a numerical advantage over your opponents.

________________






Ancillary Roles


Anticaster


        The anticaster role is always somewhat nebulous, because the degree to which anticaster tactics are successful will depend a great deal on factors that are outside the scope of building a Pathfinder PC. What type of caster are you facing? What tactics do they use? What are they specialized in? What are they weak to? Being able to see through mist, fog, or smoke will be a huge boon if you’re square off against a caster who specializes in conjuration or evocation magic, but only of limited use against a caster who specializes in enchantment magic. Being able to see or otherwise detect invisible creatures will be phenomenal against arcane casters, who can cast invisibility, but less so against divine casters, who generally can’t. You get the picture, right? Depending on elemental focus, kineticists can be quite effective at using dispel magic to strip casters of buffs, piercing effects such as displacement or mirror image, seeing through, removing, or circumventing common battlefield control spells such as walls, pits, fogs, mists, smoke, etc., preventing teleportation, or banishing summoned creatures. Not every element can do all of that, of course, but some (aether, for example) can get pretty close. The Spellsieve is the resident anticaster in the Builds section, so make sure to head down there if you’re interested in learning more about this role.

________________






Recon and Mobility


        I never quite know what to call this role in my guides, but I’ve finally settled on “Reconnaissance and Mobility,” because I envision it as a role that allows you to infiltrate a location quickly and quietly, see what you need to see or grab what you need to grab, then exfiltrate just as quickly and quietly. The kineticist’s suitedness for this role depends a great deal on your element, but most elements have some form of at-will flight, at-will burrowing, at-will swimming, etc. that could feasibly help you get into places you shouldn’t be, and at-will invisibility or stealth capabilities aren’t unheard of, especially in the aether and light elements. In some instances you might not even need to physically enter a location in order to scout it out—utility talents like Greater Windsight (air), Photographic Transference (light), or Passive Telepuppet (mind) can allow you to spy from afar without ever needing to place your physical body on the line.

________________






Skills


        Skills…*sigh.* Kineticists aren’t great at skills. It’s not their fault! They only get 4 skill ranks per level, and their Intelligence modifiers are not likely to be high—why would they need to be?—so you often end up with a character that doesn’t have a lot of traditional skill utility. Certain elements (mind in particular) can really flip this narrative on its head, but even so, it’s an uphill battle to turn a kineticist into a skill monkey. See the Professor build in the Builds section for my take on how it might be accomplished.

🧝 Races
KIN 201: Races




As usual for my guides, I’ll do a full review only of the core races, and leave you with some general guiding principles for how to go about identifying other races that might suit a kineticist’s needs. When choosing a race, it boils down to about five principles, ranked more or less in order of importance:


   * Character concept. Duh.
   * Stat spread. This is the big one for kineticists, who need bonuses to Dexterity or Constitution (preferably both, although very few races hit that target) or Strength/Constitution in order to get the most out of their career. Prefer Constitution if you can—it helps quite a bit with damage, save DCs, burn capacity, etc., and you’ll never regret having more HP or a better Fortitude save.
   * Favored class bonuses. As one of the occult classes, the kineticist never got a full complement of favored class bonuses assigned to it, and those that were tend to be solid but not mind-blowing. With the exception of the gathlain. GMs, never let your players play a gathlain kineticist.
   * A useful way to assess the value of favored class bonuses is to think about how many feats you would have to spend in order to get their benefits. HP and skill ranks, though decent, can both be duplicated by one feat (Toughness and Cunning, respectively), so a full 20 levels of those favored class bonuses don’t do anything more than give you one feat. +⅙ of a wild talent will get you three bonus wild talents by 20th level, so that’s the equivalent of three Extra Wild Talent feats. You get the idea.
   * Alternative racial traits. Races grant traits, and those traits can either be helpful or irrelevant. By and large, kineticists don’t need weapon proficiencies, bonuses to schools of magic, or skill bonuses, so you’re left hunting for better vision and save bonuses, which will be the big-ticket items for most players.
   * Darkvision and low-light vision. If at all possible, you want darkvision. Clear enough?




Core Races


Dwarf


        Dwarves are everything you want in a kineticist: great Constitution bonus with a penalty to an unimportant stat, good favored class bonus, the Hardy racial trait to shore up your poor Will save, darkvision, Slow and Steady to enable kinetic knight builds more easily...dwarves have it all. Easily one of the best core races, and certainly the best race to play a geokineticist.


Pros
	Cons
	Great stat bonuses. Constitution is the #1 stat for kineticists, and Wisdom is highly appreciated because of the kineticist’s shoddy Will save.
	None. Like the earth itself, dwarves are just solid all the way around. (I know there are oceans. Hush. It was meant to be pithy.)
	Great racial traits. Darkvision and Hardy are the only two racial traits that kineticists should emphatically not get rid of; the others are left to your discretion. Speaking of Hardy, it’s hard to understate how good it is: spells and spell-like abilities comprise a huge portion of the most dangerous effects in the Pathfinder universe, many reliant on Will saves that you won’t be equipped to handle. Between Hardy and your natural Wisdom bonus, your Will save may not suck so hard!
	

	Darkvision.
	

	Good geokineticist favored class bonus. Dwarves are associated with stone, ya know, and the favored class bonus definitely makes sense from that perspective. More damage on earth blasts basically makes it a cinch that you should play a dwarven geokineticist.
	

	





Elf


        Elves are unfortunately outperformed in most classes by half-elves, and the kineticist is no exception. The Fair Folk’s Constitution penalty is simply too heavy an albatross about their collective neck, and with no meaningful favored class bonuses or racial traits that couldn’t be replicated by the half-elf, there’s simply no reason to play an elven kineticist.


Pros
	Cons
	

	Poor stat bonuses. The Dexterity boost is nice, but Intelligence is more or less irrelevant, and the penalty to Constitution is downright awful. You need Constitution.
	

	Okay racial traits. Elven Magic is interesting if you want to use energy blasts, but Weapon Familiarity is irrelevant for the class.
	

	Good favored class bonus, but poached by half-elves. Adding more damage from elemental overflow is nice and all, but because half-elves count as elves for the purposes of favored class bonuses, there’s really nothing that you’re getting here that you couldn’t get there.
	

	Low-light vision, but no darkvision without Blended View.
	





Gnome


        With the exception of a middling stat bonus package, gnomes don’t have much to recommend them as kineticists. Their favored class bonus is poor, and most of their alternative racial traits are wasted on the kineticist. Love ‘em for Charisma-based classes, and hate ‘em for just about everything else. (You could turn a gnome into a pretty good overwhelming soul kineticist, but the best overwhelming soul is still considerably weaker than the worst base kineticist.)


Pros
	Cons
	Fairly good stat bonuses. It’s the Constitution bonus we’re primarily interested in; bonuses to Charisma and penalties to Strength don’t matter one bit.
	Exceptionally poor favored class bonus. Let’s be real: kineticists are not meant to be the party face. Getting these bonuses only when interacting with very particular types of monsters is kind of a joke.
	

	Irrelevant alternative racial traits. Gnome Magic and its alternatives are the big aces in the hole for gnomes, and they unfortunately do nothing for kineticists. You’re left with quite the mediocre package.
	

	Low-light vision, but no darkvision without Blended View.
	





Half-Elf


        Half-elves always clock in near the top of the pack, no matter the class, and kineticists get the nod, too. Half-elves’ floating stat bonus serves them just fine, since it’s extremely difficult to find a race that grants bonuses to both Dexterity and Constitution, and their favored class bonuses and racial traits are on point. Cool. Cool, cool, cool.


Pros
	Cons
	Floating stat bonus. Just what the doctor ordered. If you’re playing with physical blasts, you could always choose Dexterity; if you’re playing with energy blasts, you can choose Constitution; if you’re playing a kinetic knight, you can choose Strength. Very few races have bonuses to both Dexterity and Constitution, the lack of two stat bonuses is actually not a big drawback for the kineticist.
	Low-light vision, but no darkvision without Blended View.
	Your pick of two great favored class bonuses. You can either lean toward damage (that’s the elven favored class bonus) or utility (that’s the human favored class bonus). More choice never, ever hurts.
	

	Great alternative racial traits. My recommendation would be to trade Adaptability for Dual-Minded (basically free Iron Will!) and Multitalented for Blended View. The rest is up to you.
	

	





Halfling


        Halflings are the most middle-of-the-road core race. Stat spread, favored class bonus, racial traits—all just kinda straight down the middle. You can do much better, but you could also do far worse.


Pros
	Cons
	Decent stat spread. A bonus to Dexterity is good enough; we don’t need either Charisma or Strength, so I’m fine with those adjustments.
	No darkvision or low-light vision.
	Decent favored class bonus. Increasing the size of your internal buffer is fine, helping you stretch yourself a little longer in really difficult fights. It’s not overwhelmingly good, though.
	

	Decent racial traits. Halfling Luck/Adaptive Luck are stand-outs, as always. You won’t need Weapon Familiarity, so I guess...Shadowhunter?
	

	





Half-Orc


        Half-orcs are as strong as half-elves when it comes to building a kineticist, and are especially good pyrokineticists, thanks to their strong favored class bonus for the fire element. Their racial traits (Sacred Tattoo + the Fate’s Favored trait) are time-honored classics, and the floating stat bonus is peachy.


Pros
	Cons
	Floating stat bonus. Just as good on the half-orc as it was on the half-elf.
	None.
	Great racial traits. Fey Thoughts, Shaman’s Apprentice, and Sacred Tattoo are the ones I would pick. None of the half-orc’s base racial traits do much for kineticists.
	

	Darkvision.
	

	Good favored class bonus for a pyrokineticist. Half-orcs are to pyrokineticists what dwarves are to geokineticists. Why not make this a goblin favored class bonus, tho, Paizo? Why?
	

	





Human


        Kineticists are predominantly a ranged class, and like many ranged classes, they need the Point-Blank Shot and Precise Shot feats to even get started. That’s where the human comes in, with its clutch bonus feat at 1st level. Humans may not get any of the other fun tricks of half-orcs or half-elves, but they do get a bonus feat. Pity the kineticist is so feat-light in later levels.


Pros
	Cons
	Bonus feat. We get it, humans. You’ve got a bonus feat.
	No darkvision or low-light vision.
	Great favored class bonus. More wild talents never hurt anyone.
	

	



Selected Other Races


Aasimar (Agathion, Archon, Garuda)


        Due to their abundance of mixed ancestries, aasimar are like humans, half-elves, and half-orcs: there’s almost always something in the race that will fit your build needs. While there’s nothing that particularly stands out about aasimar, they are consistently solid. You could do a great deal worse.


Pros
	Cons
	Scion of Humanity for the human favored class bonus. Aasimar don’t have any favored class bonus of their own for kineticists, so you’ll want to take the Scion of Humanity trait in order to act like a human for those purposes.
	None, really, but there’s little here that couldn’t be gained elsewhere, too.
	Bonuses to Constitution or Dexterity, depending on your ancestry. Archon is the most powerful, but suffers a bit on skills and spell-like abilities. Garuda is faster and has better skills and spell-like abilities, but no Constitution bonus.
	

	Darkvision.
	

	Good spell-like abilities, resistances, and skill bonuses. Middling, but quite passable. Garuda-blooded aasimar make great aerokineticists.
	

	





Ganzi


        Ganzi are similar to aasimar in almost all ways, with the exception that they have no option to be treated as humanoids rather than native outsiders. They wouldn’t be my top pick, but they’re also not bad kineticists.


Pros
	Cons
	Bonus to Constitution. It’s actually fairly difficult to find.
	No favored class bonus. Just stick with HP if you’re going to play a ganzi.
	Darkvision.
	

	Quibble. A good curse effect that can help you, a friend, or an enemy reroll a d20 once per day. Potentially very useful.
	

	





Gathlain


        Gathlains are the one race that a GM should never, ever allow players to use. Why? Mechanically, they wipe the floor with every other race in Pathfinder, for one simple reason: their favored class bonus is insane. Every six levels, they get to remove an additional point of burn from their infusions or kinetic blasts when they gather power or supercharge. While this may not seem like a big deal (and isn’t, from levels 1-5) it radically changes how much damage and destruction gathlains are capable of putting out—by my math, gathlain kineticists can do double the damage of non-gathlain kineticists on single-target blasts by 20th level, and that’s just not acceptable. I have no idea why Paizo allowed the race to achieve those kinds of numbers, but when a race is so powerful that there is no other reasonable choice (from a mechanical perspective, at least) you know that something is very, very wrong.


Pros
	Cons
	The most busted favored class bonus ever. Bulking up gather power and supercharge this much is a recipe for trouble.
	Low-light vision, but no darkvision.
	Tree-Born gets rid of the Constitution penalty. This variant of the gathlain loses its Constitution penalty—yet another reason why gathlains simply shouldn’t be allowed.
	

	Fey. Fey creatures aren’t innately immune to anything, but they aren’t humanoids, and will therefore be immune to effects like charm person.
	

	





Goblin


        Goblins are a bit of a weird choice for a kineticist, but with Small size and a whopping +4 bonus to Dexterity, you’ve got to at least give them a once-over...and it turns out they’re pretty good! With their insane +8 bonus to Stealth at 1st level (+4 size, +4 racial) they make excellent recon specialists, especially when paired with elements like aether or light, who can get at-will invisibility pretty early on. In fact, the goblin aetherkineticist Roll With It tank is one of my favorite off-the-wall kineticist builds, so look for that build (the Aether Gorblin) down at the bottom.


Pros
	Cons
	Huge Dexterity bonuses with no real drawbacks. Strength and Charisma are both pretty irrelevant for kineticists, so you’re left with a huge boost to hit.
	No favored class bonus. HP is the way to go.
	Darkvision.
	

	Normal speed and skill bonuses check out. As I said, the bonuses to Stealth are particularly welcome.
	

	





Hobgoblin


        Goblins would rule the roost in the “Other Races” section...if not for their cousins the hobgoblins, who are bigger, meaner, and worth more in Scrabble than regular goblins. With stat bonuses to the kineticist’s two most important attributes, darkvision, and great racial skill bonuses, it’s very difficult to go wrong with a hobgoblin kineticist. I only wish they got a favored class bonus.


Pros
	Cons
	100% perfect stat allocations. You literally couldn’t do better for kineticists than boosts to Dexterity and Constitution with no drawbacks. Perfection.
	No favored class bonus. HP is the way to go.
	Darkvision.
	

	Same bonuses to Stealth as the goblin, but with other options. Pit Boss is particularly nice if you’re building a Visceral Whip kineticist—in fact, I’ll probably use a hobgoblin for that build down in the Builds section.
	

	





Geniefolk (Ifrit, Oread, Suli, Sylph, Undine)


        The geniefolk used to be pretty one-note until the Plane-Hopper’s Handbook gave them variant heritages, just like aasimar and tieflings. Now? Now they’re pretty good! In these races, we’re mainly interested in the narrative unity of playing literal avatars of elements, and also in scooping up some good stat bonuses. The geniefolk are the only ones who can jump into the cool third-party elemental brethren archetype, too, and that’s fun. Some general rankings:


   * Ifrit
   * Base Ifrit
   * Magma Ifrit
   * Solar Ifrit
   * Oread
   * Base Oread
   * Crystal Oread
   * Metal Oread
   * Suli
   * Base Suli
   * Sylph
   * Base Sylph
   * Fume Sylph
   * Lightning Sylph
   * Undine
   * Base Undine
   * Vapor Undine
   * Frost Undine


Pros
	Cons
	At least one good stat allocation variant heritage per race. As usual, Constitution and Dexterity are critical, Wisdom and Intelligence are nice, Strength can be worked with for Elemental Knights, and Charisma is irrelevant.
	Surprisingly, no favored class bonus. How did Paizo miss this one?
	Darkvision.
	

	Some pretty good spell-like abilities. Trade away your affinity racial feature in all circumstances, and trade away the spell-like ability if you feel it won’t be helpful to you.
	

	





Shabti


        Shabti were late additions to the Pathfinder racial roster, and they’re pretty boss: deathless simulacra of humans who were afraid to pass into the Great Beyond and imbued a construct with their consciousness. For various reasons that you can see in The Professor build, shabti are by far the strongest class for skill monkey and knowledge jockey kineticists, and pair exceptionally well with the third-party mind element.


Pros
	Cons
	Bonus to Constitution. If you were a human, half-elf, or half-orc, this is where you’d assign your floating stat bonus anyway. The bonus to Charisma just lets you dump that stat down to 7 or 8 without any real penalty.
	No favored class bonus unless you go Facsimile. The extra wild talents are probably worth being treated as a human unless you’re in a campaign that features a ton of energy drain.
	Immune to level drain. It will still kill you if you take enough, but being immune to energy drain is a huge boon. Don’t sleep on this one.
	

	Darkvision.
	

	Servant in the House of Truth or Lector Priest racial feats. Between gaining all Knowledge skills as class skills and this feat, which allows you to roll every Knowledge check twice and take the better result, shabti are among the premiere Knowledge jockeys in the game. Shout-out to Lector Priest as well, which is great for counterspelling and crafting builds.
	

	Pharaonic Will. Kineticists have no real business using a Charisma-based suggestion spell-like ability, but I’ll gladly take a 1/day reroll on charm, compulsion, and fear effects. Not like a good Will save ever goes out of style.
	

	





Tiefling (Demodand, Devil, Kyton)


        Like aasimar, tieflings also got a bunch of variant heritages handed to them at some point, making their stat distributions highly flexible for whatever you’re building. There’s no shortage of Dexterity bonuses among the tiefling heritages, if that’s what you’re looking for, but I prefer to hunt for the sweeter meats of Constitution bonuses, which can only be found in the demodand-, devil-, and kyton-spawn heritages.


Pros
	Cons
	Great stat distributions. Both of the recommended heritages get Wisdom bonuses on top of Constitution bonuses, which is perfect for a class with a chronically bad Will save.
	No favored class bonus unless you go Pass for Human. Pass for Human is difficult to take when there are so many cool tiefling racial traits, but it’s probably optimal in many instances, both for the additional wild talents gained from the human favored class bonus and also for preventing people from being racist a**holes to you.
	Darkvision.
	

	Cool racial traits. Maw and Claw, Fiendish Resistance, Prehensile Tail, and Scaled Skin are all favorites of mine.
	

	

🃏 Wild Talents


➖ Kinetic Blast Wild Talents
Kinetic Blast Wild Talents


The wild talent that each and every kineticist will probably use more than any other is their kinetic blast, which they receive at 1st level. The kinetic blast is typically activated as a standard action (although it can be activated as part of a charge or full-attack action with some form infusions) and deals damage that scales with your kineticist level. For the vast majority of your career, you’ll likely be blasting with simple blasts, as these blasts cost 0 burn and are therefore sustainable over the course of an adventuring day. You may pull out composite blasts on turns when you need to deal some huge nova damage, but for the most part you should get used to the concept that your simple blasts determine much of your combat efficacy. Ratings for these wild talents are determined based on a few criteria. First, damage typing: for energy blasts, how prevalent is energy resistance or immunity to the damage type? For physical blasts, is the blast capable of dealing more than one damage type (bludgeoning, piercing, slashing) or restricted to only one? Second, the infusion complement. Generally speaking, the better the form infusion list, the better that kineticist will be at AoE damage; the better the substance infusion list, the better that kineticist will be at debuffing. Both are important, but sometimes an element sacrifices one for the other. Fire Blast, for example, has great form infusions but poor substance infusions, while Mental Blast has relatively poor form infusions but great substance infusions. It all depends on what you’re building for!
Of note, third-party infusion talents introduced a third category of kinetic blasts beyond simple and composite blasts. Although they’re officially classified as composite blasts, and function exactly as composite blasts normally would for composite specialization, etc., they generally cost 3 or 4 burn, rather than 2 burn, and require the kineticist to have gained access to all three of their elements in order to qualify. I’ve called these blasts improved composite blasts, but you should know that’s just a phrase I made up! They’re composite blasts, from a mechanical standpoint. My ratings of kinetic blasts and the infusion wild talents that are associated with them come from Ultimate Kineticists, which was a compilation and overhaul of all the first- and third-party kineticist material at the end of Pathfinder 1e’s production run. This book reconciled many of the issues with wild talent overlap between first- and third-party material, and is highly recommended for the full kineticist experience. You can find all of this material at the Library of Metzofitz, here. I’ve made every effort to separate third-party content from first-party content in the tables that follow, but only for first-party blasts; if you’re using a third-party blast from a third-party element, I’m going to assume your GM is okay with third-party infusions, too.




Simple Blasts


Physical


Air Blast: Air Blast is, most unfortunately, about as bad as simple physical blasts get. With the exception of Torrent, you don’t have any truly decent form infusions, and the substance infusions are about as bad. To cap it all off, Air Blast can’t even deal multiple types of damage, like Earth Blast or Telekinetic Blast—it’s relegated to bludgeoning only. Blech. Unless you have a very compelling reason, aerokineticists should almost always select Electric Blast as their simple blast.

Bone Blast: Bone Blast is absolutely one of the strongest simple physical blasts available to kineticists, featuring variable damage typing and a bevy of useful infusions. The form infusions lack a bit, but that’s a very minor gripe—even with just Impale, Foe Throw, and Fragmentation, you can do things that plenty of other blasts simply can’t. As for the substance infusion list, well, you’ll never be hurting for choice. Viscera is good to its kineticists like that.

Earth Blast: Earth Blast is pretty good, as first-party simple blasts go, with a generally strong form infusion list that will set you up early with Impale and keep on giving once you get to higher levels with Deadly Earth and Fragmentation. The substance infusion list, unfortunately, leaves much to be desired, even if you have access to third-party infusions. You’ll basically stick with Entangling Infusion for your entire career until you can get Imprisoning Infusion (well, if you can get Imprisoning Infusion).

Gravity Blast: Gravity Blast is the void element’s simple physical blast, and woof, it struggles a lot. Throwing in gravity manipulation with third-party materials is always recommended, as it lets void kineticists fill the role of battlefield controller more easily.

Junk Blast: I often think of the machine element as being quite overpowered, and in fairness, that’s an accurate assessment. Well, Junk Blast is one more area in which the element excels. In addition to being able to choose between all three physical damage types as you blast, it has all kinds of strong substance infusions, some of which (Magnetic Infusion, Rare-Metal Infusion) are normally relegated to very specific blasts (Electric Blast, Metal Blast) in first-party materials. Once you get to Entangling Infusion, you’ll never look back. The form infusion complement is limited, but not weak—Impale and Fragmentation do just fine on their own, and you can always grab Kinetic Bomb, Snake, etc. from the universal wild talent pool if you want more versatility.

Energy


Acid Blast: Acid is a heavily debuff-oriented element, so it makes some sense that its simple blast, as a kineticist’s main vehicle for debuffing, would also excel in that area. Acid Blast deals a rarely resisted damage type, has any number of hard-hitting substance infusions, and enough form infusions to function passably in the AoE damage sphere. Laced Infusion will probably be your most common substance infusion for delivering poisons to enemies, but it has a hard time with multiple enemies—for area damage, try Torrent or Spray + Melting Infusion or Burning Infusion.

Chrono Blast: Chrono Blast is an odd bird. It deals untyped damage, which allows it to bypass energy resistances or immunities, but also has only a d4 damage die, instead of the normal d6. Practically, this means that Chrono Blast will outperform against enemies that would be highly resistant to most energy blasts, but underperform against enemies with few elemental immunities or resistances, including when using metakinesis. The substance infusion complement is fine, but as with many of the more esoteric blasts (Telekinetic, Negative, Mental) it has huge problems dealing AoE damage without the addition of third-party universal infusions.

Cold Blast: There are a few things holding Cold Blast from greatness. First, the first-party infusion complement is shockingly limited: with only one form infusion, you can’t take great advantage of the strong substance infusions for AoE debuffing—and that makes me mad! Adding third-party wild talents gives you more and better substance infusions, but you’re still going to be relying on universal form infusions like Kinetic Bomb to deal any AoE damage. Second, cold resistances or immunities are as common as fire resistances; unlike Fire Blast, however, which has Searing Flame + Burning Infusion to punch through high resistances, Cold Blast has Penetrating Infusion and nothing else. Womp womp. I’m not thrilled about either of water’s simple blasts, but it’s a strong element otherwise.

Electric Blast: Now that’s more like it! Even among the relatively strong energy blasts, Electric Blast is a gem: somewhat rare energy type, decent-enough form infusions, and one of the best substance infusion lists for any simple blast. Magnetic Infusion in particular will be a huge help, because unlike Metal Blast, which as a composite blast can’t sustain Magnetic Infusion at 0 burn, Electric Blast can sustain Magnetic Infusion, giving your party huge bonuses to hit enemies affected by your blasts. Chain is great, Lock Up Infusion is great. The blast is obviously better with third-party content, but it’s still great even with first-party content alone.

Fire Blast: Awww, yiss. Fire Blast is here. It’s Fire Blast, guys! In addition to bringing back good memories of Pokemon Red in 1998, Fire Blast is one of the stronger energy blasts on tap, whether you’re considering first-party infusions alone or with third-party infusions added. The linchpin of any pyrokineticist build is going to be Burning Infusion + Searing Flame, of course, as whittling away enemies’ fire resistance will be necessary in all kinds of fights—fire resistance is just that common. If you add third-party wild talents, however, Fire Blast can also become a surprisingly potent debuffer (Melting Infusion, Overload Infusion) or battlefield controller (Smoke Infusion, Improved Smoke Infusion). Improved Burning Infusion is also a great buy for chewing through energy resistances at an accelerated rate. Finally, Fire Blast deserves some mention for its place as the premiere AoE blaster in Pathfinder: Fan of Flames is the only AoE form infusion below 3rd level—a real swarm killer in the early levels—and its later AoE infusions like Eruption and Explosion are phenomenal, as well.

Mental Blast: Man, where do you even begin with Mental Blast? For starters, it has access to almost every single infusion from the mind element—and there are a lot of those. You’ll be spoiled for choice here. Although its form infusions may seem lacking, Empathetic Surge and its various upgrades are actually everything you’d want out of AoE blasts: large, long-range, and able to exclude allies as needed. It even deals untyped damage, so no need to worry about energy resistances or immunities! But there is one major fly in the ointment that you need to remember about Mental Blast: it’s always a mind-affecting effect. Constructs? Immune. Undead? Immune. Vermin? Immune. You are contractually obligated to take Reifying Infusion so that you can continue to deal damage to these foes, but I can’t guarantee that you’ll be able to reliably affect them with infusions—that +8 save bonus is just too high. At any rate, it’s a great blast, but you have to know what you’re signing up for.

Negative Blast: Paizo really boned Negative Blast in their first-party wild talents: there’s precisely one decent infusion in the whole mamajama, and while Negative Blast can bypass energy resistances and immunities, it can’t damage undead at all. That’s a huge flaw when undead are one of the most common enemy types in any campaign. With third-party wild talents, the infusion complement opens up considerably, and starts to allow chaokineticists to actually create and heal undead, as they always should have been able to do. Well, selon moi, at least.

Positive Blast: Ai, ai, ai. What are we going to do with you, Positive Blast? It’s cool to be able to damage haunts and incorporeal undead at 1st level—don’t get me wrong—but how can you be a blaster when only undead are susceptible to the damage you’re dealing? The first-party infusion complement is abysmal, easily qualifying as the worst in the game; if you add third-party material, Positive Blast can carve a niche for itself in undead-heavy games as long as you’re also toting around a physical blast like Wood Blast—the remaining third-party form and substance infusions are uniformly strong, so you can safely ignore all of the Paizo-official garbage and go straight to the good stuff.

Sonic Blast: Much like Chrono Blast, Sonic Blast pays for its unusual damage typing with a lower damage die (d6 → d4) but otherwise functions nicely as an energy blast. The infusion complement is large, with particular strength among the substance infusions, and goodness knows not many enemies are going to be resistant or immune to sonic damage. Yep, a solid B for this blast.

➕ Composite Blast Wild Talents
Kinetic Blast Wild Talents


The wild talent that each and every kineticist will probably use more than any other is their kinetic blast, which they receive at 1st level. The kinetic blast is typically activated as a standard action (although it can be activated as part of a charge or full-attack action with some form infusions) and deals damage that scales with your kineticist level. For the vast majority of your career, you’ll likely be blasting with simple blasts, as these blasts cost 0 burn and are therefore sustainable over the course of an adventuring day. You may pull out composite blasts on turns when you need to deal some huge nova damage, but for the most part you should get used to the concept that your simple blasts determine much of your combat efficacy. Ratings for these wild talents are determined based on a few criteria. First, damage typing: for energy blasts, how prevalent is energy resistance or immunity to the damage type? For physical blasts, is the blast capable of dealing more than one damage type (bludgeoning, piercing, slashing) or restricted to only one? Second, the infusion complement. Generally speaking, the better the form infusion list, the better that kineticist will be at AoE damage; the better the substance infusion list, the better that kineticist will be at debuffing. Both are important, but sometimes an element sacrifices one for the other. Fire Blast, for example, has great form infusions but poor substance infusions, while Mental Blast has relatively poor form infusions but great substance infusions. It all depends on what you’re building for!
Of note, third-party infusion talents introduced a third category of kinetic blasts beyond simple and composite blasts. Although they’re officially classified as composite blasts, and function exactly as composite blasts normally would for composite specialization, etc., they generally cost 3 or 4 burn, rather than 2 burn, and require the kineticist to have gained access to all three of their elements in order to qualify. I’ve called these blasts improved composite blasts, but you should know that’s just a phrase I made up! They’re composite blasts, from a mechanical standpoint. My ratings of kinetic blasts and the infusion wild talents that are associated with them come from Ultimate Kineticists, which was a compilation and overhaul of all the first- and third-party kineticist material at the end of Pathfinder 1e’s production run. This book reconciled many of the issues with wild talent overlap between first- and third-party material, and is highly recommended for the full kineticist experience. You can find all of this material at the Library of Metzofitz, here. I’ve made every effort to separate third-party content from first-party content in the tables that follow, but only for first-party blasts; if you’re using a third-party blast from a third-party element, I’m going to assume your GM is okay with third-party infusions, too.

Bioelectric Blast (Bone + Electric): Bioelectric Blast deals both a physical damage type (slashing) and an energy damage type (electric), so it automatically gets dinged a little for requiring you to beat both DR and energy resistances/immunities. The form infusion list is quite weak, but the substance infusion—especially at later levels—catches up nicely, and you’ll never regret having Magnetic Infusion available. I’d recommend you pick up some cheap AoE form infusions like Kinetic Bomb to help cover the gaps in your area damage capabilities.

Charged Water Blast (Electric + Water): If you’re going with the air/water elemental combo, Charged Water Blast is definitely the composite blast you want, rather than Blizzard Blast. The damage types are less commonly resisted, the form infusion complement is better for most of the game, and the substance infusion complement is on point, as well. I wouldn’t call it blue—not quite—but it’s better than Blizzard Blast.

Lightning Blast (Electric + Light): Don’t ask me how a blast of literal lightning doesn’t qualify as an energy blast, and definitely don’t ask me how Paizo didn’t make this a composite blast in their core materials. Anyway, Lightning Blast inherits a lot of fantastic substance infusions from both the Electric Blast and Light Blast side of things—enough that I can overlook the bad form infusion list. Get Kinetic Bomb and Torrent, and you’ll be mostly fine.

Noxious Blast (Acid + Air): Quite nice, actually. Acid Blast and Air Blast together grant you some pretty good form infusions, and acid’s characteristically excellent substance infusion list continues to deliver. Acid + bludgeoning is a minor party foul for its ability to be penalized by both DR and energy resistance, but it is what it is.

Force Blast (Telekinetic + EE: Aether): Without third-party infusions, Force Blast has almost nothing going for it: the infusion complement is terrible, there are few instances in which force damage will be a meaningful improvement over a different composite blast, and you have to stay in aether as your expanded element to get it. It gets a bit better with third-party infusions, but not enough to bump it up to green.

Radiation Blast (Telekinetic + Junk): Radiation Blast deals some wacky damage (half acid, half force), which is a net positive for it, but its infusion complement leaves something to be desired. Magnetic Infusion is, of course, always welcome, but the paucity of form infusions outside of universal wild talents makes it harder to parlay into an effective damage dealer.

Other


Aetheric Boost (Telekinetic + Any Simple): It’s hard for me to rate Aetheric Boost based on its infusion complement, because the infusion complement will depend entirely on which simple blast you’ve paired with the boost. On the whole, though, changing your kinetic blast die size from a d6 to a d8 is not worth the extra 2 points of burn. Use that on infusions, metakinesis, etc. before you think about jumping up to this composite blast.




Alteration Amplification (Chrono + Any Simple): 




Cerebral Empowerment (Mental + Any Simple): 




Gravitic Boost (Gravity + Any Simple): 
✖️ Improved Composite Blast Wild Talents
Kinetic Blast Wild Talents


The wild talent that each and every kineticist will probably use more than any other is their kinetic blast, which they receive at 1st level. The kinetic blast is typically activated as a standard action (although it can be activated as part of a charge or full-attack action with some form infusions) and deals damage that scales with your kineticist level. For the vast majority of your career, you’ll likely be blasting with simple blasts, as these blasts cost 0 burn and are therefore sustainable over the course of an adventuring day. You may pull out composite blasts on turns when you need to deal some huge nova damage, but for the most part you should get used to the concept that your simple blasts determine much of your combat efficacy. Ratings for these wild talents are determined based on a few criteria. First, damage typing: for energy blasts, how prevalent is energy resistance or immunity to the damage type? For physical blasts, is the blast capable of dealing more than one damage type (bludgeoning, piercing, slashing) or restricted to only one? Second, the infusion complement. Generally speaking, the better the form infusion list, the better that kineticist will be at AoE damage; the better the substance infusion list, the better that kineticist will be at debuffing. Both are important, but sometimes an element sacrifices one for the other. Fire Blast, for example, has great form infusions but poor substance infusions, while Mental Blast has relatively poor form infusions but great substance infusions. It all depends on what you’re building for!
Of note, third-party infusion talents introduced a third category of kinetic blasts beyond simple and composite blasts. Although they’re officially classified as composite blasts, and function exactly as composite blasts normally would for composite specialization, etc., they generally cost 3 or 4 burn, rather than 2 burn, and require the kineticist to have gained access to all three of their elements in order to qualify. I’ve called these blasts improved composite blasts, but you should know that’s just a phrase I made up! They’re composite blasts, from a mechanical standpoint. My ratings of kinetic blasts and the infusion wild talents that are associated with them come from Ultimate Kineticists, which was a compilation and overhaul of all the first- and third-party kineticist material at the end of Pathfinder 1e’s production run. This book reconciled many of the issues with wild talent overlap between first- and third-party material, and is highly recommended for the full kineticist experience. You can find all of this material at the Library of Metzofitz, here. I’ve made every effort to separate third-party content from first-party content in the tables that follow, but only for first-party blasts; if you’re using a third-party blast from a third-party element, I’m going to assume your GM is okay with third-party infusions, too.

Complex Negative Admixture (Negative + Any Composite Energy): 




Darklight Blast (Telekinetic + Negative + EE: Aether): Half of Darklight Blast’s damage is force, and the other half is negative energy, a set-up that will basically guarantee that you never have to deal with energy resistances or immunities. (Even against undead, Pure Negative Infusion can ensure that you deal at least some damage.) The infusion complement isn’t what I would call fabulous for an improved composite blast, but the unusual damage typing and fun with illumination levels you can have with dark-light and blacklight more than merit a blue rating.

Silverlight Blast (Telekinetic + Light + EE: Aether): For my money, silverlight isn’t as useful of an effect as dark-light or blacklight, the spell effects that Darklight Blast recreates, and positive energy damage will probably be much less effective in normal combat encounters than negative energy. Other


Esoteric Modification (Telekinetic + Chrono + Gravity): Esoteric Modification is capable of pumping out some big damage numbers, at least in theory, but since you’re relegated to blasts that you’d get by taking aether, time, and void as your elements (none of which are known for their high-power composite blasts) you’re ultimately still playing catch-up with other elemental pairings. The burn cost is huge, too.

🪐 Universal Wild Talents
Universal Wild Talents


        Universal wild talents can be selected by any kineticist of any element, and the good news is that there are quite a few of them (first-party or third-) that are well worth your time.
