The utility talent list is a bit scanty, but the infusions can always help elements with weaker blasting lineups get fill their primary combat roles.






	Mean Rating 
(Paizo Content)
	Mean Rating 
(3PP Content)
	Mean Rating (Combined)
	Form Infusions
	3.4
	3.1
	3.3
	Substance Infusions
	1.8
	2.6
	2.2
	All Infusions
	2.8
	2.9
	2.8
	Utility Talents
	2.6
	2.9
	2.7
	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Empty Infusion: I’m a huge Empty Infusion fan. Look: sometimes you’re not looking to deal maximum damage, right? Sometimes you want to debuff a wide area, create difficult terrain, knock enemies on their butts, etc., and when you do that, your save DCs need to be as high as humanly possible. If you’ve got a great AoE form infusion and a great debuffing substance infusion lined up, but only a simple blast to pair it with and a save for ¼ damage against enemies with DR or energy immunities, why not simply delete the damage and focus on the main course—the AoE debuffing? In some fights you may be dealing no damage anyway, so use that fact deliberately and reap a huge benefit. Especially for 0 burn and no loss of your substance infusion slot, just about everyone needs this one.


Incorporeal Infusion: There are a couple things that just plain suck in Pathfinder: swarms suck, underwater combat sucks, fog and darkness sucks, and incorporeal creatures suck. 1 point of burn and no loss of your substance infusion slot seems like a totally reasonable price to pay in order to blow up ghosts with your mind, especially if you’re playing with the legendary kineticist chassis and have a few extra infusions to spare.


Countering Infusion: Countering Infusion isn’t any great shakes on its own, which is why its base rating is yellow—even with the ability to extend your counterspelling to spell-like, supernatural, and extraordinary abilities, there’s simply not enough coverage with your one or two elements to make a noticeable dent in the amount of stuff you can counterspell. If you pick up the Improved Elemental Counterspell feat, though, everything changes, as you’re suddenly counted as if you had every element for the purposes of what you can counterspell with your blasts. Pick up Spellturning Infusion a few levels after that, and you can make life hell for a not-inconsiderable number of monsters and casters.
Draining Infusion: Draining Infusion is wacky because it’s doing so much stuff in such a smol space. Let’s break this down into its component parts: 1) no matter what blast you use, it targets touch AC, allows spell resistance, and only works against creatures with the elemental subtype (fire, water, earth, air, etc.) that matches your element; 2) it ignores damage reduction, energy resistance, and energy immunity; 3) it reduces the burn cost of one blast you make before the end of your next turn by 1 or 2, depending on your level. By far, this infusion’s biggest weakness is its reliance on enemy typing. Pyrokineticist fighting undead? Nuh-uh, won’t work. Hydrokineticist fighting evil outsiders? Nope, still nothing. Even against enemies that match your blast’s element, you’re still making the gamble that the reduced damage you’ll likely deal this round will be offset by the increased damage you can deal next round thanks to the lower burn costs. Draining Infusion is therefore best in highly elementally similar campaigns (Reign of Winter, Ruins of Azlant, etc.) where you can drain a group of foes with a blast that matches their element, then wind up for a huge round with the diametrically opposed expanded element—for example, if you’re fighting winter wolves, you drain with cold blast, then blow ‘em up next round with your expanded element fire blast. It’s finicky and niche, but it’s interesting.


Bullying Infusion: If you’ve got something like the Intimidate Skill Unlock from Pathfinder Unchained, I might start to be a little more interested in Bullying Infusion, but on its own, the Intimidate skill is pretty garbage at any kind of debuffing. Even with that feat, Kinetic Awe is a better use of your actions for area demoralization.


2nd Level


Kundalini Infusion: The elemental ascetic, the archetype most closely aligned with monk stuff, doesn’t even give you a ki pool, which you need to qualify for Kundalini Infusion. As far as I know, Kundalini Infusion therefore requires you to go variant multiclass monk, dip monk, or gestalt monk in order to use it. Chakra stuff is cool, but requires a bunch of feats to use well, and the infusion only gets more expensive as you try to block more and more crucial chakras.


Venom Admixture: If you’re looking to deliver poisons at range through your kinetic blasts, the correct answer is the poison element’s Laced Infusion, not Venom Admixture. Venom Admixture stinks.


3rd Level


Aligned Infusion: DR/alignment is one of those things that base kineticists simply aren’t equipped to handle without the aid of third-party materials. Good news! Aligned Infusion will solve your problems and make those pesky outsiders a walk in the park. Nice to get a little boost to your save DCs in the process, too.


Stylish Infusion: Unchained monk style strikes are incredibly powerful, so if you have access to, say, a gestalt unchained monk/kineticist build, this infusion will be really cool and really powerful. For everyone else, though, you’re going to have to dip brawler or unchained monk in order to even get to this infusion.


4th Level


Crushing Blast: Absolutely splattering a mook with a kinetic blast crit is among the more fun Pathfinder experiences you can have, but even assuming you took Improved Critical, you’re paying 3 burn (possibly 5!) for a 10% chance to deal extra damage. There’s a high opportunity cost to that level of burn expenditure, whether from other infusions or utility talents. See my math below for justification of this rating.[1]
Keen Blast: Again, see my math below for my justification.[2]
Venom Infusion: I’m all for debuffing, and sickened is probably the strongest of the “lesser” status conditions, but even so, 3 burn for a 1-round sickened condition that’s negated by a Fortitude save does not seem like a great use of your resources, even with infusion specialization running. Probably bumps up to green if you have regular access to form infusions that are going to hit a bunch of enemies at the same time.


5th Level


Grappling Infusion: The median CMD at 10th level, the first level you’d be able to pick up Grappling Infusion, is 32. Your CMB bonus on this infusion is likely to be around +18 (2 + 10 Level + 6 Constitution), giving you about a 30% chance to grapple a foe on the first round and a 55% chance to maintain. Those aren’t great odds, so you should primarily use this infusion on crowds of lower-CR enemies that you’ll be able to snag more easily. All of the form infusions listed are also quite costly, so any Grappling + Wall/Cloud/Deadly Earth combo is going to be a turn of huge burn; use it wisely.


7th Level


Arbitrating Infusion: A ridiculously high cost, ridiculously restrictive alignment clause, and ridiculously weak spell being emulated do not a good infusion make. Skip this with all possible haste.
Venom Infusion, Greater: In addition to the widespread poison immunity present at high levels, the ability damage is pitiful and two Fortitude saves negate. You’re going to be disappointed with this infusion. I guarantee it.
Shepherd of Souls: Why on earth would you spend 4 burn to prevent an enemy from being resurrected? Stuff their body in a bag of holding, burn them and scatter the ashes, plane shift the corpse to the Positive Energy Plane—there are so many better options than this.


8th Level


Nullifying Infusion: Expensive, and no one’s denying it. You’re now at 16th level, though, and high-level Pathfinder plays for keeps. At minimum, you might get only an extra 5 damage from this infusion if your opponent has Resistance 5 and you negate that. What about if you’re dealing 225 damage on an empowered, maximized composite blast against an enemy with immunity, though? All of a sudden you’re getting an effective 205 extra damage (225 - Resistance 20). With energy immunities being as commonplace as they are, Nullifying Infusion is a necessary part of any high-level kineticist’s lineup—the Fortitude save to negate is the only thing holding it back from purpledom.




Infusions: Form


1st Level


Extended Range: Limiting kinetic blasts to 30 feet is a huge problem, which is precisely why Extended Range is so welcome. Unless you’re planning to jump right into a Kinetic Blade build, Extended Range should be almost everyone’s very first infusion. Plus, Extended Range is a prerequisite for a huge number of other infusions and talents, so you’ll need it if you’re going to get anywhere in this class.


Kinetic Blade: As with Extended Range, many kineticists will benefit from having Kinetic Blade and Weapon Finesse. Kinetic Blade can be combined with multiple different action types, including charges, attack actions, and full-attack actions, and it allows you to deal your full kinetic blast damage (sans elemental overflow) in melee range without worrying about provoking attacks of opportunity for ranged attacks or spellcasting. Elemental Knights will obviously make this infusion a core component of their playstyle, but it’s equally good for many different types of kineticists. As I mentioned above, Weapon Finesse is a no-brainer in order to help you hit more easily and conserve your point buy—highly recommended.
Kinetic Bomb: Kinetic Bomb is an awesome form infusion, and really helps AoE-light elements (aether, void, wood, time, mind) deal damage to tightly packed enemies. The cheapest form infusion for turning you into a swarm killer, too.


Kinetic Fist: Unarmed kineticist builds are rare, but third-party content introduces a number of ways to succeed at a natural attack build, and Kinetic Fist is a cheap way to add on buckets of damage to those builds.


2nd Level


Blade Rush: It’s like the force swords from Halo 2! Ah, good times. Blade Rush is great for three reasons: first, it starts to break the action economy in your favor, as it essentially combines Rhino Charge with a standard action to manifest your Kinetic Blade while still leaving you a move action. Second, you ignore all attacks of opportunity for movement during the blade rush. If you need to rush past enemies to hit a boss, no problem! Blade Rush doesn’t specify any Acrobatics rolls, movement speed penalties, or restrictions on charging through enemy squares or difficult terrain; you pick an enemy and can move up to 30 feet to hit them, regardless of their positioning. (I do assume that you have to have line of effect to your target, though.) Third, Blade Rush is excellent for tagging low-hanging flying enemies—if you’re fighting a troublesome flier early in the game, just ready a standard action Blade Rush to pop them as soon as they come in range. Quite good!
Ricochet: 2 burn seems about right for what is a slightly less powerful, slightly cheaper version of air’s Chain infusion. The limitation to blasts that deal bludgeoning damage is a bit of a letdown, but the vast, vast majority of physical blasts will meet that criterion. Be warned that Kinetic Bomb or its Improved cousin will be better against tightly packed enemies.


Focused Blast: So long as you’re okay with Focused Blast being wildly expensive—and the opportunity cost of not being able to use any other form infusion with your kinetic blast—the talent is plenty good, especially for energy blasts that need to be able to reliably beat spell resistance. For physical blasts, there are probably cheaper, more effective options.
Seeking Infusion: Snake is a better choice if you need to get around total cover, but Seeking Infusion is awesome for beating effects like blur. Its one weakness is that it can’t function beyond 30 feet (the form infusion slot competes with Extended or Extreme Range, unlike Snake, which folds in a 120-foot range) but it’s otherwise very solid.


Spindle: Let’s remember a portion of the infusion rules text here: “When a kineticist modifies a kinetic blast with a form infusion and a substance infusion that both require saving throws, each target first attempts a saving throw against the form infusion. If a target succeeds and a successful save negates the infusion’s effects, the entire kinetic blast is negated; otherwise, the target then attempts a saving throw against the substance infusion.” Using this infusion is therefore a bit of a gamble: if your targets save, they take no damage at all from your kinetic blast. At 2 burn, you’re best off using this infusion against tightly packed, large, slow enemies, especially if you have an energy blast. Physical blasts will just want to skip it.


3rd Level


Flurry of Blasts: Flurry of Blasts runs into a definite damage disadvantage when compared to single composite blasts bulked up with metakinesis, but where it shines is in its pairings with substance infusions designed to debuff enemies. Enemies take penalties depending on how many times you hit them, and you gain bonuses on caster level checks (not only to overcome spell resistance, but all caster level checks) and CMB checks depending on how many times you hit them. Elements that are very dependent on single damage types and caster level checks to beat spell resistance (lookin’ at you, fire) will definitely benefit from being able to force their conditions and spell resistance without much say-so from enemies. Probably less useful overall for physical blasts, which won’t hit enemies as often. Third-party infusions add on to Flurry of Blasts in a big way, so don’t rule this one out until you’ve read ahead.
Kinetic Whip: Where Kinetic Blade only lets you hit squares next to you, and only for one attack (even on a full attack, you can only manifest it for one attack), Kinetic Whip lets you hit all enemies within 10 feet of reach, and the whip stays manifested until your next turn so that you can continue to attack with it. Weapon Finesse + Combat Reflexes + Kinetic Whip can be a very powerful combo in close quarters, especially if you’ve bulked up a composite blast with metakinesis and can then deal that damage to every enemy who provokes an attack of opportunity.
Improved Kinetic Bomb: Bigger splash radius = more bang for your buck, literally. The fact that you can scale the radius of Kinetic Bomb and Improved Kinetic Bomb depending on enemy positioning and your burn budget is phenomenal, and you’re still likely to deal good damage to secondary splash targets.
Snake: With the ability to bend it like Beckham around corners and into squares you can’t see, you basically have Improved Precise Shot. As long as you have a hole through which your blast can travel, you can hit anyone, anywhere. Although it’s not explicitly stated, I’m assuming that you still take a 50% miss chance when shooting into squares that you can’t see. That seems only fair, right?


Elemental Trap: With a good radius, good damage, and permanent duration until triggered, Elemental Trap is actually quite good. Toss it down in a busy charge lane and wait for someone to blow themselves to smithereens.
Extreme Range: Extreme Range is probably overkill in a lot of cases, but Air’s Reach + Extreme Range can turn you into a sniping monster who shoots people from almost 1,000 feet away. Invest in Perception.
Mobile Blast: Mobile Blast is the kineticist’s version of flaming sphere. It’s great for when you have enemies bottlenecked somewhere and you can just park it on their squares, forcing them to either eat the damage as they try to hold position or flee and give up their ground. The 30-foot range limitation is pretty hard to take, and you’re better off using your move action to gather power in most rounds, but it can be a great source of passive damage if enemy positioning is favorable for you.


Follow-Up Shot: Follow-Up Shot is narrowly useful for instances in which you need to deal damage to a single target, but have maxed out how much damage you can push through with metakinesis and composite blasts. It competes for the form infusion slot, though, so remember that you’ll need to be within 30 feet and have unobstructed line of sight to your enemy in order to use this infusion to its fullest effect. Improved and Greater Flurry of Blasts are going to work better for both damage and substance infusion shenanigans in the long run.


4th Level


Nebulous Shape: The point here, I can only surmise, is to help composite physical blasts avoid being penalized by both energy resistance/immunity and DR at the same time. It’s not bad for that purpose—an enemy’s DR won’t help if you’re not attacking with physical damage—but feels a little expensive for such a niche effect, especially one that by default will need to be applied to an already expensive composite blast.
Vital Blade: Vital Strike multiplies the base damage die of your blast (1d6), not whatever your blast is currently sitting at, so Vital Blade doesn’t add a lot of damage. It helps create a more mobile melee kineticist, though, so perhaps of note for kinetic knights and other Kinetic Blade/Whip builds.


5th Level


Improved Flurry of Blasts: For a hasted 20th-level kineticist, Flurry of Blasts puts out six attacks for 2 points of burn, each dealing either 1d6 + 1 + Constitution modifier (for physical blasts) or 1d6 + ½ Constitution modifier (for energy blasts); assuming conservatively that only about ⅔ of your blasts hit, even at your highest BAB, enemies will take about a -3 penalty to saves against your substance infusions (4 hits - 1 initial hit). Improved Flurry of Blasts your number of blasts to 11. Assuming ⅔ of these hit, not only are you dealing double the damage of the original wild talent, you’re also forcing an enemy to reroll its save against your substance infusions about three times (first on the initial hit, second on hit #3, third on hit #6, etc.). Substance infusions in Paizo-official content don’t typically specify whether the duration of status conditions stacks, because, well, extending them is not an option in Paizo-official content. Even assuming your GM rules that the durations don’t stack, though, you’re still greatly increasing the chances that enemies will fail at least one save against your substance infusion. Average roll of 1d20 is 10.5; average of of 3d20, take the lowest is 5.5. I like those odds.
Wall: Battlefield control is always a useful thing for a blaster to be able to pull out of their back pocket, and Wall is a form of battlefield control that every kineticist has access to. The damage is pretty lackluster, especially if you’re working with a physical blast, but in general physical blasts are better at single-target damage while energy blasts are better at AoE damage—just a fact of life.


Blade Whirlwind: The utility of Blade Whirlwind depends a great deal on how often you foresee yourself getting mobbed on all sides by enemies. If that’s often (like, for instance, if your table plays with the 4e minion rules and throws in trash mobs that can be wiped out in a single hit) then Blade Whirlwind can be a good purchase. I do wish that the blade and whip talents were separate from one another; my feeling is that the increased level and burn cost of the whip talents are enough to delineate them without resorting to using the blade talents as prerequisites for the whip talents, but that’s Paizo’s call. Anyway, Whip Hurricane is much better, so you’ll probably want to progress to that if you’re already here.
Improved Mobile Blast: I like the intent here—you’re essentially trying to make your rolling tumbleweed of fire, aether, earth, etc. more autonomous. It’s great that you no longer need to use a move action to direct it each turn, as that leaves you free to continue gathering power and blasting while it does damage. Still only useful for fights taking place on the ground, but I’d probably bump it up to a blue for energy blasts.


Vital Mobility: 4 points of burn for Spring Attack and a little more damage seems highly suspect to me, but you’re welcome to pick this up if you really, really like your melee kinetic blast routine.


6th Level


Whip Hurricane: Whip Hurricane can be devastating if you have the right set-up. With infusion specialization reducing the cost, a composite blast paired with supercharge and metakinesis feats like empower or maximize, this talent lets you deal a truly shocking amount of damage to every enemy within 10 feet if your attack rolls are good enough. Expensive, yet powerful.


Spellturning Infusion: On their own, Countering Infusion and Spellturning Infusion are too narrowly confined to your element to be of much use, even if it is hilarious to turn spells, spell-like abilities, supernatural abilities, and extraordinary abilities back on their originators. With the Improved Elemental Counterspell feat, though, you become able to counterspell any of the above classes of effects with the air, cold, darkness, earth, electricity, fire, force, light, or water descriptors, which is a mind-bogglingly wide array of abilities. Once you remember that you can counterspell all day and blast when you’re not counterspelling, you see how powerful this infusion can be in the right hands.


8th Level


Greater Flurry of Blasts: For no greater burn cost than Improved Flurry of Blasts, Greater Flurry of Blasts just goes nuts on the number of blasts: 16, to be precise, accounting for haste. Again, assuming about ⅔ of these blasts hit, you’re dealing 1d6 + some portion of your Constitution modifier on ten hits, forcing four separate saves against your substance infusions. Under those circumstances, you’re basically guaranteed at least one failed save in addition to all the damage; this makes Greater Flurry a worthy capstone infusion indeed. Don’t forget that metakinesis can still apply here, whether empowered, maximized, or doubled (base kineticist) or empowered, persistent, or doubled (legendary kineticist). How would you like to hit someone for 32 attacks in a round? Doable.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Elemental Whispers: Familiars are, without exception, powerful—less so for what they can do on their own, and more for what they can do for you. You’ll get Alertness as a feat when the familiar is in your mind, as well as a bonus that’s often the equivalent of its own feat (Improved Initiative, Iron Will, Great Fortitude, Lightning Reflexes, etc.). Add on the fact that your familiar can perform risk-free scouting (if it ever gets killed, it just appears back in your mind) and you’ve got a heck of a package for 0 burn. Every kineticist should take this, at later levels with Extra Utility Talent if not at early levels.
Kinetic Awe: Dazzling Display has always been a good feat for classes that can get halfway decent at Intimidate, and kineticists definitely can, either through gather power and the innate bonuses that Kinetic Awe grants or other skill options. Pair with Violent Display, Shatter Defenses, Disheartening Display, and the Intimidate Skill Unlock for maximum effectiveness, and you’ll be debuffing huge crowds of enemies through the floor.


Nine-Tailed Kineticist: Magical Tail is a really fun feat that I first came into contact with through the wrecking mysticism curse for oracles and have been a fan of ever since. I’d be happy to give Nine-Tailed Kineticist a blue if not for the fact that all its save DCs are Charisma-based, which is the stat more likely than any other to be dumped by kineticists. Maybe not kitsune kineticists, though?


Enhanced Fist: A great utility talent for natural attack builds, which will appreciate the improved damage throughput.
Kinetic Dissolution: Wayangs are going to be a fairly niche race for kineticists, although they’re not a bad option for void kineticists in particular. Light and Dark isn’t a great racial trait, but Dissolution’s Child is passable, acting as vanish. For 1 burn, though, vanish is pretty expensive. Meh.
Skilled Kineticist: Nearly all elements have at least one skill that they’ll get a lot of mileage out of: void and fire have Escape Artist, water has Swim, air has Fly, and aether has Sleight of Hand, which they use to steal things from a distance with Telekinetic Finesse. Getting big bonuses on these checks isn’t a terrible idea, especially if you have a feat to spare later in your career for Extra Utility Talent.
Venom Speaker: Between Venom Admixture, Venom Infusion, Greater Venom Infusion, and Venom Speaker, kineticists do have some decent tools with which to poison people. You’re not going to have the tools to craft your own poisons, though, which still makes it a very expensive and save-reliant hobby. You’re better off turning to the poison element if you want to pursue this playstyle.


Infernal Bargain: Conditional favor is a generally poor spell, and Infernal Bargain is no better.
Mesmerizing Elementalist: Hypnotism is a bad spell that no one should pay for access to. No.
Tengu Blade Lore: With a Constitution penalty, tengu are already pretty bad choices for kineticists, and Tengu Blade Lore does them no favors. Weapon special features are pretty weak, by and large, unless you’re focusing on combat maneuvers. You shouldn’t be doing that in this class.


Cantikinesis / Improved Cantikinesis / Greater Cantikinesis: Huge bonuses to hit and the ability to take simple and composite blasts that don’t belong to your element? Unfortunately, this is so powerful that there’s no mechanical argument for playing any other way, and that reduces the variety in your games rather than increasing it. Veto these.
        Suggested Fix: There’s really nothing. These abilities are simply too powerful, from top to bottom.


2nd Level


Kinetic Crafter: Crafting is a fantastic way to help your party, although it’s a bummer that kineticists don’t really need a lot of equipment themselves. And hey, having prerequisites eliminated for free every few levels is a cheap and easy way to make sure you pass the crafting checks you need! Way, way better than the Kinetic Crafting feat.


Greater Skilled Kineticist: Greater Skilled Kineticist is nice because it lets you choose where you want your +½ level bonus to go. UMD, Perception, Stealth, and Acrobatics are probably the main skills you’d want to boost, depending on your party role.


Past Life Evoker: I suppose it’s okay if you expect to run into energy immunities or huge energy resistances early in your career, but is that really much of a problem? Samsarans are a really bad kineticist race anyway, with no Dexterity bonus, a penalty to Constitution, and no favored class bonus. Blerg.


Advanced Kinesis / Greater Advanced Kinesis: Another pair of utility talents that’s simply too powerful to be allowed. Unlike Kinetic Invocation, which you have to purchase as a feat in order to even access the spell-like abilities you’re purchasing with utility talent slots, Advanced Kinesis and Greater Advanced Kinesis simply plop two domain abilities and nine spells in your lap as soon as you take them. It doesn’t stop there, either: with the Basic Kinetic Training feat, you could theoretically acquire more basic kinesis wild talents, unlocking a new domain every time. Far, far too powerful for play at any table.
Suggested Fix: Using Kinetic Invocation as a template, allow players who have selected these wild talents to purchase domain abilities or spell-like abilities as utility talents in their own rite, each costing either zero or 1 burn, depending on their power; allow the spell-like abilities to be activated with the normal casting time the corresponding spell would require. Better yet, simply fold the domain abilities or spell-like abilities that you deem balanced into Kinetic Invocation. It’s already a great feat that many kineticists would be well-advised to take, so there’s no need to create two separate paths to achieve the same effect.


3rd Level


Overwhelming Skill: The only thing stopping Overwhelming Skill from attaining a purple rating is that you need three utility talents (Skilled Kineticist, Greater Skilled Kineticist, Overwhelming Skill) and the overwhelming soul archetype in order to take it. If you can fit this in somewhere around your early double digit levels, it’s like getting the bardic knowledge class feature, but for every skill there is. Talk about bang for the buck.


Greater Elemental Whispers: There are some pros and cons to Greater Elemental Whispers. On the pro side, wysps are pretty good familiars: their resonance ability grants kineticists within 30 ft. a +1 competence bonus to all attack and damage rolls (awesome) and they can use the aid another action to boost your skill checks by 4, rather than 2 (pretty good). On the con side, you lose your original familiar’s bonus and have to actually replace them if they die, rather than just letting them pop up in your head. The call is yours to make, but either way, I wouldn’t recommend an elemental under any circumstances—the resonance from a wysp is too good for kineticists to ignore.
Kinetic Restoration: You’d hate to have to use a bunch of burn on lesser restoration, but when you need it, you need it.
Penetrating Burn: I like it quite a bit as a passive buff, and it grows more powerful in the hands of single-element energy blasts—fire comes to mind for mulching through resistance with Searing Flame and Burning Infusion.


Charged Attacks: Essentially mandatory for natural attack builds, one of which I will have in the Sample Builds section.
Natural Rush: Ha! Pounce as a utility talent. Yes, that’s fantastic for natural attack builds.
Skill Hack: I don’t want to be that guy who’s like, “I need a vacation from my vacation!” but Unchained Skill Unlocks would be a lot better if they got a second round of unchaining. Most just clear away penalties that few GMs and even fewer players bother to remember, rather than introducing something new or cool to do with the skill. Since you’re limited to the bonus skills granted by your primary element, too, you’re unlikely to get fun stuff.


Elemental Grip: Hold monster is already pretty dependent on failed saves from monsters, and the restrictions to only targets that match your element is very restrictive. This gets better as you add more elements, but not by much.


4th Level


Composite Versatility: Composite Versatility tends not to benefit single-element builds very much, but it’s excellent for two-element builds. A pretty effortless way to build out your versatility in combat.
Dimensional Anchor: Dimensional lock is a great spell, and will prevent all manner of casters and outsiders from escaping when you’ve got them right where you want them.
Expanded Defense: Expanded Defense is an excellent talent. Defensive talents are often amazing, granting large bonuses to AC (wood, water), temporary HP (aether), DR (stone), and more. You want as many of them as possible, as they’re mostly passive effects that will keep you alive.


Guided Ricochet: Ricochet can only hit two targets, so Guided Ricochet is almost completely wasted if that’s your avenue. If you have the Chain infusion, Guided Ricochet becomes much better, because each new target gets a cumulative bonus. Chain only applies to Electric Blast, though, and as an energy blast it’s not likely to miss much anyway.
Implant Blast: The concept is wicked cool, but its uses are primarily intrigue-related, and that’s pretty niche.


5th Level


Plane Shift: Sure, I’ll happily take some interplanar travel if it’s on offer. This wouldn’t be the kind of thing you’d want every day, but an entropist sure could get on board.


Kinetic Form: 10-foot reach is the only thing you’d want Kinetic Form for—everything else you either don’t get or don’t want. So...decent for Kinetic Blade and Kinetic Whip builds, I guess? Gets a lot better when paired with some of the third-party Kinetic Form utility talents like Armored Form or Durable Form.
Spark of Life: 1 point of burn in order to get only one type of summoned creature is a generally bad trade. It is nice that you can create your servant for free and command it to run into traps, but that’s a pretty niche application.


6th Level


Ride the Blast: Basically at-will teleportation, especially with Extended Range and Extreme Range. You can target squares, remember! A great way for melee kineticists to close with enemies, to get over walls or out of pits, etc.


Armored Form: If you’re building a Kinetic Form build that takes advantage of lots of attacks of opportunity and bigger reach to deal damage on the off-round, Armored Form just enhances what you’re already doing. Carry on.
Durable Form: Another utility talent that’s specifically for Kinetic Form builds. Durable Form beats out Armored Form by a decent margin, primarily because you can use it to keep stretching out healing with burn. If you’re 20th level and a monster hits you for 40 damage to deplete your buffer, you can essentially take 20 damage from burn, heal the 40 temporary HP, and keep on trucking. It’s like the enemy did only half damage, and meanwhile your elemental overflow and defense talent abilities just got stronger.


7th Level


Improved Composite Versatility: The burn costs here are almost unfathomable, but it’s decent as a 1/day supercharge if you need to let loose with everything you’ve got.


8th Level


Dimensional Lock Aura: More great anticaster tools from third-party content. Stop right there, arcane scum!
Reverse Shift: A great late-game addition to your arsenal, Reverse Shift lets you ghost through walls and infiltrate like a boss. 0 burn cost is just...mwah. You’ll never want to stay ethereal long enough to take burn if you can possibly avoid it.


Elemental Exile: You’d have to use a bunch of burn getting your entire team to the same plane, they’re all going to get sent to different locations, and you can’t even choose which elemental planes you want to go to. Unless you’re trying to shove an enemy into the Positive Energy Plane for fun, you shouldn’t generally use Elemental Exile.
❄️ Elements
KIN 251: Elements & Wild Talents


Wild talents are the bread and butter of the kineticist class: they determine what you’re good and bad at, what kind of damage your kinetic blasts do, how you defend yourself from attacks, what kind of debuffing you’ll be able to pull off, etc. As a reminder, there are six types of wild talents, each of which has a different role. Simple blasts and composite blasts, together called kinetic blast wild talents, are what you’ll use to shoot baddies, either at range or in melee. Form infusions and substance infusions, together called infusion wild talents, either turn your blast into different shapes (form) or tag extra debuffs onto your blast (substance). Defense wild talents are the least numerous of any wild talents, and are assigned to you at 2nd level in accordance with your elemental focus; as the name implies, they help to keep you from dying. Finally, utility wild talents are the most varied of the bunch. They can provide passive bonuses or permanently alter the way your kinetic blasts work, grant new movement modes, help you to buff, debuff, control, or tank, and even grant attacks that you didn’t have before. If you’re still feeling uncertain about how all these wild talents are broken down, now is the time to review before jumping into the section below—we can’t have you building on a shaky foundation.




Sidebar: Vetoed Wild Talents


Let’s face it: sometimes the reputation that third-party content gets as being massively overpowered is justified. My rule for vetoing wild talents can be boiled down to one rule: third-party content is only useful insofar as it meaningfully expands a kineticist’s choices. If a talent is out-and-out better than anything available at its level, it becomes the default option for all kineticists and narrows choices. Take Wind Blade, a 1st-level utility talent that can be selected in place of Basic Aerokinesis. Basic Aerokinesis gives you a +2 bonus to saves against heat, breath weapons, and gas effects. Um...cool, I guess? Potentially useful in certain encounters, but not game-changing. Wind Blade, by contrast, allows you to deal either slashing or bludgeoning damage with Air Blast, opens up more infusions, and grants a +1 untyped bonus to attack and damage. Which one is a motivated min-maxer going to pick? The smart money is on Wind Blade. In this case, by introducing a wild talent into the ecosystem that is way more powerful than anything else, you’ve effectively narrowed a player’s choices—every kineticist needs to take Wind Blade, no questions asked. Third-party publishers only really succeed when they introduce content that is neither violently red nor violently purple. One is so bad that you’d be a fool to take it; the other is so good that you’d be a fool not to take it. In either case, you (the GM) have done nothing to meaningfully expand a kineticist’s choices. Where possible, I’ll give potential fixes to the third-party wild talents, because they’re often created with good intentions, just poor execution. Wild talents that I believe should be vetoed or changed will be marked in black italics, italics to represent third-party content (as usual) and black to represent that I’m not going to dignify it with a rating as-is.
🖐️ Aether
Aether


        Out of all the first-party elements, aether is by far the most flexible in what it can do. In fact, aether kineticists play more like rogues than they do like kineticists: you can turn invisible at will with Telekinetic Invisibility,  rig impromptu traps or create mobile cover with Telekinetic Haul, pick people’s pockets or disable traps from across a room with Telekinetic Finesse, trip people or perform dirty tricks with Telekinetic Maneuvers, and even throw aetherically charged weapons at enemies. Long-time readers will know that I love skill monkey and utility classes, and you can’t get much more of that flavor in the kineticist class than in aether. Unfortunately, aether falls victim to a bit of “robbing Peter to pay Paul” syndrome: in order to make such a name for itself in skills, utility, and general sneakiness, it sacrifices a great deal of its offensive potential. Most kineticists will play with simple blasts only for the majority of their career, so aether doesn’t completely fall apart when composite blasts come online and the element is stuck with either Force Blast or Aetheric Boost; where it does suffer, however, is in its infusions. Aether has few form infusions (and fewer substance infusions) that are truly worth your investment, and certainly none that will help you deal AoE damage or meaningfully debuff enemies.


        As far as third-party content goes, 3PP wild talents allow aether to flex into more anticaster operations with Stitching Infusion, Dismissing Infusion, Unweave Magic, Greater Unweave Magic, etc., and also improve the element’s ability to control the battlefield. What aether needed most was a few more genuinely worthwhile infusions to balance out its weaknesses—and it definitely gets those! Third-party universal infusions are particularly nice for the element, as low-cost form infusions like Kinetic Bomb really help the elements that were denied access to any AoE blasting capabilities in the original Paizo kineticist material. It still takes a special brand of person to want to play an aether kineticist, but that brand of person is going to have an absolute ball with this element. It’s probably what I’d pick to play in a one-off if I was aiming for my own pure enjoyment.


First-Party: Anticaster, Recon, Skills > Control, Ranged, Summoning > Buffing, Debuffing, Melee
Third-Party: Anticaster > Control, Recon, Skills > Melee, Ranged, Summoning > Buffing, Debuffing






	Mean Rating 
(Paizo Content)
	Mean Rating 
(3PP Content)
	Mean Rating (Combined)
	Form Infusions
	3.0
	4.0
	3.2
	Substance Infusions
	1.3
	3.0
	2.4
	All Infusions
	2.3
	3.2
	2.7
	Utility Talents
	3.1
	2.7
	2.9
	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.


Class Skills


        On any other class, I’d give Sleight of Hand a red. This, however, is not any other class. This is MFing aether, and things play differently here. The primary utility of Sleight of Hand for an aetherkineticist is Telekinetic Finesse, which lets you steal objects using your mind within close range. Add Disable Device, and you can disable traps from afar, too! Knowledge (Engineering) is used for Aether Architect, but otherwise doesn’t get you much.




Defensive Talent


Force Ward: Unlike defensive talents like Flesh of Stone, Force Ward only works until the temporary HP are gone—and once they’re gone, they’re gone until the next combat. What’s interesting about Force Ward is that hits that don’t fully penetrate the ward don’t even count as hits. If you’re fighting an enemy with nasty energy drain, on-hit save riders, etc., you won’t have to contemplate any of those problems until they exhaust your ward. Accepting burn for Force Ward can extend how long you have before that happens, but keep in mind that every point of burn gives you your character level in nonlethal damage: accepting 2 burn will increase your temp HP by your character level, but it’ll also give you twice your character level in nonlethal damage! My advice is to spend burn on Force Ward early in the day if you intend to do so—the cost-benefit analysis only breaks in your favor if your ward has the chance to get depleted and refilled multiple times throughout an adventuring day.




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Aetheric Song Blast: Dealing sonic damage with your Telekinetic Blast may come in real handy sometimes, and the price is cheap.
Bloody Infusion: Several classes of enemy are outright immune to bleed damage, and it can be healed almost by accident if you’re fighting creatures with fast healing, regeneration, or divine magic. Still, Bloody Infusion is cheap, scales nicely with composite blasts, and deals free damage over time. A cheap way for aether to perform a little better on the damage front.


Pushing Infusion: CMD scores get incredibly high in later levels, and as a ¾-BAB class with no real reason to invest in feats like Improved Bull Rush, Pushing Infusion is just going to be wasted burn about 90% of the time.


2nd Level


Bowling Infusion: If anything, CMD scores against trip maneuvers are even higher than CMD scores against bull rush maneuvers...and Bowling Infusion is more expensive than Pushing Infusion. No.


3rd Level


Telekinetic Weapon: Unassuming, but actually quite powerful. Telekinetic Blast needs all the help it can get on attack and damage rolls, both of which Telekinetic Weapon provides, and the ability to scale up your enhancement bonus means you’ll have less trouble than ever penetrating DR types other than DR/magic. Unless you have a very specific need, Telekinetic Weapon and Hyper-Dimensional Blast should be an aetherkineticist’s go-to infusions.


Telekinetic Bungee: A decent way to apply touchsight to creatures.


5th Level


Dismissing Infusion: Outsiders are incredibly common as high-level enemies, and they usually come packed to the rafters with spell-like abilities, weird auras, exotic DR, energy immunities, etc. that can make a kineticist’s life hell. Think of Dismissing Infusion as a save-or-die effect against these enemies, forcing them out of your plane on a failed save. A good candidate for Improved/Greater Flurry of Blasts and Empty Infusion for when you need to banish something now.


Stitching Infusion: If Dismissing Infusion is great for getting creatures you don’t want near you away from you, Stitching Infusion is great for stopping creatures you want near you from getting away. Or something. Dimensional anchor is a fabulous anticaster spell, made only slightly weaker here by the Will save to negate. Pretty rad against outsiders and enemy casters.


Improved Bloody Infusion: Given the drastically increased cost, I still don’t think Improved Bloody Infusion is much better than its younger cousin. For blood kineticists, though, it probably comes out to at least a green.


6th Level


Disintegrating Infusion: Disintegrating Infusion can only be used with Force Blast, which you’ll only get if you select aether as your base element and then select it again as your expanded element. This is not a good path to take. Even if we examine Disintegrating Infusion on its own merits, though, we run into trouble: Fortitude saves are going to be exceptionally high by the early double-digit levels when you’ll get access to this infusion, which means that more often than not it’ll be a huge burn expenditure for even less damage than aether already deals. Disintegrating walls of force and stuff is cool, but not necessarily worth it, given the circumstances.




Infusions: Form


1st Level


Telekinetic Boomerang: Telekinetic Boomerang can’t be used with the “loosening the strands” option of Telekinetic Blast, so don’t go getting it into your head that you can use this form infusion like a returning weapon, throwing your special +5 adamantine dagger and pulling it back to you in the same action. No, the best part of Telekinetic Boomerang is that you can roll over any substance infusions or metakinesis applied to your blast on your next turn if you miss! That’s pretty great, as it saves you from winding up for a big hit and then whiffing completely. Good burn-saver, and pretty cheap at 1 burn.


3rd Level


Foe Throw: I’m generally a fan of Foe Throw. It’s cool as heck, works well on puny casters who you want to jankily teleport to your team, and doesn’t cost too much. The Fortitude save is definitely going to be problematic against many enemies in the later game, so pick your targets wisely: rogues, wizards, sorcerers, that kind of thing.


Force Hook: Force Hook is kind of like Ride the Blast, but at a much earlier level. And Ride the Blast is great, don’t get me wrong! RtB doesn’t cost any burn, though, whereas Force Hook does, and RtB doesn’t require you to select aether as your element twice. The mobility you get here (especially since it still provokes attacks of opportunity) is going to be of negligible benefit.


4th Level


Hyper-Dimensional Blast: Ignoring cover and targeting flat-footed AC is awesome, especially when you’re getting Extended Range rolled in for free. Particularly well-suited for physical blasts, which will have a harder time hitting enemies in general.


8th Level


Many Throw: Finally, aetherkineticists can flex into some AoE damage! Many Throw is great against tightly clustered groups of enemies. You’re still dealing fairly unimpressive damage with your simple blast, but if you can hit even two foes, you’ve matched the damage of a composite blast; if you’re lucky enough to hit more than two, you can start to get quite a bit of efficiency in terms of damage per burn.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Basic Telekinesis: Mage hand has always been a good cantrip, and Basic Telekinesis is functionally an improved mage hand. The versatility here is exceptionally hard to beat, and certainly more universally useful than many other elements’ basic kinesis talents. It gets passive improvements as you take Extended Range and Extreme Range, too, which is just icing on the cake.
Telekinetic Finesse: I’m a sucker for rogue-like characters and skill monkeys, as anyone who’s played a game with me knows. What could be more rogue-like than using your mind to disable traps, unlock doors and chests, plant incriminating evidence on rivals, and steal objects from enemies and friends alike? A song for every heart! A dagger for every back! Per the Sleight of Hand rules text, “You cannot use this skill to take an object from another creature during combat if the creature is aware of your presence.” But with Telekinetic Invisibility, what on earth makes you think anyone will be regularly aware of your presence?
Threaded Armor: Ghost touch armor is normally a +3 enhancement bonus, which seems way too strong for a 1st-level talent. On the other hand, incorporeal encounters are rare, so it’s not like Threaded Armor will be saving your life every day. It’s nice to be able to give others a free ghost touch weapon property on command—can really make those fights a lot easier.


Kinetic Healer: I’m a fan of Kinetic Healer for two reasons: first, it’s healing, and pretty strong healing, at that. Second, it’s a wild talent that teaches your team about burn. Drop Kinetic Healer on a party member a few times and they’ll learn very quickly that you can’t be running around taking 4 burn every turn to drop mooks in encounters. Burn is a resource to be conserved, respected, and feared, and Kinetic Healer teaches your sweet, innocent tablemates that lesson in a hurry.


Imbue Kinesis: Good only at low levels when you can’t effectively blast things and might instead need to help out an ally. It certainly doesn’t scale well at all, so I would let it go in one of your wild talent upgrades.
Kinetic Cover: Flexible access to total cover is always to be applauded, but the mini-wall has laughably few HP and no hardness—a stiff breeze will destroy this thing, much less an enemy’s concentrated effort. Still, powerful enough to merit a yellow. There’s a particularly fun episode of Side Quest Side Sesh on the Glass Cannon Network in which this wild talent saves a bunch of PCs’ lives.
Material Affinity: There’s enough of a penalty to your non-affinity blasts and enough of an opportunity cost in not selecting Basic Telekinesis that I think Material Affinity is just fine on power. It wouldn’t be my preferred utility talent as an aetherkineticist, though.


Aetheric Adaptability: Once you’ve identified the type of DR that an enemy has, you can adjust Telekinetic Blast to suit that type of DR, if they have any. Why would you need two damage types at once? Am I missing something here?
Aetheric Tempering: Aetheric Tempering technically opens you up to play a thrown weapon build as an aetherkineticist, but since you’re almost guaranteed to do less damage that way than simply blasting with random environmental debris, I can’t think why you would.
Dual Telekinetic Control: I’m really, really having trouble seeing the point of this wild talent. It’s certainly not better than Basic Telekinesis, which is one of the more flexible, powerful basic kinesis talents out there.
Life After Undeath: It’s certainly not relevant for PCs, and probably not all that relevant even for GMed monsters.


2nd Level


Distorted Area: It’s unclear to me whether the area of difficult terrain moves with you, or stays in the place you create it. I’m inclined to think the former, and if that’s the case, Distorted Area is quite good, preventing enemies from charging you, five-foot stepping into or out of squares adjacent to you, etc. Good area denial talent.
Telekinetic Haul: Yes. Utility galore here. Being able to exert this much force means you can pick up statues, fallen trees, dead bodies, etc., all the way up to huge boulders, ships, and more if you’re willing to spend 1 point of burn on the effect. It gets even better when you’ve got Extended Range and Extreme Range, as you can start to wreak havoc from 400+ feet away. A pity that throwing heavier objects with Telekinetic Blast doesn’t deal more damage—I mean, I get why mechanically, but narratively it makes less than no sense to hit someone with a 900-pound statue and deal 20 damage.
Urgent Restoration: Being able to heal yourself as a swift action is an incredible boost in combat effectiveness, even if you are going to have to take some burn to do it. Much appreciated for tanking builds.


Sharding Cover: Sharding Cover is weird in that it’s a gamble that your kinetic cover will be broken at some point, preferably sooner rather than later. This puts you into an awkward position: do you take just Kinetic Cover and Sharding Cover, knowing that your wall is going to be about as flimsy as tissue paper but trusting in the damage and difficult terrain to deal with enemies? Or do you take Improved and Greater Kinetic Cover in order to create truly impassable walls, knowing that they’re unlikely to be damaged enough to create shards? You can do both, of course, but that’s four utility talents spent. Lucky battlefield control is generally so strong.


Angelic Protection: With such a short duration, the only reason I can think of that you would use Angelic Protection is if you wanted to grant an ally a second saving throw against a mind-affecting or compulsion effect in the middle of combat. That’s good enough to rate it a yellow, at least.


Heirloom Affinity: Remember that you only get to use a weapon’s special properties if you loosen the aether strands surrounding it, which basically forces you to treat it as a thrown weapon. So now you can only throw one weapon, and if it gets stolen, lost, or sundered, you’re screwed. I’m still just not seeing the point behind these talents.


3rd Level


Telekinetic Invisibility: Even with slightly lower Stealth bonuses than invisibility, Telekinetic Invisibility is still giving you at-will invisibility for 0 burn, plus the equivalent of the excellent Dampen Presence feat. Invest in Stealth even a little as an aetherkineticist, and you’re going to be incredibly difficult to find. I’m going to quote the invisibility text here directly, as it has a great deal of impact on which actions will break your invisibility and which won’t:


The spell ends if the subject attacks any creature. For purposes of this spell, an attack includes any spell targeting a foe or whose area or effect includes a foe. Exactly who is a foe depends on the invisible character's perceptions. Actions directed at unattended objects do not break the spell. Causing harm indirectly is not an attack. Thus, an invisible being can open doors, talk, eat, climb stairs, summon monsters and have them attack, cut the ropes holding a rope bridge while enemies are on the bridge, remotely trigger traps, open a portcullis to release attack dogs, and so forth.


        Telekinetic Invisibility definitely breaks if you use any of your blasts to target enemies, as do talents like Telekinetic Maneuvers or Primal Aether. But what about using Telekinetic Haul to float a giant block of marble above an enemy’s head, then drop it? Nope, doesn’t break the effect. Using Kinetic Healer? Nope, just fine. Stealing the enemy’s sword that your fighter just disarmed? Totally fine.
Unweave Magic: Aether gets an abundance of fantastic utility talents at 3rd level, and Unweave Magic is one of the best, allowing you to ready a Telekinetic Blast to counterspell spells and spell-like abilities. This makes you the bane of casters everywhere, as you can essentially counter an unlimited number of spells! Make sure you pick up Dispel Focus and Greater Dispel Focus if you’re leaning into this playstyle, as it will only make your counterspells more deadly.


Aetheric Sniper: At-will invisibility is one of the aetherkineticist’s main tricks, so you’ll want to keep that running as best you can even during combat, making it harder for enemies to pinpoint where you are as you pelt them with rubble. Aetheric Sniper makes it a lot easier.
Improved Kinetic Cover: Improved Kinetic Cover fixes all the problems of Kinetic Cover and then some, primarily by giving it a lot more HP and some hardness. Now enemies will have to actually work to punch through, rather than blowing it over with a light breeze.


Corporeal Tear: Narratively gross, and mechanically too powerful. Give it spell resistance and make it cost 1 burn, for crying out loud. If you take it, though, you have a fairly steady damage source from having your allies jump through the tear in an opponent’s body.
Friend Throw: Since aetherkineticists are more about support and utility than damage, it’s very possible that your best action in a given round might be getting an ally into position.        
Primal Aether: Primal magic events can be good or bad for your team, but they’re always unpredictable. Since the only truly beneficial effect is 33-38, where everyone is affected by heal, it’s easiest to throw this preemptively into a room crowded with baddies, shut the door, and mop up what’s left once you’ve figured out what’s going on. At 0 burn, what’s the harm in trying? You could get effects as limited as changing color, or you could cast harm or produce a huge energy storm! I was about to recommend a 1-level dip in oracle with the Spellscar mystery in order to pick up the Primal Mastery revelation, which would allow you to better regulate which effects you got, but I note that the rules text for that revelation says “whenever you cast a spell that triggers a primal magic event,” thereby excluding supernatural abilities like Primal Aether. Pity.
Self Telekinesis: Flame Jet, Self Telekinesis, and Gravity Control all work the same way, as do their older cousins. These abilities won’t be quite combat-ready at the time you get them, since you need to keep investing your standard action in flight, but for out-of-combat purposes, they’re aces. Besides, you need these to progress to the Greater versions of all these talents, and you definitely want to do that.
Touchsight: Let me be clear: the purpose of Touchsight is not to spy on enemies or anything like that—the duration is way too short. No, what it’s primarily good for is letting you cut through magical darkness, mists, fogs, mirror image, blur, displacement, and every other nasty caster trick you can think of. If you can hit an enemy even once with Touchsight, you’re in the clear for every blast you aim their way for the foreseeable future.


Automatic Reconstruction: Fast healing this slow is unfortunately not going to cut it in combat, so there’s no point spending burn on Kinetic Cover, Improved Kinetic Cover, etc. This might come into play later on for “healing” structures created with Aether Architect, Kinetic Cityscape, etc., but I’d say those are fairly niche, endgame uses.
Seismic Stomp: The action economy here is very good, giving you potentially multiple trips for a move action—and you get your Constitution modifier to CMB, to boot! Unfortunately, combat maneuvers just don’t scale well into the endgame, so this tactic will only get less effective as time goes by.
Spellthief: I guess it’s nice that you can do this as often as your immediate/swift action economy allows, but how many times per day are you being targeted with spells specifically delivered via melee touch attack? It can’t be many, right?


Visceral Whip: Gross, first of all, and second of all, incredibly ineffective. Whips are really hard to use without a bunch of feat investment, and while you’ll get that once you reach Improved Visceral Whip, for now you’re going to provoke attacks of opportunity and be unable to deal damage to any enemy with a natural armor bonus. Skip until you can get Improved at 10th level.


Dimensional Rip / Improved / Greater / Deceptive / Enlarged / Invasive / Vacuum / Corporeal: The dimensional ripper archetype is really, really cool, allowing you to tear holes in reality and shove enemies and blasts through them like you’re playing a game of Portal. Both the archetype and the utility talents that mimic many of the archetype’s class features are massively overpowered, however; as it stands, there’s little reason to play another archetype—and certainly not a first-party archetype—when dimensional ripper is available. It takes a lot of third-party options away from aether, time, and void, but if you want my advice, leave these wild talents out of your games.
Suggested Fix: Eliminating the various upgrades to Dimensional Rip might do it, honestly, along with bumping Dimensional Rip up to a 5th-level ability. Keep the burn cost at one, for sure.


4th Level


Telekinetic Maneuvers: I know, I know, I’m not usually a fan of combat maneuvers. Telekinetic Maneuvers is different, though. You get your strongest ability scores added to your CMB (in addition to your caster level, if your GM rules you have one, which they should, as expanded element stipulates that you get a CL boost at 15th level if you’ve maintained the same element all along, and you can’t boost a non-existent CL), you can perform just about any maneuver in the game without provoking an attack of opportunity (well, except for the one if you fail your concentration check), and you can even start performing steals and dirty tricks if you have Telekinetic Finesse (you do, right?). Being able to perform any maneuver at range with 0 burn cost and no risk of retaliation is fantastic, and in some cases (e.g., grappling a caster) potentially more useful than damage.
Vital Fibers: Why bother building for natural attacks when you can steal other people’s limbs? This is a pretty wacky ability, but extra limbs always have great uses, so I can’t rate it poorly. The burn cost will keep you from becoming a centaur for longer than an afternoon, at least.


Flash Step: So it’s a quick runner’s shirt, but as a utility talent. There’s certainly a lot you can do here, not least gathering energy twice in a single turn. If you don’t use your swift or immediate actions for much (e.g., quicken metakinesis) you’re probably safe to use this quite liberally.
Kinetic Shelter: A fun little bungalow for you and your team, although other tactics like rope trick are probably a little less conspicuous.
Spying Touchsight: So listen: I’m a huge fan of divination and spying abilities. Being able to use Mind Eye on an occultist to snoop out the entire layout of a dungeon is just incredibly useful—I don’t care who’s asking. Spying Touchsight is similar: for the price of a ranged touch attack and 0 burn, you can just sit there meditating as your target feeds you the entire layout of their lair. You don’t get to control where the target/sensor goes, which is a point in the con column, but there really are no other downsides here. Even if someone discovers the strand of aether with see invisibility, you’ll still have ample time to sever the connection before they find you. Pretty awesome espionage tool.


Healing Burst: The inability to direct where the burn goes and half healing knocks the ability down too far for it to be consistently worth purchasing. It’s way better if you have positive blast, but if you have positive blast, you’re the world’s biggest chump anyway.
Telekinetic Grasp: With a Fortitude save every round to negate, Telekinetic Grasp is hard to use effectively, and you have Friend Throw if you want to use willing creatures. I’d stick with that in most instances.


5th Level


Greater Self Telekinesis: With your standard action no longer gobbled up, you can fly in combat with no trouble, including hover checks. You won’t be able to gather power unless you’re hovering, stationary, however, so keep that in mind.
Greater Unweave Magic: Better range and better action economy on an already great utility talent make Greater Unweave Magic aces. Casters will continue to hate you.
Improved Visceral Whip: Improved Visceral Whip actually gives you the tools you need to succeed at a whip playstyle. If you purchased the feats already (as seems likely) then you can retrain them; if not, and you’re jumping in for the first time, this wild talent takes care of everything.
Reactive Touchsight: Aether keeps on giving! Yeah, this is some great uncanny dodge flavor, and the ability to always participate in the surprise round if enemies are emerging within 30 feet of you is fantastic. Definitely a buy.


Aether Puppet: Animated objects are going to be quite weak by the time you get them at this level—your base buddy is a CR 3 creature, and you’ll only ever scale up to a CR 9 creature. Still, you can animate as many objects as you like, moving them into flanking position with your teammates, harassing casters, and more. A decent strategy when you’re running out of burn for the day is to switch on Telekinetic Invisibility and just start animating two aether puppets in every encounter, hanging back and using your two move actions to direct them both. Will they hit often? No. But they can soak damage and make nuisances of themselves quite easily, especially once you start creating them out of rarer metals. Feel free to run them into traps, have them burst through doors to absorb damage in a surprise round, or act as soft cover for an ally who needs to get out of Dodge in a creature’s giant threatened radius—there will always be more where they came from.
Flicker Charge: It’s an interesting mobility tool, and certainly helpful when you need to kite an enemy. It’s a pity that the elements that get Flicker Charge aren’t usually the ones you’d be building a melee attacker with, though.


Force Barrier: I don’t know why you would choose to take 1 point of burn when you could just sacrifice another standard action at the start of your turn to renew the barrier. Anyway, Force Barrier is a big middle finger to any encounter that doesn’t have a caster with disintegrate prepared, incorporeal encounters included. It’s a great panic button if you need to turtle up, but the lack of ability to shape, move, or expand the force effect to other teammates limits it significantly.
Improved Spellthief: I don’t think that Improved Spellthief does nearly enough to improve upon its predecessor, but your mileage may vary. How often are casters around you holding their charges?


Lingering Energy: I’m not a fan. It costs 1 burn and eats your immediate action, and even if an enemy does proc the blast, they’re likely to avoid all the damage. Nah.


6th Level


Greater Kinetic Cover: Battlefield control is one of the most helpful things any PC can offer to a party, and there are few battlefield control tools more effective than a wall of stone. Loads of HP, big area, more flexible positioning than the spell, and it’s not even instantaneous, so you don’t have to worry about leaving a big old wall lying around to obstruct you.
Long-Distance Travel: Long-distance teleportation is universally useful, and this talent is no exception.


Force Gloves: It’s a real pity that Quivering Defense is such a bad utility talent, because Force Gloves is pretty good. The shield bonus is fine, I guess, but the real headliner is the jarring shield quality your gloves take on when you’re under the effects of Force Ward. Every time someone hits you and you have HP remaining in the buffer, as an immediate action you can make them save or be dazed for one round. Gets you some breathing room, at least.
Suffocate: Suffocate can be an exceptional tool for killing arcane spellcasters before they can do any harm to you; for any other target, the slow speed and concentration will be dealbreakers.


Dimensional Traveler: Very narrowly useful for Kinetic Blade, Kinetic Whip, Visceral Whip, and natural attack builds, for whom Dimensional Traveler basically acts as pounce. If you’re a run-of-the-mill ranged kineticist using a kinetic blast once a round, Dimensional Traveler won’t do a thing for you.
Kinetic Revivification: I’m of the opinion that other classes should generally be handling this kind of thing, but there’s no denying that resurrection is powerful in a pinch. It’s definitely worth considering if you’re playing a kinetic chirurgeon.


7th Level


Greater Visceral Whip: Greater Visceral Whip completes the picture for whip builds, no burn cost, an unlimited duration, more damage, more feats, and more discounts on substance infusions. Perfection.


Elemental Eater: How useful Elemental Eater is will depend almost entirely on on what element you’re using it with, and how prevalent that element is in your campaign. For common elements like fire, this ability could rise to a blue; for rare elements like force (aether), negative energy (void), or sonic (sound), it’ll likely be red.
Improved Dimensional Traveler: Even for Kinetic Blade, Kinetic Whip, Visceral Whip, and natural attack builds, Dimensional Maneuvers and Dimensional Savant are not as necessary as Dimensional Assault and Dimensional Dervish. Can be nice if you’re using whips to trip or reposition foes, flank with yourself, etc., though.
Spell Deflection: Spell turning has a lot of weaknesses, and Spell Deflection has even more weaknesses than spell turning. Many spells, for example, are outright ineligible to be affected by spell turning, so you take the full effect of the spells if you’re unlucky. For those spells that are eligible, you still have to deal with the fact that you’re getting 1d10-1 levels of protection (average 4.5) instead of spell turning’s 1d4+6 (average 8.5) levels of protection. If you get unlucky on that d10 rolls, you won’t have any protection. I know you don’t take any burn for using it as a standard action, but even so, I’m just not very excited about it.


8th Level


Telekinetic Deflection: Telekinetic Deflection is a lot of fun—I mean, come on, just look at that material component! “I’m rubber, you’re glue, bounces off me and sticks to you!” Deflection can be a great help if you ever find yourself pinned down in melee, and only grows more potent as you stack on AC-boosting defensive talents like Flesh of Wood or Shroud of Water. Keep in mind that high-level enemies have a lot of creative ways to destroy players, though; attack rolls are generally the very least of your problems.
Telekinetic Globe: Potentially useful for capturing larger enemies (that sphere diameter is problematic, but you can definitely tag Large and Huge enemies at this level) and taking advantage of their poor Reflex saves to move them around at will.


9th Level


Aether Architect: Cooool. So now you can build your own floating city out of walls of force, which is just the most bad-owls thing I’ve ever heard of. Is it combat-effective? Not in the slightest, unless you spend a lot of time preparing a battlefield well ahead of time. But it’s wicked cool.
Kinetic Cityscape: Unfortunately for aether, Kinetic Cityscape kinda steals the wind out from Aether Architect’s sails; fortunately for everyone else, this is a really cool capstone ability that can help you found a kineticist city once you’re done with your campaign. There aren’t a lot of combat-effective 9th-level utility talents, so why not?




Kinetic Invocations


2nd Level


Instant Armor: Scaling armor from light to medium to heavy is a bizarre option for most Pathfinder classes, which stick with one type of armor for the entire game. Even for light armor users, the short duration and slow enhancement bonus scaling make this a generally poor use of burn. I’ll crank it up to yellow because it’s effective against incorporeals, but I wouldn’t envision spending a wild talent on this unless you were really out of viable options. If you’re playing with third-party content, grabbing the Threaded Armor utility talent is probably what you want.
Kinetic Reverberation: Kinetic reverberation loses some of its ranking right off the bat because so few monsters in the Pathfinder bestiaries use manufactured weapons. For those that do use manufactured weapons, kinetic reverberation will have to overcome the weapon’s hardness and Fortitude save in order to even deal damage...and after all that, you’re still breaking or destroying your own loot. It can be effective in campaigns chock full of humanoid enemies, but it’s niche in many scenarios.


3rd Level


Protective Spirit: A rare hunter/ranger-exclusive spell, protective spirit is fabulous for classes that are going to be provoking a lot of attacks of opportunity. Kineticists are a ranged spellcasting class that often needs to duck out of melee range, so you can expect to provoke a lot of attacks of opportunity playing one. I wish the duration were longer, but with your decent BAB and excellent Dexterity scores, you can expect this spell to protect you from quite a bit of damage over the course of your career if you use it wisely.


4th Level


Telekinetic Charge: If you could use gather power on utility talents, I’d say sure, go for it. But you can’t. What are you left with? 2 points of burn in exchange for positioning your ally closer to an enemy and granting them a free attack. Seems pretty stiff on the costs for not much benefit, but it’s undeniably cool. Who doesn’t want to alley-oop their party’s fighter into flanking position on that backline mage?


5th Level


Etheric Shards: Etheric shards is probably second only to wall of thorns in Pathfinder for battlefield control. It’s shapeable, can affect cubes in the air to tag flying enemies, makes freaking ghosts bleed, halves movement speed, and forces vicious, stacking bleed damage on absolutely everything for trying to attack, cast spells, move, or perform any other action. Very, very nasty trick for any kineticist to be able to pull out of their sleeve, and I’m proud of aether for hanging onto it. You’ll want this ability.
⚡ Air
Air


        Within the sphere of Paizo-official elements, air is one of the best all-around jacks of all trades you could ask for. You’ve got classics for mobility like Wings of Air, good defensive powers like Aerial Evasion and Greater Air Shroud, team buffs like Celerity, ranged combat buffs like Air’s Reach, battlefield control abilities like Greater Magnetism, Engulfing Winds, Weather Master, and Wind Manipulator, and even some espionage/intrigue abilities like Windsight and Voice of the Wind. Since air is more capable than any other element of keeping you out of arm’s reach, you’ll typically hover a few hundred feet back as the ranged air support, letting loose range-boosted blasts with Air’s Reach and Extended Range while trusting to flight from Wings of Air and evasion/miss chances from Aerial Evasion and Enveloping Winds to save you from stray hits. If you ever find yourself needing to buff your team rather than deal damage yourself, Magnetic Infusion and Celerity make a great combo for ensuring that your allies can hit better, faster, and harder than ever. Of note, Electric Blast is quite a bit stronger than Air Blast, so I’d recommend that most air kineticists focus on energy blasts instead of physical blasts. Chain alone makes it all worth it!


        Air gets the biggest boost from third-party wild talents in its battlefield control department, where the Cloud Cover utility talent allows it to create fog clouds at a height of 30 feet at will. Once you’ve flown into these cloudbanks, you can use Windsight to see out of them; enemies won’t often be able to reach you there, and they certainly won’t be able to visually pinpoint your location. Later iterations of this utility talent line allow you to teleport between cloudbanks, shoot kinetic blasts out of any point within a cloud bank, and more. Between the inherent difficulty of even finding an air kineticist once they’re in such a defensible position and the futility of targeting them with AoE blasts—thanks, Aerial Evasion—an air kineticist can be extremely hard to take down if given the right conditions. Tight quarters, subterranean or indoor battles, places with obstructed long-range sightlines, etc. will eliminate some of your main strengths as an air kineticist, so try to avoid or compensate for those environments whenever you can. Of note, even though Recon is listed as an air kineticist’s main strength, it’s actually mobility that they’re best at—the Recon portion of Recon & Mobility is actually fairly limited in this element.


First-Party: Recon > Buffing, Ranged > Control > Anticaster, Debuffing, Skills > Melee, Summoning
Third-Party: Recon > Buffing, Control, Ranged > Anticaster, Debuffing, Melee, Skills > Summoning






	Mean Rating 
(Paizo Content)
	Mean Rating 
(3PP Content)
	Mean Rating (Combined)
	Form Infusions
	2.5
	1.0
	2.3
	Substance Infusions
	2.7
	2.7
	2.7
	All Infusions
	2.6
	2.3
	2.5
	Utility Talents
	3.0
	2.8
	2.9
	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Class Skills


        Can you guess what movement mode the air element will spend most of its time in? If you guessed flying, you’re right! Can you see why having ranks in Fly might be important here? Fly checks are fortunately quite easy to beat, especially with kineticists’ generally excellent Dexterity scores, low armor check penalties, and Fly as a class skill. Nevertheless, it merits your investment in case you need to fly in high winds, etc. Knowledge (Nature) is also a decent Knowledge skill that’s useful for identifying fey, animals, and more. Could do a great deal worse here.




Defensive Talent


Enveloping Winds: Yeah, Enveloping Winds really isn’t bad. Miss chances scale much better into the endgame than AC does, so that’s fine. Being unable to block massive ranged weapons or magical ranged attacks is problematic, but I can see why Paizo omitted that. At any rate, I wouldn’t really recommend spending burn on this ability unless you know you’re going to be fighting a lot of archers; if you are, however, there’s nothing quite like Enveloping Winds. Certainly ranged attacks will be one of the only modes of attack that can even catch a well-prepared aerokineticist.




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Bloody Infusion: Several classes of enemy are outright immune to bleed damage, and it can be healed almost by accident if you’re fighting creatures with fast healing, regeneration, or divine magic. Still, Bloody Infusion is cheap, scales nicely with composite blasts, and deals free damage over time.
Gusting Infusion: You really shouldn’t try to use Gusting Infusion offensively to blow creatures away, although you theoretically could against swarms and other very small creatures. No, you should use Gusting Infusion to blow away mists, fogs, gases, and other caster tricks that get used against you. It’s not a fabulous substance infusion, but it’s better than nothing.


Thundering Infusion: Deafened isn’t a super powerful status condition, and given how strong monsters become at Fortitude saves in later levels, this infusion will have a short shelf-life.


Pushing Infusion: CMD scores get incredibly high in later levels, and as a ¾-BAB class with no real reason to invest in feats like Improved Bull Rush, Pushing Infusion is just going to be wasted burn about 90% of the time.


2nd Level


Penetrating Infusion: I personally think that Penetrating Infusion should have been a 1-burn infusion, but maybe that’s just me. At any rate, Penetrating Infusion is very mediocre for air, as the proportion of creatures with electricity resistance in a given CR tranche rarely rises above about 8%. Cold and fire resistance, however, are much more common, hence this infusion is correspondingly better for water and fire.


3rd Level


Magnetic Infusion: Air will be more judicious in its use of Magnetic Infusion than earth will, but even so, it’s a phenomenal infusion, reasonably cheap and providing an excellent buff for dang near any martial ally who’s attacking your target. Untyped bonus, too. You want this.


Synaptic Infusion: Mind-affecting effects won’t be universally useful—constructs, oozes, plants, undead, and vermin are all immune, as well as some outsiders—and a Will save to negate is also a bit unfortunate. Nevertheless, staggered is a strong condition.
