I’m not sure why Paizo gave enemies the option to remove the condition with a move action; you’re still left with just a standard, and isn’t that kind of the point of the staggered condition anyway? Maybe it’s to give enemies a chance to 5-foot step. Anyway, it’s still okay for denying enemies access to the action economy, but a bit too limited to attain an A- or S-tier ranking.


5th Level


Lock Up Infusion: Your very first save-or-die infusion, in essence. Once an enemy is paralyzed, it’s all too easy to walk up and coup de grace them while they’re helpless. And a nice duration, too!


Bloodrust Infusion: Guaranteed Constitution damage is certainly worth the burn.


Improved Bloody Infusion: Given the drastically increased cost, I still don’t think Improved Bloody Infusion is much better than its younger cousin. For blood kineticists, though, it probably comes out to at least a green.
Unfolding Wind Infusion: Another one that’s strictly for monk/kineticist gestalts. Why?


7th Level


Overload Infusion: A really interesting take on a damage-over-time ability. The burn cost is obviously high at this level, but you’ll have several tiers of infusion specialization to mitigate that. Even so, it will almost certainly be a substance infusion that you can’t really pair with a form infusion due to the sheer cost of such a proposition. Fun to watch enemies melt themselves, though.




Infusions: Form


1st Level


Energize Weapon: This would be pretty good if you were playing a gestalt kineticist with a more mainstream martial class, as it’s essentially free damage on a full-attack action. Not very much damage, though, and since it locks you out of other form infusions (including Kinetic Blade and Kinetic Whip) I’m having a lot of difficulty seeing why a single-class kineticist would choose to use it.


Discharging Infusion: The campaigns in which Discharging Infusion would be unconditionally worth your investment are few and far between (Ruins of Azlant and Skull & Shackles are really the only Paizo-official APs, but if you’re playing those, why the fork aren’t you a hydrokineticist?) so it’s hard to judge it favorably.


3rd Level


Torrent: Sure, why not? Line effects are more niche than cones, but corridors are a thing in Pathfinder, and you’ll often be able to hit one or more enemies in a line. As always, energy blasts (fire and blue flame in particular) come out stronger here than anything else, so especially consider this infusion if you’re playing a pyrokineticist.


Bolt: There are a few reasons I can think of why you might wish to use Bolt. First, and perhaps most compellingly, you want to avoid cover. If any enemies or walls get in the way, poof, you attack from above. That’s not bad, as these things go, but you also have Snake available for the same cost and at the same level, which can even attack around corners—Bolt can’t do that. Second, you can hit enemies in the air and on the ground with Bolt if they’re in the same square on the x- and y-axis. Please name for me the last time one enemy was positioned in the air precisely above another. I’ll wait. Third, you deal extra damage when you’re attacking outside in a storm. This is true, but the fact is that just by using Bolt, you’ve opened yourself up for a Reflex save for half damage, which will probably result in less damage for more burn. Bad combo, not to mention the requirement of being outside and in a thunderstorm. No, I’ll take Snake every time.


4th Level


Cyclone: Here’s the key phrase: all creatures and objects. RAW, you’re destroying not only enemies but also allies and every piece of equipment (yours included!) in the radius. And since it’s centered on you, you can never do any of the creative positioning that’s possible with effects like fireball. That’s what we in the biz like to call “bad.”


5th Level


Chain: Chain is quite good. If you can hit even three targets, you’ve dealt as much damage as a composite blast, just spread out over a wider area. Of course, energy resistance penalizes smaller hits more than bigger hits; even so, you’re likely to hit with electric blast’s touch attacks at your highest BAB, no matter what kinds of enemies you’re facing, which compensates for lower damage a bit. I’d only use it when you have a bunch of enemies very tightly clustered, though.


7th Level


Cloud: There you go. Cloud is what Cyclone wanted to be: moveable, no save, continuous damage over multiple rounds, and even some battlefield control thrown in there for good measure. A fitting capstone for your form infusions.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Air’s Reach: Unlike Extended Range and Extreme Range, which are infusions that gobble up burn and your form infusion slot for any given blast, Air’s Reach is a passive buff that affects all simple and composite blasts that have some element of air. You’ll still want Extended Range (and Extreme Range can be fun for long-distance sniping, if you’re into that) but otherwise, Air’s Reach has you covered. Remember that it won’t apply to blasts from any other element, only air’s simple and composite blasts!
Elemental Acceleration: This is basically the monk’s fast movement class feature, for the cost of a utility talent. Movement speed rarely breaks the power curve, so I don’t feel the need to veto this one, but it is very good. Aerokineticists in particular will appreciate the bonuses to their flight speed from Wings of Air.


Air Cushion: Neither Air Cushion nor Air’s Leap is particularly astounding, but you need to pick one as a prerequisite for Wings of Air, so...well, this would be my choice. Air’s Leap is completely outmoded by Wings of Air, whereas Air Cushion can still be useful once you get Wings of Air in case of dispel magic, control weather, etc. It might just save your life in early levels, too, if you’re about to take massive falling damage.
Voice of the Wind: Yeah, I’ll take that! Voice of the Wind is a great tool for communicating with members of your team over long distances, which never hurt anyone. A solid B.


Air’s Leap: Full disclosure? I actually like Air’s Leap a lot more than Air Cushion. Air Cushion is safe, mechanically optimal in comparison with Air’s Leap...but boring. Wouldn’t you rather jump 100 feet at a stretch without even trying that hard? I mean, I can’t honestly recommend this as your prerequisite for Wings of Air, but it’s a whole lot more fun.
Basic Aerokinesis: In addition to making you look dashing AF in a cloak as it billows about you, a +2 on saves vs. breath weapons, gases, and clouds ain’t half bad. It ain’t half good, either, but it ain’t half bad.
Imbue Kinesis: Good only at low levels when you can’t effectively blast things and might instead need to help out an ally. It certainly doesn’t scale well at all, so I would let it go in one of your wild talent upgrades.


Aerial Adaptation: Altitude sickness? Electricity resistance? How often do you come up against either of those things? Eminently skippable.
Air Shroud: Good for aquatic adventuring, of course, but if you know you’re going to be in an aquatic campaign, why not be a hydrokineticist? This isn’t worth a wild talent.


Basic Electrokinesis: Big Disable Device bonuses, big damage bonuses on all electric blasts, and you can select it in place of the relatively weak Basic Aerokinesis? Far too powerful. Veto.
Suggested Fix: Eliminate the damage bonuses, reduce the skill bonuses to ¼ kineticist level, and perhaps give something like trapfinding for any trap that would produce electricity, flavored as being able to sense the presence of current within 10 ft. of yourself.
Simple Versatility: Paizo designed kinetic blasts to be used with only the infusions they get on the label; opening up other infusions often has unintended consequences, and is certainly way too powerful for a 1st-level utility talent. See, e.g., Composite Versatility and Improved Composite Versatility, which have burn costs and per-day use limits, for an example of how you might be able to expand access to infusions without breaking the power curve.
Suggested Fix: Pick one infusion belonging to your element’s other simple blast; you can apply that to the first simple blast. None of this BS about applying every infusion you want.
Wind Blade: Some third-party content has a nasty habit of introducing utility talents that can be selected in place of your basic kinesis utility talents; in some cases, these are harmless, but in others, they grant bonuses to attack and damage that are simply too mechanically optimal for players to consider leaving their basic kinesis utility talent alone. 
Suggested Fix: Eliminate the attack and damage bonuses, and don’t allow Wind Blade to be taken in place of Basic Aerokinesis. It’s a 1st-level talent, but you can’t get it for free.


2nd Level


Aerial Supremacy: A little boost to attack and damage doesn’t feel super Cheaty McCheaterson at 2nd level, especially because you won’t be flying until you acquire Wings of Air as a 3rd-level ability. Some interesting bonuses for melee builds, too.
Greater Voice of the Wind: Ooh, yeah, some nice upgrades here. I’m happy to give both Voice of the Wind and its big cousin the pass they deserve; they’re utility and intrigue abilities, but then again these are utility wild talents. If you’ve got no utility to go with your utility wild talents, you’re what I like to call “a pyrokineticist.” Oh! Body blow.
Magnetic Pull: The rare ability that actually allows you to tank, and tank a bunch of creatures, no less! Geokineticists are certainly up to the task with their boatload of HP and DR/adamantine; aerokineticists, probably less so, since they’re flimsier and operate at range more often. Do note that it affects only metal weapons, so this is best taken in games that pit you against mostly humanoid or native outsider enemies.


Cloud Cover: On its own, Cloud Cover isn’t any great shakes. The fact that the cloud is parked in the air (and not on the ground, where enemies tend to be) is a definite strike against it. If you have regular access to fog-cutting lenses, goz masks, a wand of ashen path, or the Windsight utility talent, you should be able to take much better advantage of your own clouds, especially once you get Wings of Air and can fly up into them.
Lightning Step: Five feet of movement probably isn’t going to make a huge difference when you’re up against breath weapons, etc., and you won’t face off against Reflex saves so often that you’ll get lots of movement out of this wild talent in every combat. It’s not useless, just a bit situational.


Living Capacitor: Body capacitance is a bad spell, even when it’s constant. Guess what deals massive electricity damage? Creatures that tend to be immune to electricity. Yeah, no. Embrace these turds, my children, that you might select better talents in their place.


3rd Level


Wings of Air: Fly is, quite simply, one of those game-changing spells that no one can afford to ignore. Being under the effects of fly constantly is a benchmark in power that you’ll never want to turn away from once you’ve tasted it.


Aerial Evasion: Yeeesss. Yes, indeed. With your (likely) high Dexterity score and strong Reflex save progression, aerokineticists will be in a prime position to succeed at Reflex saves, thereby taking no damage. I wish there were some way to buy into improved evasion or avoid spending the burn on this ability, but it is what it is.
Celerity: Haste is one of those spells that’s so indispensable that literally every encounter is made easier by its addition. And at-will haste? Well, at-will haste is simply too tasty to ever be ignored, especially if you can open up some 3rd-level spell slots for your caster friends by expending a little burn. The only question you need to ask yourself is, are you going to take Celerity or Wings of Air first? (I think I’d pick Wings of Air, but they’re both element-defining wild talents.)
Windsight: Windsight would be worth your investment purely for the ability to see through fog and mist—that alone is excellent. The latter half of Windsight is somewhat difficult to make sense of (and probably a real pain in the neck for your GM to have to keep track of—how often do they know the direction and speed of the wind off the top of their heads?) but cool for scouting and spying.


Engulfing Winds: Wind wall is a generally useful spell, excellent for blocking small creatures and projectiles from harming your party. Battlefield control is certainly not the worst use of your burn.
Improved Magnetic Pull: The increased radius is the big-ticket item here—you can probably forget the clause about redirecting electric spells.


Magnetism: The limitation to only bull rush and drag maneuvers greatly limits Magnetism in comparison with, say, Telekinetic Maneuvers from aether. And only metal objects or creatures? That’ll be functional some of the time, but certainly not all.
Storm Diffuser: Gale-force winds probably aren’t the best for flying aerokineticists, which is why it might be nice not to have to deal with them. Probably not a top-priority wild talent, but nice to have in some circumstances.


Oppressive Atmosphere: The movement penalties scale way too quickly, Reflex save penalties are strong when you’re lobbing around form infusions left and right, and there’s no burn cost, making it essentially constant.
Suggested Fix: Reduce the radius to 15 ft., reduce the movement penalties to 10 ft./5 levels, and give it a save and spell resistance if it’s going to cost 0 burn—say, a Fortitude save each round while enemies remain in the radius of the atmosphere. GMs should also consider making this affect allies.
Storm Cloud: It’s the huge damage bonuses that kill this one. A 20th-level kineticist using Greater Flurry of Blasts can shoot 16 blasts; Storm Cloud adds a +10 bonus to each, for 160 extra damage. As level-dependent damage, this would also be subject to empower metakinesis, for a grand total of 240 extra damage. See the problem?
Suggested Fix: The ability to make enemies second-guess themselves about where you are in your clouds is the real power of Storm Cloud, so lean on that. I’d be fine with a +1 damage bonus to each blast in a Flurry of Blasts, non-stacking. No more. Always think, “How would my min-maxing players abuse and break this?”


4th Level


Fog Step: Fog Step + Cloud Cover + Storm Cloud is a great combo, as you can pop around inside several storm clouds, shooting electric blasts out of random points in any cloud. It would be very difficult under these circumstances for enemies to figure out where you are.


Flash Step: So it’s a quick runner’s shirt, but as a utility talent. There’s certainly a lot you can do here, not least gathering energy twice in a single turn. If you don’t use your swift or immediate actions for much (e.g., quicken metakinesis) you’re probably safe to use this quite liberally.


Air’s Overarching Reach: Kineticists will probably not be using thrown weapons or projectile weapons. Not sure why that was included. Increasing the range of your utility talents? Now that’s more interesting. There aren’t a huge number of utility talents that would benefit, but those that do become even better.
Body of Air: Yeesh. After the almost meth-like high of all the great 3rd-level air talents, Body of Air is aggressively mediocre. Gaseous form is generally considered a pretty weak spell, good only for very slow scouting and infiltration. The fact that you can’t even defend yourself while shifted is also a big strike against it.
Fade into the Mist: Gaseous form is still a decent scouting spell, especially as you can use your base speed to start and add Elemental Acceleration if you feel the need for speed. It’s absolute poison to a kineticist if you get into combat, though, as the spell eliminates your supernatural abilities, which include gather power, infusions, elemental defense, metakinesis, internal buffer, expanded element, supercharge, composite specialization, metakinetic master, and omnikinesis. Gulp. So, like, really don’t use this if there’s a chance you might need to fight something.


Improved Celerity: Turning ¾-BAB classes into full-BAB classes is something that should be done with the greatest of care. Don’t hand out these kinds of bonuses willy-nilly.
Suggested Fix: Give players the option when accepting 1 point of burn to cast Celerity with a duration of rounds/level to also accept an amount of burn equal to the increase in BAB they’d like, to a maximum BAB of their kineticist level. If you’re a 20th-level kineticist, for example, with BAB +15, you could accept 5 points of burn to be treated as BAB +20 whenever you accept 1 point of burn to cast Celerity with a duration of rounds/level. Sure, you’ll be way more deadly, but now you’ll also have to take 100 points of damage in order to become so.


5th Level


Complete Circuit: I love Complete Circuit. It’s inventive, it’s hella cool, and amps up (no pun intended) the AoE damage that air is capable of. GMs, you need to increase the burn cost to 1 burn for using the ability, and 2 burn for dealing more than minimum damage.
Greater Air Shroud: Life bubble is an awesome spell, protecting you against many common caster and monster tricks (cloudkill, stinking cloud, inhaled poisons, etc.) while also acting as long-lasting endure elements and protection against pressure. A pity that the prerequisite is so stanky.
Greater Windsight: A great intrigue and spying ability. And since Greater Windsight’s prerequisite is actually good, there’s a much better chance that you’ll get around to taking this talent than Greater Air Shroud.


Lingering Energy: I’m not a fan. It costs 1 burn and eats your immediate action, and even if an enemy does proc the blast, they’re likely to avoid all the damage. Nah.


Crushing Atmosphere: As with Oppressive Atmosphere, this just needs a more robust save and spell resistance. It’s too powerful without.
Suggested Fix: Make it a Fortitude partial save: half damage and no status condition on a successful save, full damage and fatigued on a failed save, and specify that enemies can’t become exhausted by failing the save twice. Set spell resistance to yes.


6th Level


Wind Manipulator: Why on earth are you getting this for 0 burn? Even if you have to spend a standard action each round, you can easily use this to tear down buildings, destroy ships, blow entire encounters away, throw an enemy camp into disarray, etc. while hovering comfortably 300 feet away. Heck of a lot a creative player could do with this.


Suffocate: Suffocate can be an exceptional tool for killing arcane spellcasters before they can do any harm to you; for any other target, the slow speed and concentration will be dealbreakers.


Winds of Freedom: As I’ve mentioned before, gaseous form is terrible for combat applications, which means that you’re probably running if you’re using Winds of Freedom. Running really quickly. It can be useful for slipping into a different position if your enemy has you pinned down somewhere. Probably gets better with Elemental Acceleration.


7th Level


Magnetic Chain: A really fun power for positioning enemies on the battlefield. No ruling on whether this movement provokes or not; if so, it would probably rate purple.
Sky Walk: Someone on the team should have a means of granting flight to everyone by now, but this certainly ain’t bad: ten times the duration of fly, and everyone on the team gets it? For 1 burn? I’m sold.


Empowered Chain: This one’s interesting, actually. You’re spending 1 point of burn per target, but (theoretically) getting more damage each time you do. If you spend 1 point of burn, you get +1d6 damage. 2 points, +3d6. 3 points, +6d6. 4 points, +10d6. How far are you willing to push it?


Elemental Eater: How useful Elemental Eater is will depend almost entirely on on what element you’re using it with, and how prevalent that element is in your campaign. For common elements like fire, this ability could rise to a blue; for rare elements like force (aether), negative energy (void), or sonic (sound), it’ll likely be red.


Elemental Airstrike: It’s a mythic spell. What on earth makes you think players should have this for 1 burn?
Suggested Fix: I dunno. Make it a summon monster clone, but for your element’s elemental type. Don't do elemental bombardment.


8th Level


Weather Master: Good for all the same reasons that Wind Manipulator is good. The wind-up (forgive the pun) is a little slower, but correspondingly more devastating. Enjoy for 0 burn.


9th Level


Greater Magnetism: An improved version of a druid-only 8th-level spell, at will. And no save or spell resistance! If any creature at all uses metal anything, you’ve got ‘em—you can bull rush people 40 ft. per round with Greater Magnetism, and your lack of reliance on a rounds/level duration means that you can scoot people anywhere you want, anytime you want. My favorite for your 9th-level wild talent slot.


Hurricane Queen: Haha, this wild talent is so freaking metal, and I love it. Twelve consecutive DC 40 Fly checks to fly around a hurricane that’s persisted for 100 years and was caused by the death of a god? Yeah, I’ll take two. I’m not smart enough to calculate what kind of bonus you would need to ensure success on this task, but if you manage it, it’s well worth your time: an extra 25% on your Enveloping Winds talent is pretty good, but being able to completely ignore wind and weather effects is even better for someone who casters will always try to blow out of the sky.




Kinetic Invocations


1st Level


Body Capacitance: It’s a bad spell, plain and simple. It relies on electricity damage being dealt to you often, it deals weak damage and relies on a touch attack, and it’s discharged after one hit. Blech.


2nd Level


Air Step: Kineticists will almost certainly have the ranks in Fly to make decent use of air step, but the problem is that you’re going to have Wings of Air as your 3rd-level utility talent, and that blows air step out of the sky with both hands tied behind its back. You really don’t need this as a kinetic invocation.


3rd Level


Cloak of Winds: Ah, now we’re getting somewhere. Cloak of winds is a pretty good spell. I wish the duration were longer, but a -4 penalty against all ranged attacks—rays and massive attacks included, since it just says “ranged attack rolls” with no caveats—is huge for an element that will spend basically the entire game at range, between Air’s Reach, Extended Range, Extreme Range, and Wings of Air. Being able to fly safely through windstorms is also a huge deal when squaring off against casters, and the swarm protection (though situational, since most swarms are earth-bound) is nice to have.


Gaseous Form: I’ve never been a big fan of gaseous form. I don’t think most people are. With such a slow movement speed, the only real use is scouting, infiltration, etc., but even those are dubious. And besides, you have Body of Air available at no burn cost! Even if you wanted gaseous form, that wild talent is superior to this kinetic invocation.


4th Level


Cloud Shape: Just like gaseous form, cloud shape gets dinged because it’s competing against Wings of Air, which is available to everyone at this level unless you selected air as your 7th-level expanded element. You get a great disguise out of the bargain, but with a burn cost and slower movement speed, is that worth it to you?


6th Level


Wind Walk: Although it’s hard to imagine that no one in your party will have teleport by this point, wind walk is still a great traveling spell that can eat up miles in a hurry (maximum speed is about 70 mph). You could do worse.
🪨 Earth
Earth


        For my money, earth is the strongest first-party element available to kineticists. Its blasts, especially Metal Blast, are hugely powerful and capable of dealing a wide variety of damage types, its infusions are consistently rated either blue or purple, its utility talents are quite strong, its defensive talent is the strongest in the game, and its kinetic invocations are rivaled only by void’s. Geokineticists are beefy, hard-hitting tanks that will have very little trouble in combat, although their utility might leave players used to aether or air scratching their heads. The usual combat flow for a geokineticist will involve using Earth Glide for positioning, then firing off blasts with the Impale, Deadly Earth, or Fragmentation form infusions and the Entangling or Magnetic substance infusions, with Rare-Metal as a backup in case you’re facing DR. This routine provides a nice mix of single-target and AoE damage, buffing and debuffing, and will never fall out of style. Defense will be handled primarily by the incomparable Flesh of Stone, and as you level up you can use Shift Earth, Stone Sculptor, Greater Shift Earth, Greater Magnetism, Seismic Master, and rampart or slowing mud from Kinetic Invocation to exert considerable influence over the battlefield itself and individual enemies. Of note, earth really benefits from having access to Metal Blast, the composite blast you’ll get if you select earth twice; like fire, earth does best when it’s your main focus.


        Third-party wild talents really solidify earth as the queen of battlefield control, giving you fun toys like Pitfall, Sinkhole, Acid Pit, Lava Pool, Improved Kinetic Cover, and Greater Kinetic Cover to play with. If you’ve ever wanted to absolutely fill a room with lethal traps, impassable corridors, and deadly dead-ends, then earth is the element you want to be playing. Third-party content also really dials earth’s durability and melee power up to 11. Magnetic Pull and Improved Magnetic Pull are some of the only abilities I know of in Pathfinder that can allow you to truly tank in the classic MMORPG sense, and earth even picks up some natural attacks that you can use to eke out a bit more melee damage when you’re full-attacking. Finally, earth gets a limited form of flight (Earth Rider + Greater Earth Rider) in third-party content, which puts its mobility on par with—or even ahead of—elements like aether or fire. The combination of a climb speed, earth glide, and flight is not to be trifled with.


First-Party: Melee > Control > Debuffing, Ranged > Anticaster, Buffing, Recon, Skills, Summoning
Third-Party: Control, Melee > Debuffing, Ranged, Recon > Anticaster, Buffing, Skills, Summoning

	Mean Rating
