	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Class Skills


        Earth is one of the few elements that doesn’t gain access to some kind of flight, so Climb isn’t totally wasted, but you’ve got Earth Climb to give you a climb speed if you want, and expanded element to get you access to flight if you’re really hurting. Knowledge (Dungeoneering), while still one of the six Knowledge skills that will let you identify enemies, is not one that gets used often—it’s only for aberrations and oozes, and they comprise a fairly small portion of the bestiaries. Overall, skills are not earth’s forte.




Defensive Talent


Flesh of Stone: Ha! DR/adamantine of any magnitude is excellent, and this DR scales so hard that you might as well be an invulnerable rager barbarian. Coupled with your massive Constitution score and statue from Kinetic Invocation, and you should be darn near impossible to take down, at least through physical attacks—Will saves will still be the stuff of nightmares. Flesh of Stone easily earns its place as the supreme ruler of kineticist defensive talents, and makes Expanded Defense all but mandatory if you select earth as your expanded element.




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Bloody Infusion: Several classes of enemy are outright immune to bleed damage, and it can be healed almost by accident if you’re fighting creatures with fast healing, regeneration, or divine magic. Still, Bloody Infusion is cheap, scales nicely with composite blasts, and deals free damage over time.


Persistent Infusion: Probably the coolest part about Persistent Infusion is that it leaves parts of your element lying around that can then be used for utility talents, etc. Kind of like giving yourself an alley-oop in conditions where there’s not a lot of your element present naturally. The caltrop part is nice, but caltrops have an attack bonus of +0, which will fail to hit many 1st-level enemies, let alone higher-level enemies. Probably jumps up to at least a green if you have cheap form infusions like Kinetic Bomb or Improved Kinetic Bomb.


Pushing Infusion: CMD scores get incredibly high in later levels, and as a ¾-BAB class with no real reason to invest in feats like Improved Bull Rush, Pushing Infusion is just going to be wasted burn about 90% of the time.


2nd Level


Entangling Infusion: Entangling Infusion is reasonably cheap, has a nice long duration with a great set of debuffs, and targets the weakest save (on average) by CR. For my money, this is one of the best infusions a kineticist has, and a real feather in the cap of the elements that get access to it.


Bowling Infusion: If anything, CMD scores against trip maneuvers are even higher than CMD scores against bull rush maneuvers...and Bowling Infusion is more expensive than Pushing Infusion. No.


3rd Level


Magnetic Infusion: Not only can Magnetic Infusion buff martial allies attacking your target, it can also buff you! A +4 is no joke, especially with an untyped bonus, and the penalty to Reflex saves will make many of your form infusions that much easier to slap on foes. One of earth’s best infusions.


Rare-Metal Infusion: Another Metal Blast exclusive, and perhaps one of the primary reasons you’d consider going with an all-earth build. Unlike with manufactured weapons, which can be enchanted or forged out of different materials to pierce DR, kineticists have to make do with the precious few infusions or utility talents that allow them to do so. Rare-Metal is one such infusion. If you’re playing in a campaign that features a lot of enemies with DR/adamantine (constructs and robots; potentially Iron Gods), DR/cold iron (fey and various evil outsiders; definitely Wrath of the Righteous or Hell’s Rebels, and potentially other campaigns), or DR/alchemical silver (various daemons, kytons, devils, and lycanthropes; no campaigns that I know of) then you definitely want Rare-Metal Infusion. Think of it like a +5 to +20 damage bonus.


Adamantine Infusion: Adamantine Infusion is practically begging to be used against constructs, who often have DR/adamantine as well as hardness. Unless you’re playing in an Iron Gods campaign, though, you’re unlikely to come up against enough constructs to make this infusion worth your time—Rare-Metal is more versatile, so you should generally opt for that.


5th Level


Improved Bloody Infusion: Given the drastically increased cost, I still don’t think Improved Bloody Infusion is much better than its younger cousin. For blood kineticists, though, it probably comes out to at least a green.
Untwisting Iron Infusion: Again, possibly for that monk/kineticist gestalt?


8th Level


Imprisoning Infusion: Ice Tomb is a fantastic witch hex, and it does just as well for a kineticist as it does for a witch. The Fortitude save to negate is a bit unfortunate, as is the high cost, but save-or-suck effects tend to be like this.




Infusions: Form


3rd Level


Impale: In my mind, Impale is superior to Torrent, and doubly so if you attach it to a Metal Blast. The fact that you can use Impale to freaking burst through walls and stab enemies is just so cool. With no save, a good area of effect, and a reasonable cost, I can’t see any reason to ding Impale.


5th Level


Tremor: How often do you need to be able to attack burrowing creatures and incorporeals hiding in walls? Is it often to spend 3 burn on? Didn’t think so.


6th Level


Deadly Earth: Yeah, I like it. Both Cloud and Deadly Earth have very similar feelings: same damage, same radius, same battlefield control tacked on as a bonus. Good stuff, although who knows whether you’ll be fighting ground-based enemies at this level.


Gorgon Gaze: There are a number of tricks enemies can use to avoid your gaze attack, and a Will save to negate what was half damage to begin with ain’t great. Moreover, you can’t do anything but use utility talents while Gorgon Gaze is active unless you want the effect cancelled. I remain (mostly) unimpressed.


7th Level


Fragmentation: Fragmentation is quite expensive, but dang is it good for clearing tightly clustered groups of enemies. Once you have a few instances of infusion specialization, you should use Fragmentation regularly in conjunction with metal blast to just blow the crap out of mobs. Deadly Earth is going to be better at dealing sustained damage if you can contain enemies in one spot for a few rounds; Fragmentation excels when you only have one shot and need to make it count.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Stone Vandal: If you’re going to do any kind of stone and earth manipulation as a geokineticist (Shift Earth, Pitfall, Distorted Area, etc.) then you need to have Stone Vandal. Personally, this seems like a talent tax that I’d give to players for free, but your mileage may vary.


Basic Geokinesis: Given enough time, a 1st-level geokineticist could create a very passable wall, moat, trench, etc. Think of it like a slow expeditious excavation. A...non-expeditious excavation.
Fist of Granite: Absolutely necessary for natural attack builds, and good for threatening in melee range for everyone else.


Earth Walk: The CMD and Acrobatics bonuses are so niche that they may as well not exist, but ignoring difficult terrain caused rocks, earth, mud, etc. actually lets you ignore a fair number of common battlefield control spells cast by druids, wizards, hunters, and more. You could certainly do worse as a passive ability, although as usual it’s completely outmoded if you have access to flight (Wings of Air, Flame Jet, Gravity Control, Self Telekinesis, etc.).
Kinetic Cover: Flexible access to total cover is always to be applauded, but the mini-wall has laughably few HP and no hardness—a stiff breeze will destroy this thing, much less an enemy’s concentrated effort. Still, powerful enough to merit a yellow. There’s a particularly fun episode of Side Quest Side Sesh on the Glass Cannon Network in which this wild talent saves a bunch of PCs’ lives.


Detect Radioactivity: Not necessary unless you’re playing in some post-apocalyptic Fallout-style game.
Elemental Limb: Elemental Limb is a bad utility talent (+2 to Strength checks? Really?) but it does open the way for Improved and Greater Elemental Limb, both of which are pretty much required for natural attack builds.
Joint Lock: Armor check penalties are ultimately of negligible use against enemies. If an enemy were to use this against a PC trying to swim, climb, or fly in heavy armor, though…
Radioactive Adaptation: As with Detect Radioactivity, saves against radiation are vanishingly rare. Probably not worth a wild talent slot.
Spined Armor: Why would you do this? Kineticists are far more strapped for feats and utility talents than they are for money; just buy some armor spikes if you really want this perk.


Glass Cannon: Some third-party content has a nasty habit of introducing utility talents that can be selected in place of your basic kinesis utility talents; in some cases, these are harmless, but in others, they grant bonuses to attack and damage that are simply too mechanically optimal for players to consider leaving their basic kinesis utility talent alone. 
Suggested Fix: Eliminate the attack and damage bonuses. If players want to control glass instead of dirt, that’s their prerogative.


2nd Level


Distorted Area: It’s unclear to me whether the area of difficult terrain moves with you, or stays in the place you create it. I’m inclined to think the former, and if that’s the case, Distorted Area is quite good, preventing enemies from charging you, five-foot stepping into or out of squares adjacent to you, etc. Good area denial talent.
Magnetic Pull: The rare ability that actually allows you to tank, and tank a bunch of creatures, no less! Geokineticists are certainly up to the task with their boatload of HP and DR/adamantine; aerokineticists, probably less so, since they’re flimsier and operate at range more often. Do note that it affects only metal weapons, so this is best taken in games that pit you against mostly humanoid or native outsider enemies.


Armor of Nature: I’m a bit conflicted about Armor of Nature. On the one hand, it grants you the equivalent of a chain shirt in terms of AC, but you can’t make it masterwork or mithral to improve its max Dexterity bonus, and you (theoretically) can’t give it enhancement bonuses or special armor enchantments like you could normal armor. On the other hand, it has no armor check penalty, a scaling armor bonus, and no penalty to your speed. If your GM rules that it’s a valid target for spells like magic vestment, I might consider rating it blue.
Crystal Skin: Crystal Skin is a good talent for light/earth builds, and for the price of 1 burn will nearly entirely negate the need for a ring of protection. Not that kineticists need more loose change, but…
Improved Elemental Limb: Improved Elemental Limb is actually a great talent, although it takes some thinking to get there. It’s obviously required for natural attack builds, but other kineticists can benefit from it, too. First of all, it lets you threaten in melee range without using Kinetic Blade, but takes up no hands to interfere with gathering power (you can shape it at will with no listed action cost). Second, the elemental limb accepts feats applied to your kinetic blasts, so Spell Penetration, Weapon Focus, Weapon Finesse, etc. should all still be fair game. Third, you get to decide what type of damage your natural attack deals as you manifest it, which can be useful in some instances for punching through DR/bludgeoning, slashing, piercing, etc. Pretty good, on the whole.
Sharding Cover: Sharding Cover is weird in that it’s a gamble that your kinetic cover will be broken at some point, preferably sooner rather than later. This puts you into an awkward position: do you take just Kinetic Cover and Sharding Cover, knowing that your wall is going to be about as flimsy as tissue paper but trusting in the damage and difficult terrain to deal with enemies? Or do you take Improved and Greater Kinetic Cover in order to create truly impassable walls, knowing that they’re unlikely to be damaged enough to create shards? You can do both, of course, but that’s four utility talents spent. Lucky battlefield control is generally so strong.


Earth Climb: If you’re not planning to eventually transition into flight, Earth Climb is pretty good. If you’re about to grab air as an expanded element (or are coming to earth as your expanded element from an element with flight) then you’ll definitely skip Earth Climb.
Fist of Nature: Fist of Nature is a fantastic utility talent, but man, are the prereqs steep. You need earth or wood as your primary element, then expanded element to get the other, then Expanded Defense to get the other defense talent, then Fist of Granite and Fist of Oak (which, by the way, are totally redundant), then Fist of Nature. Woof. Natural attack builds will still want it, since it builds on Elemental Limb, but man, talk about a drag.
Metal Blades: Metal Blades is excellent for natural attack builds, as it lets you bypass a bunch of DR types and gives you bonuses to attack and damage, to boot. Not worthwhile for anyone else.
Metal Scent: There’s such a lot of metal everywhere that having a good nose for it is likely to prove more frustrating and confusing than enlightening. Metal Scent is the prerequisite for the amazing Metalsense, though, so you’d better take this wild talent.


Improved Spined Armor: Spined Armor is weak, and the Improved version change what needs changing. Blech.


3rd Level


Acid Pit: By pairing acid pit and hungry pit, you create an extremely deadly trap for enemies with poor Reflex saves and climb abilities. Highly recommended if you’re going earth + poison.
Clockwork Heart: Um, yeah, Metal Blast! Way to be! Winding yourself up is easy as can be, and in return you get Lightning Reflexes and Improved Initiative, both of which are awesome feats. You’re only going to gain access to this wild talent if you’re investing heavily in earth, but it’s a heck of a payoff.
Construct Crafter: Crafting constructs is certainly a worthwhile application of your resources, and it’s great that you don’t have to deal with any of the prerequisites.
Improved Kinetic Cover: Improved Kinetic Cover fixes all the problems of Kinetic Cover and then some, primarily by giving it a lot more HP and some hardness. Now enemies will have to actually work to punch through, rather than blowing it over with a light breeze.
Tremorsense: Tremorsense can’t detect flying enemies, but otherwise functions exactly like blindsight; it’s therefore a pretty hard counter to blur, displacement, obscuring mist, blindness/deafness, and a host of other conditions or effects that could prevent you from hitting with your blasts. Not sure why they included the bit about concealment and total concealment—if you can detect an enemy with tremorsense, they wouldn’t get a miss chance anyway. At any rate, highly recommended for any kineticist.


Earth Rider: Earth Rider and its Greater counterpart are functionally identical to Self Telekinesis, Flame Jet, Gravity Control, and the other utility talents that allow limited flight. I’m giving these the exact same rating.
Improved Magnetic Pull: The increased radius is the big-ticket item here—you can probably forget the clause about redirecting electric spells.


Automatic Reconstruction: Fast healing this slow is unfortunately not going to cut it in combat, so there’s no point spending burn on Kinetic Cover, Improved Kinetic Cover, etc. This might come into play later on for “healing” structures created with Aether Architect, Kinetic Cityscape, etc., but I’d say those are fairly niche, endgame uses.
Earth Child: Wow, that is wacky. I don’t know of any other effect in Pathfinder that lets you become a hybrid of two races on a permanent basis. This talent will give you a 20-foot movement speed, acid resistance 5, +2 AC vs. rays, darkvision 60 ft., and immunity to all the usual humanoid-only spells and effects—both helpful and harmful. Whether that’s worth it to you is up to you, but I’m inclined to think that’s not a game-breakingly powerful suite of abilities.
Improved Joint Lock: When you’re not proficient with armor, your armor check penalty applies to attack rolls in addition to any skill check that involves movement. If you’re a PC, that’ s a relatively minor deal, as not every enemy will wear armor, and not every enemy that wears armor will wear heavy enough armor to be hamstrung by those penalties. For GMs targeting a weak Reflex save, however, you can absolutely cripple a character.
Magnetism: The limitation to only bull rush and drag maneuvers greatly limits Magnetism in comparison with, say, Telekinetic Maneuvers from aether. And only metal objects or creatures? That’ll be functional some of the time, but certainly not all.
Seismic Stomp: The action economy here is very good, giving you potentially multiple trips for a move action—and you get your Constitution modifier to CMB, to boot! Unfortunately, combat maneuvers just don’t scale well into the endgame, so this tactic will only get less effective as time goes by.


Desolate: So...let me get this straight: you’re going to spend 1 point of burn to make it harder to grow crops in a 10 ft. x 10 ft. square? Yeah, no.
Greater Detect Radioactivity: Still as unnecessary as Detect Radioactivity.
Jagged Flesh: Paltry damage that will be easily negated by even the smallest amounts of DR. If it scaled, it might be worth it...but it doesn’t scale.
Pillar: What can Pillar do that climbing or flight couldn’t? It’s certainly got no combat applications. So why?


Pitfall: Pitfall is an excellent utility talent, but is strange: it never costs more than 0 burn, but its save DCs also don’t scale, so you’re likely to waste some actions while you try to get enemies to fall into a hungry pit.
Suggested Fix: Simple: scale the burn cost and save DC depending on which version of the pit you create. Want only a simple create pit at 6th level with a DC 13 + Constitution modifier save? Fine, that’s 0 burn. But if you want spiked pit at 10th level and DC 15 + Constitution modifier, that’s 1 burn; hungry pit at 14th level and DC 17 + Constitution modifier is 2 burn.


4th Level


Enduring Earth: Enduring Earth won’t apply to every (or even most) effects in earth, but when it does, it’s just...mwah. Spice meatball. And especially as a passive ability that costs no burn? Yes, you’ll take Enduring Earth.
Shift Earth: A fitting upgrade to Basic Geokinesis that will leave you feeling like a true geokineticist, sculpting the landscape to meet your needs. If you have the opportunity to shape a battlefield beforehand, do it—creating walls, chokepoints, high ground, etc. all have huge tactical ramifications.
Sinkhole: More great battlefield control for the geokineticist. Pairing it with Pitfall is almost a must, although the higher burn cost is worth considering.


Elemental Hand: I’ve cooled on the hand suite of spells over the years, mostly because the CMB upgrades that grapple, bull rush, etc. can’t be counted on to work reliably against creatures of the same CR. It still works well to keep enemies away from you, slowing them as they try to approach and granting you cover bonuses if they try to attack from range. Might even soak some damage!
Kinetic Shelter: A fun little bungalow for you and your team, although other tactics like rope trick are probably a little less conspicuous.


Earthmeld: Forget intrigue and spying—I’d use Earthmeld for hiding from enemy patrols. Scout up ahead, and if you hear anyone coming, just slip into the wall until the danger is past. Still, pretty niche, especially since earth is not the element you’d think of for scouting and infiltration.


Remove Radioactivity: There’s only a minuscule chance that you’ll encounter radiation once in your campaign, much less enough times that you need a wild talent for it.
Twisted Metal: Unfortunately, Twisted Metal deals damage to the armor, weapon, etc. you’re targeting, which will potentially destroy the very equipment you’re hoping to loot. And what’s the benefit, even? That you get to originate a blast elsewhere? No. Maybe if you’re in an Iron Gods campaign and are fighting constructs 24/7, but not for anyone else.


Metalsense: Good gracious, that’s powerful. So now you can perfectly detect and pinpoint metallic enemies, creatures with metallic blood (that’s most living things, by the way), and metallic objects with no penalty at a range of up to 100 feet, even passing through doors, ceilings, etc. Crazy.
Suggested Fix: Strike the clause allowing this utility talent to detect creatures with metallic blood. It functions as blindsight for metallic creatures and objects, and is blocked by barriers normally.


5th Level


Metal Manipulator: Metal Manipulator radically redefines what the geokineticist is capable of. You can sculpt metal with Stone Sculptor, burrow through metal with Earth Glide, create sinkholes and pitfalls in metal with their respective talents, speak with metal objects with Earth Tongue, and more. It’s an element-defining talent.


Earth Glide: Hey, I’ll take it! Earth Glide is maybe a little less spectacular than Wings of Air, but no less useful: when you can earth glide, you can zoom into the ground, burrow away, pop up in a different location, and get right back to blasting. Just watch out for arcane casters who might be readying a transmute mud to rock. That’s instant death.
Stone Sculptor: The economy of kineticist wild talents sometimes forces radical reinterpretations of the usefulness of the spells they imitate. As a 3rd- or 4th-level spell that competes with spells like haste, fly, magic circle against evil, etc., who in their right mind would say that stone shape is powerful? But what if you got it at will, as is the case here? Given enough time, you can mold tunnels, build walls, houses, or bridges, seal off passages, entomb enemies alive as an interrogation tactic, and do pretty much anything else your twisted munchkin brain can think of. Just...a lot of versatility.


Clockwork Puppet: I gave Aether Puppet a green, so it seems only fitting that Clockwork Puppet gets the same rating. These constructs aren’t the sturdiest things in the world, but as essentially infinite summoned meat shields, they can be treated as disposable automata: run them into traps, push them into rooms to soak surprise round alpha strikes, give soft cover or flanking to allies, etc. It’s all fine. They’re robots. They can’t feel it.
Elemental Transmission: It’s kinda like sending, but with a longer duration and the caveat that you can’t send messages across planes. I like it! Every team needs a means of communicating across long distances, and this fits the bill.
Spark of Innovation: Spark of Innovation is a nice upgrade over Spark of Life. Earth elementals don’t have any supernatural or spell-like abilities, so losing them isn’t a blow, and the upgrades you get with construct traits and the clockwork subtype are generally well worth your trouble, granting greatly improved defenses. Pretty good!


Greater Tremorsense: Commune with nature already isn’t my favorite spell—it’s a little non-specific, and depends on GM fiat to provide useful information—and limiting it to natural underground settings (caverns, basically) makes it even more niche. It’s not a trap, per se, since you get it for no burn cost, but it also wouldn’t be anywhere close to my first pick.


Frightful Statue: All this for a couple of lousy Intimidate checks? No, no, no.


6th Level


Endlessly Enduring Earth: Even more duration on your infusions. This will save you a lot of burn, because you can tag an enemy with something like Magnetic Infusion, accept 1 point of burn to raise the duration to two rounds, get those two rounds doubled by Enduring Earth, and you’re off to the races with four rounds of Magnetic Infusion!
Greater Earth Rider: Earth Rider and its Greater counterpart are functionally identical to Self Telekinesis, Flame Jet, Gravity Control, and the other utility talents that allow limited flight. I’m giving these the exact same rating.
Greater Kinetic Cover: Battlefield control is one of the most helpful things any PC can offer to a party, and there are few battlefield control tools more effective than a wall of stone. Loads of HP, big area, more flexible positioning than the spell, and it’s not even instantaneous, so you don’t have to worry about leaving a big old wall lying around to obstruct you.
Lava Pool: Talk about amazing battlefield control. Big area, big damage, and the entangled condition, all for 0 burn.


Greater Elemental Limb: Still as necessary for natural attack builds as ever it was, but now with the ability to manifest extra arms that might help you use magic items, etc.


Improved Elemental Hand: For my money, Improved Elemental Hand isn’t really worth it. Only weak casters would have CMD scores low enough to get grappled at this level, and they’ll have freedom of movement running basically permanently, ensuring that they can’t be grappled at all. Thpbbb.


7th Level


Greater Shift Earth: Sure, why not take a 6th-level spell at will? Similar to Shift Earth and Stone Sculptor, Greater Shift Earth gets better as you get more creative, and favors the player who takes the time to consider how a battlefield might be best prepared to suit their party’s needs.


Steel Rider: I guess the benefit is that you get to take your allies along for the ride now? I dunno why you would really want to attack someone with your Magneto platform, but I guess the option is nice.


Fist of Eternity: Let’s go over all the prerequisites that you need to hit before you qualify to take this talent, shall we? Fist of Eternity specifies Fist of Nature and Hydraulic Fist as its formal prerequisites. Hydraulic Fist requires two utility talents: Subdermal Plating and Hard Knuckle. Fist of Nature also requires two: Fist of Oak and Fist of Granite. Between those two, you’re already looking at six utility talents, most of which are completely redundant. Then you’ve got to get two instances of Expanded Defense—and since you can’t take the utility talents in your third and final expanded element until at least 14th level, you probably won’t qualify to take Fist of Eternity until...16th? 17th level? And that’s when you start to ask: Is eight utility talents really worth what I’m getting here? Sure, two slams at 2d8/x3 are nice, but are they eight utility talents nice? I’ll leave that judgment to you, but my inclination is to respond in the negative.


Elemental Airstrike: It’s a mythic spell. What on earth makes you think players should have this for 1 burn?
Suggested Fix: I dunno. Make it a summon monster clone, but for your element’s elemental type. Don't do elemental bombardment.


8th Level


Earth Tongue: If your GM ever starts to grouse about being forced to play the part of pumice or basalt for the nth time, just remind them that this is what they signed up for! Anyway, Earth Tongue is a fun flavor ability, especially as a constant spell-like ability, but whether you’ll get anything useful from it is highly dependent on GM fiat.


Greater Remove Radioactivity: There’s only a minuscule chance that you’ll encounter radiation once in your campaign, much less enough times that you need a wild talent for it.


9th Level


Greater Magnetism: An improved version of a druid-only 8th-level spell, at will. And no save or spell resistance! If any creature at all uses metal anything, you’ve got ‘em—you can bull rush people 40 ft. per round with Greater Magnetism, and your lack of reliance on a rounds/level duration means that you can scoot people anywhere you want, anytime you want. My favorite for your 9th-level wild talent slot.
Kinetic Cityscape: Unfortunately for aether, Kinetic Cityscape kinda steals the wind out from Aether Architect’s sails; fortunately for everyone else, this is a really cool capstone ability that can help you found a kineticist city once you’re done with your campaign. There aren’t a lot of combat-effective 9th-level utility talents, so why not?
Seismic Master: Once again, an 8th-level spell at will can do a lot of things that you normally wouldn’t do with spell slots. Tearing down buildings, causing cave-ins, and reshaping the terrain for 0 burn is pretty awesome, even if I wouldn’t normally give earthquake a great rating.




Kinetic Invocations


1st Level


Stone Shield: Bonuses to AC and Reflex saves will never be irrelevant, especially when they’re available as immediate actions. It’s debatable whether you’d want to spend burn on this when you have so many other priorities, but it’s at least stronger than expeditious excavation.


Expeditious Excavation: For 0 burn cost, expeditious excavation isn’t a bad purchase at low levels, but its offenses don’t scale at all well into the end game. Perpetually useful on utility, though.


2nd Level


Groundswell: Geokineticists are more likely than other elements to operate in melee range, where groundswell’s hard counter on flanking and ability to grant higher ground bonuses are always well-placed. Pretty decent!


4th Level


Slowing Mud: Slowing mud is a weird spell. If you’re staggered, you can take only a move or standard action each round, but in order to end the spell’s staggered condition, you have to take a full-round action. Huh. At any rate, slowing mud targets a generally stronger save than slow, and the blindness it imparts is easily removed; these factors would make me rate it lower than slow if this were a normal casting class. But it’s not! Everything costs 1 burn, flat. It’s therefore quite good.


7th Level


Statue: Ha! Hahaha. Surely someone at Paizo is punking me. Statue is already one of the strongest defensive spells in the game, essentially giving you DR 8/— against everything for hours at a time. And for 0 burn!? Seriously? Per the universal monster rules, hardness on a creature does not allow it to ignore half the damage from energy or ranged attacks, like an object would, but statue is still an incredible pick-up for tanky geokineticists. How could they have made this free?


Rampart: Rampart is an excellent battlefield control spell that’s cheap at 1 point of burn. Good get.


9th Level


Imprisonment: One of the cool things about burn, mental focus, and other spell-like abilities that occult classes tend to get is that they tend to cost the same no matter what level of resource you’re spending it on. Sure, you could spend 5 burn to cast expeditious excavation 5 times...or you could spend 5 burn to cast imprisonment 5 times. Imprisonment is one of the worst save-or-die effects ever, and it can be yours for just 1 burn. Plus, what else are you going to spend an 18th- or 20th-level utility talent on?
🔥 Fire
Fire


        There’s absolutely nothing subtle about a pyrokineticist’s approach to life: burn, baby, burn. Yes, kineticists who specialize in the element of fire can be expected to blast pretty much exclusively. With the exception of a few gems, their utility talent and Kinetic Invocation lists are very weak, but their blasts and infusions are quite good; for this reason, fire is an excellent choice to add to a more defense- or utility-heavy element (aether, air, earth). Probably the strongest move to make with fire is to double down on it, however: Blue Flame Blast, fire’s single-element composite blast, is the only first-party composite energy blast in the game, and between Searing Flame, Burning Infusion, Firesight, Unraveling Infusion, and Fire’s Fury, you can broil just about anything that doesn’t have outright fire immunity. Full pyrokineticists have to be prepared, however, for some fights simply not to break their way: even though Searing Flame, Burning Infusion, etc. can help you lower fire resistance substantially, this element can do nothing about fire immunity, which is the single most prevalent energy immunity in the game. Fire tends to be—forgive the pun—very hot and cold, either working like gangbusters against enemies with no fire resistance or immunity or else being completely ineffective in fights against fire-immune enemies. I know that the lure of Blue-Flame Blast is difficult to resist, but I want you always to weigh the consequences of having no alternative damage typing available against the benefits you’re receiving.


First-Party: Ranged > Control, Melee, Recon > Anticaster, Buffing > Debuffing, Skills, Summoning
Third-Party: Ranged > Control, Melee, Recon > Anticaster, Buffing > Debuffing, Skills, Summoning






	Mean Rating 
(Paizo Content)
	Mean Rating 
(3PP Content)
	Mean Rating (Combined)
	Form Infusions
	3.3
	—
	3.3
	Substance Infusions
	2.6
	3.0
	2.8
	All Infusions
	2.9
	3.0
	2.9
	Utility Talents
	2.3
	2.8
	2.5
	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Class Skills


The class skills for fire are pretty decent, actually. Escape Artist is a nice addition for kineticists, who (as ¾-BAB ranged combatants) really don’t want to get grappled. Depending on your burn investment in Searing Flesh and Flame Shield, enemies may have other reasons for not wanting to grapple you, but knowing that you have the option to break through is always good. Knowledge (Nature) is a pretty standard monster identification skill, at least in the early game when you’re fighting lots of animals and fey. I’ll take it!




Defensive Talent


Searing Flesh: I’m really not a fan of Searing Flesh. It’s weak, easily superseded by fire resistance, and provides no actual defense, only retaliatory offense—fitting for fire, but disappointing nevertheless. You could theoretically amp up this effect to deal 40 damage per hit at 20th level, but why would you spend 8 burn on that effect? Heck, it doesn’t even apply to all attacks, just natural attacks and unarmed strikes! No, color me unimpressed. It gets marginally better with Flame Shield, but not by much.




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Burning Infusion: Burning Infusion needs to be every pyrokineticist’s first substance infusion, no questions asked. Not only does it light enemies on fire, reduce further fire resistance (with the Searing Flame utility talent), and allow you to ignore miss chances from concealment or total concealment (with the Firesight utility talent), it also grants you a bunch of bonuses to save DCs, attack rolls, and CL checks to penetrate spell resistance for as long as the targets are on fire. Almost every battle at high levels should begin with Burning Infusion + a relevant form infusion in order to light as many enemies on fire as possible. You need to give yourself time for Searing Flame to work, you want all those bonuses right off the bat, and you want enemies to be thinking about wasting actions trying to put the flames out. Definitely an element-defining ability.


Dazzling Infusion: Wow, what a stinker. Dazzled is easily the least effective status condition in Pathfinder (well, fascinated might give it a run for its money) and with a Will save to negate there’s absolutely no reason you would ever use this when you have Burning Infusion available for the same cost.
Dazzling Infusion: For some reason, third-party content rewrote Dazzling Infusion. It’s still terrible, but this time a Fortitude save negates instead of a Will save.


Painful Infusion: In general, one-burn infusions should not be able to lock enemies out of making any attacks for rounds at a time. I don’t know who thought Painful Infusion was a good idea as written, but they were very, very wrong.
Suggested Fix: A -1 penalty to attack rolls with one type of natural weapon (wing, bite, slam, claw, etc.) per 4 kineticist levels, minimum 1. Increase duration to 1 round + 1 round per 5 kineticist levels if they choose to expend burn. It will still help your allies on defense, but restricting you to one type of natural weapon is reasonable. You shouldn’t be able to hit a dragon with a 1st-level infusion and take away all or even most of its toys.


2nd Level


Improved Burning Infusion: Burning Infusion is the OG five-star infusion for fire, due to its ability to deal more damage, amp up your save DCs, attack rolls, and spell resistance checks, and synergize with Searing Flame and Firesight. Improved Burning Infusion doesn’t add a lot more functionality for its cost, but it does deal more damage that then gets translated via Searing Flame into faster destruction of enemies’ fire resistance. You should absolutely use it against foes with hefty fire resistance, as the extra +1d6 burn damage will greatly improve your damage in subsequent rounds.
Melting Infusion: I would probably frontload the scaling of Melting Infusion a little more if I were rewriting it (maybe 2 + ⅕ kineticist level) but it’s otherwise a very solid infusion for making enemies easier to hit.


Penetrating Infusion: I personally think that Penetrating Infusion should have been a 1-burn infusion, but maybe that’s just me. Cold and fire resistance are much more common than electricity resistance, hence this infusion is correspondingly better for water and fire than it is for air.
Smoke Infusion: Thanks to Firesight, pyrokineticists don’t have a great deal to fear from smoke, and can continue blasting away at enemies with no penalty, even as they try desperately to see through it. The Improved version is even better, but note that the Explosive Flame utility talent probably beats both.


3rd Level


Foxfire Infusion: I gave Foxfire, the utility talent, a yellow rating, but that might honestly be too generous for Foxfire Infusion. For 2 burn with a Will save for partial effect? When enemies can remove the condition as a full-round action and it already doesn’t do too much? Here’s an idea: light enemies on fire if you’re concerned about them going invisible—you’ll be able to hit them just fine with Firesight once they’re lit up.


4th Level


Improved Smoke Infusion: Once you’ve wreathed a target in smoke, they can’t shake it! ISI is a great infusion for solo pyrokineticists against hard targets, but make sure you remember to shell out for a wand of ashen path so that your team can see through your smoke, too. Don’t want to burn any bridges with your teammates. Bdum-tsh.


Flash Infusion: Blinded is a fantastic status condition in Pathfinder, so don’t think that this infusion is getting dinged because of its effect. But with a Will save to negate and a burn cost of 3, it’s a very expensive and chancy maneuver to pull. Probably the best argument for it is against huge, stupid bruisers who you can hit easily with Fire Blast and keep blinded for extended lengths of time. Somewhat niche, though.


5th Level


Unraveling Infusion: Unraveling Infusion is getting to be pretty pricy, but it’s an incredibly important component of a pyrokineticist’s lineup. If you’re fighting an arcane caster who has protection from energy running, it doesn’t matter how good you are at stripping away fire resistance—they’re not taking any damage. If you dispel protection from energy first, though, all bets are off, and your blast might still deal full damage! And Unraveling Infusion isn’t relegated to spells that would protect subjects from fire damage—this baby can dispel anything. Because the infusion specifically designates that it acts as a targeted dispel magic, I would think that you’d be eligible for feats like Dispel Focus which improve your CL check.


Greater Burning Infusion: This infusion is bizarre because it’s a form infusion masquerading as a substance infusion. Why not just use Improved Burning Infusion with Fan of Flames, Eruption, or Explosion? In theory, you could do some interesting things with GBI by pairing it with form infusions. Shoot a Torrent that blows up every enemy in its line, causing a chain of explosions that affect enemies who are slightly off-kilter from the line. Tag an enemy with GBI while using Extreme Range to stay well back from the action. It’s always nice to have the option, of course, I just don’t think it will be worth its increased burn cost most of the time.
Unblinking Flame Infusion: For that monk/kineticist gestalt build you keep looking at? True seeing is quite good, at least.


6th Level


Pyroclastic Infusion: I dunno, man. I’m unclear about how this is even an infusion: do you have to hit an enemy with a blast, like most infusions? Feels like it should be a utility talent. It’s good for blowing up enemies who are spread out over a wide area, but at some point you had to light all those enemies on fire, and they were probably tightly clustered for you to be able to do that. With a high cost and a Reflex save for ¼ damage, I’m inclined to think the hype isn’t entirely earned.


Brilliant Infusion: Wow. Way, way too expensive for a one-round effect. No, no, no. If nobody in your group has the ability to deal with supernatural darkness by 12th level, you’re in trouble anyway. This isn’t the right tool for that job.


7th Level


Overload Infusion: A really interesting take on a damage-over-time ability. The burn cost is obviously high at this level, but you’ll have several tiers of infusion specialization to mitigate that. Even so, it will almost certainly be a substance infusion that you can’t really pair with a form infusion due to the sheer cost of such a proposition. Fun to watch enemies melt themselves, though.
Pure-Flame Infusion: Ridiculously expensive, of course, but you’ll have several levels of infusion specialization by this point. I dunno—every pyrokineticist should be taking Spell Penetration and Greater Spell Penetration as permanent feats, so what you’re really looking at is a trade-off: would you rather take no chances on spell resistance, but get locked out of Burning Infusion and Unraveling Infusion? Would you rather take no chances on spell resistance, but leave less burn available for Eruption or Explosion? Especially with your feat investment, I’d probably leave Pure-Flame Infusion at home most of the time, but your calculus may differ.




Infusions: Form


1st Level


Fan of Flames: I gave Torrent a blue rating, so I think it’s only fair if Fan of Flames gets the same rating, being both cheaper and probably easier to tag multiple enemies with. I actually like that the range on this infusion is rather short—it makes it easier not to hit your allies while still leaving you free to hit your enemies. Since Reflex only negates half the damage, you’ll still deal your full blast damage in aggregate if you can hit two enemies, and possibly quite a bit more. Definitely a good infusion for blowing up swarms, too.


Energize Weapon: This would be pretty good if you were playing a gestalt kineticist with a more mainstream martial class, as it’s essentially free damage on a full-attack action. Not very much damage, though, and since it locks you out of other form infusions (including Kinetic Blade and Kinetic Whip) I’m having a lot of difficulty seeing why a single-class kineticist would choose to use it.


3rd Level


Eruption: A 10-foot radius is surprisingly big, and the fact that you can also blow up flying creatures with Eruption (provided they’re not too high) is only icing on the cake. Eruption compares very favorably with Explosion, a late-game form infusion for fire; in addition to supporting more composite blasts, Eruption costs half the burn that Explosion does, making it an excellent bargain for targeted AoE damage.


Torrent: Sure, why not? Line effects are more niche than cones, but corridors are a thing in Pathfinder, and you’ll often be able to hit one or more enemies in a line. As always, energy blasts (fire and blue flame in particular) come out stronger here than anything else.


4th Level


Detonation: Detonation is starting to get a little too expensive for what it does in my book, but it’s got a nice big radius that will likely tag as many enemies as you have on the field. It applies to very few composite blasts, though, and can destroy allies or allies’ equipment if you’re not careful. The inability to aim or position the blast (unlike Torrent, Eruption, Fan of Flames, or Explosion) is its greatest weakness, and one that you’ll have to manage very carefully. If you find yourself fighting enemies primarily in cramped corridors, hallways, caverns, dungeons, etc., you might just want to forget it.


7th Level


Explosion: Explosion is actually not that great! On the one hand, sure, it’s fantastic to be able to shoot off a variably sized AoE Blue Flame Blast that deals full damage to anything within 120 feet, but let’s take a look at the negatives, too: Explosion is monstrously expensive at 4 burn, only works on one composite blast, can’t hit fliers and earth-bound enemies at the same time, and doesn’t deal any damage above and beyond what Eruption would have given you at half the cost. Is the ability to shape the blast radius to 5, 15, or 20 feet really worth 2 extra points of burn? To be clear, I think that any dedicated fire build should still get this infusion, as there may be times when you truly want a 20-foot radius blast—but the vast majority of the time, Eruption makes more sense from a cost-benefit standpoint.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Elemental Acceleration: This is basically the monk’s fast movement class feature, for the cost of a utility talent. Movement speed rarely breaks the power curve, so I don’t feel the need to veto this one, but it is very good. Aerokineticists in particular will appreciate the bonuses to their flight speed from Wings of Air.
Fire’s Fury: A passive damage bonus that scales with your ability to take on more burn. I can get behind that, especially since fire’s method for dealing with fire resistance is, “Maybe...we should deal more fire damage to it?”


Flame Conduit: A very interesting and creative utility talent that primarily helps you deal with cover or position your blasts better for form infusions. If you’ve got a crowd of enemies across the map and manage to light one on fire with Burning Infusion, it doesn’t matter that you’re too far away to use Fan of Flames on that crowd yourself—you can use your burning enemy as the source of the blast. Definitely rewards tactical and inventive play. The extra damage and penalty to saves are mostly window-dressing.


Basic Pyrokinesis: Fairly uninspiring cantrips that every adventuring party will already have. Meh.
Imbue Kinesis: Good only at low levels when you can’t effectively blast things and might instead need to help out an ally. It certainly doesn’t scale well at all, so I would let it go in one of your wild talent upgrades.


Cold Adaptation: Not unless you’re in a Reign of Winter campaign, no.
Fire Sculptor: When are you ever facing ongoing fire effects?
Heat Adaptation: As Cold Adaptation, although the slight polish on this turd is that fire damage is considerably more common than cold damage.
Sealing Wound: Bleed effects are vanishingly rare. Nah.


2nd Level


Searing Flame: Searing Flame is an element-defining utility talent because it lets you scorch away fire resistance from enemies over time. Every pyrokineticist will take Burning Infusion anyway, and you positively must have a means of dealing with fire resistance given how common it is, so why not pick this method?


Explosive Flame: Pyrotechnics is a great battlefield control spell (particularly with a wand of ashen path) but is always just a little bit hindered by whether you have a source of fire available or not. With Burning Infusion, no pyrokineticist is ever going to lack for open flame! I’d choose the smoke option in nearly every instance—the fireworks are great, but they affect allies as well as enemies, and the radius is way too big for anything but an alpha strike. If you can use it as a flashbang grenade to start a surprise round, that’s much better.


Foxfire: I like faerie fire as much as the next Pathfinder guide writer, but because this utility talent affects only a single target and can be removed with a full-round action, the rating is getting knocked down pretty dramatically. Wouldn’t be my first pick.


Fire Steed: Kineticists don’t get pets, so the utility here would depend entirely on whether you have a party member who has an animal companion that fits this bill. Even then, it’s likely not worth a slot.
Flame Trap: This is something that an NPC kineticist would use to make PCs wary about opening chests. I doubt it’s seriously intended for PC use.


3rd Level


Firesight: Firesight is great for two reasons: first, it lets you see through smoke and fire, which are common ways for both enemy casters and allies with smokesticks to create some cover. Seeing through it gives you a decisive tactical advantage. Second, creatures that you can light on fire never gain concealment or total concealment from you through effects like blur, displacement, or invisibility. Guess what makes people immolate? Burning Infusion, the infusion you’re already using like Nutella—that is, on everything. Being able to light up casters, destroy their fire resistance, and pierce right through their magical defenses is a real power-flex that won’t ever get old.
Fire’s Explosive Fury: This upgrade to Fire’s Fury won’t work unless you actively take burn on your blast, so it’s best for really big AoE explosions where you’re going to apply the damage bonus to a bunch of enemies. More damage is more damage, ya hear?
Smoke Storm: “A source of open flame” seems to me to imply any source of open flame: candle, torch, fireplace, forge—anything. The debuff and battlefield control effects of Smoke Storm are good enough on their own, but paired with Firesight, they’re even better: you can see through your own smoke!


Flame Jet: Flame Jet, Self Telekinesis, and Gravity Control all work the same way, as do their older cousins. These abilities won’t be quite combat-ready at the time you get them, since you need to keep investing your standard action in flight, but for out-of-combat purposes, they’re aces. Besides, you need these to progress to the Greater versions of all these talents, and you definitely want to do that.
Rapid Burn: Rapid Burn is quite good for fire/time kineticists, who want to be able to proc Searing Flame as quickly as possible in order to eliminate any fire resistance an enemy might have.
Ring of Fire: It’s a freaking agni kai! I’m assuming the ring of fire isn’t solid—that it is, in fact, a ring and not just a circle—which makes it a problem only for enemies entering or exiting the thunderdome. Still, the damage is surprisingly good, and being able to add on Burning Infusion is a nice touch.


Flame Step: I’m not really sure how Flame Step works. Can you teleport between enemies you’ve lit on fire with Burning Infusion? Can you teleport around your own Ring of Fire? If yes to both, Flame Step is easily blue, but I can’t rate it super well without a better idea of how it functions.


Heat Wave: On its own, Heat Wave isn’t bad, essentially giving you a 20% miss chance against melee attackers that don’t have reach. But Heat Wave costs burn, can accidentally affect allies in tight quarters, doesn’t work against creatures with 10-foot reach or more, depends on the ambient environmental temperature to work at all, and requires the generally poor Heat Adaptation wild talent. It’s okay in very specific circumstances, but those circumstances are rare.


4th Level


Photosynthesis: Although I’d argue with the wisdom of ever taking Heat Adaptation or Roots, there’s no denying that fast healing is potent. If you’re adventuring regularly in bright light, this utility talent will save you a bunch of money on healing spells.


Purifying Flames: I’m of the opinion that kineticists shouldn’t be the ones handling heavy-duty magical healing or restoration effects like neutralize poison or remove curse, but I suppose the option is nice enough to have that I’ll rate it yellow.


Autocombustion: The fire creature template doesn’t exist in core Pathfinder; this must be some third-party template that I’m not familiar with. Applying templates to PCs is very strong in general (see Zura and Yhidothrus’ fiendish obedience boons, if you don’t believe me) so I’d be hesitant to allow this at my table, but I can’t offer a ruling unless someone knows of a link to this template.


5th Level


Flame Jet, Greater: With your standard action no longer gobbled up, you can fly in combat with no trouble, including hover checks. You won’t be able to gather power unless you’re hovering, stationary, however, so keep that in mind.


Elemental Transmission: It’s kinda like sending, but with a longer duration and the caveat that you can’t send messages across planes. I like it! Every team needs a means of communicating across long distances, and this fits the bill.
Flame Shield: This is the power that Cold Adaptation wanted to be. You halve all cold damage, deal some fairly nice damage to melee attackers, and generally make yourself an unappealing target to anything looking to smush you like the firefly you are.


Trail of Flames: Ha! That’s a lot of fun. I wish you could apply burn to get the effect to last longer, or apply the effect to more types of movement; without those options, I can’t give Trail of Flames the full-throated recommendation I’d like to. Like I said, though, it’s heckin’ fun.


Fire Corridor: When would you…? Why would you…?
Improved Fire Steed: Still no on the fire steed. It’s just not a good use of your utility talents or actions.
Lingering Energy: I’m not a fan. It costs 1 burn and eats your immediate action, and even if an enemy does proc the blast, they’re likely to avoid all the damage. Nah.


6th Level


Lava Pool: Talk about amazing battlefield control. Big area, big damage, and the entangled condition, all for 0 burn.


Purging Flame: I haven’t softened my stance on kineticists not being primary healers, but I like that Purging Flame can be activated even when you would normally be prevented from doing so, like if you get petrified or hit with a slumber hex. I have no idea where Mount Kumijinja is, though, so you might want to do your research before trying to purchase this wild talent.


7th Level


Elemental Eater: How useful Elemental Eater is will depend almost entirely on on what element you’re using it with, and how prevalent that element is in your campaign. For common elements like fire, this ability could rise to a blue; for rare elements like force (aether), negative energy (void), or sonic (sound), it’ll likely be red.


Elemental Airstrike: It’s a mythic spell. What on earth makes you think players should have this for 1 burn?
Suggested Fix: I dunno. Make it a summon monster clone, but for your element’s elemental type. Don't do elemental bombardment.


8th Level


Greater Fire Steed: It’s still a hard no.


9th Level


From the Ashes: A ridiculously fun and flavorful ability that lets you rise like the phoenix whenever you’re rendered unconscious or killed. Remember: without ash to rise from, a phoenix is just a bird getting up. Most enemies won’t think to destroy ashes, leaving you free to resurrect at your leisure. Watch out for the cost of 2 burn, though: between taking the full damage of the attack that killed you and 36-40 points of nonlethal damage, From the Ashes might very well put you unconscious again as soon as you use it.




Kinetic Invocations


1st Level


Invigorate: Super niche, and definitely not worth spending burn on.


2nd Level


Boiling Blood: What a terrible spell. Weak damage, and the only buff it gives is for full orcs? No. No, no, no.
Death Candle: A small fire elemental will get curb-stomped by just about every enemy at the time you get this power, to say nothing of the higher-level enemies who will slaughter it in a single hit. How about no?
Fury of the Sun: Even at the cost of 0 burn, fury of the sun is still a terrible spell. It’s bad enough that I’m not even willing to spend the action cost on it, much less a real resource. Fortitude negates, spell resistance, one target only, all for a measly 1d4 points of nonlethal damage? How did anyone think this was a good idea?
Hypnotic Pattern: HD caps this low are never a good thing, and even if you manage to affect some creatures, fascinated is one of the weakest debuff conditions in the game that’s automatically broken by any hint of a combat threat. No
Hypnotism: Even worse than hypnotic pattern, if that’s possible.


5th Level


Blessing of the Salamander: The CMD bonuses in blessing of the salamander can go pound sand. Fire resistance is better (and certainly better than Heat Adaptation) but what we’re really here for is that sweet, sweet fast healing 5. You’ll want to use non-burn resources to do most of your healing out of combat, but this spell is great for soaking stray hits in a tough fight.
🌑 Void
Void


        In its natural Paizo-official state, the void element does about one thing exceptionally well: summon undead minions with Kinetic Invocation + animate dead and command undead. Sure, other classes (necroccultist occultists, bones or juju mystery oracles, clerics) can raise the dead, too, but the advantage that void kineticists have is that they can spend burn on undead minions, rather than valuable spell slots. For the most part, though, the void element never got a lot of support from Paizo because void and wood were released in an even later book than Occult Adventures, which was already a pretty late addition to Pathfinder. Third-party content changes all that, and makes void one of the stronger elements in the game. Summoning is still a strength, with more infusions added (Reanimating Infusion, Reconstruction Infusion, Aura of Ash) to keep your undead minions hale and hearty, but you also get some really cool control abilities (Grasp of the Void, Gravity Sculptor, High Gravity Infusion, Low Gravity Infusion), anticaster abilities (Divine Interference, Choking Vacuum), and mobility abilities (Gravitic Freedom, Improved Gravitic Freedom, Long-Distance Travel) that make the element way, way better. With the addition of Pure Negative Infusion, there’s really no excuse not to get Negative Blast as your starting blast unless you’re playing in an undead campaign, so pick that up and get blasting.


First-Party: Summoning > Anticaster, Control, Recon > Buffing, Debuffing, Ranged > Melee, Skills
Third-Party: Anticaster, Control, Summoning > Debuffing, Recon > Melee > Buffing, Ranged > Skills






	Mean Rating 
(Paizo Content)
	Mean Rating 
(3PP Content)
	Mean Rating (Combined)
	Form Infusions
	2.0
	4.0
