	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Class Skills


Escape Artist got rated green for fire, so I suppose the same is only fair for void. Without Searing Flesh and Flame Shield to disincentivize monsters from hanging onto grapples, the skill is—if anything—even more important for chaokineticists than it was for pyrokineticists. Knowledge (Dungeoneering) is probably the least-used monster identification skill of them all, coming into play only against aberrations and oozes. Having at least one rank in it won’t hurt, though.




Defensive Talent


Emptiness: Emptiness throws a lot of different bonuses at you, but they’re all too weak or too circumstantial to make much difference. Resistance to negative energy damage? Okay, next time you fight an evil cleric who’s channeling offensively, let me know. A 5% to ignore crits and sneak attacks? You mean the bonuses that are superseded by elemental overflow? Wow, you shouldn’t have. The untyped bonus against emotion effects is probably the best argument for Emptiness, but even so, it’s not a great argument.




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Pure Negative Infusion: Basically an infusion tax so that you don’t absolutely stink against constructs and undead. Not my favorite thing in the world to be forced to purchase, but it’s not as though void has an overabundance of great infusions competing for your time.


Dampening Infusion: Dazzling Infusion got a red, so that’s what Dampening Infusion will get, too. Hey, don’t complain to me: if you want better ratings, get Paizo to write stronger abilities.
Pulling Infusion: As bad as Pushing Infusion, and that’s pretty bad. Why did they have to make these combat maneuver checks instead of simple Reflex saves to avoid getting pushed or pulled?
Pushing Infusion: CMD scores get incredibly high in later levels, and as a ¾-BAB class with no real reason to invest in feats like Improved Bull Rush, Pushing Infusion is just going to be wasted burn about 90% of the time.


2nd Level


Weighing Infusion: Entangling Infusion received a blue, and Weighing Infusion is a carbon copy. Seems only fair to give it blue, too.


Sickening Infusion: Sickening Infusion is hands-down better than Toxic Infusion, the first-party infusion that it mimics. Lower burn cost and longer duration are enough to elevate it to a solid green, but sickened isn’t powerful enough that it can go much beyond that. A nice opening debuff, though.


3rd Level


Low Gravity Infusion: Wow, how weird. This is devastating against huge melee monsters with poor Reflex saves, as they’ll just start to drift gently into the air à la Aunt Marge in Prisoner of Azkaban, unable to take any of their devastating melee attacks against the party. Meanwhile, you turn them into an aerial pincushion.


Darkness Infusion: Deeper darkness is usually what players are looking for when they seek out darkness spells, as that spell can reduce the illumination level to supernatural darkness. Darkness, meanwhile, can only take the illumination level down to darkness, which actually tends to penalize PCs more than it does monsters. Most monsters have darkvision or some form of sight that beats darkness (tremorsense, spiritsense, blindsight, etc.) while a considerable number of PC races do not. The battlefield control here isn’t all it’s cracked up to be.


Unnerving Infusion: Will negates for a fairly weak, common condition that already doesn’t work on any enemy immune to fear or mind-affecting effects. I’d maybe consider this a yellow purchase if it cost 1 burn, but not as a 2-burn ability.


4th Level


High Gravity Infusion: Although I’m rating High and Low Gravity Infusions both green, I would give more of a nod to Low Gravity Infusion—it’s cheaper, targets a weaker save, and can do fun things with AoE form infusions. High Gravity Infusion is awesome against rogues and full casters, though, so that’s something.
Lingering Darkness: There are a few too many saves here for my liking, but compared with Atrophy Infusion, Lingering Darkness is a rockstar. Ticking ability damage that can actually kill foes is a great usage of burn.


Atrophy Infusion: Too slow, too weak, too expensive. Not to mention that some enemies are immune to ability damage!
Turning Blast: Turning Blast’s associated blast talent is Negative Blast. There’s a wrinkle: Negative Blast deals no damage to undead, and thus cannot fulfill the “take damage from a turning blast” clause to even force enemies to save. So...Turning Blast does nothing for chaokineticists. I guess come back as a phytokineticist?


Immobilizing Infusion: I feel like this one is a little unfair, because enemies who are spec’ed for melee combat simply won’t be able to function if they can’t move from their squares.
Suggested Fix: Give targets a new save every round, but a longer duration to compensate (maybe 1 round/2 kineticist levels). You’ll still get some effects, but targets won’t be crippled with no secondary save.


5th Level


Dismissing Infusion: Outsiders are incredibly common as high-level enemies, and they usually come packed to the rafters with spell-like abilities, weird auras, exotic DR, energy immunities, etc. that can make a kineticist’s life hell. Think of Dismissing Infusion as a save-or-die effect against these enemies, forcing them out of your plane on a failed save. A good candidate for Improved/Greater Flurry of Blasts and Empty Infusion for when you need to banish something now.


Reanimating Infusion: Pet undead are pet undead, so I can’t rate this infusion too poorly, but having so little choice over what kind of undead you raise (zombie vs. skeleton vs. fast zombie vs. bloody skeleton, etc.) and having to spend burn to keep them animated for longer than a combat isn’t great. If you want my opinion, just get void’s Kinetic Invocation feat and pick up animate dead and command undead as spell-like abilities.


Reconstruction Infusion: Very weak healing for the cost, although if you have a form infusion attached it is nice to be able to hurt foes and heal friendly undead at the same time.
Vampiric Infusion: Vampiric Infusion doesn’t in any way improve the efficacy or burn cost of Void Healer, so you’re essentially paying 3 burn for an extra standard action. If you can get to the point where infusion specialization completely eliminates the need for you to spend burn on this infusion, it may be worth it in rare circumstances; otherwise, it’s flat-out pointless.


6th Level


Darkness Infusion, Greater: So: you can finally create supernatural darkness. Huge burn cost aside, this infusion is less likely to be useful now than it would have been in the single-digit levels; more and more enemies will be able to dispel, counter, or outright ignore supernatural darkness at this level. It’s still decent, just not mind-blowing for the cost and level.
Decaying Infusions: Constitution damage is extraordinarily dangerous: it tanks the Fortitude save, lowers HP, makes stabilizing at negative HP harder, and more. Decaying Infusion is expensive and may not be fast enough for rocket-tag fights that are decided in only a few rounds, but protracted battles can almost always yield good results for this infusion.


Leech Infusion: There’s no save involved here, so that’s something, but maximum, maximum temporary HP you could expect to get from this infusion would be about 15 HP from fighting a CR 20 enemy with 30 HD. For 4 points of burn, I can’t see how that’s worth it in most situations.


7th Level


Overload Infusion: A really interesting take on a damage-over-time ability. The burn cost is obviously high at this level, but you’ll have several tiers of infusion specialization to mitigate that. Even so, it will almost certainly be a substance infusion that you can’t really pair with a form infusion due to the sheer cost of such a proposition. Fun to watch enemies melt themselves, though.


Enervating Infusion: Fortitude will almost certainly be most monsters’ strong save at this level, which means (per the Bench-Pressing resource I often use in writing guides) we’d need a save DC on Enervating Infusion of 31 to make the effect stick about 75% of the time against a CR 14 creature. Between the spell level and base 10, we’ve got a 17, which means we’d need a Constitution modifier of +14 to get to our target. Even accounting for 19 Constitution at base, 3 through natural attribute progression, and a +6 Constitution belt, we’re still only at +9 without even factoring in immunity or bonuses to saves against energy drain, immunity to effects requiring Fortitude saves, and immunity to negative energy damage. This does not bode well for Enervating Infusion. The prospect of negative levels is cool, but it’s my opinion that you don’t have the resources to make it work effectively, and for a 4-burn infusion, that’s a big deal. Sorry.




Infusions: Form


1st Level


Energize Weapon: This would be pretty good if you were playing a gestalt kineticist with a more mainstream martial class, as it’s essentially free damage on a full-attack action. Not very much damage, though, and since it locks you out of other form infusions (including Kinetic Blade and Kinetic Whip) I’m having a lot of difficulty seeing why a single-class kineticist would choose to use it.


4th Level


Hyper-Dimensional Blast: Ignoring cover and targeting flat-footed AC is awesome, especially when you’re getting Extended Range rolled in for free. Particularly well-suited for physical blasts, which will have a harder time hitting enemies in general.


Singularity: I just don’t like Singularity that much. It’s slow-growing, deals laughable damage (enemies that save successfully will take ⅛ to ¼ of your blast damage, which is just silly), and feels way too expensive for what it does—this should cost 2 burn, max. And this is void’s only other form infusion apart from Energize Weapon! Wha…?


6th Level


Gorgon Gaze: There are a number of tricks enemies can use to avoid your gaze attack, and a Will save to negate what was half damage to begin with ain’t great. Moreover, you can’t do anything but use utility talents while Gorgon Gaze is active unless you want the effect cancelled. I remain (mostly) unimpressed.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Gravitic Pack Horse: Dumb name notwithstanding, Gravitic Pack Horse is actually a pretty good wild talent, since kineticists often tend to dump Strength. Nice to be able to extend the ant haul to your teammates, too.


Atrophy Field: On its own, Atrophy Field really isn’t much, and the fact that it destroys your own party’s ability to heal without burning immediate and standard actions to deactivate and reactivate the field makes it a tough sell. It partners up with a bunch of other higher-level utility talents, though, and that’s where the money is.
Basic Chaokinesis: Huh. Kinda like a protective penumbra, jump, and ant haul had a weak, weak baby. The carrying capacity functionality of Basic Chaokinesis is probably its best aspect unless you’re a dhampir, but even carrying capacity is mighty underwhelming.
Deathsight: Hmmm. Deathwatch doesn’t do a whole lot that you couldn’t discern with Perception checks, but I suppose it’s decent enough for a 1st-level utility talent.
Gravitic Leaper: Full disclosure? I really like any of the talents based off of Air’s Leap. And in the case of void, it’s actually a better talent than in air, because you don’t have a great means of flight!
Hide from Undead: Intelligent undead get a save to notice you, so the fact that the save DC on this will never scale past 11 + your Constitution modifier is kind of a big deal.
Imbue Kinesis: Good only at low levels when you can’t effectively blast things and might instead need to help out an ally. It certainly doesn’t scale well at all, so I would let it go in one of your wild talent upgrades.
Void Healer: Written as-is, I’m pretty sure Void Healer is brokenly powerful. Kinetic Healer, as you know, gives you the option to shift the burn cost to the healed ally, the primary downside being that they take nonlethal damage in the process. Undead, however, are immune to nonlethal damage. There’s a clause in the burn ability text that speaks to this: “Nonlethal damage from burn can’t be reduced or redirected, and a kineticist incapable of taking nonlethal damage can’t accept burn.” But the undead you create with animate dead from Kinetic Invocation (which you took, right? Right?) aren’t kineticists, just dead schmos you revivified for funsies. They should be able to skirt around that clause on a technicality. Now, I think a reasonable move from your GM is to rule that you have to pay the burn cost to heal your undead pals—someone’s got to, after all—which makes Void Healer considerably worse than Kinetic Healer; less versatility and more burn cost for you is a bad combo.
Void Resonance: Being healed by negative energy would seem to include the energy drain effects and negative levels of undead creatures, as well as spells like enervation or energy drain. That being said, if you’re playing an undead-heavy campaign like Carrion Crown, this could be a tremendous boon, allowing you to tank encounters that would leave the rest of the party unconscious or dead.


Sustained Form: When was the last time that you really needed to eat or drink in Pathfinder?


Shadow Blaster: Again, I’m not a fan of replacements for the basic kinesis utility talents, because they all seem to grant you bonuses to attack or damage.
        Suggested Fix: Veto.
Simple Versatility: Paizo designed kinetic blasts to be used with only the infusions they get on the label; opening up other infusions often has unintended consequences, and is certainly way too powerful for a 1st-level utility talent. See, e.g., Composite Versatility and Improved Composite Versatility, which have burn costs and per-day use limits, for an example of how you might be able to expand access to infusions without breaking the power curve.
Suggested Fix: Pick one infusion belonging to your element’s other simple blast; you can apply that to the first simple blast. None of this BS about applying every infusion you want.


2nd Level


Urgent Restoration: Being able to heal yourself as a swift action is an incredible boost in combat effectiveness, even if you are going to have to take some burn to do it. Much appreciated for tanking builds.


Featherweight: I can’t find any reference in the gravity rules to how this utility talent would affect a kineticist, but suffice it to say that encumbrance and Acrobatics checks to jump would likely become things of the past. Very bad for your bone health, though. Don’t believe me? Look up “microgravity bone loss.” #AdultNightmares
No Breath: Straight immunity to many inhaled effects is very good. It won’t come up in every fight, but protection from stinking cloud, immunity to drowning or suffocation, etc. all makes it worth it.
Wall Walker: Spider climb is a good spell, so it should come as no surprise that Wall Walker is also rated fairly highly. If you want mobility and don’t want to spend the talents on flight, this should be your purchase.


Eyes of the Void: How good Eyes of the Void is depends on your race. If you have no darkvision, this utility talent will be well worth taking. If you have darkvision, less so. It does open up Greater Eyes of the Void, though, and everybody wants that.
Fast Fall: If you’re really leaning into High Gravity Infusion, Fast Fall might be worth it, but otherwise it’s unlikely to come up often enough to be valuable.
Gravitic Density: Void = best combat maneuver specialist confirmed?
Heavy Hand: For any other class, at-will lead blades or gravity bow would be a massive gain. These spells can’t apply to kinetic blasts, though, so what do they apply to? Not natural attacks. Visceral Whip? Some weird kineticist that’s not using kinetic blasts at all? A conductive bow paired with an energy blast to abide by the ranged touch attack restriction?
Plains of Weeping Wounds: A very interesting ability for bleed damage builds, especially when paired with Bloody Infusion. It’s just too bad that you need to expand outward into a different element to pull one of those builds together. Note that it doesn’t need to be you causing the bleed damage, though—this utility talent just exacerbates whatever’s there.


Gravitic Adaptation: Not worth a purchase unless you’re regularly adventuring in planes with strange gravitational rules.


3rd Level


Chilling Void: A water/void hybrid talent, and one that works quite well if you’re a chaokineticist who’s focused on darkness tactics. The Fortitude save for half damage, spell resistance, and prerequisites prevent this from getting too overpowered, as does your ability to hurt your friends with cold damage. Make sure they have communal resist energy up or something, otherwise you’ll chill them to death.
Gravitic Freedom: Being constantly under the effects of subjective gravity can lead to some wacky hijinks. Down at the bottom of a pit? Just change the direction of gravity and “fall” up and out of it. Want to stay out of arm’s reach? The floor is the ceiling for you now. Want to move 300 feet per round with no action investment? Now you can. Boots of the cat and Featherweight will basically be required if you’re going to use this for anything other than long-distance travel, as you’re essentially falling...with style. Make sure you don’t take much damage and can land on your feet!
Shadowbend: I’m going to link you to a resource that my friend Zach put together, Artemis, to help you figure out which spells are eligible for Shadowbend—there should be 32 total spells between 1st and 9th level that fit the bill. Highlights from 1st through 3rd levels are haunting mists, shadow anchor, twilight haze, and shadow enchantment.


Gravity Control: Flame Jet, Self Telekinesis, and Gravity Control all work the same way, as do their older cousins. These abilities won’t be quite combat-ready at the time you get them, since you need to keep investing your standard action in flight, but for out-of-combat purposes, they’re aces. Besides, you need these to progress to the Greater versions of all these talents, and you definitely want to do that.
Night Sense: Blindsense and blindsight of any kind are good, no matter what.
Shroud of Shadows: I’ll remind you that darkness can never lower the light level to supernatural darkness, only darkness, and so any creature with darkvision is still going to be able to hit you without a miss change. Against enemies without darkvision, though, you’ll receive a miss chance that’s very nice. Shroud of Shadows is also one way of proc’ing Chilling Void, although you can’t do much with it until you get the Improved version at 5th level.


Zone of Decay: Certainly OP in some fights against enemies who rely on fast healing or regeneration to stay alive. Those are fairly rare, though, so this wouldn’t be my first choice of Atrophy Field upgrades.


Desolate: So...let me get this straight: you’re going to spend 1 point of burn to make it harder to grow crops in a 10 ft. x 10 ft. square? Yeah, no.
Undead Grip: Elemental Grip is quite bad; Undead Grip is also quite bad. Instead of halting them, why not spend a point of burn to get them under your direct control with control undead? You have Kinetic Invocations for a reason.


Dimensional Rip / Improved / Greater / Deceptive / Enlarged / Invasive / Vacuum / Corporeal: The dimensional ripper archetype is really, really cool, allowing you to tear holes in reality and shove enemies and blasts through them like you’re playing a game of Portal. Both the archetype and the utility talents that mimic many of the archetype’s class features are massively overpowered, however; as it stands, there’s little reason to play another archetype—and certainly not a first-party archetype—when dimensional ripper is available. It takes a lot of third-party options away from aether, time, and void, but if you want my advice, leave these wild talents out of your games.
Suggested Fix: Eliminating the various upgrades to Dimensional Rip might do it, honestly, along with bumping Dimensional Rip up to a 5th-level ability. Keep the burn cost at one, for sure.
Oppressive Atmosphere: The movement penalties scale way too quickly, Reflex save penalties are strong when you’re lobbing around form infusions left and right, and there’s no burn cost, making it essentially constant.
Suggested Fix: Reduce the radius to 15 ft., reduce the movement penalties to 10 ft./5 levels, and give it a save and spell resistance if it’s going to cost 0 burn—say, a Fortitude save each round while enemies remain in the radius of the atmosphere. GMs should also consider making this affect allies.


4th Level


Absentia: Interesting. Constant nondetection makes you pretty hard to pin down, which could be a great boon for closet necromancers. People might have an inkling that there’s something off about you, but they sure won’t be able to prove it when every caster level check fails against you. I like it, but then again, I’m a big intrigue fan.
Corpse Puppet: Let’s get this straight: never spend burn on Corpse Puppet. If you want undead allies, get Kinetic Invocation, pick up animate dead, and animate them the old-fashioned way. You are literally throwing burn into the garbage if you spend it here. Unfortunately, the restrictions that Corpse Puppet places on Hit Dice will probably make it such that you don’t encounter many enemies eligible for this wild talent; enemy HD tend to be quite a bit higher than character level, and you’re already relegated to animating creatures with 4 fewer HD than your character level. If you can make that work for you, though, Corpse Puppet is as good as Aether Puppet or Clockwork Puppet, which is to say pretty decent.
Curse Breaker: Bonuses on saves against curses could be worse, although they could certainly also be better. Suppressing or removing curses is better than suppressing or removing poisons, so I’m decently happy with Curse Breaker.
Death Energy: A very interesting tool for positioning your void utility talents. Obviously gets better the more you’re investing in void wild talents.


Vampiric Grasp: People actually using conjuration (healing) spells in combat is rarer than you might think, and the fact that you have to take on 1 point of burn to even attempt your Vampiric Grasp will probably outweigh any healing you might get.


Healing Burst: For chaokineticists, the healing will only apply to undead creatures. I know you have a bunch of them running around from Kinetic Invocation, but again, assuming you’re needing to take on all the burn for them since they’re immune to nonlethal damage, this adds up quickly.


Death Globe: I don’t know what anubis’ flail is, so I can’t rate this ability. Certainly doesn’t seem to be a first-party spell or item.


5th Level


Grasp of the Void: Ha! Yes, at-will black tentacles certainly qualifies as a purple talent. Pair it with Chilling Void, and you have a really effective damage talent, too. Every chaokineticist who doesn’t take this utility talent is a fool.


Divine Interference: Finally, a truly worthy upgrade for Atrophy Field. You’ll obviously bug the crap out of allied divine casters with this utility talent, but man, is it satisfying to shut down enemy divine casters.
Enervation: At-will enervation certainly qualifies as a blue wild talent.
Eyes of the Void, Greater: See in darkness is an awesome monster ability that devils usually use in conjunction with some really nasty illumination tactics to mess you up. Now you get to see in supernatural darkness like them! Creepy.
Gravity Control, Greater: With your standard action no longer gobbled up, you can fly in combat with no trouble, including hover checks. You won’t be able to gather power unless you’re hovering, stationary, however, so keep that in mind.
Gravity Sculptor: Wow, what a fun ability. Objective directional gravity probably has the most combat applications: you can cover a retreat through a narrow hallway by setting gravity to move back toward pursuing enemies, create a “gravity well” trap that flings enemies up toward the ceiling, or allow allies to move more than their complement of movement for a round by creating a “gravity corridor” pointing in the direction they want to go—if they can get into the corridor by the end of their movement, they’ll fall the rest of the way. Anyway, a creative utility talent for creative players.


Flicker Charge: It’s an interesting mobility tool, and certainly helpful when you need to kite an enemy. It’s a pity that the elements that get Flicker Charge aren’t usually the ones you’d be building a melee attacker with, though.
Improved Shroud of Shadows: Expanding your area of darkness is worthwhile, but things get really good if you have Chilling Void, which turns this utility talent into a ticking AoE cold damage aura.


Improved Featherweight: Everyone at this level will have ability to fly, which kind of negates the importance of jumping; likewise, anyone who needs it will have permanent ant haul or muleback cords to handle encumbrance. Meh.
Lingering Energy: I’m not a fan. It costs 1 burn and eats your immediate action, and even if an enemy does proc the blast, they’re likely to avoid all the damage. Nah.


Crushing Atmosphere: As with Oppressive Atmosphere, this just needs a more robust save and spell resistance. It’s too powerful without.
Suggested Fix: Make it a Fortitude partial save: half damage and no status condition on a successful save, full damage and fatigued on a failed save, and specify that enemies can’t become exhausted by failing the save twice. Set spell resistance to yes.


6th Level


Antilife Shell: Great for keeping enemies at bay while you’re trying to blast.
Improved Shadowbend: I’m going to link you to a resource that my friend Zach put together, Artemis, to help you figure out which spells are eligible for Shadowbend—there should be 32 total spells between 1st and 9th level that fit the bill. Highlights from 4th through 6th levels are shadow jaunt, shadow conjuration, cloak of shadows, shadow endurance, shadow evocation, baleful shadow transmutation, greater shadow enchantment, shadow transmutation, and shadow walk.
Long-Distance Travel: Long-distance teleportation is universally useful, and this talent is no exception.


Improved Gravitic Freedom: Now the enemy’s gate really is down.
Suffocate: Suffocate can be an exceptional tool for killing arcane spellcasters before they can do any harm to you; for any other target, the slow speed and concentration will be dealbreakers.


Aura of Ash: You can cast animate dead from Kinetic Invocation as a standard action for the same cost, and animate dead gives you more control over what skeleton/zombie template you want your critter to have. If you really need to disintegrate enemies (graveknights, vampires...uh, revenants?) then I suppose it’s worthwhile. I’d just stick with Kinetic Invocation for undead minion masters.
Dimensional Traveler: Very narrowly useful for Kinetic Blade, Kinetic Whip, Visceral Whip, and natural attack builds, for whom Dimensional Traveler basically acts as pounce. If you’re a run-of-the-mill ranged kineticist using a kinetic blast once a round, Dimensional Traveler won’t do a thing for you.


Choking Vacuum: Guys! We’ve talked about this! You can’t just hand out mass save-or-die spells like candy. Choking Vacuum essentially kills everything within 30 ft. of you, pending one or two failed Fortitude saves. As a 6th-level ability. Heck, not even wail of the banshee, a 9th-level spell, can do that.
Suggested Fix: Don’t treat it as an upgrade to Suffocate, for starters. A spitball: Fortitude partial, spell resistance yes. Expend a number of burn when activating this ability. On a failed save, creatures within 30 ft. become nauseated for a number of rounds equal to the burn you expended; on a successful save, creatures within 30 ft. become sickened instead for the same duration. This gives a little stacking synergy with Oppressive Atmosphere and Crushing Atmosphere, but forces you to spend a bunch of burn if you want to draw out the nauseated status, and gives enemies who pass their saves a much weaker status condition.


7th Level


Writhing Bodies: Why does that fiery corpse orgy know Russian? If I had a nickel for every time someone asked me that...seriously, though, this is an unusual summoning ability, but a powerful one. I’d use it to soak up dead bodies and then just plow them into the next room to soak alpha strikes.


Elemental Eater: How useful Elemental Eater is will depend almost entirely on on what element you’re using it with, and how prevalent that element is in your campaign. For common elements like fire, this ability could rise to a blue; for rare elements like force (aether), negative energy (void), or sonic (sound), it’ll likely be red.
Improved Dimensional Traveler: Even for Kinetic Blade, Kinetic Whip, Visceral Whip, and natural attack builds, Dimensional Maneuvers and Dimensional Savant are not as necessary as Dimensional Assault and Dimensional Dervish. Can be nice if you’re using whips to trip or reposition foes, flank with yourself, etc., though.


9th Level


Gravity Master: The enemy’s gate is down, huh? Well, if you ever wanted to recreate the hallway fight scene from Inception, this is your chance. It’s pretty ridiculous that you’re getting this ability at will, but I’m not complaining—there’s an immense amount of battlefield control that you can do with Gravity Master, all of it hilarious. Enjoy. You’ve earned it.


Greater Shadowbend: I’m going to link you to a resource that my friend Zach put together, Artemis, to help you figure out which spells are eligible for Shadowbend—there should be 32 total spells between 1st and 9th level that fit the bill. Highlights from 7th through 9th levels are greater shadow evocation, greater shadow transmutation, and shades.


Kinetic Lich: Even accepting that you’re an evil-aligned humanoid who wants to become a lich, the process of becoming one has pretty monumental costs (about 160,000 to 180,000 gp), isn’t guaranteed to turn you into a lich (that DC 30 Will save may not be easy to pass), and reduces your effective Constitution score by 10. Sure, you’re immortal...but at what cost?




Kinetic Invocations


1st Level


Life Channel: Only works if you’re a dhampir, I guess? Self-only makes this an extraordinarily niche spell-like ability.


2nd Level


Command Undead: Command undead isn’t a spell you’re going to use for controlling undead you find roving about the wilderness, although it could be used for that. No, command undead (as you’ll read in Brewer’s Guide to Undeath) is the second of two “buckets” of undead Hit Dice that you’ll use to be a minion master. Because unintelligent undead get no save against command undead and the spell lasts for days/level, it’s a perfect tool for moving weaker undead creatures out of your animate dead bucket in order to free up room for new, stronger summons. If your animate dead bucket is getting too full, hit them with command undead, release them from your animate dead bucket, and then enjoy all the same powers you would have had otherwise. And if your days/level duration starts to run out and you don’t feel like resubscribing to that particular minion, just send it rushing heedlessly into battle or order it to kill itself. You’ll never use intelligent undead, so there’s no reason not to!


3rd Level


Animate Dead: Animate dead as a Kinetic Invocation is still exceptionally strong, but not as strong as I initially thought. Kinetic Invocations are treated in all ways as psychic spells, including requiring emotion and thought components, and, crucially for this spell, material components. It turns out that you can’t skirt around the 25 gp x HD material component cost of animate dead on the technicality that Kinetic Invocations are spell-like abilities, not spells. Womp womp. It’s still an amazing pick-up for void, and you can pursue Deific/Fiendish Obedience boons for Urgathoa and Orcus if you’d like to push into cost-free necromancy eventually. Read up on Brewer’s Guide to Undeath and get cracking.


Unliving Rage: I don’t like that unliving rage forces you to concentrate, or that the duration is so short after you stop concentrating, but it’s a decent way to buff up the undead minions you’ll have trailing your void kineticist. I’m going to call it a yellow, since the cost is pretty high in exchange for what you’re getting.


Halt Undead: I’ve never been a huge fan of hold person, and halt undead is even more niche than hold person. A save every round and automatic dismissal of the condition when undead are attacked do not make for a strong spell.


4th Level


Death Ward: Death ward is an awesome spell. Death spells and death effects are quite common, and there’s no murderer of fun more efficient than energy drain. Death ward is a hard counter to most of these effects, and a strong defense against the rest. Another excellent pick-up for void.


8th Level


Mind Blank: If you’re looking for powerful 8th-level spells, you can’t do much better than mind blank. It lasts 24 hours, flat-out negates any divination effect pointed at the caster, and sports a whopping +8 resistance bonus to all mind-affecting effects. (This resistance bonus won’t stack with a cloak of resistance, but since the bonus here is quite a bit larger, you won’t mind the fact that mind blank supercedes the item.) I’d be proud to spend 1 burn a day on this ability.
💧 Water
Water


        I tend to think of water as the best “all-arounder” element for kineticists. It doesn’t hit as hard as fire, but it’s got good composite blasts and at least one or two blue/green infusions on both the form and substance infusion list for each; it doesn’t tank as well as earth, but its defensive talent is very solid and it can add utility talents like Shimmering Mirage or Kinetic Invocations like fluid form at later levels to increase its hardiness; it doesn’t move as well as air, but it rules the roost in any body of water and can grant you an air walk-like effect; it doesn’t boast as much utility as aether, but it can sure get some good battlefield control going between Slick, Slick Infusion, Entangling Infusion, Maelstrom, Cloud, and Kinetic Invocation’s sleet storm. The only fly in water’s ointment is that aquatic campaigns are somewhat rare, making it difficult to fully take advantage of all the water manipulation and aquatic mobility effects you have available to you in this element. There’s more than enough power to go around to recommend water as a secondary element, though, and potentially as a primary element if you’re in a campaign like Ruins of Azlant or something that’s going to involve huge amounts of underseas exploration. Ice Blast, the composite blast you’d get for sticking with water all the way, isn’t hand-over-fist more powerful than the other composite blasts available to the element, which makes it less appealing as an all-in element.


First-Party: Control, Melee, Ranged, Recon > Buffing, Debuffing, Skills > Anticaster, Summoning
Third-Party: Control, Debuffing, Melee, Ranged, Recon > Anticaster, Buffing, Skills > Summoning

	2.5
	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Class Skills


        Swim is obviously integral to the hydrokineticist’s general schtick, and Knowledge (Nature) is plenty good as a monster identification skill. I’ll take two!




Defensive Talent


Shroud of Water: All kineticists should wear the light armor that’s permitted them, so I can’t imagine using Shroud of Water for an armor bonus to AC. A shield bonus, though? Much rarer for a class that neither casts traditional spells nor wields traditional weapons. Spending burn on this ability actually isn’t a terrible idea, both because you’ll never be able to spend that much burn on it (you max out at 3 burn at 18th level in exchange for a +9 shield bonus to AC) and because avoiding one or two hits at that level will easily recoup your nonlethal damage from burn. Probably the strongest defensive talent after Flesh of Stone, and the two pair together (with Expanded Defense) to make combination water/earth kineticists incredibly tough.




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Bloody Infusion: Several classes of enemy are outright immune to bleed damage, and it can be healed almost by accident if you’re fighting creatures with fast healing, regeneration, or divine magic. Still, Bloody Infusion is cheap, scales nicely with composite blasts, and deals free damage over time.


Oil Infusion: Wow, what a wacky infusion for the...water/fire hybrid kineticist you want to build? The increase to the save DC is the biggest headline here.
Persistent Infusion: Probably the coolest part about Persistent Infusion is that it leaves parts of your element lying around that can then be used for utility talents, etc. Kind of like giving yourself an alley-oop in conditions where there’s not a lot of your element present naturally. The caltrop part is nice, but caltrops have an attack bonus of +0, which will fail to hit many 1st-level enemies, let alone higher-level enemies. Probably jumps up to at least a green if you have cheap form infusions like Kinetic Bomb or Improved Kinetic Bomb.
Shivering Infusion: A minor debuff that loses effectiveness early in the game. Might be of limited use for annoying casters, although anyone who’s invested even a little in their concentration checks will likely beat the save DC.


Pushing Infusion: CMD scores get incredibly high in later levels, and as a ¾-BAB class with no real reason to invest in feats like Improved Bull Rush, Pushing Infusion is just going to be wasted burn about 90% of the time.
Quenching Infusion: I really feel like a water blast should be allowed to do this with no burn expenditure, but hey, I didn’t write the rules. At any rate, no, this is no good. About all I can think of to use Quenching Infusion on is effects like wall of fire or burning entanglement; it’s way too niche otherwise.


2nd Level


Entangling Infusion: Entangling Infusion is reasonably cheap, has a nice long duration with a great set of debuffs, and targets the weakest save (on average) by CR. For my money, this is one of the best infusions a kineticist has, and a real feather in the cap of the elements that get access to it.
Slick Infusion: Lovely battlefield control infusion, especially when paired with the Slick utility talent and the Cloud or Spray form infusions. I’m a big fan of water’s diversification into this market.


Penetrating Infusion: I personally think that Penetrating Infusion should have been a 1-burn infusion, but maybe that’s just me. Cold and fire resistance are much more common than electricity resistance, hence this infusion is correspondingly better for water and fire than it is for air.


4th Level


Immobilizing Infusion: I feel like this one is a little unfair, because enemies who are spec’ed for melee combat simply won’t be able to function if they can’t move from their squares.
Suggested Fix: Give targets a new save every round, but a longer duration to compensate (maybe 1 round/2 kineticist levels). You’ll still get some effects, but targets won’t be crippled with no secondary save.


5th Level


Chilling Infusion: For an infusion that targets most monsters’ strongest save, is negated entirely by said save, and lasts only one round on a failed save, Chilling Infusion is far too expensive. At 1 burn, sure, I’d give it a pass, but it’s far too expensive for most applications at 3 burn. The possible exception is when it’s paired with form infusions that linger for a few rounds—I’m thinking Cloud because I’m here in water, but others exist among the universal infusions—because you can probably get a few failed saves just by the law of large numbers.
Improved Bloody Infusion: Given the drastically increased cost, I still don’t think Improved Bloody Infusion is much better than its younger cousin. For blood kineticists, though, it probably comes out to at least a green.


Unbreaking Waves Infusion: More combat maneuvers. Blech. Just leave it be.


6th Level


Destabilizing Infusion: Cold and acid resistance are very common at this level, making Destabilizing Infusion a great pick for hydro- and toxikineticists. For vibrokineticists, who will almost never have to deal with sonic resistance, this infusion really isn’t worth your time.
Rime Blast: The damage is rock-bottom for this level, but Dexterity drain is certainly interesting. I’ll rate it green.


Hypertensive Infusion: Let’s think for a second. On a normal blast, you’d deal damage immediately and have done. Using Hypertensive Infusion, a much longer process is involved: in Round 1, you need to hit the enemy and they need to fail a Fortitude save to even be affected by the infusion; in Round 2, they need to fail a second Fortitude save; in Round 3, they need to fail a third Fortitude save. Then and only then—after three failed Fortitude saves over three rounds—do you deal your blast’s damage. For 4 points of burn. It’s slow, it’s expensive, it targets the strongest save by level, and there’s no guarantee that you’ll even get your blast damage in return. No.


7th Level


Overload Infusion: A really interesting take on a damage-over-time ability. The burn cost is obviously high at this level, but you’ll have several tiers of infusion specialization to mitigate that. Even so, it will almost certainly be a substance infusion that you can’t really pair with a form infusion due to the sheer cost of such a proposition. Fun to watch enemies melt themselves, though.


8th Level


Imprisoning Infusion: Ice Tomb is a fantastic witch hex, and it does just as well for a kineticist as it does for a witch. The Fortitude save to negate is a bit unfortunate, as is the high cost, but save-or-suck effects tend to be like this.
Maelstrom: I know, I know, Maelstrom only applies in aquatic combat and is really expensive. Don’t care! How cool is this infusion, I ask you? DC 35 is actually not a gimme for some aquatic creatures at CR 16-20, and that gives you the upper hand: if enemies can’t either avoid the maelstrom or make it through the area in one round, you get to position them right back at square one on your turn. It’s a fun party trick, for sure.




Infusions: Form


1st Level


Energize Weapon: This would be pretty good if you were playing a gestalt kineticist with a more mainstream martial class, as it’s essentially free damage on a full-attack action. Not very much damage, though, and since it locks you out of other form infusions (including Kinetic Blade and Kinetic Whip) I’m having a lot of difficulty seeing why a single-class kineticist would choose to use it.


3rd Level


Impale: In my mind, Impale is superior to Torrent, and doubly so if you attach it to an Ice Blast. The fact that you can use Impale to freaking burst through walls and stab enemies is just so cool. With no save, a good area of effect, and a reasonable cost, I can’t see any reason to ding Impale.


Torrent: Sure, why not? Line effects are more niche than cones, but corridors are a thing in Pathfinder, and you’ll often be able to hit one or more enemies in a line. As always, energy blasts come out stronger here than anything else, so especially consider this infusion if you’re playing a pyrokineticist.


Dehydrating Blast: A lot less damage and the fatigued condition for 2 points of burn? I’m failing to see how this is worth it.


4th Level


Spray: With a Reflex save for half damage on top of half your damage to begin with (not to mention a pretty hefty burn cost) Spray doesn’t quite attain S-tier, but it is sitting pretty in B-tier. It’s the easiest way for a hydrokineticist to tag multiple enemies, at least until Cloud and Fragmentation come along. By all means, make do with Torrent or Impale if you can get foes lined up just right, but know that you may need to pay the extra burn for Spray much of the time.


6th Level


Greater Dehydrating Blast: The clause about firing off an extra blast is interesting, but you won’t be able to gather power for it, so any extras will be severely limited in power or very costly.


7th Level


Fragmentation: Fragmentation is quite expensive, but dang is it good for clearing tightly clustered groups of enemies. Once you have a few instances of infusion specialization, you should use Fragmentation regularly in conjunction with ice blast to just blow the crap out of mobs. Cloud is going to be better at dealing sustained damage if you can contain enemies in one spot for a few rounds; Fragmentation excels when you only have one shot and need to make it count.


Cloud: There you go. Cloud is the total package: moveable, no save, continuous damage over multiple rounds, and even some battlefield control thrown in there for good measure. A fitting capstone for your form infusions.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Elemental Acceleration: This is basically the monk’s fast movement class feature, for the cost of a utility talent. Movement speed rarely breaks the power curve, so I don’t feel the need to veto this one, but it is very good. Aerokineticists in particular will appreciate the bonuses to their flight speed from Wings of Air.


Kinetic Healer: I’m a fan of Kinetic Healer for two reasons: first, it’s healing, and pretty strong healing, at that. Second, it’s a wild talent that teaches your team about burn. Drop Kinetic Healer on a party member a few times and they’ll learn very quickly that you can’t be running around taking 4 burn every turn to drop mooks in encounters. Burn is a resource to be conserved, respected, and feared, and Kinetic Healer teaches your sweet, innocent tablemates that lesson in a hurry.
Slick: Grease is a great spell, and getting it at will is pretty good. Grease doesn’t actually cause an immediate Reflex save to avoid falling, so you’ll need to place it carefully so that enemies will blunder into it.


Basic Hydrokinesis: Pretty basic cantrips here. Nothing spectacular.
Imbue Kinesis: Good only at low levels when you can’t effectively blast things and might instead need to help out an ally. It certainly doesn’t scale well at all, so I would let it go in one of your wild talent upgrades.
Kinetic Cover: Flexible access to total cover is always to be applauded, but the mini-wall has laughably few HP and no hardness—a stiff breeze will destroy this thing, much less an enemy’s concentrated effort. Still, powerful enough to merit a yellow. There’s a particularly fun episode of Side Quest Side Sesh on the Glass Cannon Network in which this wild talent saves a bunch of PCs’ lives.
Water Adaptation: A little cold resistance probably isn’t worth a whole lot, but at least the deep-sea adventuring flavor is good for hydrokineticists.


Cold Adaptation: Not unless you’re in a Reign of Winter campaign, no.
Elemental Limb: Elemental Limb is a bad utility talent (+2 to Strength checks? Really?) but it does open the way for Improved and Greater Elemental Limb, both of which are pretty much required for natural attack builds.
Heat Adaptation: As Cold Adaptation.
Icewalker: Not unless you’re in a Reign of Winter campaign, no.
Water Alteration: Why would you…?


Basic Cryokinesis: Massively overpowered. I really wish third-party publishers hadn’t gotten it into their heads that they could replace basic kinesis talents with this kind of stuff.
Suggested Fix: Take away the bonuses to damage and natural armor, and simply give kineticists create water at will to create an equivalent amount of ice.
Hydro Cutter: More replacements for basic kinesis talents.
Suggested Fix: Same changes as for Wind Blade: eliminate the attack and damage bonuses, and don’t allow Hydro Cutter to be taken in place of Basic Hydrokinesis—it’s a 1st-level utility talent that you have to purchase like anything else. Gaining access to Bloody Infusion is fine, I suppose.
Simple Versatility: Paizo designed kinetic blasts to be used with only the infusions they get on the label; opening up other infusions often has unintended consequences, and is certainly way too powerful for a 1st-level utility talent. See, e.g., Composite Versatility and Improved Composite Versatility, which have burn costs and per-day use limits, for an example of how you might be able to expand access to infusions without breaking the power curve.
Suggested Fix: Pick one infusion belonging to your element’s other simple blast; you can apply that to the first simple blast. None of this BS about applying every infusion you want.


2nd Level


Urgent Restoration: Being able to heal yourself as a swift action is an incredible boost in combat effectiveness, even if you are going to have to take some burn to do it. Much appreciated for tanking builds.


Armor of Nature: I’m a bit conflicted about Armor of Nature. On the one hand, it grants you the equivalent of a chain shirt in terms of AC, but you can’t make it masterwork or mithral to improve its max Dexterity bonus, and you (theoretically) can’t give it enhancement bonuses or special armor enchantments like you could normal armor. On the other hand, it has no armor check penalty, a scaling armor bonus, and no penalty to your speed. If your GM rules that it’s a valid target for spells like magic vestment, I might consider rating it blue.
Improved Elemental Limb: Improved Elemental Limb is actually a great talent, although it takes some thinking to get there. It’s obviously required for natural attack builds, but other kineticists can benefit from it, too. First of all, it lets you threaten in melee range without using Kinetic Blade, but takes up no hands to interfere with gathering power (you can shape it at will with no listed action cost). Second, the elemental limb accepts feats applied to your kinetic blasts, so Spell Penetration, Weapon Focus, Weapon Finesse, etc. should all still be fair game. Third, you get to decide what type of damage your natural attack deals as you manifest it, which can be useful in some instances for punching through DR/bludgeoning, slashing, piercing, etc. Pretty good, on the whole.
Sharding Cover: Sharding Cover is weird in that it’s a gamble that your kinetic cover will be broken at some point, preferably sooner rather than later. This puts you into an awkward position: do you take just Kinetic Cover and Sharding Cover, knowing that your wall is going to be about as flimsy as tissue paper but trusting in the damage and difficult terrain to deal with enemies? Or do you take Improved and Greater Kinetic Cover in order to create truly impassable walls, knowing that they’re unlikely to be damaged enough to create shards? You can do both, of course, but that’s four utility talents spent. Lucky battlefield control is generally so strong.
Veil of Mists: Fun! Disguise self is a good spell, especially when it’s at-will. No need to accept burn for this—just re-up every few minutes.


Angelic Protection: With such a short duration, the only reason I can think of that you would use Angelic Protection is if you wanted to grant an ally a second saving throw against a mind-affecting or compulsion effect in the middle of combat. That’s good enough to rate it a yellow, at least.
Aquatic Adaptation: I mean, you’ve got Waterdancer and Greater Waterdancer coming up within the next few levels, so unless you’re looking for permanent water breathing at this level, there’s not much of a reason to take Aquatic Adaptation.


3rd Level


Chilling Void: A water/void hybrid talent, and one that works quite well if you’re a chaokineticist who’s focused on darkness tactics. The Fortitude save for half damage, spell resistance, and prerequisites prevent this from getting too overpowered, as does your ability to hurt your friends with cold damage. Make sure they have communal resist energy up or something, otherwise you’ll chill them to death.
Improved Kinetic Cover: Improved Kinetic Cover fixes all the problems of Kinetic Cover and then some, primarily by giving it a lot more HP and some hardness. Now enemies will have to actually work to punch through, rather than blowing it over with a light breeze.


Frigid Terrain: Fun for keeping enemies out of your personal space. Pity about the crappy prerequisite, though.
Waterdancer: As a hydrokineticist, swimming quickly is probably one of those things that people will expect you to be good at. So don’t disappoint them! A swim speed isn’t as good as a fly speed, but it’s sure better than nothing, especially in aquatic campaigns like Ruins of Azlant.
Water Manipulator: A little more niche than some of earth’s manipulation abilities, but still plenty good for creative players.


Aquatic Bubble: Most useful if you want to play as an aquatic race (sahuagin, locathah, merfolk, etc.) in a land-going campaign. Who cares that you’re at home in the sea? Now you can take the sea with you!
Automatic Reconstruction: Fast healing this slow is unfortunately not going to cut it in combat, so there’s no point spending burn on Kinetic Cover, Improved Kinetic Cover, etc. This might come into play later on for “healing” structures created with Aether Architect, Kinetic Cityscape, etc., but I’d say those are fairly niche, endgame uses.
Cold Snap: Cold Snap is slightly better than Heat Wave, but only slightly. Given that Cold Adaptation is still an abysmal prerequisite, I probably wouldn’t get Cold Snap, but it’s not awful on its own merits.
Water Adaptation: Probably even less necessary than Water Adaptation, but you never know—maybe your team will really need pressure protection at some point.


Pillar: What can Pillar do that climbing or flight couldn’t? It’s certainly got no combat applications. So why?


4th Level


Watersense: Tremorsense—any extravisual sense, actually—is exceptionally useful to Pathfinder PCs. Assuming you’re spending any time in water, Watersense will come in handy sooner rather than later.


Elemental Hand: I’ve cooled on the hand suite of spells over the years, mostly because the CMB upgrades that grapple, bull rush, etc. can’t be counted on to work reliably against creatures of the same CR. It still works well to keep enemies away from you, slowing them as they try to approach and granting you cover bonuses if they try to attack from range. Might even soak some damage!
Kinetic Shelter: A fun little bungalow for you and your team, although other tactics like rope trick are probably a little less conspicuous.


Healing Burst: The inability to direct where the burn goes and half healing knocks the ability down too far for it to be consistently worth purchasing. It’s way better if you have positive blast, but if you have positive blast, you’re the world’s biggest chump anyway.


Clangorous Shroud: Although it’s nice to be able to get past the nonsensical shield requirement for Quivering Defense, that ultimately wasn’t the problem with the power—the problem is that clangorous just isn’t a great shield enchantment. I’m still no fan.
Ice Sculptor: Not unless you’re in a Reign of Winter campaign, no.
Tundra’s Wrath: Not unless you’re in a Reign of Winter campaign, no.


Liquid Body: Too powerful, too early on. Fluid form is on the Kinetic Invocation list, so Paizo has actually weighed in on when they think this ability should become a utility talent, and what it should cost; they say it’s a 6th-level talent that costs 1 point of burn, and I agree.
Suggested Fix: Move the talent back to 6th level, make it cost 1 point of burn, and don’t give the option for reducing the burn cost for a shorter duration.


5th Level


Shimmering Mirage: Constant blur for the low, low cost of 1 burn. If your Shimmering Mirage negates even one hit, you’ve likely broken even. True seeing will start to be a problem around this level, but not so much that Shimmering Mirage loses its efficacy.
Waterdancer, Greater: Lots of nifty effects rolled into one. You’ll get Greater Waterdancer as a hydrokineticist, and you’ll like it. The mobility you get in water is extraordinarily nice.


Elemental Transmission: It’s kinda like sending, but with a longer duration and the caveat that you can’t send messages across planes. I like it! Every team needs a means of communicating across long distances, and this fits the bill.
Watersense, Greater: I gave Greater Windsight a blue rating, and as a slightly more niche version of Greater Windsight, Greater Watersense is going to get a green. Again, for aquatic campaigns, this is gravy.


Waterdancer, Shared: When the whole party needs to go deep-sea adventuring, you bust this out. Circumstantial, though.


Lingering Energy: I’m not a fan. It costs 1 burn and eats your immediate action, and even if an enemy does proc the blast, they’re likely to avoid all the damage. Nah.
Splash of the Styx: Modify memory isn’t a great spell, especially not at 5th level and not for 1 burn.


6th Level


Greater Kinetic Cover: Battlefield control is one of the most helpful things any PC can offer to a party, and there are few battlefield control tools more effective than a wall of stone. Loads of HP, big area, more flexible positioning than the spell, and it’s not even instantaneous, so you don’t have to worry about leaving a big old wall lying around to obstruct you.
Ice Path: Hydrokineticists don’t get another means of flight, so the air walk granted by Ice Path will have to do. You can’t really hover in place for multiple rounds while using Ice Path, but it’s still good for traversing chasms, getting up onto ledges, surmounting walls, etc.


Greater Elemental Limb: Still as necessary for natural attack builds as ever it was, but now with the ability to manifest extra arms that might help you use magic items, etc.
Suffocate: Suffocate can be an exceptional tool for killing arcane spellcasters before they can do any harm to you; for any other target, the slow speed and concentration will be dealbreakers.


Improved Elemental Hand: For my money, Improved Elemental Hand isn’t really worth it. Only weak casters would have CMD scores low enough to get grappled at this level, and they’ll have freedom of movement running basically permanently, ensuring that they can’t be grappled at all. Thpbbb.
Kinetic Regeneration: This shouldn’t be a major improvement to Kinetic Healer unless you’re regularly losing limbs to attacks. I did not see that coming!
Kinetic Revivification: I’m of the opinion that other classes should generally be handling this kind of thing, but there’s no denying that resurrection is powerful in a pinch. It’s definitely worth considering if you’re playing a kinetic chirurgeon. 


7th Level


Memories On Tap: Drinking people’s memories and abilities, eh? That’s a cool concept. Seems to me that you could just pay people to give up their feats and spells to you, especially since they don’t lose the feat or spell you take. “Hey, can I have Power Attack for the day?” “Sure, that’ll be 50 silver.” That’s as complicated as it should be to open a business in this country.


Elemental Eater: How useful Elemental Eater is will depend almost entirely on on what element you’re using it with, and how prevalent that element is in your campaign. For common elements like fire, this ability could rise to a blue; for rare elements like force (aether), negative energy (void), or sonic (sound), it’ll likely be red.


Elemental Airstrike: It’s a mythic spell. What on earth makes you think players should have this for 1 burn?
Suggested Fix: I dunno. Make it a summon monster clone, but for your element’s elemental type. Don't do elemental bombardment.


8th Level


Cryokinetic Stasis: Temporal stasis is an 8th-level spell that normally has a 5,000 gp material component. Guess what’s got two thumbs and is excited to get that for free? This guy! Sure, it’s got a burn cost, and sure, it requires a touch attack with a Fortitude save to negate, but it makes a perfect magehunting tool—slap it on an arcane caster and watch the popsicling begin.


Kinetic Purification: Heal is a phenomenal spell not only for the amount of HP it heals but also for all the status conditions it cures. This utility talent comes in very late, so what else are you going to spend it on?


Grotesque Armor: “Grotesque” is certainly the right word for it: you turn an enemy into a liquid, suck them into your water vortex, and then force them to take damage from attacks targeting you. How’d you like to be surrounded by a liquid slurry of bugbear? The stench alone makes me pale! If you’re going viscera + water, this is a fitting (i.e., gruesome) capstone, but requires quite a bit of burn to fuel for any length of time.


9th Level


Kinetic Cityscape: Unfortunately for aether, Kinetic Cityscape kinda steals the wind out from Aether Architect’s sails; fortunately for everyone else, this is a really cool capstone ability that can help you found a kineticist city once you’re done with your campaign. There aren’t a lot of combat-effective 9th-level utility talents, so why not?


Tidal Wave: I’m actually not a huge fan of tsunami. A 9th-level spell is essentially the power of a god, placed in your hands. What do you get? 8d6 bludgeoning damage with a Fortitude save for half? Nearly every enemy will pass that. A CMB check? Your CMB is likely to be around +36 (18 level + 10 Constitution modifier + 8 wave) which will succeed less than half the time against CR 18 enemies, the median CMD of which is 49. Sure, you’ll hit literally everyone on the field with your tidal wave, but at this point your infusions and kinetic blasts should be dealing far more damage than tsunami.




Kinetic Invocations


2nd Level


Silent Image: For 0 burn, sure, I’ll take silent image. It’s an incredibly versatile spell that can be used for nearly anything, and the fact that enemies don’t get any save unless they interact with it makes its low spell level and save DC a relatively small problem.


3rd Level


Sleet Storm: Sometimes you don’t need to blast—sometimes you need to control. And when you do, sleet storm is a great spell for control. Larger enemies with no ranks in Acrobatics will have to attempt raw Dexterity checks to move through the sleet storm, and those don’t have great chances of success when enemies’ Dexterity scores are 6-8 and they’re suffering big armor check penalties. Plus, it blocks all sight! Great for buying yourself some breathing room.


Water Breathing: As with ride the waves, Waterdancer and Greater Waterdancer duplicate the effects of water breathing. You can spend burn on water breathing—the hours/level duration isn’t bad—or you can go with the more permanent utility talents.


4th Level


Ride the Waves: Ride the waves gets you a 30-foot swim speed and the ability to breathe water for hours/level at the cost of 1 burn, 1 feat, and 1 utility talent. Waterdancer and Greater Waterdancer get you a 20-foot swim speed and the ability to breathe water permanently for 0 burn and 2 utility talents. I’ll leave it up to you whether that’s worth it!


6th Level


Fluid Form: DR 10/slashing is good for everyone, but an extra 10 feet of reach doesn’t do as much for kineticists as it would for predominantly melee classes; swim speeds are also pretty commonplace for hydrokineticists. Even so, fluid form is an excellent buff with a decent duration, and probably jumps up to purple for Kinetic Blade or Kinetic Whip builds.
🌿 Wood
Wood


        Thanks to some pointed advice from people who have more experience playing with the wood element than I do, I’ve actually developed a respect for it. Look: it’s trying really, really hard, and while it’s still the weakest of the kineticist elements, it’s actually not half-bad, especially once you factor in third-party talents. Assuming that you’re doing so, the best argument for a phytokineticist is as a battlefield controller, using utility talents such as Verdant Overgrowth to create cover for your allies and foul enemies up in vines and grasses. This strategy is a bit more reliant on terrain than I would like, but Verdant Aura can help ensure that you’re always toting around at least some semblance of a forest wherever you go. Phytokineticists can also be built into passable melee combatants with third-party talents, although viscera and earth are too firmly seated on that throne for wood to effectively dislodge; the element makes a halfway decent summoner at high levels, too, with utility talents like Plant Puppet, Horticultural Animation, Wood Soldiers, and Forest Siege allowing you cosplay as that scene from the end of the Battle of Helm’s Deep in Two Towers.
        With that said, wood still has some fairly significant weaknesses. Its substance infusions are intensely lackluster, one of its kinetic blasts doesn’t affect any enemy type except undead, its defense talent is ho-hum, and many of its utility talents won’t work (or won’t work well) outside of a forest environment. For very specific campaigns, though—Ironfang Invasion comes to mind—wood could end up doing surprisingly good work, despite Paizo’s general lack of support for the element.


First-Party: Summoning > Control, Debuffing, Ranged, Recon > Anticaster, Buffing, Melee, Skills
Third-Party: Control, Melee, Summoning > Buffing, Debuffing, Recon > Ranged > Anticaster, Skills






	Mean Rating 
(Paizo Content)
	Mean Rating 
(3PP Content)
	Mean Rating (Combined)
	Form Infusions
	4.0
	—
	4.0
	Substance Infusions
	1.7
	2.0
	1.7
	All Infusions
	2.0
	2.0
	2.0
	Utility Talents
	2.3
	2.7
	2.5
	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Class Skills


        I’ve given Knowledge (Nature) a decent rating elsewhere, so we won’t ding wood just for being a garbage element in other domains. Handle Animal, however, is a garbage skill, especially as wood has no class features that involve interactions with animals.




Defensive Talent


Flesh of Wood: Water knows how to do a defensive talent right: you give a fairly small bonus to start, you scale it up nicely, and you keep the maximum burn cost relatively low. Flesh of Wood is not such a defensive talent. First, enhancement bonuses don’t stack, so this talent is a replacement for an amulet of natural armor, rather than a complement to it. Second, the bonus never scales! Sure, you can spend up to 6 burn on it to make it scale, but compare these two: wood spends 6 burn for an eventual +7 bonus to AC; water spends 3 burn for an eventual +9 bonus to AC. Which seems better to you?




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Bloody Infusion: Several classes of enemy are outright immune to bleed damage, and it can be healed almost by accident if you’re fighting creatures with fast healing, regeneration, or divine magic. Still, Bloody Infusion is cheap, scales nicely with composite blasts, and deals free damage over time.


Persistent Infusion: Probably the coolest part about Persistent Infusion is that it leaves parts of your element lying around that can then be used for utility talents, etc. Kind of like giving yourself an alley-oop in conditions where there’s not a lot of your element present naturally. The caltrop part is nice, but caltrops have an attack bonus of +0, which will fail to hit many 1st-level enemies, let alone higher-level enemies. Probably jumps up to at least a green if you have cheap form infusions like Kinetic Bomb or Improved Kinetic Bomb.


Dazzling Infusion: Wow, what a stinker. Dazzled is easily the least effective status condition in Pathfinder (well, fascinated might give it a run for its money) and with a Will save to negate there’s absolutely no reason you would ever use this when you have other substance infusions available.
Photokinetic Infusion: Positive Blast is incredibly limited in its target selection, so few people will end up selecting Photokinetic Infusion.
Pushing Infusion: CMD scores get incredibly high in later levels, and as a ¾-BAB class with no real reason to invest in feats like Improved Bull Rush, Pushing Infusion is just going to be wasted burn about 90% of the time.


2nd Level


Beacon Infusion: Beacon Infusion is closest to Magnetic Infusion in function, but the two are different enough that they deserve different ratings. Magnetic Infusion affects only metallic weapons, but lasts for an entire round, whereas Beacon Infusion affects all attack rolls, but only lasts for the next attack. Of the two, I prefer Magnetic Infusion, but there’s no denying that Beacon Infusion is great if you want to set an ally up for a big, big hit.
Entangling Infusion: Entangling Infusion is reasonably cheap, has a nice long duration with a great set of debuffs, and targets the weakest save (on average) by CR. For my money, this is one of the best infusions a kineticist has, and a real feather in the cap of the elements that get access to it.


3rd Level


Foxfire Infusion: I gave Foxfire, the utility talent, a yellow rating, but that might honestly be too generous for Foxfire Infusion. For 2 burn with a Will save for partial effect? When enemies can remove the condition as a full-round action and it already doesn’t do too much? Here’s an idea: light enemies on fire if you’re concerned about them going invisible—you’ll be able to hit them just fine with Firesight once they’re lit up.


4th Level


Pacifying Infusion: Either a foe is neutered or else it has to waste a bunch of actions trying to overcome the fact that it’s neutered. Either way, you win.


Toxic Infusion: 3 burn and a Fortitude save to negate, all for the sickened condition? Is this all you conjure, Saruman? Yeah? Okay, then I’m not giving you the pass on Toxic Infusion. Just use Entangling Infusion.


Turning Blast: Unlike Negative Blast, Turning Blast at least functions when paired with Positive Blast. The bad news? It’s still a really bad infusion, expensive, weak, and niche. Still a nope.


5th Level


Improved Bloody Infusion: Given the drastically increased cost, I still don’t think Improved Bloody Infusion is much better than its younger cousin. For blood kineticists, though, it probably comes out to at least a green.
Spore Infusion: The only reason Spore Infusion even makes a yellow is because it gives you bonuses to attack rolls, save DCs, and spell resistance checks while it’s affecting enemies, similar to Burning Infusion. The differences are that Burning Infusion targets Reflex (weak) while Spore Infusion targets Fortitude (strong) and Burning Infusion costs 1 burn (good) while Spore Infusion costs 3 burn (bad). Disease is bad, too. Enemies will either be immune, save successfully, or die before the disease ever takes effect.


6th Level


Slumber Infusion: It’ll render enemies helpless just like Lock Up Infusion, but I’d sooner dole out paralysis on the cheap than sleep for a higher cost. Sleep can be broken pretty easily; paralysis can’t, except with magic.


7th Level


Overload Infusion: A really interesting take on a damage-over-time ability. The burn cost is obviously high at this level, but you’ll have several tiers of infusion specialization to mitigate that. Even so, it will almost certainly be a substance infusion that you can’t really pair with a form infusion due to the sheer cost of such a proposition. Fun to watch enemies melt themselves, though.


Arboreal Infusion: Even worse than Greater Toxic Infusion...somehow. Even if the Constitution damage stacks, you’d have to make an enemy fail a bunch of Fortitude saves and then just stand around getting drained for multiple rounds in order to do significant damage. Play a toxikineticist if you want to deal ability damage.
Toxic Infusion, Greater: Per the bench-pressing resource I use when determining PC benchmarks, you’d need a save DC of 28 to reach a yellow rating on Greater Toxic Infusion. You get base 10, plus 7 for the infusion level, which means you’d need a +11 Constitution bonus to get even close to making this infusion workable—a +14 Constitution bonus, if you want to be safe. You’re nowhere near that.


8th Level


Imprisoning Infusion: Ice Tomb is a fantastic witch hex, and it does just as well for a kineticist as it does for a witch. The Fortitude save to negate is a bit unfortunate, as is the high cost, but save-or-suck effects tend to be like this.




Infusions: Form


1st Level


Energize Weapon: This would be pretty good if you were playing a gestalt kineticist with a more mainstream martial class, as it’s essentially free damage on a full-attack action. Not very much damage, though, and since it locks you out of other form infusions (including Kinetic Blade and Kinetic Whip) I’m having a lot of difficulty seeing why a single-class kineticist would choose to use it.


3rd Level


Impale: In my mind, Impale is superior to Torrent, and doubly so if you attach it to an Ice Blast. The fact that you can use Impale to freaking burst through walls and stab enemies is just so cool. With no save, a good area of effect, and a reasonable cost, I can’t see any reason to ding Impale.


4th Level


Hyper-Dimensional Blast: Ignoring cover and targeting flat-footed AC is awesome, especially when you’re getting Extended Range rolled in for free. Particularly well-suited for physical blasts, which will have a harder time hitting enemies in general.


6th Level


Deadly Earth: Yeah, I like it. Both Cloud and Deadly Earth have very similar feelings: same damage, same radius, same battlefield control tacked on as a bonus. Good stuff, although who knows whether you’ll be fighting ground-based enemies at this level.


Gorgon Gaze: There are a number of tricks enemies can use to avoid your gaze attack, and a Will save to negate what was half damage to begin with ain’t great. Moreover, you can’t do anything but use utility talents while Gorgon Gaze is active unless you want the effect cancelled. I remain (mostly) unimpressed.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Fist of Oak: Forget Brutal Club—Fist of Oak is the way to go if you just want to threaten in melee range and be done with it. If not for viscera’s ascendancy as the best element for natural attack builds, I’d give an earth/wood Fist of Nature build a try, but it’s got too many bogus prereqs.
Wood Healer: Just a way to shoehorn wood into the Kinetic Healer game.


Brutal Club: Shillelagh is a great spell for most classes, but less so for kineticists, who rarely attack with conventional melee or ranged weapons. It gets better at 9th level, when you can at least use the conductive property to deliver kinetic blasts.
Kinetic Cover: Flexible access to total cover is always to be applauded, but the mini-wall has laughably few HP and no hardness—a stiff breeze will destroy this thing, much less an enemy’s concentrated effort. Still, powerful enough to merit a yellow. There’s a particularly fun episode of Side Quest Side Sesh on the Glass Cannon Network in which this wild talent saves a bunch of PCs’ lives.
Metal-Wood: I suppose if you’re intending to use wood shape consistently, Metal-Wood will let you create shapeable metal implements. Is it worth it to you?
Roots: Earth Walk got a yellow, so it seems only fair that Roots get a yellow, too. I’ll reiterate that you’re interested in this talent primarily for its ability to allow you to ignore difficult terrain—nothing else.
Woodland Step: I’ll say this: Woodland Step is perhaps the only means I know of to escape a druid’s wall of thorns unharmed. Usually that spell (or entangle, sickening entanglement, burning entanglement, etc. at lower levels) is game over. Even so, that’s a really, really niche application.


Basic Phytokinesis: So you can tend a garden with your mind. So what? Even among the mediocre Basic Kinesis talents, Basic Phytokinesis manages to distinguish itself by being aggressively mediocre.
Detect Radioactivity: Not necessary unless you’re playing in some post-apocalyptic Fallout-style game.
Elemental Limb: Elemental Limb is a bad utility talent (+2 to Strength checks? Really?) but it does open the way for Improved and Greater Elemental Limb, both of which are pretty much required for natural attack builds.
Hide from Animals: Animals are really the least of your concerns as an adventuring party.
Life After Undeath: It’s certainly not relevant for PCs, and probably not all that relevant even for GMed monsters.
Radioactive Adaptation: As with Detect Radioactivity, saves against radiation are vanishingly rare. Probably not worth a wild talent slot.
Spined Armor: Why would you do this? Kineticists are far more strapped for feats and utility talents than they are for money; just buy some armor spikes if you really want this perk.


Paper Control: Same song as Glass Cannon.
Suggested Fix: No attack or damage bonuses. If people would rather control paper than wood as their basic kinesis talent, that’s their choice, but you shouldn’t get combat bonuses because of it.
Simple Versatility: Paizo designed kinetic blasts to be used with only the infusions they get on the label; opening up other infusions often has unintended consequences, and is certainly way too powerful for a 1st-level utility talent. See, e.g., Composite Versatility and Improved Composite Versatility, which have burn costs and per-day use limits, for an example of how you might be able to expand access to infusions without breaking the power curve.
Suggested Fix: Pick one infusion belonging to your element’s other simple blast; you can apply that to the first simple blast. None of this BS about applying every infusion you want.


2nd Level


Merciful Frondescence: Sure, I’ll happily take bonuses to attack and damage in exchange for dealing nonlethal. This is nothing but benefit in a lot of circumstances.
Urgent Restoration: Being able to heal yourself as a swift action is an incredible boost in combat effectiveness, even if you are going to have to take some burn to do it. Much appreciated for tanking builds.


Armor of Nature: I’m a bit conflicted about Armor of Nature. On the one hand, it grants you the equivalent of a chain shirt in terms of AC, but you can’t make it masterwork or mithral to improve its max Dexterity bonus, and you (theoretically) can’t give it enhancement bonuses or special armor enchantments like you could normal armor. On the other hand, it has no armor check penalty, a scaling armor bonus, and no penalty to your speed. If your GM rules that it’s a valid target for spells like magic vestment, I might consider rating it blue.
Improved Elemental Limb: Improved Elemental Limb is actually a great talent, although it takes some thinking to get there. It’s obviously required for natural attack builds, but other kineticists can benefit from it, too. First of all, it lets you threaten in melee range without using Kinetic Blade, but takes up no hands to interfere with gathering power (you can shape it at will with no listed action cost). Second, the elemental limb accepts feats applied to your kinetic blasts, so Spell Penetration, Weapon Focus, Weapon Finesse, etc. should all still be fair game. Third, you get to decide what type of damage your natural attack deals as you manifest it, which can be useful in some instances for punching through DR/bludgeoning, slashing, piercing, etc. Pretty good, on the whole.
Sharding Cover: Sharding Cover is weird in that it’s a gamble that your kinetic cover will be broken at some point, preferably sooner rather than later.
