This puts you into an awkward position: do you take just Kinetic Cover and Sharding Cover, knowing that your wall is going to be about as flimsy as tissue paper but trusting in the damage and difficult terrain to deal with enemies? Or do you take Improved and Greater Kinetic Cover in order to create truly impassable walls, knowing that they’re unlikely to be damaged enough to create shards? You can do both, of course, but that’s four utility talents spent. Lucky battlefield control is generally so strong.
Wall Walker: Spider climb is a good spell, so it should come as no surprise that Wall Walker is also rated fairly highly. If you want mobility and don’t want to spend the talents on flight, this should be your purchase.
Wood in the Blood: Healing from ability damage faster is especially nice, and I won’t say no to healing from ability drain, either. Not a top priority, but still very choice.


Angelic Protection: With such a short duration, the only reason I can think of that you would use Angelic Protection is if you wanted to grant an ally a second saving throw against a mind-affecting or compulsion effect in the middle of combat. That’s good enough to rate it a yellow, at least.
Fist of Nature: Fist of Nature is a fantastic utility talent, but man, are the prereqs steep. You need earth or wood as your primary element, then expanded element to get the other, then Expanded Defense to get the other defense talent, then Fist of Granite and Fist of Oak (which, by the way, are totally redundant), then Fist of Nature. Woof. Natural attack builds will still want it, since it builds on Elemental Limb, but man, talk about a drag.
Foxfire: I like faerie fire as much as the next Pathfinder guide writer, but because this utility talent affects only a single target and can be removed with a full-round action, the rating is getting knocked down pretty dramatically. Wouldn’t be my first pick.
Verdant Aura: Verdant Aura really doesn’t do much on its own—it’s really more of a combo starter for Verdant Overgrowth, Briarthorn Overgrowth, and Grasping Overgrowth. Not like Woodland Step and Greater Woodland Step do much. One interesting tidbit is that you can use this to give an allied ranger their bonuses from favored terrain (forest) more or less permanently, which they should be very interested in.


Improved Spined Armor: Spined Armor is weak, and the Improved version change what needs changing. Blech.
Merciful Foliage: Let someone else handle nonlethal damage. kineticists are all about nuking enemies until they explode.


3rd Level


Improved Kinetic Cover: Improved Kinetic Cover fixes all the problems of Kinetic Cover and then some, primarily by giving it a lot more HP and some hardness. Now enemies will have to actually work to punch through, rather than blowing it over with a light breeze.
Warp Wood: Finally, something good! Warp wood is a great spell for destroying enemies’ equipment—bows, spears, axes, or anything else that’s hafted will do. Great that it costs no burn, too.


Hostile Flora: Hostile Flora lets you treat trees and plants as though they were the origins of your kinetic blasts, which can make you an absolute bear to find in hit-and-run guerrilla tactics. Good for maneuvering around total cover, too. I could easily see this being a fun utility talent to take into a campaign like Ironfang Invasion.


Automatic Reconstruction: Fast healing this slow is unfortunately not going to cut it in combat, so there’s no point spending burn on Kinetic Cover, Improved Kinetic Cover, etc. This might come into play later on for “healing” structures created with Aether Architect, Kinetic Cityscape, etc., but I’d say those are fairly niche, endgame uses.
Brachiation: I’m not sure why Brachiation had to wait until 3rd level while Earth Climb got to join the party at 2nd, but we’ll deal with it. Climb speeds are always pretty circumstantial, especially when Wings of Air and fly become major players at 3rd level.


Desolate: So...let me get this straight: you’re going to spend 1 point of burn to make it harder to grow crops in a 10 ft. x 10 ft. square? Yeah, no.
Fungal Infestation: Fungal Infestation mimics garden of peril, which is ultimately a fairly tame debuff spell. With a Fortitude save to negate, a low talent level, and the poison descriptor, there’s little chance that Fungal Infestation will make it into the higher levels unscathed. It gets better with Fungal Infection, but you have to ask whether it’s worth your burn to use this talent consistently.
Greater Detect Radioactivity: Still as unnecessary as Detect Radioactivity.
Pillar: What can Pillar do that climbing or flight couldn’t? It’s certainly got no combat applications. So why?
Thorn Flesh: Jagged Flesh is one of earth’s worst utility talents. Thorn Flesh isn’t any better.


4th Level


Verdant Overgrowth: Entangle is a great spell, so getting it at will is a nice pick-up. It’s unclear to me whether or not you can see out of your own patch of overgrown weeds without greensight; if not, you’ll need to grab Greensight in order to do so.
Vital Fibers: Why bother building for natural attacks when you can steal other people’s limbs? This is a pretty wacky ability, but extra limbs always have great uses, so I can’t rate it poorly. The burn cost will keep you from becoming a centaur for longer than an afternoon, at least.


Elemental Hand: I’ve cooled on the hand suite of spells over the years, mostly because the CMB upgrades that grapple, bull rush, etc. can’t be counted on to work reliably against creatures of the same CR. It still works well to keep enemies away from you, slowing them as they try to approach and granting you cover bonuses if they try to attack from range. Might even soak some damage!
Herbal Antivenom: Quite nice, actually. Poison effects (whether actual poisons or just poison effects, such as stinking cloud) are shockingly common in Pathfinder, so a permanent +5 alchemical bonus to save against them is great, even if your Fortitude save should be so high that you just start laughing anytime a monster tries to hit you with a poison. You know what’s greater than a +5 for yourself? Being able to offer that bonus to your wimpier colleagues at no cost. Invest in the Heal skill and get cracking.
Kinetic Shelter: A fun little bungalow for you and your team, although other tactics like rope trick are probably a little less conspicuous.
Wood Prison: It’s great for forcing large enemies with terrible Reflex saves to waste actions repeatedly destroying your cages, but this thing is suuuper flimsy. Starts to taper a bit as enemy saves get better and your DC stays static.
Wood Shape: Versatile enough that it earns a green rating. Wood shape is a great spell for all-purpose adventuring, albeit a little less powerful than stone shape.


Healing Burst: The inability to direct where the burn goes and half healing knocks the ability down too far for it to be consistently worth purchasing. It’s way better if you have positive blast, but if you have positive blast, you’re the world’s biggest chump anyway.
Hostile Woodwork: It’s neat, but you have to be aware that at some point all equipment you target will be magical, and magical items use their wearer’s saves. Fortitude negates starts to look a little more daunting in that light, no?
Plant Disguise: I mean, I guess…? You could perform long-term observations of enemies, stage ambushes, hide from pursuit, etc., but turning into a bush isn’t going to solve many problems.


Greensight: Greensight is extraordinarily niche. Even constant, this is still not really worth your investment.
Remove Radioactivity: There’s only a minuscule chance that you’ll encounter radiation once in your campaign, much less enough times that you need a wild talent for it.
Toxic Barbs: Weak effect, poison tag, Fortitude negates. What else needs to be said?


5th Level


Briarthorn Overgrowth: Alright, now we’re talking. If enemies try to stay outside your verdant aura, they can’t see you and your teammates, but if they step in, they can get entangled, grappled, and continually beaten to death by plants. Plus your allies are immune and can finally see through the foliage!
Continuous Regrowth: Fast healing is fast healing, and Flesh of Wood’s relatively high burn cost actually works to your advantage here, lowering your effective ceiling through unhealable nonlethal damage and granting you faster healing.
Wild Growth: In the right circumstances—the right circumstances being anywhere there are both trees and brush available—plant growth is a ridiculously good battlefield control spell. Lots of enemies will fly at this point, but those that can’t will be forced to play your game. It’s just a shame that you never get any of the spells that would allow you to benefit from the increased save DC.


Plant Puppet: I’d normally begiving Aether Puppet a green rating, but plants are often a bit more difficult to find in dungeons, castles, etc. Seems like a yellow to me, but it might rate green if you’re using Verdant Aura consistently.
Woodland Step, Greater: I guess for a forest campaign like Ironfang Invasion?


Thorn Wall: Wall of thorns is widely acknowledged to be one of if not the most powerful battlefield control spells in the game, but the balance here is off. At-will spell-like abilities that cost 0 burn are usually given at Spell Level + 2, whereas at-will spell-like abilities that cost 1 burn are usually given at Spell Level + 0.
Suggested Fix: Even though wood desperately needs a win, I have to nerf this in good conscience. Either keep it at 5th level and give it a burn cost of one, or bump it to 7th level and keep the burn cost at zero.


6th Level


Arboreal Teleport: Functionally unlimited teleportation within forests. Since you can’t take friends and it’s a little slower than normal teleportation, I’m fine with leaving this as-is.
Grasping Overgrowth: More battlefield control in the “Oh No No” zone of your Verdant Aura. I’m lovin’ it.
Greater Kinetic Cover: Battlefield control is one of the most helpful things any PC can offer to a party, and there are few battlefield control tools more effective than a wall of stone. Loads of HP, big area, more flexible positioning than the spell, and it’s not even instantaneous, so you don’t have to worry about leaving a big old wall lying around to obstruct you.


Antiundeath Shell: Antiundeath Shell can’t quite keep up with antilife shell, but that’s only because undead are a little less common than living creatures.
Greater Elemental Limb: Still as necessary for natural attack builds as ever it was, but now with the ability to manifest extra arms that might help you use magic items, etc.
Green Tongue: I gave Earth Tongue a green rating, so it seems only fair to offer Green Tongue the same rating. This isn’t a spell that I’d necessarily seek out, but as a constant ability that’s always operating at no cost, it’s quite nice.


Improved Elemental Hand: For my money, Improved Elemental Hand isn’t really worth it. Only weak casters would have CMD scores low enough to get grappled at this level, and they’ll have freedom of movement running basically permanently, ensuring that they can’t be grappled at all. Thpbbb.
Kinetic Revivification: I’m of the opinion that other classes should generally be handling this kind of thing, but there’s no denying that resurrection is powerful in a pinch. It’s definitely worth considering if you’re playing a kinetic chirurgeon. 


7th Level


Rapid Regrowth: Yup, that’s fun. Regeneration is an awesome universal monster ability that basically won’t let you die unless you’re hit with cold or fire damage, and the increase on your HP cap will let you heal more effectively than ever.


Green Tongue, Greater: As with speak with plants, commune with nature isn’t necessarily a spell I’d be going to great lengths to acquire. For 0 burn, though? Sure, why not. Not like wood is overflowing with other excellent utility talents.
Horticultural Animation: You could theoretically create your own trees to animate with Arboreal Infusion, but who’s got the time for that? Just take this utility talent if you envision yourself adventuring in forests a lot, and don’t if you can’t.
Tree Step: Tree step is a pretty good teleportation spell if you’re spending lots of time in forests or jungles, and the fact that you get it once per day for free (if I’m reading the text of the ability correctly, that is) makes it an even better investment. The only unfortunate part is that both Woodland Step and Greater Woodland Step are extremely mediocre utility talents. (Note: this talent is totally outmoded by Arboreal Teleport if you’re playing with third-party content.)


Elemental Eater: How useful Elemental Eater is will depend almost entirely on on what element you’re using it with, and how prevalent that element is in your campaign. For common elements like fire, this ability could rise to a blue; for rare elements like force (aether), negative energy (void), or sonic (sound), it’ll likely be red.


Fist of Eternity: Let’s go over all the prerequisites that you need to hit before you qualify to take this talent, shall we? Fist of Eternity specifies Fist of Nature and Hydraulic Fist as its formal prerequisites. Hydraulic Fist requires two utility talents: Subdermal Plating and Hard Knuckle. Fist of Nature also requires two: Fist of Oak and Fist of Granite. Between those two, you’re already looking at six utility talents, most of which are completely redundant. Then you’ve got to get two instances of Expanded Defense—and since you can’t take the utility talents in your third and final expanded element until at least 14th level, you probably won’t qualify to take Fist of Eternity until...16th? 17th level? And that’s when you start to ask: Is eight utility talents really worth what I’m getting here? Sure, two slams at 2d8/x3 are nice, but are they eight utility talents nice? I’ll leave that judgment to you, but my inclination is to respond in the negative.
Greater Remove Radioactivity: There’s only a minuscule chance that you’ll encounter radiation once in your campaign, much less enough times that you need a wild talent for it.


8th Level


Wood Soldiers: An advanced wood golem is a CR 7 creature, which are really, really going to struggle when you’re 16th level. It’s cool that they get these fun little flavor abilities, but that’s not really enough to make them useful in combat. Use them as portable sacks of HP, flanking, and soft cover, and ignore their attacks to the extent that you’re able.


9th Level


Forest Siege: You’re getting a 9th-level spell, so there’s really no reason to complain. The 10-minute casting time is really the only fly in Forest Siege’s ointment, as it means that you can’t summon trees to aid you in battle without considerable ramp-up time. Once you get there, though...watch out. Heavy catapults deal 8d6 damage per boulder, and you can summon a maximum of 6 of them, all firing at once. They can’t hurl a 90-kg projectile over 300 m, of course, like another superior siege engine I could name, but we can’t get everything we want.
Kinetic Cityscape: Unfortunately for aether, Kinetic Cityscape kinda steals the wind out from Aether Architect’s sails; fortunately for everyone else, this is a really cool capstone ability that can help you found a kineticist city once you’re done with your campaign. There aren’t a lot of combat-effective 9th-level utility talents, so why not?


Clicker: Attribute bleed on all your mental scores is indeed something to be frightened of, but the Fortitude save to negate and the fact that it’s a disease—a poison? One of the two, anyway—prevents it from being too threatening at 18th-20th level.




Kinetic Invocations


1st Level


Goodberry: As a 1st-level spell, goodberry is pretty good. As a utility talent costing 1 burn, it’s terrible. The healing never scales, but the amount of nonlethal damage you take from burn does. You’re healing an average of 5 points of damage each time you cast goodberry, but how much nonlethal damage will you take? 10 points? 15 points? Just get Kinetic Healer and do things that way.


2nd Level


Lesser Restoration: Strictly inferior to Kinetic Restoration, which can be cast as a standard action for in-combat healing where lesser restoration takes 3 rounds. And you can’t even avoid the material component cost with a Kinetic Invocation!


3rd Level


Ward of the Season: I certainly wouldn’t recommend normal spellcasters blow a 3rd-level spell on ward of the season, but it’s not awful as a utility talent. Yellow, maybe?


4th Level


Grove of Respite: You’d probably want to trade in grove of respite for fairy ring retreat once you eventually get that spell, but that will take a while. It’s a good one to cast at the end of the day when you still have a few burn remaining.


Command Plants: Be honest: how often do you fight plant creatures? Once per book of a Paizo AP? This isn’t worth your investment.


7th Level


Fairy Ring Retreat: Your very own demiplane! Definitely not something most kineticists can do. Lovely.
❄️ Elements (3PP)


🔆 Light
[3PP] Light


        I’m a really big fan of the light element, which is a new element introduced in the Kineticists of Porphyra supplements by Purple Duck Games. It’s not super great at damage output, with only a few infusions that tend more toward team buffing or enemy debuffing than straight damage, but what it lacks in straight DPR it more than makes up for in utility, stealth, and general trickery. In fact, I’d go so far as to say that light might even beat aether in utility, although the contest is very close. At any rate, photokineticists can be built as debuffers (Blinding Flare/Greater, Colorburst Infusion), battlefield controllers (Kinetic Cover/Improved/Greater, Sharding Cover, Explosive Cover), illusionists (Phantom Image, Hard Light Illusions, Additional Illusion, Intelligent Illusion, Photo Doppelganger), scouts or infiltrators (Optical Camouflage/Greater, Deceptive Image, Traceless Light, Photographic Transference), or even Dimensional Agility-style teleporting natural attackers. There’s a lot to be fond of here, and I don’t doubt you’ll see a few photokineticists in the Builds section.


Third-Party: Recon, Summoning > Anticaster, Buffing, Control, Debuffing, Ranged, Skills > Melee






	Mean Rating 
(Paizo Content)
	Mean Rating 
(3PP Content)
	Mean Rating (Combined)
	Form Infusions
	4.0
	—
	4.0
	Substance Infusions
	1.3
	3.1
	2.5
	All Infusions
	2.2
	3.1
	2.7
	Utility Talents
	—
	3.1
	3.1
	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Class Skills


Disguise is actually pretty serviceable on a photokineticist, because they’re meant to be tricky illusionists. Knowledge (Nature) is also a decent Knowledge skill used for identifying animals, fey, etc.




Defensive Talent


Illusory Duplicates: Mirror image is a mainstay of spellcasters’ defensive lineups for a reason: it’s an effective all-or-nothing damage soak. Doesn’t matter whether the opponent would have done 1000 damage or 1 damage: if they hit an image, you’re safe. Anyway, your images regenerate at a decent clip, and they’ll be effective at keeping you safe from stray hits. Photokineticists should be difficult to pin down to hit at all, so trust in your defensive talent.




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Illuminating Infusion: Faerie fire is a great spell, perfect for dealing with stealthy, invisible, or heavily concealed enemies. As a cheap substance infusion in an element that’s not overly crowded with good infusions (to put it charitably) Illuminating Infusion should be on every photokineticist’s shopping list.


Dazzling Infusion: Wow, what a stinker. Dazzled is easily the least effective status condition in Pathfinder (well, fascinated might give it a run for its money) and with a Will save to negate there’s absolutely no reason you would ever use this when you have Burning Infusion available for the same cost.
Dazzling Infusion: For some reason, third-party content rewrote Dazzling Infusion. It’s still terrible, but this time a Fortitude save negates instead of a Will save.
Photokinetic Infusion: Positive Blast is incredibly limited in its target selection, so few people will end up selecting Photokinetic Infusion.


2nd Level


Beacon Infusion: Beacon Infusion is closest to Magnetic Infusion in function, but the two are different enough that they deserve different ratings. Magnetic Infusion affects only metallic weapons, but lasts for an entire round, whereas Beacon Infusion affects all attack rolls, but only lasts for the next attack. Of the two, I prefer Magnetic Infusion, but there’s no denying that Beacon Infusion is great if you want to set an ally up for a big, big hit.


3rd Level


Daybreak Infusion: Supernatural darkness is always a bummer to deal with, and Daybreak Infusion takes care of it pretty handily. You’ll fail your dispel checks sometimes, of course, but that’s just part and parcel of using the equivalent of dispel magic.


4th Level


Flash Infusion: Blinded is a fantastic status condition in Pathfinder, so don’t think that this infusion is getting dinged because of its effect. But with a Will save to negate and a burn cost of 3, it’s a very expensive and chancy maneuver to pull. Probably the best argument for it is against huge, stupid bruisers who you can hit easily with Fire Blast and keep blinded for extended lengths of time. Somewhat niche, though.


5th Level


Obfuscating Infusion: Concealment imposes a 20% miss chance on enemies, which isn’t so big that I’m leaping up and down for joy—especially not at the price point of 3 burn. It’s fun to toss on enemies that use sneak attack damage and watch them gnash their teeth in frustration, though.


6th Level


Brilliant Infusion: Wow. Way, way too expensive for a one-round effect. No, no, no. If nobody in your group has the ability to deal with supernatural darkness by 12th level, you’re in trouble anyway. This isn’t the right tool for that job.


7th Level


Overload Infusion: A really interesting take on a damage-over-time ability. The burn cost is obviously high at this level, but you’ll have several tiers of infusion specialization to mitigate that. Even so, it will almost certainly be a substance infusion that you can’t really pair with a form infusion due to the sheer cost of such a proposition. Fun to watch enemies melt themselves, though.


8th Level


Colorburst Infusion: Colorburst Infusion comes online late—very late—but it’s hard to overstate the power of prismatic spray when it’s being applied to every enemy hit in an AoE light blast. You’ve got energy damage, death poisons, flesh to stone, insanity, and more. Definitely a worthy capstone infusion for light.




Infusions: Form


3rd Level


Eruption: A 10-foot radius is surprisingly big, and the fact that you can also blow up flying creatures with Eruption (provided they’re not too high) is only icing on the cake. Eruption compares very favorably with Explosion, a late-game form infusion for light; in addition to supporting more composite blasts, Eruption costs half the burn that Explosion does, making it an excellent bargain for targeted AoE damage.


7th Level


Explosion: Explosion is actually not that great! On the one hand, sure, it’s fantastic to be able to shoot off a variably sized AoE blast that deals full damage to anything within 120 feet, but let’s take a look at the negatives, too: Explosion is monstrously expensive at 4 burn, can’t hit fliers and earth-bound enemies at the same time, and doesn’t deal any damage above and beyond what Eruption would have given you at half the cost. Is the ability to shape the blast radius to 5, 15, or 20 feet really worth 2 extra points of burn? To be clear, I think that any dedicated light build should still get this infusion, as there aren’t many other ways to shape light’s blasts; the vast majority of the time, however, Eruption makes more sense from a cost-benefit standpoint.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Phantom Image: Illusions are very powerful in the right hands, especially since enemies don’t receive a Will save to disbelieve most illusions unless they interact with them directly. Hide your team within an illusion of a boulder, dig a pit and make it look like there’s flat earth over the top, fake enemies out by making it look as though you cast wall of force to cover your retreat...there are a lot of possibilities here. Pairing Phantom Image with the sound element makes it all the better, as you can add auditory components to your illusions.


Basic Photokinesis: Basic kinesis talents should be thought of as being about on par with cantrips: not very strong and mostly good as party tricks. A rare few (Basic Telekinesis comes to mind) actually continue to have utility throughout the game, and Basic Photokinesis is one such talent. Light is a perennially good cantrip, and changing the color of something could have a host of intrigue uses.
Deceptive Image: Deceptive Image is one of the reasons why the Disguise skill is actually pretty good in this element. Thanks to at-will disguise self, you can blend into your surroundings or assume different identities at the drop of a hat. I gave Veil of Mists a green, and that seems fair here, too.
Traceless Light: I wish that photokineticists got a way to make their invisibility from Optical Camouflage last a little longer, like aetherkineticists get with Aetheric Sniper, but this ain’t bad, either. Remember that your gather power class feature creates an extremely loud, visible display of elemental power within 20 feet, so you’re not going to be stealthy if you have to gather power at all (or unless you take the Stealth Gathering and Improved Stealth Gathering feats). With a little effort, though, it can be very difficult for enemies to determine whether they’re under attack or not.


Kinetic Cover: Flexible access to total cover is always to be applauded, but the mini-wall has laughably few HP and no hardness—a stiff breeze will destroy this thing, much less an enemy’s concentrated effort. Still, powerful enough to merit a yellow. There’s a particularly fun episode of Side Quest Side Sesh on the Glass Cannon Network in which this wild talent saves a bunch of PCs’ lives.


Elemental Limb: Elemental Limb is a bad utility talent (+2 to Strength checks? Really?) but it does open the way for Improved and Greater Elemental Limb, both of which are pretty much required for natural attack builds.
Life After Undeath: It’s certainly not relevant for PCs, and probably not all that relevant even for GMed monsters.
Strobe Lights: Fascinated is a bad condition that’s basically impossible to use effectively in combat: “Any potential threat, such as a hostile creature approaching, allows the fascinated creature a new saving throw against the fascinating effect. Any obvious threat, such as someone drawing a weapon, casting a spell, or aiming a ranged weapon at the fascinated creature, automatically breaks the effect. A fascinated creature’s ally may shake it free of the spell as a standard action.” Especially with the low level of this utility talent, any enemy in later levels will make its save without any effort at all.


2nd Level


Blinding Flare: This utility talent will go over like gangbusters in the early game, but the lack of a scaling save DC will make it drop off in effectiveness as enemies get better and better Fortitude saves. Good thing you’ve got a Greater version to boost the DC a bit!


Crystal Skin: If you’re coming at Crystal Skin from the photokinesis side of things, you do actually have to select earth as your expanded element—no way around it. It’s still a good talent, and for the price of 1 burn will nearly entirely negate the need for a ring of protection. Not that kineticists need more loose change, but…
Eyes of Light: Eyes of the eagle are an extremely common eye slot wondrous item, because, well, Perception is the God Skill in Pathfinder 1e. Unless you’re a human, you generally won’t need low-light vision, but a +5 untyped bonus to Perception is just what the doctor ordered, especially as that bonus type stacks with absolutely anything else.
Improved Elemental Limb: Improved Elemental Limb is actually a great talent, although it takes some thinking to get there. It’s obviously required for natural attack builds, but other kineticists can benefit from it, too. First of all, it lets you threaten in melee range without using Kinetic Blade, but takes up no hands to interfere with gathering power (you can shape it at will with no listed action cost). Second, the elemental limb accepts feats applied to your kinetic blasts, so Spell Penetration, Weapon Focus, Weapon Finesse, etc. should all still be fair game. Third, you get to decide what type of damage your natural attack deals as you manifest it, which can be useful in some instances for punching through DR/bludgeoning, slashing, piercing, etc. Pretty good, on the whole.
Perfect Disguise: Assumed likeness can be a bit dicey, since targets who pass their saves don’t assume you’re anyone. It’s an easy way to get big, big bonuses on your Charisma-based skill checks, though, especially if you opt for the one-burn version of Perfect Disguise.
Photographic Transference: Ooooh, now that’s fun. An enduring problem of mundane scouting is that if you get caught out in front of the party and ambushed, who’s going to help you? Turning your illusions into scouts is fantastic for these purposes, especially since you can disguise an illusion much more easily than you can disguise yourself. Walk a “guard” along the parapet, checking out the castle’s defenses; walk a local “animal” through the forest; walk a noble’s “trusted servant” through the halls of their manor. It’s all doable! Do note that you’re going to need auditory components if you want to get really good at intrigue uses of Photographic Transference, however, and that will require taking sound as your expanded element.
Sharding Cover: Sharding Cover is weird in that it’s a gamble that your kinetic cover will be broken at some point, preferably sooner rather than later. This puts you into an awkward position: do you take just Kinetic Cover and Sharding Cover, knowing that your wall is going to be about as flimsy as tissue paper but trusting in the damage and difficult terrain to deal with enemies? Or do you take Improved and Greater Kinetic Cover in order to create truly impassable walls, knowing that they’re unlikely to be damaged enough to create shards? You can do both, of course, but that’s four utility talents spent. Lucky battlefield control is generally so strong.


3rd Level


Optical Camouflage: At-will invisibility will certainly change how you play Pathfinder. There are pros and cons between Telekinetic Invisibility and Optical Camouflage, but they’re both element-defining utility talents that everybody must take. An easy purple.


Improved Kinetic Cover: Improved Kinetic Cover fixes all the problems of Kinetic Cover and then some, primarily by giving it a lot more HP and some hardness. Now enemies will have to actually work to punch through, rather than blowing it over with a light breeze.
Shadowbend: I’m going to link you to a resource that my friend Zach put together, Artemis, to help you figure out which spells are eligible for Shadowbend—there should be 32 total spells between 1st and 9th level that fit the bill. Highlights from 1st through 3rd levels are haunting mists, shadow anchor, twilight haze, and shadow enchantment.


Explosive Cover: Between Sharding Cover, Explosive Cover, Kinetic Cover, and Improved Kinetic Cover, you can make life miserable for enemies who are trying to bust through your walls of light. If they fail to destroy the cover, they’ve wasted actions trying; if they succeed at destroying the cover, they take damage and create difficult terrain that continues to proc damage over time. Not great options. You could easily make an entire playstyle out of just the four options above, and in fact I think I will in the builds section!


Automatic Reconstruction: Fast healing this slow is unfortunately not going to cut it in combat, so there’s no point spending burn on Kinetic Cover, Improved Kinetic Cover, etc. This might come into play later on for “healing” structures created with Aether Architect, Kinetic Cityscape, etc., but I’d say those are fairly niche, endgame uses.
Explosive Illusion: I don’t know that making your illusions blow up is quite in keeping with the “subtlety is king” playstyle of the photokineticist. The damage isn’t super great, and there’s no chance to see whether your opponent fails their Will save to disbelieve if you keep blowing up your illusions.
Illusion Alteration: Altering an illusion implies that it’s not one you created, which makes this wild talent some kind of anticaster countermeasure. That’s likely to be fairly limited in scope.


Hypnotic Pattern: Command usually lasts for only one round, so I’m confused about how this talent lasts for rounds per level. Is it really meant to act as greater command? The trouble with Hypnotic Pattern is that, again, it’s absurdly easy for enemies to be broken out of the fascinated condition, and you only get to issue commands if they’re fascinated. Very little combat utility.


4th Level


Additional Illusion: Whether you’re trying to bulk up the number of allies you appear to have in a negotiation, scout two locations at once using Photographic Transference, blow up multiple foes with Explosive Illusion, etc., you’ll need Additional Illusion. It’s a must-have for anyone going down the illusionist road with a photokineticist.
Psychosomatic Malady: There are multiple rounds of saves involved in most of these applications, but the fact that you can use one spell to 1) impart huge penalties to attack rolls, saves, and skill checks, 2) deal ability damage to any physical attribute in line with an injury poison, or 3) deal slow Constitution damage in line with an ingested poison all adds up to a great utility talent. It gets even better with Psychosomatic Calamity, too.


Elemental Hand: I’ve cooled on the hand suite of spells over the years, mostly because the CMB upgrades that grapple, bull rush, etc. can’t be counted on to work reliably against creatures of the same CR. It still works well to keep enemies away from you, slowing them as they try to approach and granting you cover bonuses if they try to attack from range. Might even soak some damage!
Kinetic Shelter: A fun little bungalow for you and your team, although other tactics like rope trick are probably a little less conspicuous.


Hard Light Illusions: One of the vulnerabilities of the illusionist playstyle is that many enemies with supernatural perceptual modes (blindsight, tremorsense, true seeing, etc.) simply aren’t convinced by illusions. No saves, nothing: for all intents and purposes, enemies with those perceptual modes can simply ignore illusions entirely. Not. Any. More. Hard Light Illusions is a very, very necessary talent for illusionists playing at high levels. It’s perhaps a bit too early for it at 8th level, when you can first take it, but it’ll certainly be relevant by 10th or 12th level.


5th Level


Light Speed Travel: At-will, medium-range teleportation certainly qualifies as an element-defining ability. Every photokineticist can and should purchase this utility talent.


Greater Blinding Flare: A very worthy upgrade to Blinding Flare. In addition to the higher save DC from the increased utility talent level, you also get better range and better duration on the blindness. Don’t bother making blindness permanent unless you’re a GM—for PCs, the rest of an enemy’s life from the time is usually less than a minute, so you’d just be wasting burn.


Intelligent Illusion: Persistent images are more self-controlled, so you can “set them and forget them” more easily than silent images, which require concentration. It’s not a world-changing upgrade, but it is very solidly nice to have.


6th Level


Greater Kinetic Cover: Battlefield control is one of the most helpful things any PC can offer to a party, and there are few battlefield control tools more effective than a wall of stone. Loads of HP, big area, more flexible positioning than the spell, and it’s not even instantaneous, so you don’t have to worry about leaving a big old wall lying around to obstruct you.
Improved Shadowbend: I’m going to link you to a resource that my friend Zach put together, Artemis, to help you figure out which spells are eligible for Shadowbend—there should be 32 total spells between 1st and 9th level that fit the bill. Highlights from 4th through 6th levels are shadow jaunt, shadow conjuration, cloak of shadows, shadow endurance, shadow evocation, baleful shadow transmutation, greater shadow enchantment, shadow transmutation, and shadow walk.
Long-Distance Travel: Long-distance teleportation is universally useful, and this talent is no exception.


Greater Elemental Limb: Still as necessary for natural attack builds as ever it was, but now with the ability to manifest extra arms that might help you use magic items, etc.
Visual Hallucination: Great for intrigue applications, and as always, better if your expanded element is sound. Illusionists basically have to expand into sound if they want to compete effectively.


Dimensional Traveler: Very narrowly useful for Kinetic Blade, Kinetic Whip, Visceral Whip, and natural attack builds, for whom Dimensional Traveler basically acts as pounce. If you’re a run-of-the-mill ranged kineticist using a kinetic blast once a round, Dimensional Traveler won’t do a thing for you.
Explosive Illusion: A decent upgrade for illusionists, but not at all necessary for other builds.
Improved Elemental Hand: For my money, Improved Elemental Hand isn’t really worth it. Only weak casters would have CMD scores low enough to get grappled at this level, and they’ll have freedom of movement running basically permanently, ensuring that they can’t be grappled at all. Thpbbb.


7th Level


Greater Optical Camouflage: Between at-will invisibility and at-will greater invisibility, I wouldn’t be surprised if your party just kinda forgets that you exist after a while. Another element-defining talent.


Psychosomatic Calamity: Big, brutal AoE debuffs are just what I like about neurokineticists, and porting it over to photokineticists is great.


Improved Dimensional Traveler: Even for Kinetic Blade, Kinetic Whip, Visceral Whip, and natural attack builds, Dimensional Maneuvers and Dimensional Savant are not as necessary as Dimensional Assault and Dimensional Dervish. Can be nice if you’re using whips to trip or reposition foes, flank with yourself, etc., though.


8th Level


Glorious Bastion: So Glorious Bastion is...unusual. Obviously prismatic wall is an enormously powerful spell, and being surrounded by it at all times is a pretty hard counter to a lot of things. A few caveats apply, however: first, both your Shroud of Water and Illusory Duplicate talents must be active. Shroud is active more or less all the time, but Illusory Duplicates can be destroyed or suppressed while you’re using Optical Camouflage. Second, enemies touching the wall automatically dispel the outermost layer. Sure, they’ll take some hefty damage or debuffs in the process, but your protections will be stripped away automatically. Third, accepting any burn or using any kinetic blast strips away a layer. Perhaps the most onerous requirement of all, as it basically locks you into using zero-burn utility talents while under the effects of Glorious Bastion. It’s powerful, to be sure, but I don’t think it merits a purple rating because of its limitations.


9th Level


Greater Shadowbend: I’m going to link you to a resource that my friend Zach put together, Artemis, to help you figure out which spells are eligible for Shadowbend—there should be 32 total spells between 1st and 9th level that fit the bill. Highlights from 7th through 9th levels are greater shadow evocation, greater shadow transmutation, and shades.
Kinetic Cityscape: Unfortunately for aether, Kinetic Cityscape kinda steals the wind out from Aether Architect’s sails; fortunately for everyone else, this is a really cool capstone ability that can help you found a kineticist city once you’re done with your campaign. There aren’t a lot of combat-effective 9th-level utility talents, so why not?


Photo Doppelganger: Being able to swap places with your clone is a nifty trick, but I’m overall not super excited about this as a capstone utility talent, especially since it can be destroyed easily and you’ll take burn whenever it is.
⚙️ Machine
[3PP] Machine


        Did I say that time was the most overpowered of all the third-party elements? Slip of the tongue. Machine probably is. Where time gives you laughably powerful debuffs (and some powerful buffs), though, machine’s specialty is in turning you, the player, into a one-person killing machine. Diesel Heart, Subdermal Plating + Improved, Urgent Restoration, Nanite Surge + Improved, Clockwork Heart, Twitch Reflexes, Nanite Evolution, and Bioablative Regeneration all ensure that you’re basically unkillable on defense, and Technograft + Improved + Greater, Experimental Machinery + Improved + Greater, Hydraulic Fist, Overclocked Offense + Improved, and Mech Synthesis are all incredibly powerful offensive buffs. Much like with time, I’ve had to either change or outright veto a large proportion of the machine element’s utility talents just to keep the power level remotely within reason.


Third-Party: Melee > Ranged > Buffing, Control, Debuffing, Summoning > Anticaster, Recon, Skills






	Mean Rating 
(Paizo Content)
	Mean Rating 
(3PP Content)
	Mean Rating (Combined)
	Form Infusions
	4.5
	—
	4.5
	Substance Infusions
	3.0
	2.8
	2.8
	All Infusions
	3.4
	2.8
	3.1
	Utility Talents
	2.6
	3.0
	2.9
	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Class Skills


Knowledge (Engineering) actually isn’t totally worthless in this element, since it can be used to identify robots, and there’s a good chance you’re playing in an Iron Gods campaign if you’re playing a technokineticist. Disable Device doesn’t get any particular support from the element, but you could take Skilled Kineticist to become a halfway decent trap disabler.




Defensive Talent


        Diesel Heart: Diesel Heart is at least as powerful as Flesh of Stone, and that’s saying something. This defense talent’s DR/adamantine isn’t quite as strong, but the chance for crit or sneak attack negation that stacks with elemental overflow is really, really nice. Remember, paying for that ability on armor would cost you either a +1 enhancement bonus (25% negation), a +3 enhancement bonus (50% negation), or a +5 enhancement bonus (75% negation). Not too shabby.




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Oil Infusion: Wow, what a wacky infusion. The increase to the save DC is the biggest headline here.
Persistent Infusion: Probably the coolest part about Persistent Infusion is that it leaves parts of your element lying around that can then be used for utility talents, etc. Kind of like giving yourself an alley-oop in conditions where there’s not a lot of your element present naturally. The caltrop part is nice, but caltrops have an attack bonus of +0, which will fail to hit many 1st-level enemies, let alone higher-level enemies. Probably jumps up to at least a green if you have cheap form infusions like Kinetic Bomb or Improved Kinetic Bomb.


Pushing Infusion: CMD scores get incredibly high in later levels, and as a ¾-BAB class with no real reason to invest in feats like Improved Bull Rush, Pushing Infusion is just going to be wasted burn about 90% of the time.


2nd Level


Entangling Infusion: Entangling Infusion is reasonably cheap, has a nice long duration with a great set of debuffs, and targets the weakest save (on average) by CR. For my money, this is one of the best infusions a kineticist has, and a real feather in the cap of the elements that get access to it.


Bowling Infusion: If anything, CMD scores against trip maneuvers are even higher than CMD scores against bull rush maneuvers...and Bowling Infusion is more expensive than Pushing Infusion. No.
Breaching Infusion: Sunder is a pretty bad combat maneuver at the best of times, and kineticists continue to be ¾-BAB classes that can’t do much with them.


3rd Level


Magnetic Infusion: Not only can Magnetic Infusion buff martial allies attacking your target, it can also buff you! A +4 is no joke, especially with an untyped bonus, and the penalty to Reflex saves will make many of your form infusions that much easier to slap on foes. One of machine’s best infusions.


Rare-Metal Infusion: Unlike with manufactured weapons, which can be enchanted or forged out of different materials to pierce DR, kineticists have to make do with the precious few infusions or utility talents that allow them to do so. Rare-Metal is one such infusion. If you’re playing in a campaign that features a lot of enemies with DR/adamantine (constructs and robots; potentially Iron Gods), DR/cold iron (fey and various evil outsiders; definitely Wrath of the Righteous or Hell’s Rebels, and potentially other campaigns), or DR/alchemical silver (various daemons, kytons, devils, and lycanthropes; no campaigns that I know of) then you definitely want Rare-Metal Infusion. Think of it like a +5 to +20 damage bonus.


Oxidizing Infusion: Destroying equipment isn’t the best use of your resources, but the broken condition can be mended fairly easily. This is almost unfairly powerful in an Iron Gods campaign, where the vast majority of enemies will be made of metal.


Adamantine Infusion: Adamantine Infusion is practically begging to be used against constructs, who often have DR/adamantine as well as hardness. Unless you’re playing in an Iron Gods campaign, though, you’re unlikely to come up against enough constructs to make this infusion worth your time—Rare-Metal is more versatile, so you should generally opt for that.


5th Level


Bloodrust Infusion: Guaranteed Constitution damage is certainly worth the burn.
Self-Destructive Infusion: Also known as the “Stop hitting yourself!” infusion. Assuming you can sneak this baby past that Fortitude save, it truly can be destructive for an enemy. Either they have to waste an entire full-round action trying to throw off the compulsion to smack themselves in the face, or they have to use a swift action and deal a bunch of damage to themselves. It’s a heads-you-win, tails-they-lose kind of infusion both in terms of action economy and damage. Unfortunately, the enemies you most want to hurt themselves will also be the most likely to pass the Fortitude save.


8th Level


Imprisoning Infusion: Ice Tomb is a fantastic witch hex, and it does just as well for a kineticist as it does for a witch. The Fortitude save to negate is a bit unfortunate, as is the high cost, but save-or-suck effects tend to be like this.




Infusions: Form


3rd Level


Impale: The fact that you can use Impale to freaking burst through walls and stab enemies is just so cool. With no save, a good area of effect, and a reasonable cost, I can’t see any reason to ding Impale.


7th Level


Fragmentation: Gears everywhere! Fragmentation got a great rating in earth, and it works as well if not better for machine, which can apply the infusion to basically all of its simple and composite blasts.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Technograft: Mama pajama, that’s a good utility talent! Technograft singlehandedly ensures that normal melee and ranged builds are possible for kineticists, and provides you with pretty unparalleled versatility in what weapons you bring to a fight—highly useful for penetrating DR/damage type, flexing into different crit ranges or damage dice, etc. Heck, you can even enchant your graft so that it’s conductive, allowing you to deliver kinetic blasts while you hit them with your weapon. And that’s not even getting into the wackiness of allowing you to create technological weapons on a whim...


Subdermal Plating: Subdermal Plating is quite a bit better than Armor of Nature, in that it has no maximum Dexterity bonus and—once you get the Improved version—scales its armor bonus with no further burn investment from you. Don’t worry too much about that crit clause: crits aren’t super common anyway, and Diesel Heart + elemental overflow will gradually cause more and more threatened crits to fail.


Basic Technokinesis: You may very well be crafting your own constructs in this element, so it’s not a bad strategy to give you mending. For my money, though, there are better options available at 1st level for technokineticists.
Emergency Repairs: You technically count as a construct for the purposes of this ability, so you can indeed heal yourself with Emergency Repairs. Finding other viable targets will be a bit harder, though, unless you’re regularly crafting your own constructs.


Elemental Limb: Elemental Limb is a bad utility talent (+2 to Strength checks? Really?) but it does open the way for Improved and Greater Elemental Limb, both of which are pretty much required for natural attack builds.
Joint Lock: Armor check penalties are ultimately of negligible use against enemies. If an enemy were to use this against a PC trying to swim, climb, or fly in heavy armor, though…


Mechanical Overdrive: Bonuses to skill checks are generally quite balanced. So why am I rating this as a change? This little gem from p. 178 of the Core Rulebook: “At the start of a battle, each combatant makes an initiative check. An initiative check is a Dexterity check.” (Emphasis mine.) Adding Constitution to your initiative rolls is a massive bonus that, in conjunction with all the other bonuses you get, easily drives this talent above the purple zone. The machine element in general is quite a bit stronger than other elements, so this is a bit beyond the pale.
Suggested Fix: Mechanical Overdrive applies to all Strength and Dexterity checks except initiative checks. Even without that clause, this utility talent would still rate green or blue for its ability to boost your Escape Artist, Acrobatics, Swim, Climb, etc.; you don’t need initiative bonuses on top of that.


2nd Level


Experimental Machinery: It’s everything that Unnatural Evolution is, and more. Adding eidolon evolutions to PCs is and always will be extraordinarily powerful. Comboing this talent with Unnatural Evolution and the viscera element in general is bordering on unfair, though, so make sure you’re communicating with your GM about what you’re doing and why.


Magnetic Pull: The rare ability that actually allows you to tank, and tank a bunch of creatures, no less! Geokineticists and technokineticists are certainly up to the task with their boatload of HP and DR/adamantine; aerokineticists, probably less so, since they’re flimsier and operate at range more often. Do note that it affects only metal weapons, so this is best taken in games that pit you against mostly humanoid or native outsider enemies.
Urgent Restoration: Being able to heal yourself as a swift action is an incredible boost in combat effectiveness, even if you are going to have to take some burn to do it. Much appreciated for tanking builds.


Hard Knuckle: Two slams, check, benefits from elemental overflow, check check, synergizes with earth and wood, check check check. Yep, Hard Knuckle is plenty good for ranged builds, but even better for natural attack builds.
Improved Elemental Limb: Improved Elemental Limb is actually a great talent, although it takes some thinking to get there. It’s obviously required for natural attack builds, but other kineticists can benefit from it, too. First of all, it lets you threaten in melee range without using Kinetic Blade, but takes up no hands to interfere with gathering power (you can shape it at will with no listed action cost). Second, the elemental limb accepts feats applied to your kinetic blasts, so Spell Penetration, Weapon Focus, Weapon Finesse, etc. should all still be fair game. Third, you get to decide what type of damage your natural attack deals as you manifest it, which can be useful in some instances for punching through DR/bludgeoning, slashing, piercing, etc. Pretty good, on the whole.


Metal Blades: Metal Blades is excellent for natural attack builds, as it lets you bypass a bunch of DR types and gives you bonuses to attack and damage, to boot. An extremely durable rationale for going viscera/earth for a natural attack build.
Metal Scent: There’s such a lot of metal everywhere that having a good nose for it is likely to prove more frustrating and confusing than enlightening. Metal Scent is the prerequisite for the amazing Metalsense, though, so you’d better take this wild talent.


Living Capacitor: Body capacitance is a bad spell, even when it’s constant. Guess what deals massive electricity damage? Creatures that tend to be immune to electricity. Yeah, no. Embrace these turds, my children, that you might select better talents in their place.


Nanite Surge: The burn cost helps rein Nanite Surge in slightly, but not enough. Huge bonuses to saves are particularly problematic.
Suggested Fix: Change the circumstance bonus so that it’s equal to ¼ your kineticist level. You’ll still get a flexible bonus, but it’s not so ludicrously overpowered.


3rd Level


Improved Technograft: Better weapons, better damage...yeah, Improved Technograft is really, really strong. I get that the machine element’s “thing” is technological weaponry, but GMs might need to veto some of the tech weapons available to PCs through this utility talent. Like...a rocket launcher can hit for 12d6 damage and targets touch AC. You don’t want players having that willy-nilly.


Clockwork Heart: Um, yeah, Junk Blast! Way to be! Winding yourself up is easy as can be, and in return you get Lightning Reflexes and Improved Initiative, both of which are awesome feats. You’re only going to gain access to this wild talent if you’re investing heavily in earth, but it’s a heck of a payoff.
Construct Crafter: Crafting constructs is certainly a worthwhile application of your resources, and it’s great that you don’t have to deal with any of the prerequisites.
Hydraulic Fist: Hydraulic Fist just keeps on giving for natural attack builds, which turn into quite the melee monsters in this element. The damage bonuses and ability to bypass DR/adamantine are excellent, of course, but don’t sleep on the fact that Hydraulic Fist also allows you to bypass hardness. This will make a huge difference when you’re fighting robots and constructs (have I mentioned that this element would be amazing for an Iron Gods campaign?) but also when you’re sundering walls, doors, weapons, armor, and more. Want to punch your way through a stone wall? Gain a couple of levels, and it’ll be as easy as punching through paper.
Improved Subdermal Plating: Scaling armor bonuses are excellent! You’re essentially replacing the need for manufactured armor here, and you’ll never have to worry about your Dexterity bonus getting too high or your armor check penalties getting too onerous.


Improved Magnetic Pull: The increased radius is the big-ticket item here—you can probably forget the clause about redirecting electric spells.
Sharding Cover: Sharding Cover is weird in that it’s a gamble that your kinetic cover will be broken at some point, preferably sooner rather than later. This puts you into an awkward position: do you take just Kinetic Cover and Sharding Cover, knowing that your wall is going to be about as flimsy as tissue paper but trusting in the damage and difficult terrain to deal with enemies? Or do you take Improved and Greater Kinetic Cover in order to create truly impassable walls, knowing that they’re unlikely to be damaged enough to create shards? You can do both, of course, but that’s four utility talents spent. Lucky battlefield control is generally so strong.


Automatic Reconstruction: Fast healing this slow is unfortunately not going to cut it in combat, so there’s no point spending burn on Kinetic Cover, Improved Kinetic Cover, etc. This might come into play later on for “healing” structures created with Aether Architect, Kinetic Cityscape, etc., but I’d say those are fairly niche, endgame uses.
Improved Joint Lock: When you’re not proficient with armor, your armor check penalty applies to attack rolls in addition to any skill check that involves movement. If you’re a PC, that’ s a relatively minor deal, as not every enemy will wear armor, and not every enemy that wears armor will wear heavy enough armor to be hamstrung by those penalties. For GMs targeting a weak Reflex save, however, you can absolutely cripple a character.
Magnetism: The limitation to only bull rush and drag maneuvers greatly limits Magnetism in comparison with, say, Telekinetic Maneuvers from aether. And only metal objects or creatures? That’ll be functional some of the time, but certainly not all.


Pillar: What can Pillar do that climbing or flight couldn’t? It’s certainly got no combat applications. So why?


Overclocked Offense: Well, that’s just patently overpowered. For comparison, Celerity easily earns a blue or even purple rating, and Overclocked Offense has a better action cost (swift vs. standard) plus a boost to kinetic blast damage? Thanks, I hate it.
Suggested Fix: Eliminate the damage bonuses, at the very least—haste is powerful enough on its own without any help. I’m fine with the swift action action cost as long as the utility talent can never be used on allies. That seems to balance out Celerity (high action cost, multiple targets) with Overclocked Offense (low action cost, personal).
Twitch Reflexes: Wow, more overpowered buffs for the machine element. Who could have guessed? Anyway, allowing players to roll three times for initiative is far too powerful. Veto.
Suggested Fix: Eliminate the Reflex save bonus, and change the “roll three times, take the highest” option so that it costs 2 points of burn. Still very high-powered, just more balanced on the costs.


4th Level


Vital Fibers: Why bother building for natural attacks when you can steal other people’s limbs? This is a pretty wacky ability, but extra limbs always have great uses, so I can’t rate it poorly. The burn cost will keep you from becoming a centaur for longer than an afternoon, at least.


Elemental Hand: I’ve cooled on the hand suite of spells over the years, mostly because the CMB upgrades that grapple, bull rush, etc. can’t be counted on to work reliably against creatures of the same CR. It still works well to keep enemies away from you, slowing them as they try to approach and granting you cover bonuses if they try to attack from range. Might even soak some damage!
Kinetic Shelter: A fun little bungalow for you and your team, although other tactics like rope trick are probably a little less conspicuous.


Construct Hacker: In a normal, run-of-the-mill Pathfinder game, Construct Hacker won’t do much for you, since constructs tend to be fairly uncommon enemies. In an Iron Gods campaign? Could be much more valuable.
Construct Hunter: There seems to be quite a bit of overlap here with Metalsense, so you probably wouldn’t want both. Again, in an Iron Gods campaign, this would be gravy.


Twisted Metal: Unfortunately, Twisted Metal deals damage to the armor, weapon, etc. you’re targeting, which will potentially destroy the very equipment you’re hoping to loot. And what’s the benefit, even? That you get to originate a blast elsewhere? No. Maybe if you’re in an Iron Gods campaign and are fighting constructs 24/7, but not for anyone else.


Bioablative Regeneration: No, no, machine, you’re getting more unbalanced as you go, not less! This is a ridiculous power that could allow you to survive huge amounts of HP damage—the only limits being your total burn capacity and your standard action economy.
Suggested Fix: There’s really no fixing this one. Veto.
Metalsense: Good gracious, that’s powerful. So now you can perfectly detect and pinpoint metallic enemies, creatures with metallic blood (that’s most living things, by the way), and metallic objects with no penalty at a range of up to 100 feet, even passing through doors, ceilings, etc. Crazy.
Suggested Fix: Strike the clause allowing this utility talent to detect creatures with metallic blood. It functions as blindsight for metallic creatures and objects, and is blocked by barriers normally.


5th Level


Improved Experimental Machinery: More evolution points, and you can still swap them out however you want. Still crazy good.


Clockwork Puppet: I gave Aether Puppet a green, so it seems only fitting that Clockwork Puppet gets the same rating. These constructs aren’t the sturdiest things in the world, but as essentially infinite summoned meat shields, they can be treated as disposable automata: run them into traps, push them into rooms to soak surprise round alpha strikes, give soft cover or flanking to allies, etc. It’s all fine. They’re robots. They can’t feel it.
Spark of Innovation: Spark of Innovation is a nice upgrade over Spark of Life. Earth elementals don’t have any supernatural or spell-like abilities, so losing them isn’t a blow, and the upgrades you get with construct traits and the clockwork subtype are generally well worth your trouble, granting greatly improved defenses. Pretty good!


Improved Nanite Surge: If you accept my changes to Nanite Surge, the improved version actually isn’t too bad. I would still make some changes.
Suggested Fix: Make the rerolls cost 1 point of burn, as well (i.e., 1 point of burn for the Nanite Surge bonus, and 1 additional point of burn for the reroll).
Nanite Evolution: Way, way too powerful. These spells are 6th-level spells, and 1 point of burn isn’t a huge cost to pay for that much flexibility, especially when it affects your entire team.
Suggested Fix: Move Nanite Evolution to 2nd level and make it mimic the single-target bull’s strength, cat’s grace, and bear’s endurance.


6th Level


Greater Elemental Limb: Still as necessary for natural attack builds as ever it was, but now with the ability to manifest extra arms that might help you use magic items, etc.


Forced Reboot: Probably a tool for reviving any crafted constructs you may have, nothing more. Circumstantial.
Improved Elemental Hand: For my money, Improved Elemental Hand isn’t really worth it. Only weak casters would have CMD scores low enough to get grappled at this level, and they’ll have freedom of movement running basically permanently, ensuring that they can’t be grappled at all. Thpbbb.


Greater Technograft: There was no need to improve the Technograft line to this degree—it’s already ridiculously powerful.
Suggested Fix: Veto. The damage die size and BAB bonuses are particularly egregious.
Improved Overclocked Offense: I gave Improved Celerity a change rating, and that’s absolutely appropriate here.
        Suggested Fix: Veto. Haste really doesn’t need to be any more powerful.


7th Level


Improved Construct Hacker: Another one that’s amazing for an Iron Gods campaign, but of limited use elsewhere. At least the duration is now getting high enough that you don’t have to meet all that many constructs in order to bring allies along.


Steel Rider: I guess the benefit is that you get to take your allies along for the ride now? I dunno why you would really want to attack someone with your Magneto platform, but I guess the option is nice.
Turing Computation: Awaken construct is going to be a fairly niche spell, even for those crafting automata to serve them.




Fist of Eternity: Let’s go over all the prerequisites that you need to hit before you qualify to take this talent, shall we? Fist of Eternity specifies Fist of Nature and Hydraulic Fist as its formal prerequisites. Hydraulic Fist requires two utility talents: Subdermal Plating and Hard Knuckle. Fist of Nature also requires two: Fist of Oak and Fist of Granite. Between those two, you’re already looking at six utility talents, most of which are completely redundant. Then you’ve got to get two instances of Expanded Defense—and since you can’t take the utility talents in your third and final expanded element until at least 14th level, you probably won’t qualify to take Fist of Eternity until...16th? 17th level? And that’s when you start to ask: Is eight utility talents really worth what I’m getting here? Sure, two slams at 2d8/x3 are nice, but are they eight utility talents nice? I’ll leave that judgment to you, but my inclination is to respond in the negative.


Mech Synthesis: Wow, no way. An effective +6 to natural armor and every physical attribute? No way in Hades PCs should be allowed this talent.
        Suggested Fix: There’s really nothing to do but veto.


8th Level


Greater Experimental Machinery: Still excellent for technokineticists and corpokineticists.


9th Level


Greater Construct Hacker: Sure, why not? Get your very own construct pets.
Greater Magnetism: An improved version of a druid-only 8th-level spell, at will. And no save or spell resistance! If any creature at all uses metal anything, you’ve got ‘em—you can bull rush people 40 ft. per round with Greater Magnetism, and your lack of reliance on a rounds/level duration means that you can scoot people anywhere you want, anytime you want. My favorite for your 9th-level wild talent slot.
Kinetic Cityscape: Unfortunately for aether, Kinetic Cityscape kinda steals the wind out from Aether Architect’s sails; fortunately for everyone else, this is a really cool capstone ability that can help you found a kineticist city once you’re done with your campaign. There aren’t a lot of combat-effective 9th-level utility talents, so why not?
🧠 Mind
[3PP] Mind


        Mind is, frankly speaking, the most interesting element created for the kineticist class. That’s not a criticism of the other elements, just high praise for this one, which was released as part of the Legendary Kineticists series by Legendary Games. Mind is also the most self-contained element in the extended kineticist repertoire—very few if any infusions or utility talents are shared with other elements—and so it has a lot of freedom to try risky design choices and take chances that pay off in flavorful and actually quite balanced ways. You can build a neurokineticist as a skill monkey, a social spider who sweet-talks enemies and then wipes their memories, an AoE debuffer, an enchantment specialist who takes direct control of enemies and uses them for nefarious purposes, a support buffer who uses teamwork feats and mesmerist tricks to keep the party safe...there are just a billion different ways to build one of these kineticists, all of them cool. I’m guessing there will probably be three or four mind builds in the Sample Builds section, because I simply have to try my hand at building out these various playstyles.
        On the mechanical side, probably the biggest thing to know about mind is that all of its wild talents (kinetic blasts, infusions, utility talents, you name it) are considered mind-affecting effects. This makes campaigns in which undead or constructs are primary foes (Carrion Crown, Iron Gods, Tyrant’s Grasp) exceptionally poor choices for a neurokineticist. Sure, the element has some tools for working around resistances or outright immunities to mind-affecting effects, but it’s still an uphill climb the whole way—why bother if there’s an easier element to be played? Another thing to note is that mind is more Will save-oriented than any other element. I don’t think there’s a single Reflex or Fortitude save in the entire shebang, all 100+ wild talents, and that means 1) you’re incredibly dependent on good Constitution scores to pump your save DCs, and 2) some fights are simply going to be tough for you. Identifying and then targeting weak saves is a time-honored tradition of all casters, but neurokineticists won’t be able to do that as well as other elements. Mind benefits more than any other element from having scaling save DCs on utility talents (it’s a base feature of the legendary kineticist chassis, or can be purchased through the third-party Adaptive Utility feat) but I’m still on record as saying that this option should not be open to kineticists. Utility talents are typically at-will abilities, and what holds them in check is that their save DCs remain more or less static throughout the game. Take that balance away, and they can get overpowered in a hurry. To the GMs out there, altering the Adaptive Utility feat to apply to one utility talent is a reasonable compromise that won’t get too overpowered.


Third-Party: Buffing, Skills > Anticaster, Debuffing, Summoning > Ranged > Control, Melee






	Mean Rating 
(Paizo Content)
	Mean Rating 
(3PP Content)
	Mean Rating (Combined)
	Form Infusions
	—
	3.3
	3.3
	Substance Infusions
	5.0
	3.0
	3.1
	All Infusions
	5.0
	3.1
	3.1
	Utility Talents
	3.0
	3.0
	3.0
	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Class Skills


Knowledge (History) is traditionally regarded as worthless, hence its relegation to the Background Skill roster, but Sense Motive is a nice addition, helping play into the theme of a kineticist who’s inside your head, able to tell exactly when you’re lying and when you’re telling the truth.




Defensive Talent


Numbness: Numbness is most similar to void’s defensive talent, Emptiness, but allows you to convert lethal damage into nonlethal damage as you take it. This doubles the effectiveness of healing you gain, but is otherwise fairly unremarkable.




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Reifying Infusion: Remember: all mind wild talents are mind-affecting effects unless otherwise stated. If you run into something with immunity, there is absolutely nothing that you can do to help. Reifying Infusion fixes this problem for your kinetic blasts, at least, even if your utility talents will still be more or less worthless. A must-get infusion for each and every neurokineticist.


Doubting Infusion: Much of the damage that monsters deal to people is static damage from Strength bonuses, two-handed weapon bonuses, Power Attack, enhancement bonuses, etc., so Doubting Infusion isn’t going to drop anyone’s damage down to laughable levels. It will consistently blunt how hard martial characters can hit you and your allies, though, and in conjunction with a reasonable burn cost and a Will save to negate (used primarily against targets with soft Will saves, of course) it can do good work for you throughout your kineticist career.


Aphasia Infusion: I’m a speech-language pathologist, and I always loved that Paizo got at least a little awareness of aphasia out into the public consciousness. It’s a pretty devastating ability to hit arcane or divine casters with; the fact that targets get a new save each round to end the effect is offset by the fact that the infusion save DC will scale with your kineticist level.


Dizzying Infusion: Dazzled is easily the weakest status condition in Pathfinder 1e. Don’t bother.


2nd Level


Cupid Infusion: Unnatural lust can be an encounter-ender in some circumstances, and it’s especially good as an infusion with a scaling save DC. 


Wrathful Infusion: Interesting, interesting. If you use the three-burn version of Wrathful Infusion, it essentially becomes a buff for your party, allowing you to roid out martial allies. The two-burn version can be used as a standard magehunting infusion, locking casters out of their spellcasting (which requires patience and concentration). A good multi-purpose infusion.


Amnesia Infusion: Assuming you can get sequential failed Will saves, it would be pretty funny to shoot someone to death with mind bullets as they kept forgetting they were in a combat...but it doesn’t have a lot of uses other than that with its short duration, so I can’t rate it very highly.
Overloading Infusion: We really don’t care about enemies not being able to take 20 on skill checks, but the clause about denying spellcasters concentration is interesting. Does this mean that they automatically fail concentration checks to cast defensively, respond to damage that interrupts their spellcasting, etc.? If so, Overloading Infusion would rate at least a green. If it just means that enemies can’t cast spells with a duration of concentration, that will mostly affect illusionists, and that’s considerably weaker.
Spellbane Infusion: Not a huge number of monsters are straight spellcasters—it’s more common to have spell-like abilities in the bestiaries, and Spellbane Infusion won’t do a thing against those. I dunno, it seems underwhelming to me, especially with a Will save to negate. Spellcasters are generally great at those.


Overtaxing Infusion: Not many spells require concentration to keep active, so Overtaxing Infusion is quite poor. And stopping someone from taking 20 on skill checks—when would that even come up?


3rd Level


Scrambling Infusion: Woof, confused for multiple rounds for only 2 points of burn. That’s harsh. Confused is one of the best status conditions you can dole out, though, as enemies are left with only a one-in-four chance in a given round of actually doing the thing they intended to do.


Improved Wrathful Infusion: Blood scent and jealous rage are weaker than rage. Still a decent ability, just not quite the powerhouse that Wrathful Infusion is.
Stumbling Infusion: The utility of Stumbling Infusion will depend a great deal on how much enemies are moving around, but in general, if you can stick this infusion on a big, lumbering enemy, they’ll just keep falling prone, allowing your team to take attacks of opportunity or trip them up again.
Synaptic Infusion: Mind-affecting effects won’t be universally useful—constructs, oozes, plants, undead, and vermin are all immune, as well as some outsiders—and a Will save to negate is also a bit unfortunate. Nevertheless, staggered is a strong condition. I’m not sure why Paizo gave enemies the option to remove the condition with a move action; you’re still left with just a standard, and isn’t that kind of the point of the staggered condition anyway? Maybe it’s to give enemies a chance to 5-foot step. Anyway, it’s still okay for denying enemies access to the action economy, but a bit too limited to attain an A- or S-tier ranking.


Paranoia Infusion: Meh. Attacks of opportunity and refusing to accept harmless spells make for a pretty meager debuff. Not totally useless, just not what I would prioritize spending burn on.


4th Level


Disabling Infusion: We’re getting expensive here, but Disabling Infusion is highly versatile and boasts a decent duration that more than compensate for its shortcomings. Great to be able to nullify things like truesight and blindsight, too.
Pacifying Infusion: Either a foe is neutered or else it has to waste a bunch of actions trying to overcome the fact that it’s neutered. Either way, you win.


Psychic Connection: An interesting ability that ensures that enemies truly can’t run or hide from you—anywhere they go (short of plane shifting away) you’ll be able to hit them with kinetic blasts or utility talents.
Synaptic Shock: Synaptic scramble isn’t quite as deadly to enemies as confusion is, and the fact that Synaptic Infusion is more expensive than Scrambling Infusion doesn’t help. It’s a fun trick, though, especially if you can strand an enemy away from the group where things like attacks aren’t likely to help them.


5th Level


Lock Up Infusion: Your very first save-or-die infusion, in essence. Once an enemy is paralyzed, it’s all too easy to walk up and coup de grace them while they’re helpless. And a nice duration, too!
Unraveling Infusion: Mind is lucky to get Unraveling Infusion, which is normally a pyrokineticist-exclusive wild talent. Dispel magic is versatile and powerful. Good stuff.


Psychotropic Infusion: The Will save to negate is what really sells Psychotropic Infusion for me. Picture this: you’ve got a big bruiser henchman and his spellcaster boss, alone in a room. Bruiser has a bad Will save and a relatively low Wisdom score, so you hit him with Psychotropic Infusion. He fails his Will save, taking Wisdom damage and lowering his Will save even further. What’s more, he starts attacking his spellcaster boss! Now, dual mind-affecting and poison tags mean that you have to be very careful about which enemies you use this infusion on, but when it works, it really works.
Self-Destructive Infusion: Also known as the “Stop hitting yourself!” infusion. Assuming you can sneak this baby past that Fortitude save, it truly can be destructive for an enemy. Either they have to waste an entire full-round action trying to throw off the compulsion to smack themselves in the face, or they have to use a swift action and deal a bunch of damage to themselves. It’s a heads-you-win, tails-they-lose kind of infusion both in terms of action economy and damage. Unfortunately, the enemies you most want to hurt themselves will also be the most likely to pass the Fortitude save.


Stupefying Infusion: Probably your best bet is to target martial enemies and deal Wisdom damage on each successful blast. It’ll soften them up for future infusions or utility talents, and render them unconscious if things get bad enough. Better a pliable puppet than a snoozing bruiser, though.


6th Level


Slumber Infusion: It’ll render enemies helpless just like Lock Up Infusion, but I’d sooner dole out paralysis on the cheap than sleep for a higher cost. Sleep can be broken pretty easily; paralysis can’t, except with magic.


7th Level


Improved Synapse Shock: Hours/level seems like overkill, since most battles only last a few rounds. It can make it very difficult for enemies to flee effectively or dispel the condition on themselves, though, and that’s to be commended.


Improved Stupefying Infusion: Probably not worth the extra cost. Martial enemies should be the main targets you use Stupefying Infusion on, and they don’t become any less dangerous with lower Intelligence or Charisma scores.


8th Level


Improved Psychic Connection: Being able to tag a target anywhere on the same plane, forever, with whatever blasts or utility talents you want is incredibly good. A great way to ensure that the BBEG of a Paizo AP meets their demise as intended.


Dominating Infusion: Very expensive for such a short duration, but that’s the price of total control.


9th Level


Lobotomizing Infusion: A save-or-die effect. Eh, I guess. Expensive, single-target save-or-die effects don’t usually get me jazzed up.




Infusions: Form


3rd Level


Empathetic Surge: Standard blast range, a little AoE, and the ability to exclude allies at will. Solidly good.


5th Level


Improved Empathetic Surge: Better range and a much better AoE radius. Again, solidly good for the cost.


6th Level


Gorgon Gaze: There are a number of tricks enemies can use to avoid your gaze attack, and a Will save to negate what was half damage to begin with ain’t great. Moreover, you can’t do anything but use utility talents while Gorgon Gaze is active unless you want the effect cancelled. I remain (mostly) unimpressed.


7th Level


Greater Empathetic Surge: A 30-foot radius mind nuke is certainly something to sit up and take notice of.




Utility Talents


1st Level
