Predicted Maneuvers: In a lot of instances it’s not too difficult to guess what an enemy is going to do: spellcasters are going to cast spells, martial enemies are going to charge you from range, etc. The save DC increases you get here are incredibly, incredibly worth your time.
Psychic Persuasion: The spells this talent grants will be useful at low levels, and once the save DC falls off eventually you’ll have plenty of upgrades to move on to. A cornerstone of the charm/dominate style of neurokineticist.
Stimulated Intellect: Mama pajama! Ten class skills for free and the ability to use your Constitution modifier on every single Intelligence-based skill. What a package. More combat-oriented neurokineticists will probably still want Basic Neurokinesis, but Stimulated Intellect sure ain’t pulling punches.


Basic Neurokinesis: There’s a bit to unpack here. The telepathy is of such limited range that it doesn’t even pass Covid-19 social distancing standards, so that’s not great; it is, however, just a cornerstone upon which other utility talents will build, so never fear. The bonus on Intelligence-based skill checks is great, and really solidifies the neurokineticist as the only way to build a skill monkey in this class, as you’ll see later. Lastly, the ability to know whether enemies have failed their saves against your wild talents is fantastic. No need to guess whether an enemy has been mind-controlled or not—you just know.
Esoteric Knowledge: I was about to make a quip about how Esoteric Knowledge wasn’t any better than the Cunning feat, which grants one extra skill rank per HD, but it’s really a lot better than that because you can switch to another skill without much effort. Have a crucial Knowledge (History) check that you know no one’s going to be able to crush? Now you can! One delicious part of a complete skill monkey breakfast.
Expanded Telepathy: I wish that the range scaled a bit harder, but Expanded Telepathy is still an excellent way to establish wordless communication between you and anyone you may need to talk to. Plus it’s a prerequisite for Improved Mind’s Eye, so you’ll likely want to take it sometime.
Kinetic Healer: I’m a fan of Kinetic Healer for two reasons: first, it’s healing, and pretty strong healing, at that. Second, it’s a wild talent that teaches your team about burn. Drop Kinetic Healer on a party member a few times and they’ll learn very quickly that you can’t be running around taking 4 burn every turn to drop mooks in encounters. Burn is a resource to be conserved, respected, and feared, and Kinetic Healer teaches your sweet, innocent tablemates that lesson in a hurry.
Smooth Negotiator: Implant urge makes people easier to deal with when making Diplomacy checks, sure, but it also imparts a -2 penalty against any mind-affecting effect that would cause the target to go forward toward the goal you name. If you hit someone with Smooth Negotiator, then ready a Psychic Persuasion using Predicted Maneuvers go to get a bonus, you can net an effective +3 on your save DC as early as 4th level. A must-have for any neurokineticist who wants to navigate social encounters with tact and finesse, rather than brute force.


Mental Poison: If you’re going for a mind/poison hybrid build, Mental Poison is 100% non-optional. One of the huge problems with poison builds is that they can’t often target saves other than Fortitude. Mental Poison lets you target Will, which can be an enormous gift. Now you can craft anticaster poisons that target Fortitude saves, and antimartial poisons that target Will saves! Everyone wins, except your enemies. But, well, not everyone can win.
Mindlink: You’ll learn only very basic emotional information from Mindlink, and the Will save to shunt you out will be easily passed once a few levels go by. A prerequisite for All In Your Head, though, if you want to play with that.


Life After Undeath: It’s certainly not relevant for PCs, and probably not all that relevant even for GMed monsters.


2nd Level


Thought Shield: It’s basically elemental overflow for your Will saves, which you desperately need. Every neurokineticist should take this talent.


All In Your Head: A very interesting utility talent that enables true stealth play: no one else can see your blasts but you and the target. Throw in a little Telekinetic Invisibility from aether, boosts to your Stealth skill from the various skill monkey utility talents, and any feats or abilities that allow you to snipe better, and you might be able to kill enemies without anyone realizing that you’re even there. You wouldn’t just fall into this build, though—you’d have to plan for it.
Mind Reader: Since spell-like abilities have no somatic, verbal, or material components, there’s nothing to prevent you from simply using Mind Reader over and over anytime you’re with other people in order to get a sense of what they’re thinking about. Sure, enemies won’t always fail their saves, but it can be a great way to open a negotiation or social interaction. Someone who seems to know instinctively what you’re thinking can be very charming indeed.
Perfect Disguise: Assumed likeness can be a bit dicey, since targets who pass their saves don’t assume you’re anyone. It’s an easy way to get big, big bonuses on your Charisma-based skill checks, though, especially if you opt for the one-burn version of Perfect Disguise.


Blinded Mind’s Eye: This reminds me most of the cipher investigator, which can totally erase itself from enemies’ perception in order to hide. While the cipher is not a good archetype, Blinded Mind’s Eye...well, I’m not convinced it’s a good utility talent, either. If your save DCs scaled, sure, it’d be good, but without scaling nearly every enemy will eventually be able to see right past this trick.
Cerebral Manipulation: With its relatively short duration, false belief is not a fantastic spell even for the intrigue applications that it was clearly designed for. It does lead into higher-level utility talents for inception, though, so you’ll need to stomach the bad stuff if you want to get to the good stuff.
Flash of Amnesia: It’ll work if you’re targeting commoners, but thanks to the lack of scaling save DCs, just about any enemy will pass their saves and you’ll be left one full-round action short. Doesn’t erase anything more than a theft, pickpocketing, or a few rounds of combat, either.
Psychic Duelist: I remember I tried to read the rules for psychic duels and binary mindscapes one time and literally felt my eyes going crossed as I did so. It’s nice to know the option is here, but I can’t imagine most players being like, “You know what I’m most excited about in this element? A utility talent that will leave my physical body prone and helpless on the ground!”


Combat Traitor: Since the burn of utility talents can’t be mitigated in any way, it’s really, really hard to endorse Combat Traitor, even though it could theoretically let you charm an enemy in the middle of a combat. The stickier wicket when dealing with charm—in this case—isn’t actually the save DC of the spell-like ability, but the fact that you need to win an opposed Charisma check in order to convince the target to do something it wouldn’t normally do. What’s your average kineticist’s Charisma modifier going to be? -2? And that’s what you have to use to convince enemies to attack their friends? Doesn’t pass muster, Dave and Buster.
Dyslexia: The casters you’d want to slap this on will have a bunch of ways to skirt around the penalties. Pass.
Eclectic Knowledge: I made fun of Esoteric Knowledge for being a Cunning clone, and decided it was actually better than that; I could make equal fun of Eclectic Knowledge for being a Cunning clone, but it’s actually worse than that, because ranks from Eclectic Knowledge can only be placed in skills keyed off of mental attributes. Just leave it alone.


3rd Level


Piercing Thoughts: Literally every mind wild talent is a mind-affecting effect, so Piercing Thoughts is the biggest no-brainer in the world. You need your save DCs to be as high as possible (and enemies’ saves as low as possible) in this element to make it work, and Reifying Infusion, the most common source of bonuses against mind-affecting effects, is one of the most essential infusions for a neurokineticist to have.


Accelerated Cognition: Big bonuses to Perception, haste, ability to use it as a swift action...if it weren’t for the short duration, I’d easily give this a purple rating. As it is, you can choose to use the one-burn version as an in-combat buff when haste is absent, or else the zero-burn version when you just want the Perception bonuses.
Additional Allies: Very much needed if you’re going to be charming or dominating professionally.
Mesmerizing Suggestion: Wooow. Mesmerist tricks for a kineticist. These babies are tricky to use (see what I did there?) but can be incredibly powerful and versatile in the right circumstances. Check out my mesmerist guide, Follow My Voice, for suggestions about which tricks will be worth purchasing.


Decelerate: It’s the debuffing counterpart to Celerity! Slow is a good spell, but you’ll generally want to save it for when you’re up against hordes of weaker enemies—high-level targets will most likely pass the Fortitude save here. Probably a good candidate for retraining later on, since you can’t effectively scale the save DC.
Haphephobia: If—and this is a big if—you can get an enemy to fail their save against Haphephobia, it can be absolutely crippling to unarmed or natural attackers. Bad Touch builds like debuffing clerics and witches would probably also suffer, although they typically use things like spectral hand or a familiar to deliver their touch debuffs.
Hivemind: Giving your entire team telepathy is good enough, but the upgrades to this talent are bonkers if you have an allied cavalier, inquisitor, hunter, or someone else who knows a lot of teamwork feats. Just you wait, Henry Higgins. We’ll get there soon.
No Filter: Hilarious and potentially very damning in social encounters. Heck, if an enemy hates you enough, you can get them to blurt those thoughts out, then appeal to onlookers to clap them in irons. Classic diplomatic wombo combo.


Habitual Liar: A fun ability for getting rivals nicked by the coppers, but with no upgrades on the horizon and a Will save to negate, foes will eventually start shrugging this utility talent off. Always fun to make trouble for commoners, though. I know you want to. We’re all murder-hobos at heart.
Mania/Phobia: I’m not saying it’s useless, just that it’s way too finicky for me. They’ve got to fail the save, then see the object that they like/fear, then fail another save to avoid interacting with it, then the penalties aren’t great...nah.
Spellthief: I guess it’s nice that you can do this as often as your immediate/swift action economy allows, but how many times per day are you being targeted with spells specifically delivered via melee touch attack? It can’t be many, right?


Implant Dream: Okay, so this is actually Inception, but because you have no control over how the person interprets what they hear (and hell, some of us don’t remember anything about our dreams upon waking) this seems unlikely to yield great results. Certainly not worth 1 point of burn. It gets better with the improved version, but not by much.


4th Level


Enduring Control: If you want to keep someone mind-controlled in the long term, you need Enduring Control. Period.
Improved Hivemind: Teamwork feats can be exceptionally powerful—and I should know, I wrote a guide to the inquisitor class. The fact that you automatically get access to allies’ teamwork feats can be golden if you have an allied cavalier, inquisitor, or hunter, and being able to hand out your own teamwork feats for the reasonable price of 1 burn can turn you into a pocket buffer. With enough support from allies, this could easily even be purple.
Improved Smooth Negotiator: There are so, so many applications for inveigle person. If possible, you should actually talk to your GM to see whether they’ll allow you to take inveigle monster as a separate 7th-level utility talent—that +3 on the save DC would really, really help.
Mental Erasure: Effectively unlimited negative levels (pending failed saves and passed spell resistance checks, of course) can really ramp up quickly, and can brutalize spellcasters if you’re lucky enough to get some failed saves. Use Predicted Maneuvers to ready the action “If the spellcaster casts a spell, I’m going to use Mental Erasure” and you’ll be set to go in no time.
Psychosomatic Malady: There are multiple rounds of saves involved in most of these applications, but the fact that you can use one spell to 1) impart huge penalties to attack rolls, saves, and skill checks, 2) deal ability damage to any physical attribute in line with an injury poison, or 3) deal slow Constitution damage in line with an ingested poison all adds up to a great utility talent. It gets even better with Psychosomatic Calamity, too.


Improved Mind Reader: A better save DC and more knowledge about what your enemies are fearful or desirous of can’t hurt for social builds.
Mind’s Eye: Blindsense isn’t foolproof, but this is a prerequisite to Improved Mind’s Eye, which grants blindsight. That’s a great deal better.
Passive Telepuppet: An excellent espionage ability that only gets better as you control more and more thralls with Psychic Persuasion.


Mental Fortress: Being dazed for a round isn’t great, but it may still be better than whatever mind-affecting effect you were about to be subjected to. I would still just take Thought Shield + Iron Will and hope for the best.


5th Level


Flood Psyche: I’ll put it this way: if an enemy fails a save against mind fog while you’re around, you own them. A great opening move so that you can charm or dominate one or more minions. Basic Neurokinesis will also ensure that you know exactly who failed their saves.


Improved Mesmerizing Suggestion: Where before you could only use mesmerist tricks on yourself, the Improved version of this wild talent allows you to use them on allies within range of your telepathy. That’s fantastic. Note that you’ll likely need Expanded Telepathy in order to make that feasible.


Improved Cerebral Manipulation: Always fun to be able to monkey with people’s memories. The burn cost is a going concern, but I’m still of the opinion that it’s worth it, especially as the Greater version of this utility talent is so exceptionally powerful.
Psychic Network: I’d probably hold off on Psychic Network until you can pick up the Improved Psychic Connection infusion, as you’ll need to be able to make the connection permanent (or at least long-lasting) before you can use this utility talent reliably. Anyway, this is great for maintaining a stable of spies, informants, disposable allies, etc., and lends itself well to the Psychic Persuasion playstyle.


Hypochondria: A one-round sickened debuff doesn’t exactly cripple enemies, but if you have a reliable way to make them sickened (allowing you to proc nauseated instead) then Hypochondria becomes significantly better.
Improved Implant Dream: Tampering with dreams is a little more effective here than in Implant Dream, but not much more effective.
Improved Spellthief: I don’t think that Improved Spellthief does nearly enough to improve upon its predecessor, but your mileage may vary. How often are casters around you holding their charges?
Mindscape Architect: I don’t have any direct experience with mindscapes, so I’m not comfortable giving this utility talent a rating. It’d be a decent way to have a conversation with an enemy with no risk that they’d kill you, similar to astral projection, project image, etc., but I’m not sure that’s worth the talent. If someone knows of huge mindscape applications that I’m missing, please write me on Reddit and let me know.


Lingering Energy: I’m not a fan. It costs 1 burn and eats your immediate action, and even if an enemy does proc the blast, they’re likely to avoid all the damage. Nah.


6th Level


Psychic Enslavement: Dominate person/monster is pretty much the pinnacle of any enchantment build, and you’ve got it now. If you’ve been investing in the Psychic Persuasion playstyle where you’re treating enemies like marionettes, this is the logical conclusion to all that investment.


Active Telepuppet: Possession is already one of the best psychic spells out there for taking direct control of enemies (certainly a cut above magic jar!) and retaining control of your body during the possession removes one of the spell’s only weaknesses. Wonderful.
Failsafe: Contingency is always wicked powerful, although the relatively constrained choices you have available in utility wild talents drops it down from purple.
Improved Mind’s Eye: Blindsight is always amazing. I have spoken. Pray that you don’t run into any undead, constructs, vermin, etc., though, because this talent won’t work against them.


Greater Hivemind: The only real change of note between Improved and Greater Hivemind is that the greater version can let you steal enemies’ teamwork feats. In most instances, enemies won’t have any, but if you meet any hunters, inquisitors, or cavaliers, this might significantly expand your repertoire.
Suffocate: Suffocate can be an exceptional tool for killing arcane spellcasters before they can do any harm to you; for any other target, the slow speed and concentration will be dealbreakers.


Kinetic Regeneration: This shouldn’t be a major improvement to Kinetic Healer unless you’re regularly losing limbs to attacks. I did not see that coming!
Psychological Healing: Folks, kineticists just don’t make great healers. I mean, there will be times when this could work for you, but is it enough to justify the talent expenditure?
Sleepwalker: It’s kind of like a proactive modify memory. I’d say the neurokineticist has enough other tools for this kind of intent that Sleepwalker is redundant, though.


7th Level


Greater Mesmerizing Suggestion: Your mesmerist cosplay has now reached its zenith, its apex, its peak, and man, does it work for you. Being able to leave tricks implanted after they’re used saves you on action economy and allows you to keep reusing those tricks, making this a fabulous talent for any support-focused neurokineticist. At least one sample build is going to use this talent line, so watch out for that.
Memories On Tap: Drinking people’s memories and abilities, eh? That’s a cool concept. Seems to me that you could just pay people to give up their feats and spells to you, especially since they don’t lose the feat or spell you take. “Hey, can I have Power Attack for the day?” “Sure, that’ll be 50 silver.” That’s as complicated as it should be to open a business in this country.
Psychosomatic Calamity: Big, brutal AoE debuffs are just what I like about neurokineticists. Yeah, this is good.


Psychic Hotline: You used to call me in my mindscape / Late night when you need my love...hey, don’t hate the player, hate the Drake. Anyway, Psychic Hotline is interesting because it lets you use mind wild talents (including blasts, infusions, and utility talents) through enemies, including enemies that are anywhere in the world. That’s a lot of versatility, but the fact that you have to keep spending burn on it does blunt its cost-effectiveness.
Psycho Crusher: For my money, I like feeblemind better than insanity. It’s easier to stick on casters, who are the main threats you’ll face in these levels, and can be used repeatedly if those casters have some contingency + break enchantment setup going on.


Mind Break: So here’s my objection: Scrambling Infusion is one of the best substance infusions you have. It costs only 2 points of burn that at this level are entirely negated by infusion specialization, it can be paired with form infusions to cause multiple creatures to become confused, it can benefit from Empty Infusion to boost its save DC as needed, and its save DC will scale with your level. Functionally, the difference between an enemy going temporarily insane and permanently insane is likely negligible. Martials won’t be able to do a thing about it either way, and casters will simply teleport away and break enchantment to stop the effect. So why waste burn here when Scrambling Infusion will do? And if you’re trying to ruin some NPC’s life, why not use one of your other many tools?
Spell Deflection: Spell turning has a lot of weaknesses, and Spell Deflection has even more weaknesses than spell turning. Many spells, for example, are outright ineligible to be affected by spell turning, so you take the full effect of the spells if you’re unlucky. For those spells that are eligible, you still have to deal with the fact that you’re getting 1d10-1 levels of protection (average 4.5) instead of spell turning’s 1d4+6 (average 8.5) levels of protection. If you get unlucky on that d10 rolls, you won’t have any protection. I know you don’t take any burn for using it as a standard action, but even so, I’m just not very excited about it.


8th Level


Kinetic Purification: Heal is a phenomenal spell not only for the amount of HP it heals but also for all the status conditions it cures. This utility talent comes in very late, so what else are you going to spend it on?
True Amnesia: Much like feeblemind, if you stick the save on this utility talent, an enemy is simply done for. A great way to Jason Bourne-ify someone.


Improved Mindscape Architect: Even though I don’t know much about mindscapes, I do know that having a permanent one with this many mutable traits is likely to be cool narratively and mechanically.


Mind Over Matter: Wow, what a weird spell. I’ve actually never run across this one before. It’s interesting—kind of like becoming incorporeal toward only one enemy—but has only limited uses. It’s good if there’s a minion or lieutenant who’s hassling you while you’re trying to focus on the boss. “Lalalala, I can’t see you, so you must not exist!”


Grant Sentience: It seems appropriate for neurokineticists—who are all about the mind—to be able to literally give mindless creatures minds, but it’s got no combat applications and will therefore see limited use in most games. Potentially a cool NPC ability?


9th Level


Greater Cerebral Manipulation: The final feather in any neurokineticist’s cap, Greater Cerebral Manipulation gives you the power to turn any enemy or NPC into anybody you want. If you’d like that incredibly evil CR 20 necromancer to suddenly become a CR 6 neutral good-aligned lover of gardening, butterflies, and making children smile, you can do that. Why kill enemies when you can make them genuine friends?


Eternal Domination: Did I say that Psychic Enslavement was the peak of your domination powers? I lied. This is it. Eternal Domination ensures that one enemy is yours permanently, regardless of interplanar distance or antimagic fields. Seems like you could have just taken the Leadership feat, though, no?


Historical Erasure: Again, the logical conclusion of your memory-erasing abilities. Become the best grifter and con man there ever was. You know you came all this way just to cosplay Harold Hill. Do it. Play those 76 trombones.
🧪 Poison
[3PP] Poison


        The poison element is a third-party element that was introduced in the Kineticists of Porphyra supplements by Purple Duck Games. It’s definitely not a brokenly powerful element, although it does have a number of strong infusions and utility talents, and can make a good secondary expanded element if you’re looking for more battlefield control (pairing it with something like earth) or natural attacks (pairing it with something like viscera). Taken in isolation, it feels most similar to fire, with a simple energy blast and many of the same damage-over-time infusions that fire gets. Where the two differ is in poison’s ability to generate and deliver an almost baffling array of poisons: Laced Infusion, Biological Toxin, and Greater Biological Toxin are wild talents that nearly every toxikineticist should get, but Venom Spikes, Master Poisoner, Poison Conversion, Poison Amplification, Personalized Venom, Venom Vector, and Greater Venom Vector can all find their places in a dedicated poison build, as well. Ultimately, these infusions and utility talents let you 1) generate poisons without significant cost, 2) scale their save DCs based on your kineticist level and Constitution modifier, and 3) give you multiple modes of delivery, ranging from infused blasts to natural weapons to defensive poisons. Although the poison element does have one or two tricks for dealing with poison immunity or resistance, it’s definitely still a big problem, so you should stay away in campaigns that feature constructs, oozes, outsiders, plants, and undead heavily.


Third-Party: Debuffing > Melee, Ranged > Control > Anticaster, Buffing, Recon, Skills, Summoning






	Mean Rating 
(Paizo Content)
	Mean Rating 
(3PP Content)
	Mean Rating (Combined)
	Form Infusions
	3.3
	2.0
	3.0
	Substance Infusions
	2.3
	3.3
	3.1
	All Infusions
	2.8
	3.2
	3.1
	Utility Talents
	—
	2.7
	2.7
	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Class Skills


Knowledge (Nature) is a fairly decent pick-up that I’ve commented on elsewhere. Unlike aether, which can actually use Sleight of Hand for good stuff, poison cannot, hence this skill is about as weak in poison’s hands as it would be in any Joe Schmo’s.




Defensive Talent


Corrosive Miasma: Getting spell resistance as early as 2nd level is normally really difficult to do, and it will keep you safe from a good many spells and spell-like abilities—a definite perk, since your Will save will be very iffy. Your spell resistance scales pretty slowly, though, so where a 2nd-level caster would need to roll a 9 or higher on their CL check to beat your SR 11 miasma, a 20th-level caster can beat your SR 21 miasma even on a natural 1. Add in common bonuses to CL like Spell Penetration, Greater Spell Penetration, or otherworldly kimonos, and Corrosive Miasma gets even weaker. Spending 10 points of burn on it to bump it up to SR 31 at 20th level is almost certainly not worth the cost.




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Laced Infusion: Poisons are expensive if you’re going to use them the normal way, sure, but because Laced Infusion scales the save DCs with your infusion level and Constitution modifier, you can actually end up with really good efficacy on the cheap, low-level poisons you’ll create with Biological Toxin or Master Poisoner. This infusion should work with Mahathallah’s Tier 3 Evangelist ability, too, which would allow you to lace your blasts with drugs. Anyway, every toxikineticist needs this infusion. Cheap and effective.


Burning Infusion: Burning Infusion isn’t quite the powerhouse for poison that it is for fire. Don’t get me wrong, it’s still great, but without a lot of synergy from your utility talents (Searing Flame, Smoke Sight, etc.) it’s primarily a source of attack and damage bonuses. Like I said, still well worth investing in, just not bonkers-good.


Painful Infusion: In general, one-burn infusions should not be able to lock enemies out of making any attacks for rounds at a time. I don’t know who thought Painful Infusion was a good idea as written, but they were very, very wrong.
Suggested Fix: A -1 penalty to attack rolls with one type of natural weapon (wing, bite, slam, claw, etc.) per 4 kineticist levels, minimum -1. Increase duration to 1 round + 1 round per 5 kineticist levels if they choose to expend burn. It will still help your allies on defense, but restricting you to one type of natural weapon is reasonable. You shouldn’t be able to hit a dragon with a 1st-level infusion and take away all or even most of its toys.


2nd Level


Melting Infusion: I would probably frontload the scaling of Melting Infusion a little more if I were rewriting it (maybe 2 + ⅕ kineticist level) but it’s otherwise a very solid infusion for making enemies easier to hit.


Penetrating Infusion: I personally think that Penetrating Infusion should have been a 1-burn infusion, but maybe that’s just me. Cold and fire resistance are much more common than acid resistance, hence this infusion is correspondingly better for water and fire than it is for poison.
Sickening Infusion: Sickening Infusion is hands-down better than Toxic Infusion, the first-party infusion that it mimics. Lower burn cost and longer duration are enough to elevate it to a solid green, but sickened isn’t powerful enough that it can go much beyond that. A nice opening debuff, though.


Breaching Infusion: Sunder is a pretty bad combat maneuver at the best of times, and kineticists continue to be ¾-BAB classes that can’t do much with them.


3rd Level


Neutralizing Infusion: Neutralizing Infusion feels like a tax in some ways, because you basically can’t operate as a toxikineticist without being able to penetrate poison immunity. That said, this is amazing for the kind of playstyle a poison specialist will be building toward, so pick it up on the double.


Improved Burning Infusion: Where Burning Infusion synergizes with a number of fire’s utility talents, poison has basically no support for it. Improved Burning Infusion in this element gives you an extra +1d6 ticking acid damage for an extra point of burn, which I’m inclined to think is a pretty mediocre benefit in most instances.


4th Level


Toxic Infusion: 3 burn and a Fortitude save to negate, all for the sickened condition? Is this all you conjure, Saruman? Yeah? Okay, then I’m not giving you the pass on Toxic Infusion.


Atrophy Infusion: Too slow, too weak, too expensive. Not to mention that some enemies are immune to ability damage! Since infusion save DCs scale with your level, toxikineticists are much better off using Laced Infusion to inject an enemy with poison and deal ability damage that way. They’re all poison effects, hey?


5th Level


Psychotropic Infusion: The Will save to negate is what really sells Psychotropic Infusion for me. Picture this: you’ve got a big bruiser henchman and his spellcaster boss, alone in a room. Bruiser has a bad Will save and a relatively low Wisdom score, so you hit him with Psychotropic Infusion. He fails his Will save, taking Wisdom damage and lowering his Will save even further. What’s more, he starts attacking his spellcaster boss! Now, dual mind-affecting and poison tags mean that you have to be very careful about which enemies you use this infusion on, but when it works, it really works.


Lingering Infusion: A nice little bit of extra damage added on, particularly with composite blasts, metakinesis-affected blasts, or AoE blasts. I like it.


Greater Burning Infusion: This infusion is bizarre because it’s a form infusion masquerading as a substance infusion. Why not just use Improved Burning Infusion with Cloud or Spray? In theory, you could do some interesting things with GBI by pairing it with form infusions. Shoot a Torrent that blows up every enemy in its line, causing a chain of explosions that affect enemies who are slightly off-kilter from the line. Tag an enemy with GBI while using Extreme Range to stay well back from the action. It’s always nice to have the option, of course, I just don’t think it will be worth its increased burn cost most of the time.


6th Level


Destabilizing Infusion: Cold and acid resistance are very common at this level, making Destabilizing Infusion a great pick for hydro- and toxikineticists. For vibrokineticists, who will almost never have to deal with sonic resistance, this infusion really isn’t worth your time.


Decaying Infusions: Toxikineticists have a much better alternative in Laced Infusion, which can be used to deliver contact or injury poisons that deal ticking Constitution damage for much, much less burn. Quicklime is the contact poison of choice, while black adder venom or wyvern poison can suffice from the injury poison lineup. 


7th Level


Toxic Infusion, Greater: Per the bench-pressing resource I use when determining PC benchmarks, at 14th level you’d need a save DC of 28 to reach a yellow rating on Greater Toxic Infusion. You get base 10, plus 7 for the infusion level, which means you’d need a +11 Constitution bonus to get even close to making this infusion workable—a +14 Constitution bonus, if you want to be safe. You’re nowhere near that.


8th Level


Paralyzing Infusion: The Fortitude save to negate the condition at the end of each round prevents Paralyzing Infusion from going totally overboard on the power curve. Even so, it’s a nice save-or-die that can set an enemy up for a coup de grace if your team is positioned well.




Infusions: Form


1st Level


Energize Weapon: This would be pretty good if you were playing a gestalt kineticist with a more mainstream martial class, as it’s essentially free damage on a full-attack action. Not very much damage, though, and since it locks you out of other form infusions (including Kinetic Blade and Kinetic Whip) I’m having a lot of difficulty seeing why a single-class kineticist would choose to use it.


3rd Level


Torrent: Sure, why not? Line effects are more niche than cones, but corridors are a thing in Pathfinder, and you’ll often be able to hit one or more enemies in a line. As always, energy blasts come out stronger here than anything else, so poison is sitting pretty with its simple blast. 


4th Level


Spray: With a Reflex save for half damage on top of half your damage to begin with (not to mention a pretty hefty burn cost) Spray doesn’t quite attain S-tier, but it is solidly in B-tier. It’s the easiest way for a toxikineticist to tag multiple enemies, at least until Cloud comes along. By all means, make do with Torrent if you can get foes lined up just right, but know that you may need to pay the extra burn for Spray much of the time.


6th Level


Fungal Explosion: Expensive, easily avoided, and fairly weak. If enemies are smart at all, they’re not going to spend time hanging around on your poisonous mushroom field, and by this level there’s a better-than-average chance that enemies will be flying anyway. Fungal Infestation isn’t nearly strong enough to consider building around.


7th Level


Cloud: There you go. Cloud is the total package: moveable, no save, continuous damage over multiple rounds, and even some battlefield control thrown in there for good measure. A fitting capstone for your form infusions.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Master Poisoner: Poison lore (which is an investigator class feature, by the way, not an inquisitor class feature—and I should know) is widely thought of as a terrible class feature, and is frequently traded away in the investigator class. That said, it is quite nice to be able to complement your Biological Toxins with crafted poisons: the former is “free,” at least in a financial sense, but only lasts for 24 hours; the latter will cost you some money, but lets you stock up poisons indefinitely.


Basic Toxikinesis: I don’t think it’s a spoiler to say that toxikineticists use poison a lot, so avoiding poisoning yourself is just kind of a necessary tax. Good to have, though.
Imbue Kinesis: Good only at low levels when you can’t effectively blast things and might instead need to help out an ally. It certainly doesn’t scale well at all, so I would let it go in one of your wild talent upgrades.
Mental Poison: If you’re going for a mind/poison hybrid build, Mental Poison is 100% non-optional. One of the huge problems with poison builds is that they can’t often target saves other than Fortitude. Mental Poison lets you target Will, which can be an enormous gift. Now you can craft anticaster poisons that target Fortitude saves, and antimartial poisons that target Will saves! Everyone wins, except your enemies. But, well, not everyone can win.


Detect Radioactivity: Not necessary unless you’re playing in some post-apocalyptic Fallout-style game.
Radioactive Adaptation: As with Detect Radioactivity, saves against radiation are vanishingly rare. Probably not worth a wild talent slot.


Poison Healer: Delay poison lasts for hours/level, and the fact that you can get this ability for 0 burn basically makes your entire team immune to poison permanently. Who cares if you have eight different toxins just waiting to tick? Delay poison will never run out, so you’ll never have to know about it.
Suggested Fix: Make all forms of this talent cost 1 point of burn. This busts the power curve too much, especially for a 1st-level utility talent. I mean, it’s a 2nd-level spell, for crying out loud! If you’re going to give it at will for 0 burn, the going rate for kineticist utility talents seems to be Spell Level + 2, so a 4th-level utility talent.
Slime Control: Slime Control essentially gives you a second simple and/or composite blast as a utility talent that can be taken in place of Basic Toxikinesis. This is flagrantly OP, and shouldn’t be allowed.
Suggested Fix: Eliminate the changes Slime Control makes to blast talents, simple as that. Is it still worth purchasing if you eliminate those changes? No, but I’d rather a wild talent be brokenly weak than brokenly strong.


2nd Level


Biological Toxin: Biological Toxin + Laced Infusion is a perfectly competent way to play a toxikineticist, secreting poisons from your body and then shooting them into enemies. Since combats tend to move fast, you’ll obviously want poisons with no onset time, a 1/round frequency, and either contact or ingested. Since you can’t use injury poisons with Laced Infusion until 7th level and there are surprisingly few contact poisons that have short onset times, you might want to wait a few levels before picking up Biological Toxin. Dhabba spittle, gelidburn oil, numbing poison, black adder venom, and large scorpion venom are all good candidates that are cheaper than 200 gp.


Acid’s Corrosion: Ignoring hardness is good on two counts. First, some enemies (constructs, e.g.) have hardness that you need to punch through in order to damage them. Second, ignoring hardness allows you to destroy weaponry, armor, or even the environment to your advantage. Wood, for example, has a hardness of 5, while stone has a hardness of 8 and iron or steel have hardnesses of 10. While it’ll take a while to work your way up to a full bypass of that hardness, you’ll get there eventually, and you’ll enjoy melting your way through walls, dissolving your opponents’ weapons, etc.
Oozing Form: Any amount of DR (especially DR/type) is powerful, and the fact that you can squeeze through windows, keyholes, etc. just makes Oozing Form even better. I’m taking it down a color in poison because of its laughably bad prerequisite (well, it’s laughably bad if you’re going with my much-needed edits to the talent—laughably good if you’re not).
Poison Expert: Sure, why not? A +1 to the save DCs of all your poisons is great.
Venom Spikes: Jagged Flesh is normally a terrible utility talent, but Venom Spikes gives you enough of a damage boost and enough poison changes to start making it more worth your while. Keep in mind that only Laced Infusion scales the save DC of your poisons—if you use poison on your spikes, they’ll function at whatever the base save DC is for the poison unless you have Poison Amplification, too.


Poison’s Sting: Ignoring hardness might be important if you’re trying to damage objects or some kinds of constructs, but those are pretty niche applications and the hardness reduction doesn’t scale very well. Heck, you’d have to be a 16th-level kineticist to ignore all of the hardness of non-magical stone.


3rd Level


Acid Pit: By pairing acid pit and hungry pit, you create an extremely deadly trap for enemies with poor Reflex saves and climb abilities. Highly recommended if you’re going earth + poison.
Poison Amplification: Yep. Yep yep yep. Scaling save DCs are about the only piece of the puzzle missing once you get Biological Toxin. You could have scaled them anyway with Laced Infusion, but this allows weapon and natural attack builds to get in on the fun, too.


Haphephobia: If—and this is a big if—you can get an enemy to fail their save against Haphephobia, it can be absolutely crippling to unarmed or natural attackers. Bad Touch builds like debuffing clerics and witches would probably also suffer, although they typically use things like spectral hand or a familiar to deliver their touch debuffs.


Poison Conversion: With the ability to transform ingested and inhaled poisons into contact or injury poisons, you’re really free to cherrypick the best poisons in the game for use with Poison Amplification or Laced Infusion. Unfortunately, very few ingested or inhaled poisons have immediate onset times or 1/round frequencies, which makes them of limited use in combat.
Rapid Burn: Rapid Burn isn’t nearly as good for poison/time kineticists as it is for fire/time kineticists, who have Searing Flame to help them mulch through fire resistance. Without that, Rapid Burn is just a mediocre ability for frontloading damage. Meh.


Acid Manipulator: When on earth are you going to have to manipulate large quantities of liquid acid? Seriously, has this ever happened to anyone?
Desolate: So...let me get this straight: you’re going to spend 1 point of burn to make it harder to grow crops in a 10 ft. x 10 ft. square? Yeah, no.
Fungal Infestation: Fungal Infestation mimics garden of peril, which is ultimately a fairly tame debuff spell. With a Fortitude save to negate, a low talent level, and the poison descriptor, there’s little chance that Fungal Infestation will make it into the higher levels unscathed. It gets better with Fungal Infection and Fungal Explosion, but you have to ask whether it’s worth your burn to use this talent consistently.
Greater Detect Radioactivity: Still as unnecessary as Detect Radioactivity.


4th Level


Personalized Venom: The toxicant archetype is a cool one, and toxic secretion is its ace in the hole. Personalized Venom is a fantastic way for melee toxikineticists to deal extra damage, hand out status conditions, and generally mess up anyone who tangles with them. You’ll obviously take it with Greater Biological Toxin in order to scale your save DCs appropriately.
Poison Immunity: Uhhh, sure! Why not? Skip all the foreplay in classes like investigator where you get bonuses to saves vs. poison effects and just go straight for the good stuff. This will keep you safe from a lot of nasty supernatural, extraordinary, and spell-like abilities, not to mention spells.
Psychosomatic Malady: There are multiple rounds of saves involved in most of these applications, but the fact that you can use one spell to 1) impart huge penalties to attack rolls, saves, and skill checks, 2) deal ability damage to any physical attribute in line with an injury poison, or 3) deal slow Constitution damage in line with an ingested poison all adds up to a great utility talent. It gets even better with Psychosomatic Calamity, too.


Herbal Antivenom: Quite nice, actually. Poison effects (whether actual poisons or just poison effects, such as stinking cloud) are shockingly common in Pathfinder, so a permanent +5 alchemical bonus to save against them is great, even if your Fortitude save should be so high that you just start laughing anytime a monster tries to hit you with a poison. You know what’s greater than a +5 for yourself? Being able to offer that bonus to your wimpier colleagues at no cost. Invest in the Heal skill and get cracking.


Acid Fog: Fade into the Mist is a generally poor utility talent, mostly because gaseous form is a generally poor spell. Adding minor acid damage to it doesn’t do anything to fix its weaknesses.


Remove Radioactivity: There’s only a minuscule chance that you’ll encounter radiation once in your campaign, much less enough times that you need a wild talent for it.


5th Level


Haze of Acid: I gave Shimmering Mirage a blue rating, so that seems fair here, too. Once again, a constant blur effect negates 20% of hits made against you unless the enemy has true seeing or something similar. That’s much better than any AC bonus.


Hypochondria: A one-round sickened debuff doesn’t exactly cripple enemies, but if you have a reliable way to make them sickened (allowing you to proc nauseated instead) then Hypochondria becomes significantly better.
Venom Vector: I mean, eh? Between Venom Spikes, Laced Infusion, Personalized Venom, and Poison Conversion, I’m inclined to think that most toxikineticists should have more than enough methods of delivering poison to enemies without storing one in their body.


6th Level


Greater Biological Toxin: In my mind, Greater Biological Toxin is the capstone utility talent for the poison element. At 12th level, you can create up to 3,000 gp worth of poisons for 1 point of burn, and with Laced Infusion or Poison Amplification, all of the save DCs of these poisons scale with your kineticist level and Constitution modifier. Rainbow jellyfish toxin, mage bane, purple worm poison, and black spider marsh venom are particularly nasty in the 600-800 gp price range. Muddling heresy is also excellent for destroying casters, but has some Geryon flavor that your GM may object to. As you move toward 20th level, bloodpyre, nights-eye dust, and lungtaker are good venoms in the 800-1,250 gp price range.


Devourer: Hmmm. I like being able to deal your blast damage while targets are swallowed, and I like that you can poison them while they’re in your gullet, too. The main issue with this is the size restriction. You won’t fight many Medium-sized creatures at this level, and so Devourer simply won’t work on a vast majority of enemies. I’ll give it a yellow on the off-chance that you’re in a humanoids-only campaign.


Deadly Vapors: Cloudkill as an at-will ability. Good grief. 
Suggested Fix: Given that you can also control this version of cloudkill, I think it’s reasonable to make it cost at least 1 point of burn. I mean, come on, this is widely thought of as one of the most powerful spells in the game. Don’t just give it away for free.


7th Level


Psychosomatic Calamity: Big, brutal AoE debuffs are just what I like about toxikineticists. Yeah, this is good.


Elemental Eater: How useful Elemental Eater is will depend almost entirely on on what element you’re using it with, and how prevalent that element is in your campaign. For common elements like fire, this ability could rise to a blue; for rare elements like force (aether), negative energy (void), or sonic (sound), it’ll likely be red.
Fungal Infection: The Fungal Infestation line of abilities are simply too weak to build for. It’ll do something, it just won’t do much, especially for a 7th-level utility talent.
Greater Venom Vector: Again, I have a really hard time believing that you don’t have more efficient ways to poison people by now. For crying out loud, just get Poison Conversion and Poison Amplification, convert injury or contact poisons into inhaled poisons, then throw them. Problem solved without having to spend half a dozen talents to do it.


8th Level


Greater Remove Radioactivity: There’s only a minuscule chance that you’ll encounter radiation once in your campaign, much less enough times that you need a wild talent for it.


9th Level


Clicker: Attribute bleed on all your mental scores is indeed something to be frightened of, but the Fortitude save to negate and the fact that it’s a disease—a poison? One of the two, anyway—prevents it from being too threatening at 18th-20th level.


Eternal Atrophy: Meh. This feels like something a BBEG would cast on a PC, not something that a PC would cast on an enemy. If they’re susceptible to poison effects, you’ll have them destroyed before long anyway—no need to make the ability damage permanent or anything.
🔊 Sound
[3PP] Sound


        The sound element is from Kineticists of Porphyra, a third-party supplement published by Purple Duck Games and written by N. Jolly. As a whole, it takes a lot of unsurprising cues from the bard class, including the excellent Inspiration Amp and Greater Inspiration Amp utility talents. The infusion complement is good enough—and sonic resistance rare enough—that you could do some good work as a sound blaster, especially with the Sonic Reverberation and Building Reverberation utility talents, but my main recommendation would be to jump into sound as a secondary or tertiary expanded element. Illusionists with the light element will probably come here to make their illusions more realistic, while others might just be looking to cherrypick Greater Inspiration Amp, Greater Charming Tone, and Greater Echolocation. Kineticists don’t have a lot of support to be buffers or enchantment specialists, but sound does as good a job as any element at getting you close to the mark.


Third-Party: Buffing > Anticaster, Debuffing, Ranged, Skills, Summoning > Melee, Recon > Control






	Mean Rating 
(Paizo Content)
	Mean Rating 
(3PP Content)
	Mean Rating (Combined)
	Form Infusions
	2.7
	4.0
	3.2
	Substance Infusions
	1.3
	3.0
	2.6
	All Infusions
	2.0
	3.2
	2.8
	Utility Talents
	3.0
	2.8
	2.8
	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Class Skills


Diplomacy is always solidly good for interacting with people without making enemies, although kineticists will typically be hindered by their low Charisma scores. Knowledge (Local), as always, is good for identifying humanoids and learning more about local cultures. A solid skillset.




Defensive Talent


Victorious Aria: I’m not a huge fan of defensive talents that simply replicate common wondrous items. Wood does it with Flesh of Wood by replacing your amulet of natural armor; Victorious Aria does it by replacing your cloak of resistance. Sacrificing 5 points of burn every day for the equivalent of the cheapest item in the Big Six (magic weapon/armor, ring of protection, amulet of natural armor, cloak of resistance, physical stat belt, mental stat headband) is not what I would call an excellent trade-off. If the bonus type were any type other than resistance, I would give it a blue, but since your cloak and your aria won’t stack, my recommendation is to spend 25,000 gp on a cloak of resistance +5 and save your burn for other things.




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Attuning Infusion: Attuning Infusion is useful on its own for helping you deal with cover and total cover, but a number of sound infusions also rely on it to combo with. You’ll need it for the most powerful tricks that sound can pull.


Aphasia Infusion: I’m a speech-language pathologist, and I always loved that Paizo got at least a little awareness of aphasia out into the public consciousness. It’s a pretty devastating ability to hit arcane or divine casters with; the fact that targets get a new save each round to end the effect is offset by the fact that the infusion save DC will scale with your kineticist level.


Ringing Infusion: Penalties to hearing-based Perception checks are very poor indeed. Not sure how this is strong enough even to qualify as a 1st-level infusion.


2nd Level


Sickening Infusion: Sickening Infusion is hands-down better than Toxic Infusion, the first-party infusion that it mimics. Lower burn cost and longer duration are enough to elevate it to a solid green, but sickened isn’t powerful enough that it can go much beyond that. A nice opening debuff, though.


3rd Level


Cacophonous Infusion: Magical silence is blessedly rare, but vibrokineticists will need Cacophonous Infusion for when they do run up against it. I wish this had just been turned into a utility talent—something like, “Whenever you use a kinetic blast talent, you may choose to increase the cost of this blast by 1 point of burn in order to allow it to penetrate magical silence.”


4th Level


Disorientating Infusion: Confused is an extremely good status condition—good enough that Disorientating Infusion should be your go-to substance infusion once you’ve hit as many foes on the battlefield with Attuning Infusion as you reasonably can.


5th Level


Lingering Infusion: A nice little bit of extra damage added on, particularly with composite blasts, metakinesis-affected blasts, or AoE blasts. I like it.


6th Level


Destabilizing Infusion: Cold and acid resistance are very common at this level, making Destabilizing Infusion a great pick for hydro- and toxikineticists. For vibrokineticists, who will almost never have to deal with sonic resistance, this infusion really isn’t worth your time.


7th Level


Overload Infusion: A really interesting take on a damage-over-time ability. The burn cost is obviously high at this level, but you’ll have several tiers of infusion specialization to mitigate that. Even so, it will almost certainly be a substance infusion that you can’t really pair with a form infusion due to the sheer cost of such a proposition. Fun to watch enemies melt themselves, though.




Infusions: Form


1st Level


Energize Weapon: This would be pretty good if you were playing a gestalt kineticist with a more mainstream martial class, as it’s essentially free damage on a full-attack action. Not very much damage, though, and since it locks you out of other form infusions (including Kinetic Blade and Kinetic Whip) I’m having a lot of difficulty seeing why a single-class kineticist would choose to use it.


3rd Level


Torrent: Sure, why not? Line effects are more niche than cones, but corridors are a thing in Pathfinder, and you’ll often be able to hit one or more enemies in a line. As always, energy blasts (fire and blue flame in particular) come out stronger here than anything else, so especially consider this infusion if you’re playing a pyrokineticist.


4th Level


Cyclone: Here’s the key phrase: all creatures and objects. RAW, you’re destroying not only enemies but also allies and every piece of equipment (yours included!) in the radius. And since it’s centered on you, you can never do any of the creative positioning that’s possible with effects like fireball. That’s what we in the biz like to call “bad.”


7th Level


Cloud: There you go. Cloud is what Cyclone wanted to be: moveable, no save, continuous damage over multiple rounds, and even some battlefield control thrown in there for good measure. A fitting capstone for your form infusions.


8th Level


Resonant Detonation: Once you get a few tiers of infusion specialization up and running, the tried-and-true combat flow for a vibrokineticist should be to tag as many enemies as possible with Attuning Infusion + Torrent, then use Resonant Detonation or Attunement Burst to tear those enemies apart, preferring Resonant Detonation if enemies are dispersed, and Attunement Burst if they’re tightly packed. At any rate, Resonant Detonation and Attunement Burst will be the source of most of sound’s AoE damage, which makes it a pity that they come online so late.


9th Level


Attunement Burst: If you can get a bunch of closely clustered enemies attuned with Attuning Infusion, Attunement Burst will quite simply rip them to shreds. A fitting capstone for sound.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Sonic Reverberation: If I’m being honest, I really like Sonic Reverberation. It’s not a powerhouse of an ability, so why? The answer is simple: versatility. If you need some time to get into position, if you need to wait until an ally steps out of your way, if the enemy in front of you is almost dead and you need to draw a bead on the next...you don’t want to blast now, you want to wait for the right moment. You could hold or ready an action, of course, or you could essentially double your single-target damage for the price of a standard action when conditions are more favorable for you. Heck, if you’re willing to spend burn, you can even charge up an alpha strike to have ready going into a big boss battle. That’s nice.


Basic Vibrokinesis: Ghost sound is a good cantrip, and getting access to it at will is a reasonably good first power for sound. The save DC to disbelieve won’t scale at all well, though.
Distant Voice: Message is another good cantrip. I won’t complain about this.
Voice of the Wind: Yeah, I’ll take that! Voice of the Wind is a great tool for communicating with members of your team over long distances, which never hurt anyone. A solid B.


Charming Tone: As far as 1st-level spells go, charm person is definitely one of the good ones, ensuring that your foes can become your friends and enabling diplomatic solutions that might not otherwise be possible. As a spell-like ability, too, Charming Tone will feature no verbal or somatic components, making it exceptionally difficult for enemies to tell that you’re casting a spell. Unfortunately, it relies heavily on enemies failing their save DCs, and that simply won’t happen after a few levels have gone by—enemy saves scale, but your utility talent save DCs don’t. A good candidate to ditch at 6th level, if you ever pick it up.
Imbue Kinesis: Good only at low levels when you can’t effectively blast things and might instead need to help out an ally. It certainly doesn’t scale well at all, so I would let it go in one of your wild talent upgrades.
Sound’s Intensity: It’s Ability Focus for your infusions, which is all that you really need to know if you’re using sound as your main blasting element. For anyone who’s in sound purely for utility talents, skip it.


Simple Versatility: Paizo designed kinetic blasts to be used with only the infusions they get on the label; opening up other infusions often has unintended consequences, and is certainly way too powerful for a 1st-level utility talent. See, e.g., Composite Versatility and Improved Composite Versatility, which have burn costs and per-day use limits, for an example of how you might be able to expand access to infusions without breaking the power curve.
Suggested Fix: Pick one infusion belonging to your element’s other simple blast; you can apply that to the first simple blast. None of this BS about applying every infusion you want.


2nd Level


Urgent Restoration: Being able to heal yourself as a swift action is an incredible boost in combat effectiveness, even if you are going to have to take some burn to do it. Much appreciated for tanking builds.


Absolute Silence: A vibrokineticist wouldn’t be my first choice for a recon specialist, so I don’t know how useful the Stealth bonuses will be, but turning on silence at will is an interesting ploy to get in close with casters and shut down their verbal components. Watch out, though, because Victorious Aria and your elemental overflow bonuses comprise a significant proportion of your defense and offense. Don’t just leave this ability on willy-nilly; turn it on tactically to avoid alerting enemies to your team’s presence.
Greater Voice of the Wind: Ooh, yeah, some nice upgrades here. I’m happy to give both Voice of the Wind and its big cousin the pass they deserve; they’re utility and intrigue abilities, but then again these are utility wild talents. If you’ve got no utility to go with your utility wild talents, you’re what I like to call “a pyrokineticist.” Oh! Body blow.
Sound Mimicry: Sure, some more bard-like abilities here. I like it.


Deafening Burst: Fortitude negates, spell resistance yes, low save DC, weakish status condition. Even the Greater version of Deafening Burst doesn’t get significantly better.


3rd Level


Inspiration Amp: Inspiration Amp is a bonus-for-bonus recreation of the bard’s inspire courage class feature, which is widely acknowledged to be one of the best buffs in the game. You can’t use it on multiple allies—not yet, at least—but it’s an excellent thing to spend burn on in tough fights. Definitely an element-defining utility talent.


Building Reverberation: You’re starting to be able to layer some fairly significant damage here, and since there’s nothing to stop you from gathering power as a full-round action (or more) you could gather power, layer in an empowered composite blast, gather power, layer in an empowered composite blast, and just continue until you reach your maximum. It’s an excellent way to turn a boss into a paste on the back wall after one shot, especially if you’re willing to take on some burn to hold the charge for 10 minutes/level.


Terrifying Roar: Since there’s no spell resistance and a Will save only reduces the fear condition to shaken, you can think of Terrifying Roar as a Dazzling Display with no chance of failure. It explicitly affects only enemies, too, so you’re safe to just scare the crap out of your opponents in every fight, so long as they’re not immune to fear or mind-affecting effects.


Slumbering Serenade: Being woken out of sleep is fairly easy in Pathfinder if you have allies, and the lack of a scaling save DC will put Slumbering Serenade into deeper and deeper red territory as time goes on. But maybe it’s worth it to you to lull your allies off to sleep each night with a little berceuse?


Quivering Defense: Base kineticists aren’t proficient with shields, and need two hands free with which to gather power; legendary kineticists can gather power one-handed, but still aren’t proficient with shields. About the only builds that Quivering Defense would even apply to are kinetic knight builds, and this ability isn’t good enough for them to merit a yellow rating. You want people deafened, use Deafening Burst.
Shatter: Shatter is a generally bad spell, even when used at will. Even the combat applications vs. crystalline creatures are superseded by the fact that you’re a kineticist who blasts stuff professionally.


4th Level


Greater Charming Tone: A better save DC for a much, much better spell. Charm monster lasts days/level, affects any creature type, and is generally bonkers-good. If I were a GM, I’d make this cost 1 point of burn so that it doesn’t become abusable, although the low effective spell level will eventually catch up with your save DC and make it less effective.


Greater Deafening Burst: Even with a better save DC and radius, the deafened condition still doesn’t hit hard enough to make Greater Deafening Burst an unqualified “buy.”
Healing Burst: The inability to direct where the burn goes and half healing knocks the ability down too far for it to be consistently worth purchasing. It’s way better if you have positive blast, but if you have positive blast, you’re the world’s biggest chump anyway.


Clangorous Shroud: Although it’s nice to be able to get past the nonsensical shield requirement for Quivering Defense, that ultimately wasn’t the problem with the power—the problem is that clangorous just isn’t a great shield enchantment. I’m still no fan.


Powerful Motivation: You generally shouldn’t be able to grant allies extra actions for several rounds, even by accepting burn, and even for just a move action. Actions are power in Pathfinder.
Suggested Fix: This really just needs to be vetoed. I can’t think of a way that the original design intent can be respected while making it less powerful.


5th Level


Auditory Hallucination: Like Visual Hallucination in the light element, the only real restriction on Auditory Hallucination is your creativity. Well, and your save DCs. No way around that, though, because every sound-based illusion grants an automatic save to disbelieve it.
Echolocation: Blindsense is fine, and the action economy, burn cost, and duration is all good enough that I’m enthusiastic about giving Echolocation a green rating. The real reason we’re here is to progress to blindsight, though. That’s where the money is.


6th Level


Greater Inspiration Amp: There ya go: you can finally inspire courage as a bard of your level - 1. It’s a crazy benefit, and you’ll darn well take it right at 12th level.


Force Gloves: It’s a real pity that Quivering Defense is such a bad utility talent, because Force Gloves is pretty good. The shield bonus is fine, I guess, but the real headliner is the jarring shield quality your gloves take on when you’re under the effects of Force Ward. Every time someone hits you and you have HP remaining in the buffer, as an immediate action you can make them save or be dazed for one round. Gets you some breathing room, at least.


Kinetic Regeneration: This shouldn’t be a major improvement to Kinetic Healer unless you’re regularly losing limbs to attacks. I did not see that coming!


7th Level


Greater Echolocation: Blindsight allows you to actually locate invisible enemies, whereas blindsense only allows you to detect their absence or presence. This is a non-trivial difference. Now that you can see invisible enemies, cut through illusions, supernatural darkness, mists, fogs, and smokes, etc., there are very few caster tricks that can fool you within 30 feet. An excellent pick-up, and well worth the less-than-inspiring prerequisite.


Elemental Eater: How useful Elemental Eater is will depend almost entirely on on what element you’re using it with, and how prevalent that element is in your campaign. For common elements like fire, this ability could rise to a blue; for rare elements like force (aether), negative energy (void), or sonic (sound), it’ll likely be red.


9th Level


Sound Chamber: You truly are the master of all audio with Sound Chamber, although it feels a little underwhelming for a 9th-level ability. I guess have fun convincing 1st-level commoners that they’re hearing voices? You’ve earned it, you weirdo.
⌚ Time
[3PP] Time


        Time is a third-party element from Kineticists of Porphyra, a splatbook written by N. Jolly and published by Purple Duck Games. I’m quite comfortable saying it’s the most riotously overpowered element in the game. It’s positively crammed with team buffs and enemy debuffs, almost always so strong that there’s no way I would ever allow them at my table. Heck, I’d feel bad using a lot of time’s abilities as a player, and that tells you a lot. Now, it’s not all good news: time has a paucity of form infusions, so you’ll almost never be able to deal AoE damage unless you expand to a different element, but good lord, clear the chickens off the runway if you ever let anyone have unfettered access to this element. I had to rewrite or veto a full 50% of time’s utility talents just to prevent it from being classified as a TTRPG war crime.


Third-Party: Debuffing > Anticaster, Buffing > Ranged, Recon > Melee, Skills > Control, Summoning






	Mean Rating 
(Paizo Content)
	Mean Rating 
(3PP Content)
	Mean Rating (Combined)
	Form Infusions
	—
	2.5
	2.5
	Substance Infusions
	—
	3.1
	3.1
	All Infusions
	—
	3.0
	3.0
	Utility Talents
	4.0
	2.9
	3.0
	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Class Skills


Appraise and Knowledge (History) are both pretty worthless, although if you’re playing with Background Skills you can at least dump one rank in each without detracting from your adventuring skills. Womp womp.




Defensive Talent


Distorted Stream: Miss chances are always more useful than AC: at some point, no matter how hard you build for it, nearly every enemy will hit your AC, but miss chances never go out of style. And these miss chances are actually quite good! By 4th level you can get the equivalent of constant blur, and you can get fairly close to displacement levels of concealment in later levels. Toss in expanded element (earth) to get some DR/adamantine, and you’ll be set to pun like a machine gun. “Looks like it’s time…” *Puts on sunglasses* “...to rock.”




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Hindering Infusion: With no save and a great effect that scales well into the endgame as monsters’ reach gets bigger and bigger, there’s no reason not to love Hindering Infusion. It’s especially good if you have allies that generally rely on flanking (slayers, rogues, inquisitors, hunters, etc.) because you can help those allies skirt easily around large enemies to get into position.


Lagging Infusion: A -2 to attack rolls is a fairly weak debuff, and time has enough other options that you’ll rarely find a need for this infusion.


3rd Level


Diverging Infusion: Don’t give me any of that guff about “disadvantage.” Disadvantage isn’t a thing in Pathfinder. We’re not in 5e anymore, Toto. Anyway, I’m inclined to think well of Diverging Infusion, but it’s definitely more powerful if you’re coordinating with someone to land a big save-or-suck effect on a BBEG. Penalties to attack rolls will eventually be mitigated by monsters having insane to-hit bonuses and oodles of attacks, but saving throws never go out of style. Can you imagine working with a witch with Icy Prison, and you’ve just forced them to reroll their saves against it? (Okay, maybe a bad example, since a witch would just use quickened ill omen, but you get the idea.) A Will save to negate is all that holds Diverging Infusion back.


4th Level


Immobilizing Infusion: I feel like this one is a little unfair, because enemies who are spec’ed for melee combat simply won’t be able to function if they can’t move from their squares.
Suggested Fix: Give targets a new save every round, but a longer duration to compensate (maybe 1 round/2 kineticist levels). You’ll still get some effects, but targets won’t be crippled with no secondary save.


5th Level


Dismissing Infusion: Outsiders are incredibly common as high-level enemies, and they usually come packed to the rafters with spell-like abilities, weird auras, exotic DR, energy immunities, etc. that can make a kineticist’s life hell. Think of Dismissing Infusion as a save-or-die effect against these enemies, forcing them out of your plane on a failed save. A good candidate for Improved/Greater Flurry of Blasts and Empty Infusion for when you need to banish something now.


Lingering Infusion: A nice little bit of extra damage added on, particularly with composite blasts, metakinesis-affected blasts, or AoE blasts. I like it.


Dazing Infusion: It’s expensive, and the duration is a little short for the cost. Still, dazed is a pretty powerful status condition.


6th Level


Decaying Infusions: Constitution damage is extraordinarily dangerous: it tanks the Fortitude save, lowers HP, makes stabilizing at negative HP harder, and more. Decaying Infusion is expensive and may not be fast enough for rocket-tag fights that are decided in only a few rounds, but protracted battles can almost always yield good results for this infusion.


7th Level


Overload Infusion: A really interesting take on a damage-over-time ability. The burn cost is obviously high at this level, but you’ll have several tiers of infusion specialization to mitigate that. Even so, it will almost certainly be a substance infusion that you can’t really pair with a form infusion due to the sheer cost of such a proposition. Fun to watch enemies melt themselves, though.


8th Level


Displacing Infusion: Feels a lot like the kineticist and the Erase from Time revelation from the oracle’s time mystery had a baby. Removing enemies from combats is aces, of course, especially since they can’t buff, summon, heal, survey or control the battlefield, call for reinforcements, deal damage, etc. while they’re removed from the timestream. And you can set up all kinds of nasty surprises for when they return! Plop them into the middle of a field of etheric shards, surround them with a wall of force, open a hungry pit with mad monkeys at the bottom...the possibilities are limitless.




Infusions: Form


1st Level


Suspended Impact: Just doesn’t seem worth the burn to me. They could’ve made this a utility talent, and I’d have given it the go-ahead.


4th Level


Hyper-Dimensional Blast: Ignoring cover and targeting flat-footed AC is awesome, especially when you’re getting Extended Range rolled in for free. Particularly well-suited for physical blasts, which will have a harder time hitting enemies in general.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Elemental Acceleration: This is basically the monk’s fast movement class feature, for the cost of a utility talent. Movement speed rarely breaks the power curve, so I don’t feel the need to veto this one, but it is very good. Aerokineticists in particular will appreciate the bonuses to their flight speed from Wings of Air.


Basic Chronokinesis: Since Basic Chronokinesis is free, sure, I won’t turn my nose up at it. Kinda like guidance + mending. You should absolutely trade it in as soon as you can for Distorted Timeline, Elemental Acceleration, or Future Glimpse, though. (Remember that you can retrain any utility talents that aren’t prerequisites for something else!)
Distorted Timeline: A -1 penalty might not seem like a lot, and truthfully, it isn’t, but with no save and applying to basically every d20 roll they make for multiple rounds, it’s definitely good enough for me.


Imbue Kinesis: Good only at low levels when you can’t effectively blast things and might instead need to help out an ally. It certainly doesn’t scale well at all, so I would let it go in one of your wild talent upgrades.


Future Glimpse: Even with its burn and action cost, Future Glimpse is really powerful. Because the wording about whether or not you can apply Future Glimpse to other people’s rolls (enemies’ or allies’) is ambiguous, I’m suggesting a change to tone down the power here.
Suggested Fix: Future Glimpse now applies only to d20 rolls that you make. No more rerolling d100 teleportation mishaps or miss chances from concealment; no more rerolling damage on a kinetic blast; no more monkeying with allies or enemies. Only your d20 rolls. Something like: “As an immediate action after you roll a d20 but before the result of the check is known, you may choose to take 1 point of burn in order to reroll the d20. You must take the result of the second d20 roll, even if the result is worse.”


2nd Level


Seize the Initiative: Kineticists can do a lot with a full action complement during the surprise round. Great stuff.
Urgent Restoration: Being able to heal yourself as a swift action is an incredible boost in combat effectiveness, even if you are going to have to take some burn to do it. Much appreciated for tanking builds.


Regressive Healing: I’ve always given Kinetic Healer a green rating, and that seems right here. The ability to heal constructs and objects doesn’t particularly add anything to the package.


Temporal Preparation: Expending 1 point of burn in order to gain your Constitution modifier to initiative and be able to act in every surprise round more or less permanently is way too powerful, especially for a 2nd-level utility talent.
Suggested Fix: If it were me, I would alter this utility talent so that you could choose to take 1 point of burn at the time that initiative is rolled in order to add your Constitution modifier to your initiative roll and act in a surprise round. It certainly shouldn’t be constant.
Time’s Echo: Time’s Echo will only be of use if you’re using infusions on time blasts consistently, but I’m still proposing some nerfs. The three upgrade utility talents (Time’s Powerful Echo, Time’s Cruel Echo, and Time’s Unending Echo) are way too powerful for mainstream play, so I’m vetoing those outright.
Suggested Fix: Decrease the save DC by 1 each round. This is how real-life echoes actually work, and ensures that you can’t stun-lock enemies with a 2nd-level utility talent.


3rd Level


Celerity: Haste is one of those spells that’s so indispensable that literally every encounter is made easier by its addition. And at-will haste? Well, at-will haste is simply too tasty to ever be ignored, especially if you can open up some 3rd-level spell slots for your caster friends by expending a little burn.


Decelerate: It’s the debuffing counterpart to Celerity! Slow is a good spell, but you’ll generally want to save it for when you’re up against hordes of weaker enemies—high-level targets will most likely pass the Fortitude save here. Probably a good candidate for retraining later on, since you can’t effectively scale the save DC.


Dimensional Rip / Improved / Greater / Deceptive / Enlarged / Invasive / Vacuum / Corporeal: The dimensional ripper archetype is really, really cool, allowing you to tear holes in reality and shove enemies and blasts through them like you’re playing a game of Portal. Both the archetype and the utility talents that mimic many of the archetype’s class features are massively overpowered, however; as it stands, there’s little reason to play another archetype—and certainly not a first-party archetype—when dimensional ripper is available. It takes a lot of third-party options away from aether, time, and void, but if you want my advice, leave these wild talents out of your games.
Suggested Fix: Eliminating the various upgrades to Dimensional Rip might do it, honestly, along with bumping Dimensional Rip up to a 5th-level ability. Keep the burn cost at one, for sure.
Focused Celerity: I vetoed the Kinetic Acceleration feat for obvious reasons, and it should be equally obvious why receiving functionally unlimited haste for yourself with a cheaper action cost is going to be vetoed, too.
Suggested Fix: Time is already an element that skews toward too powerful, and it doesn’t need any help. Chronokineticists have Celerity; let ‘em be happy with that. Veto.


4th Level


Flash Step: So it’s a quick runner’s shirt, but as a utility talent. There’s certainly a lot you can do here, not least gathering energy twice in a single turn. If you don’t use your swift or immediate actions for much (e.g., quicken metakinesis) you’re probably safe to use this quite liberally.


Improved Celerity: Turning ¾-BAB classes into full-BAB classes is something that should be done with the greatest of care. Don’t hand out these kinds of bonuses willy-nilly.
Suggested Fix: Give players the option when accepting 1 point of burn to cast Celerity with a duration of rounds/level to also accept an amount of burn equal to the increase in BAB they’d like, to a maximum BAB of their kineticist level. If you’re a 20th-level kineticist, for example, with BAB +15, you could accept 5 points of burn to be treated as BAB +20 whenever you accept 1 point of burn to cast Celerity with a duration of rounds/level. Sure, you’ll be way more deadly, but now you’ll also have to take 100 points of damage in order to become so.
Time’s Powerful Echo: Yeesh, time, enough with the OP stuff already. Time’s Powerful Echo can set enemies up in a loop where they fail a save, the save DC gets higher, so they fail it again, so the save DC gets higher again, and on and on. While this is narratively cool, it’s way too powerful. For example, how would you like it if your party (wizard, rogue, cleric, fighter) got hit with an AoE Dazing Infusion? A Fortitude save negates, but your wizard and rogue fail, getting dazed for one round. At the start of their next round, the DC to save increases by 2, causing them to fail again; the save DC spiral keeps going until they roll a natural 20 and are shaken out of it. Hammering an opponent’s weak saves is good tactics, but hammering them such that they can never successfully save is a cheap trick.
Suggested Fix: Veto. Nothing more needs to be said.
Time Thief: I’m very wary in general of third-party content that takes away actions from enemies or gives actions to you. Time Thief happens to do both. A Will save negates, of course, and there’s spell resistance to consider, but this utility talent is still just too powerful.
