Players, if you would feel like your GM was cheating by using a particular wild talent, that’s a wild talent that should be removed from the game.
Suggested Fix: I got nothing. Veto.


5th Level


Time Skip: Light Speed Travel is an excellent utility talent; short-range teleportation never goes out of style. Time Skip does the same thing, so it’s just as good.


Flicker Charge: It’s an interesting mobility tool, and certainly helpful when you need to kite an enemy. It’s a pity that the elements that get Flicker Charge aren’t usually the ones you’d be building a melee attacker with, though.


Grim Future: It’s pretty widely accepted that the witch’s misfortune hex is one of the most powerful single-target debuffs in the game, and Grim Future is essentially a better version of misfortune: better range, no daily use limit per target, better duration if you choose to accept burn. Hands down, better. It’s kind of unacceptable for the kineticist to be this good at debuffing with no prerequisites.
Suggested Fix: For starters, it needs spell resistance. Make it cost 1 point of burn, always, with no option to extend the duration beyond one round. Even with all those changes, it still easily rates a blue or purple, which should tell you how powerful the misfortune hex is.
Mutable Future: STOP WRITING SUCH POWERFUL ABILITIES, TIME. For crying out loud. Rerolls are powerful enough without getting the opportunity to reroll your rerolls. 
Suggested Fix: Make Future Glimpse a prerequisite, then rewrite as follows: “Whenever you accept burn to use Future Glimpse, you may accept an additional point of burn to roll the reroll twice, taking the better result. You must decide to use Mutable Future prior to making any rerolls.”
Time’s Cruel Echo: As if Time’s Echo wasn’t already powerful enough. I’m sorry, but the time element really has a problem with third-party power creep.
Suggested Fix: My inclination is just to veto. Extending the duration of substance infusions for free is already powerful enough without adding on increased save DCs, more damage, etc. Just get rid of it.


6th Level


Failsafe: Contingency is always wicked powerful, although the relatively constrained choices you have available in utility wild talents drops it down from purple.
Long-Distance Travel: Long-distance teleportation is universally useful, and this talent is no exception.


Chronological Defiance: Yeah, this one is pretty nice, and I like that it’s not massively overpowered. The duration won’t necessarily be long enough for you to escape getting tagged by the condition eventually, but it’s an interesting gimmick to make spellcasters think that you’re immune to stuff.
Temporal Interruption: There are lots of fun uses for Temporal Interruption if you’re a creative thinker. Maybe you’re about to get walloped by a full-attack action or an area of effect spell, but you spend an immediate action to Time Skip out of the way. Maybe you get ambushed and want to use Celerity on yourself real quickly for the extra AC and movement speed as you run away. Maybe you’d like to throw up a wall with Greater Kinetic Cover, or go invisible with Optical Camouflage/Telekinetic Invisibility. There are plenty of good uses for Temporal Interruption.


Dimensional Traveler: Very narrowly useful for Kinetic Blade, Kinetic Whip, Visceral Whip, and natural attack builds, for whom Dimensional Traveler basically acts as pounce. If you’re a run-of-the-mill ranged kineticist using a kinetic blast once a round, Dimensional Traveler won’t do a thing for you.
Kinetic Regeneration: This shouldn’t be a major improvement to Kinetic Healer unless you’re regularly losing limbs to attacks. I did not see that coming!
Kinetic Revivification: I’m of the opinion that other classes should generally be handling this kind of thing, but there’s no denying that resurrection is powerful in a pinch. It’s definitely worth considering if you’re playing a kinetic chirurgeon. 


Devolve: You don’t really fight animal companions, familiars, and eidolons often enough to make this worth your while, and creatures will mostly just run if they’re “acting in their own best interests.”


7th Level


Memories On Tap: Drinking people’s memories and abilities, eh? That’s a cool concept. Seems to me that you could just pay people to give up their feats and spells to you, especially since they don’t lose the feat or spell you take. “Hey, can I have Power Attack for the day?” “Sure, that’ll be 50 silver.” That’s as complicated as it should be to open a business in this country.


Improved Dimensional Traveler: Even for Kinetic Blade, Kinetic Whip, Visceral Whip, and natural attack builds, Dimensional Maneuvers and Dimensional Savant are not as necessary as Dimensional Assault and Dimensional Dervish. Can be nice if you’re using whips to trip or reposition foes, flank with yourself, etc., though.


Past Recollection: Legend lore is a highly mediocre spell, even for learning information. Nah.


Greater Time Thief: I will absolutely not sanction stealing move actions from enemies and giving them to yourself. Way, way too powerful.
Suggested Fix: Veto. Not even a master of time should (mechanically) be able to do this, even if it works narratively.
Time’s Unending Echo: All three of the upgrades to Time’s Echo are too powerful. With Time’s Cruel Echo increasing the save DC in each echo loop, you can just immolate enemies without batting an eyelash.
Suggested Fix: Veto.


8th Level


Kinetic Purification: Heal is a phenomenal spell not only for the amount of HP it heals but also for all the status conditions it cures. This utility talent comes in very late, so what else are you going to spend it on?


9th Level


Grand Intercession: Huh? Time stop rated only green? Yes, actually. The power of time stop in the hands of sorcerers, psychics, wizards, arcanists, etc. is that they have a lot of options once they’ve stopped time: summon a bunch of extraplanar allies, partition the battlefield, buff themselves to the gills, teleport away, etc. As a kineticist, many of those options are simply closed to you; the class is a striker class at heart, and what’s a striker to do when they can’t strike anyone?
🥩 Viscera
[3PP] Viscera


        Viscera, a third-party element written by N. Jolly and released in one of the Kineticists of Porphyra books, is definitely the most grotesque of all the elements available to kineticists, giving you the power to sew enemies’ mouths shut, surround yourself with a mist of gore drawn from the entrails of dead enemies, rip out your own guts and use them as whips, and grow bony exoskeletons to keep you safe. Viscera’s biggest calling card is the Unnatural Evolution line of utility talents, which grant eidolon evolutions to your kineticist. Although these evolutions can occasionally be used creatively (e.g., to pick up multiple instances of Skilled and create the world’s foremost kineticist skill monkey) they’re typically used for what Pathfinder players always use eidolon evolutions for: turning you into a giant buzzsaw of natural attacks. Viscera can eventually turn into one of the premiere natural attackers in the game through Bone Blades, Licker, Unnatural Evolution, Explosive Evolution, etc., or you can go with the Visceral Whip line of utility talents if you’d rather build a more straightforward (if late-blooming) melee attacker. Either way, viscera’s strength lies in its ability to build sturdy, powerful melee combatants. You could try to do something different with it, of course, but my job is to tell you where the abilities point.


Third-Party: Melee > Debuffing, Ranged > Anticaster, Control > Recon, Summoning > Buffing






	Mean Rating 
(Paizo Content)
	Mean Rating 
(3PP Content)
	Mean Rating (Combined)
	Form Infusions
	4.0
	2.0
	3.5
	Substance Infusions
	1.0
	2.6
	2.5
	All Infusions
	3.3
	2.5
	2.7
	Utility Talents
	3.0
	2.7
	2.7
	N.B.: Mean ratings are based on the ordinal scale used in these guides: 1 = Red, 2 = Yellow, 3 = Green, etc.
Yes, statisticians, I know that mean scores aren’t appropriate for ordinal data. Don’t at me.




Class Skills


Disguise is applicable in this element because of the Mutable Visage utility talent, although it will be of little use otherwise, and Knowledge (Dungeoneering) is used for identifying aberrations and oozes. Heck, if you play a corpokineticist right, you’ll be more than part aberration yourself. Other people should really start using that skill to identify you.




Defensive Talent


Reactive Skin: Energy resistances are a little less universally powerful than DR, but still great to have in a pinch. On its own, though, Reactive Skin is a poor substitute for spells like resist energy: the amount of resistance you get is puny, the burn costs to scale up are horrendous, and changing the element your skin guards against is both slow and resource-heavy. Picking up the Adaptive Skin utility talent will at least solve that last problem, but won’t do anything to make your resistances more powerful. On the whole, not one of the game’s more effective defensive talents.




Infusions: Substance


1st Level


Bloody Infusion: Several classes of enemy are outright immune to bleed damage, and it can be healed almost by accident if you’re fighting creatures with fast healing, regeneration, or divine magic. Still, Bloody Infusion is cheap, scales nicely with composite blasts, and deals free damage over time.


Persistent Infusion: Probably the coolest part about Persistent Infusion is that it leaves parts of your element lying around that can then be used for utility talents, etc. Kind of like giving yourself an alley-oop in conditions where there’s not a lot of your element present naturally. The caltrop part is nice, but caltrops have an attack bonus of +0, which will fail to hit many 1st-level enemies, let alone higher-level enemies. Probably jumps up to at least a green if you have cheap form infusions like Kinetic Bomb or Improved Kinetic Bomb.
Shivering Infusion: A minor debuff that loses effectiveness early in the game. Might be of limited use for annoying casters, although anyone who’s invested even a little in their concentration checks will likely beat the save DC.


2nd Level


Sickening Infusion: Sickening Infusion is hands-down better than Toxic Infusion, the first-party infusion that it mimics. Lower burn cost and longer duration are enough to elevate it to a solid green, but sickened isn’t powerful enough that it can go much beyond that. A nice opening debuff, though.
Weakening Infusion: The more teammates you have in position, ready to take a full-attack action against an enemy, the better Weakening Infusion will do. It’s the rare infusion that actually gets more useful as time goes on.


4th Level


Disabling Infusion: We’re getting expensive here, but Disabling Infusion is highly versatile and boasts a decent duration that more than compensate for its shortcomings. Great to be able to nullify things like truesight and blindsight, too.


Atrophy Infusion: Too slow, too weak, too expensive. Not to mention that some enemies are immune to ability damage!


5th Level


Self-Destructive Infusion: Also known as the “Stop hitting yourself!” infusion. Assuming you can sneak this baby past that Fortitude save, it truly can be destructive for an enemy. Either they have to waste an entire full-round action trying to throw off the compulsion to smack themselves in the face, or they have to use a swift action and deal a bunch of damage to themselves. It’s a heads-you-win, tails-they-lose kind of infusion both in terms of action economy and damage. Unfortunately, the enemies you most want to hurt themselves will also be the most likely to pass the Fortitude save.


Improved Bloody Infusion: Given the drastically increased cost, I still don’t think Improved Bloody Infusion is much better than its younger cousin. For blood kineticists, though, it probably comes out to at least a green.


6th Level


Decaying Infusion: Constitution damage is extraordinarily dangerous: it tanks the Fortitude save, lowers HP, makes stabilizing at negative HP harder, and more. Decaying Infusion is expensive and may not be fast enough for rocket-tag fights that are decided in only a few rounds, but protracted battles can almost always yield good results for this infusion.


Leech Infusion: There’s no save involved here, so that’s something, but maximum, maximum temporary HP you could expect to get from this infusion would be about 15 HP from fighting a CR 20 enemy with 30 HD. For 4 points of burn, I can’t see how that’s worth it in most situations.
Slumber Infusion: It’ll render enemies helpless just like Lock Up Infusion, but I’d sooner dole out paralysis on the cheap than sleep for a higher cost. Sleep can be broken pretty easily; paralysis can’t, except with magic.


Hypertensive Infusion: Let’s think for a second. On a normal blast, you’d deal damage immediately and have done. Using Hypertensive Infusion, a much longer process is involved: in Round 1, you need to hit the enemy and they need to fail a Fortitude save to even be affected by the infusion; in Round 2, they need to fail a second Fortitude save; in Round 3, they need to fail a third Fortitude save. Then and only then—after three failed Fortitude saves over three rounds—do you deal your blast’s damage. For 4 points of burn. It’s slow, it’s expensive, it targets the strongest save by level, and there’s no guarantee that you’ll even get your blast damage in return. No.


7th Level


Crippling Infusion: This is the equivalent of a save-or-suck effect for enemies that are heavily reliant on one type of natural weapon or appendage; the large burn cost offsets the power to achieve a nice balance.


8th Level


Imprisoning Infusion: Ice Tomb is a fantastic witch hex, and it does just as well for a kineticist as it does for a witch. The Fortitude save to negate is a bit unfortunate, as is the high cost, but save-or-suck effects tend to be like this.


9th Level


Eviscerating Infusion: Even on a successful save, you’ll get at least 1 point of Constitution bleed, which can make Eviscerating Infusion a nice effect to slap on a boss right as combat begins—they’ll either have to slow themselves down healing it, or else continue to watch their HP and Fortitude save dribble away into the gutter. Used with an AoE form infusion, you’ve got a whole lot of panicking enemies.




Infusions: Form


2nd Level


Osteotomy: On its own, Osteotomy isn’t very noteworthy. The blast only hits if the enemy fails their save, and Fortitude saves are notoriously high among enemy creatures—not to mention that the creature needs to have a skeleton in order for it to work at all. Caltrops are pretty ineffective, too, with a +0 BAB. Kinetic Bomb + the Kinetic Remnants feat can do a better job with caltrops than Osteotomy can. I say “On its own…” because Osteotomy is the subject of one specific utility talent upgrade that adds a lot of power to these bone slivers. We’ll get to Divisive Osteotomy later.


3rd Level


Impale: Skewering enemies on a long fragment of bone is an undeniably metal image, and as usual Impale gets a good rating for being a fairly cheap form infusion. You’ll have to rely on universal form infusions like Kinetic Bomb if you want to hit tightly clustered enemies that aren’t in a line—viscera doesn’t get any in-house tools for that until Fragmentation at 7th level.


Foe Throw: I’m generally a fan of Foe Throw. It’s cool as heck, works well on puny casters who you want to jankily teleport to your team, and doesn’t cost too much. The Fortitude save is definitely going to be problematic against many enemies in the later game, so pick your targets wisely: rogues, wizards, sorcerers, that kind of thing.


7th Level


Fragmentation: Bone shards everywhere! Fragmentation got a great rating in earth, and it works as well if not better for viscera, which can apply the infusion to basically all of its simple and composite blasts.




Utility Talents


1st Level


Unnatural Evolution: Eidolon evolutions applied to PCs are incredibly powerful; every corpokineticist will want to take this utility talent and its two upgrades. I’ll rate the evolutions in their own section below, but there are a few general paths you’ll want to consider taking:
   * Magic Attacks (1 Point) → Limbs (2 Points) → Claws (1 Point) + Rend (2 Points): This is probably the most effective path if you just want to become a natural attack blender. Throw in Bone Blades + Metal Blades, and you’ll have four claw attacks that can penetrate DR/magic, alignment, cold iron, silver, and adamantine, plus the ability to rend if any two claw attacks hit.
   * Magic Attacks (1 Point) → Flight (2 Points) → Wing Buffet (1 Point) + Gore (2 Points): This path nets you one primary natural attack and two secondary natural attacks, plus the ability to fly. Perhaps a little less powerful in combat, but far more maneuverable.
   * Magic Attacks (1 Point) → Head (2 Points) → Bite (1 Point) + Bite (1 Point) + Bite (1 Point): A bit of a dark horse, this path focuses on getting you two heads, each of which can bite for 1d6 + 1.5 * Strength. Probably less powerful, on the whole, but a two-headed kineticist certainly sends a message, and the message is, “Oh, f#@&!”
   * Skilled all the way: If you’d rather not build yourself into a human blender, you can just select the Skilled evolution six times over the course of these three talents, getting a +8 to skills each time you do. Not gonna lie, this is the low-key best way to build a kineticist skill monkey. And they said it couldn’t be done.


Bone Blades: Everyone can benefit from Bone Blades. If you’re making a natural attack build, of course, Bone Blades is the easiest and fastest way to pick up three natural attacks at 1st level, but even ranged builds can benefit from being able to threaten in melee, flank with melee allies, etc. The Prototype sample build goes into greater depth on this playstyle.


Petrified Innards: The main problem with Petrified Innards is that it doesn’t stack with the ability to negate crits or sneak attack damage granted by elemental overflow. Petrified Innards scales much faster than elemental overflow, though, and that may be worth it to you.


Basic Corpokinesis: Probably the best argument for Basic Corpokinesis is stabilize, and even that’s not a great argument. I’m genuinely uncertain why you’re being given a pass for Eldritch Heritage—seems narratively unconnected to being able to control your body—but your Charisma score likely won’t be high enough for that feat line anyway. Definitely swap this out for Bone Blades or Unnatural Evolution once you get your first utility talent retrain level.
Emerging Form: Vestigial arm is useful for alchemists because they have such a limb-intensive playstyle, drawing bombs, potions, extracts, elixirs, alchemical items, weapons, etc. at a moment’s notice. For kineticists, who are non-casters with no weapons to use, the utility is kind of lost. I could endorse one instance of Emerging Form, but certainly not three.
Kinetic Cover: Flexible access to total cover is always to be applauded, but the mini-wall has laughably few HP and no hardness—a stiff breeze will destroy this thing, much less an enemy’s concentrated effort. Still, powerful enough to merit a yellow. There’s a particularly fun episode of Side Quest Side Sesh on the Glass Cannon Network in which this wild talent saves a bunch of PCs’ lives.
Tongue Twister: Agile Tongue is of only marginal use to corpokineticists, but opens up access to the Licker utility talent, which will be of great interest to natural attack builds. You’re safe skipping Tongue Twister unless you’re building for natural attacks.


Corpomancy: Prognostication is fun, but mechanically unnecessary. If you can pull out a creature’s entrails, you don’t really need to know what class levels it had—it’s dead.
Elemental Limb: Elemental Limb is a bad utility talent (+2 to Strength checks? Really?) but it does open the way for Improved and Greater Elemental Limb, both of which are pretty much required for natural attack builds.
Spined Armor: Why would you do this? Kineticists are far more strapped for feats and utility talents than they are for money; just buy some armor spikes if you really want this perk.


2nd Level


Oozing Form: Any amount of DR (especially DR/type) is powerful, and the fact that you can squeeze through windows, keyholes, etc. just makes Oozing Form even better. Mutable Visage is a worthy prerequisite, too.
Urgent Restoration: Being able to heal yourself as a swift action is an incredible boost in combat effectiveness, even if you are going to have to take some burn to do it. Much appreciated for tanking builds.


Improved Elemental Limb: Improved Elemental Limb is actually a great talent, although it takes some thinking to get there. It’s obviously required for natural attack builds, but other kineticists can benefit from it, too. First of all, it lets you threaten in melee range without using Kinetic Blade, but takes up no hands to interfere with gathering power (you can shape it at will with no listed action cost). Second, the elemental limb accepts feats applied to your kinetic blasts, so Spell Penetration, Weapon Focus, Weapon Finesse, etc. should all still be fair game. Third, you get to decide what type of damage your natural attack deals as you manifest it, which can be useful in some instances for punching through DR/bludgeoning, slashing, piercing, etc. Pretty good, on the whole.
Sharding Cover: Sharding Cover is weird in that it’s a gamble that your kinetic cover will be broken at some point, preferably sooner rather than later. This puts you into an awkward position: do you take just Kinetic Cover and Sharding Cover, knowing that your wall is going to be about as flimsy as tissue paper but trusting in the damage and difficult terrain to deal with enemies? Or do you take Improved and Greater Kinetic Cover in order to create truly impassable walls, knowing that they’re unlikely to be damaged enough to create shards? You can do both, of course, but that’s four utility talents spent. Lucky battlefield control is generally so strong.
Venom Spikes: Jagged Flesh is normally a terrible utility talent, but Venom Spikes gives you enough of a damage boost and enough poison changes to start making it more worth your while. Keep in mind that only Laced Infusion scales the save DC of your poisons—if you use poison on your spikes, they’ll function at whatever the base save DC is for the poison unless you have Poison Amplification, too.
Wall Walker: Spider climb is a good spell, so it should come as no surprise that Wall Walker is also rated fairly highly. If you want mobility and don’t want to spend the talents on flight, this should be your purchase.


Benign Cyst: An interesting talent for Kinetic Healer builds, but the burn cost is kinda crappy. Still, it’s normally pretty difficult to heal someone at range, regardless of your class.
Flesh Servant: Tumor familiars are great for alchemists, who don’t normally have the means of acquiring one, but less inspiring for kineticists, who can just take Elemental Whispers at 1st level.
Licker: You already know where this is going, right? Natural attack builds will love Licker, because it grants you a primary natural attack that functions at reach with full BAB. I’d just ignore the ability to grapple, if I were you, but it could theoretically be used to build a decent grappler.
Metal Blades: Metal Blades is excellent for natural attack builds, as it lets you bypass a bunch of DR types and gives you bonuses to attack and damage, to boot. An extremely durable rationale for going viscera/earth for a natural attack build.
Visceral Corruption: A minor—but free—debuff. I’m inclined to think it won’t scale well, but YMMV.


Improved Spined Armor: Spined Armor is weak, and the Improved version change what needs changing. Blech.


3rd Level


Adaptive Skin: N. Jolly reports that Adaptive Skin was originally a part of Reactive Skin, and it makes sense. The viscera element has a bit of an Alex-Mercer-Prototype-Darwin-from-X-Men-First-Class feel to it, where you’re adapting to new stimuli by changing your body. Makes sense to be able to swap your energy resistances quickly and without burn.
Improved Kinetic Cover: Improved Kinetic Cover fixes all the problems of Kinetic Cover and then some, primarily by giving it a lot more HP and some hardness. Now enemies will have to actually work to punch through, rather than blowing it over with a light breeze.


Corporeal Tear: Narratively gross, and mechanically too powerful. Give it spell resistance and make it cost 1 burn, for crying out loud. If you take it, though, you have a fairly steady damage source from having your allies jump through the tear in an opponent’s body.
Decelerate: It’s the debuffing counterpart to Celerity! Slow is a good spell, but you’ll generally want to save it for when you’re up against hordes of weaker enemies—high-level targets will most likely pass the Fortitude save here. Probably a good candidate for retraining later on, since you can’t effectively scale the save DC.
Endure Pain: Endure Pain essentially doubles the effectiveness of all magical or non-magical healing, forever. That’s easily worth a green rating, right?
Haphephobia: If—and this is a big if—you can get an enemy to fail their save against Haphephobia, it can be absolutely crippling to unarmed or natural attackers. Bad Touch builds like debuffing clerics and witches would probably also suffer, although they typically use things like spectral hand or a familiar to deliver their touch debuffs.
Mutable Visage: At-will alter self is great for snagging better vision, a swim speed, or a few new natural attacks. A few refresher points about polymorph spells: you can’t assume the form of specific creatures, and you “lose any class features that depend upon form.” Your GM may rule that this includes Unnatural Evolutions, natural attacks, Bone Armor, etc., in which case turning into a new form will absolutely not be worth it. Talk to your GM about how they’ll rule Mutable Visage works with regard to viscera’s utility talents before taking any action.
Stretchy Tongue: Licker takes some fairly significant utility talent investment, but it’s worth it in the end. I...don’t really want to think about how much reach this is with a tongue, if I’m honest.


Automatic Reconstruction: Fast healing this slow is unfortunately not going to cut it in combat, so there’s no point spending burn on Kinetic Cover, Improved Kinetic Cover, etc. This might come into play later on for “healing” structures created with Aether Architect, Kinetic Cityscape, etc., but I’d say those are fairly niche, endgame uses.
Bone Armor: If you’re not expanding into the earth element, Bone Armor is about the equivalent of the defending bone spell, but with no maximum caster level. Many natural attack builds will want to expand into earth for Flesh of Stone and Metal Blades, though, and DR/adamantine beats DR/bludgeoning.
Undead Evolution: If you’re a viscera/void necromancer, nothing really beats Undead Evolution. You can grant your undead minions pounce, reach, extra attacks! Crazy good.


Bone Spikes: Jagged Flesh is a horrible utility talent that’s not significantly improved by most third-party content. I say skip it.
Defensive Osteotomy: The AC bonus scales quite poorly if you’re predominantly using simple blasts, and the bonus type won’t stack with a ring of protection. Meh.
Offensive Osteotomy: You still have to pay the full cost of Offensive Osteotomy, your kinetic blast, and any substance infusions applied to it, which is going to be wicked expensive for something that’s dealing only half damage. I’m not a fan.
Pillar: What can Pillar do that climbing or flight couldn’t? It’s certainly got no combat applications. So why?
Visceral Whip: Gross, first of all, and second of all, incredibly ineffective. Whips are really hard to use without a bunch of feat investment, and while you’ll get that once you reach Improved Visceral Whip, for now you’re going to provoke attacks of opportunity and be unable to deal damage to any enemy with a natural armor bonus. Skip until you can get Improved at 10th level.


4th Level


Improved Unnatural Evolution: See Unnatural Evolution for why this talent is great, and the Unnatural Evolutions section below for information about which evolutions you should pick.


Vital Fibers: Why bother building for natural attacks when you can steal other people’s limbs? This is a pretty wacky ability, but extra limbs always have great uses, so I can’t rate it poorly. The burn cost will keep you from becoming a centaur for longer than an afternoon, at least.


Death Globe: Corpse hammer is a pretty good spell, but I’m not entirely sure that this is better than Improved Mobile Blast, even at higher levels. The reduced action economy of Improved Mobile Blast is certainly hard to beat.
Elemental Hand: I’ve cooled on the hand suite of spells over the years, mostly because the CMB upgrades that grapple, bull rush, etc. can’t be counted on to work reliably against creatures of the same CR. It still works well to keep enemies away from you, slowing them as they try to approach and granting you cover bonuses if they try to attack from range. Might even soak some damage!
Kinetic Shelter: A fun little bungalow for you and your team, although other tactics like rope trick are probably a little less conspicuous.


Deadly Premonition: Corpomancy is bad; Deadly Premonition is also bad, even without Visceral Whip.
Skin Slough: Skinsend has one and only one acceptable use in Pathfinder: injecting an infused skinsend into enemies to part them from their bodies as an alchemist or investigator. Using it as a spell yourself? Blech, who would want to do that?


5th Level


Advanced Exoskeleton: The only weapons that can pierce your advanced exoskeleton are +4 bludgeoning or adamantine bludgeoning weapons, and that’s not a huge range of weapons. Now, theoretically, Advanced Exoskeleton can’t scale as hard as Flesh of Stone can, but at 10th level, it beats Flesh of Stone pretty handily. A geokineticist would have to invest 5 points of burn in order to get DR 10/adamantine for the whole day, whereas a corpokineticist can spend 1 point of burn for DR 10/bludgeoning and adamantine for 100 points of damage, re-subscribe once that’s gone for another point of burn...the exoskeleton is definitely more cost-effective, even if its power will fall behind as you go into the double digit levels. Doubling 1d6 damage on Bone Spikes/Jagged Flesh is still a terrible option.


Continuous Regrowth: Fast healing is fast healing, and viscera does just as well with it as (if not better than) wood.
Explosive Evolution: More evolution points never hurt anyone, although make sure you check out the restrictions in the spell text.
Improved Visceral Whip: Improved Visceral Whip actually gives you the tools you need to succeed at a whip playstyle. If you purchased the feats already (as seems likely) then you can retrain them; if not, and you’re jumping in for the first time, this wild talent takes care of everything.


Bone Feast: Fortitude saves are obviously problematic, but gaining temporary HP in the middle of a combat like this can help you outlast the competition in a serious way.
Malignant Cyst: Poor prerequisite notwithstanding, Malignant Cyst is quite good, either debuffing an enemy against your attacks or else granting you total control for a while. Watch out for those Fortitude saves.


Hypochondria: A one-round sickened debuff doesn’t exactly cripple enemies, but if you have a reliable way to make them sickened (allowing you to proc nauseated instead) then Hypochondria becomes significantly better.


6th Level


Greater Kinetic Cover: Battlefield control is one of the most helpful things any PC can offer to a party, and there are few battlefield control tools more effective than a wall of stone. Loads of HP, big area, more flexible positioning than the spell, and it’s not even instantaneous, so you don’t have to worry about leaving a big old wall lying around to obstruct you.


Flesh Mask: It’s like that one horrifying scene at the start of The Matrix, no? Anyway, Flesh Mask is a pretty gnarly debuff that’s cheap at 0 burn. If you really want to push it, you can even try suffocating casters by going for both the mouth and the nose.
Greater Elemental Limb: Still as necessary for natural attack builds as ever it was, but now with the ability to manifest extra arms that might help you use magic items, etc.


Improved Elemental Hand: For my money, Improved Elemental Hand isn’t really worth it. Only weak casters would have CMD scores low enough to get grappled at this level, and they’ll have freedom of movement running basically permanently, ensuring that they can’t be grappled at all. Thpbbb.
Kinetic Regeneration: This shouldn’t be a major improvement to Kinetic Healer unless you’re regularly losing limbs to attacks. I did not see that coming!
Kinetic Revivification: I’m of the opinion that other classes should generally be handling this kind of thing, but there’s no denying that resurrection is powerful in a pinch. It’s definitely worth considering if you’re playing a kinetic chirurgeon. 


Devolve: You don’t really fight animal companions, familiars, and eidolons often enough to make this worth your while, and creatures will mostly just run if they’re “acting in their own best interests.”
Preserved Flesh: I’ll save you the hassle of looking up mummification: it grants you immunity to cold damage, paralysis, nonlethal damage, and sleep effects. A nice little package, but there’s one problem from the burn rules text that I don’t think the creator of this utility talent remembered: “Nonlethal damage from burn can’t be reduced or redirected, and a kineticist incapable of taking nonlethal damage can’t accept burn.” So...say goodbye to most of what makes you a kineticist if you take this utility talent. If your GM is willing to waive the immunity to nonlethal damage, this would probably rate at least a green, perhaps a blue.


7th Level


Greater Unnatural Evolution: See Unnatural Evolution for why this talent is great, and the Unnatural Evolutions section below for information about which evolutions you should pick.
Rapid Regrowth: Yup, that’s fun. Regeneration is an awesome universal monster ability that basically won’t let you die unless you’re hit with cold or fire damage, and the increase on your HP cap will let you heal more effectively than ever.


Greater Visceral Whip: Greater Visceral Whip completes the picture for whip builds: no burn cost, an unlimited duration, more damage, more feats, and more discounts on substance infusions. Perfection.
Roiling Flesh: Viscera has no shortage of save-or-die effects keyed off of Fortitude saves, but Roiling Flesh is certainly nasty. Y’all come get some.


Writhing Bodies: Why does that fiery corpse orgy know Russian? If I had a nickel for every time someone asked me that...seriously, though, this is an unusual summoning ability, but a powerful one. I’d use it to soak up dead bodies and then just plow them into the next room to soak alpha strikes.


8th Level


Divisive Osteotomy: Horrifying, and horrifyingly effective. 1d6 Constitution damage and staggered on a successful save? Wow. I mean, it’s powerful, and it’ll kill people in a hurry, but it costs a lot of burn and a lot of actions to keep using in successive rounds. Pretty much guaranteed to end a BBEG’s life, though.


Greater Explosive Evolution: A corpokineticist’s only means of acquiring 4-point evolutions. 
Kinetic Purification: Heal is a phenomenal spell not only for the amount of HP it heals but also for all the status conditions it cures. This utility talent comes in very late, so what else are you going to spend it on?


Grotesque Armor: “Grotesque” is certainly the right word for it: you turn an enemy into a liquid, suck them into your water vortex, and then force them to take damage from attacks targeting you. How’d you like to be surrounded by a liquid slurry of bugbear? The stench alone makes me pale! If you’re going viscera + water, this is a fitting (i.e., gruesome) capstone, but requires quite a bit of burn to fuel for any length of time.


9th Level


Kinetic Cityscape: Unfortunately for aether, Kinetic Cityscape kinda steals the wind out from Aether Architect’s sails; fortunately for everyone else, this is a really cool capstone ability that can help you found a kineticist city once you’re done with your campaign. There aren’t a lot of combat-effective 9th-level utility talents, so why not?


Clicker: Attribute bleed on all your mental scores is indeed something to be frightened of, but the Fortitude save to negate and the fact that it’s a disease—a poison? One of the two, anyway—prevents it from being too threatening at 18th-20th level.


Eternal Atrophy: Meh. This feels like something a BBEG would cast on a PC, not something that a PC would cast on an enemy. If they’re susceptible to poison effects, you’ll have them destroyed before long anyway—no need to make the ability damage permanent or anything.
Kinetic Lich: Even accepting that you’re an evil-aligned humanoid who wants to become a lich, the process of becoming one has pretty monumental costs (about 160,000 to 180,000 gp), isn’t guaranteed to turn you into a lich (that DC 30 Will save may not be easy to pass), and reduces your effective Constitution score by 10. Sure, you’re immortal...but at what cost?




Unnatural Evolutions


1-Point Evolutions


Hooves: You’ll already have two claws and a bite from Bone Blades, and since your base form is bipedal, you can now stack on two secondary hoof attacks for five natural attacks (minimum!) by 2nd level.
Magic Attacks: Natural attack builds will likely expand into the earth element in order to access Metal Blades, which can help you penetrate DR/adamantine, cold iron, silver, etc.; Magic Attacks helps you get past DR/magic and alignment. Much needed.
Skilled: A +8 to any skill you want is nothing to shrug at! Perception, UMD, Stealth, Acrobatics, Escape Artist, or Knowledge checks might all be viable options.


Bite: Stacks nicely with Bone Blades in order to give you more static damage from your Strength modifier.
Bleed: Decent, especially when applied to natural attacks that you can get more than one of (claws, tentacles, etc.).
Reach: More reach is nice.
Tentacle: Inferior to hooves, at least for corpokineticists, but here if you’re looking for even more natural attacks.
Wing Buffet: Not intrinsically bad, but you’d have to come back after the Flight Evolution to pick this one up.


Climb: A Climb speed isn’t the worst thing in the world, but it’s also not the best.
Gills / Swim: An interesting way for a non-hydrokineticist to get in on the aquatic adventuring action.
Improved Natural Armor: Less essential for kineticists (who can wear armor) than for eidolons (who generally can’t).
Resistance: Likely to be outmoded by Reactive Skin, but you never know—Resistance is less flexible, but more powerful.
Sticky: About the only way I’d consider a grapple build is with Tongue Twister + Licker + Sticky evolution.
Sting / Tail Slap: The tail prerequisite is the main snag here. Since you can get a tail attack through the Elemental Limb utility talent line, I’d prefer to spend precious evolutions on other stuff.


Basic Magic: Weak.
Claws: Natural attack builds should be getting two claw attacks through Bone Blades. If you're getting Limbs, the calculus here is totally different.
Improved Damage: Weak.
Low-Light Vision: Weak and likely superseded by racial abilities.
Mount: Bipedal forms are ineligible.
Pincers: Stick with the claws from Bone Blades.
Pounce: Bipedal forms are ineligible—more’s the pity.
Pull / Push: Why would you? Size restrictions will be a problem anyway.
Scent: Nah. Let someone else handle this.
Shared Evolution: Corpokineticists are ineligible.
Slam: Stick with the claws from Bone Blades.
Slippery: If you wanted this, you should have just taken Skilled (Escape Artist).
Unnatural Aura: Yes, by all means, keep animals away from you. /s


2-Point Evolutions


Flight: Useful on its own, and grants the ability to grab the Wing Buffet evolution for two additional secondary natural attacks.
Limbs: Paves the way to add on two additional primary claw attacks, if that’s your jam.
Rend: Excellent if you go into Limbs + Claws + Bone Blades to have four primary claw attacks at your disposal; you’re basically guaranteed to proc Rend every round.


Energy Attacks: With the number of natural attacks a corpokineticist can get, you could easily be looking at +6d6 to +8d6 extra damage on all hits. Choose your element carefully, though.
Gore: Another natural attack that you likely won’t have.
Head: Very interesting. Very interesting. More primary bite attacks are great.
Immunity: Depending on the campaign, energy immunities could be really clutch.
Shadow Blend: Basically constant blur unless you’re out in full daylight. Could do a great deal worse, defensively.
Tremorsense: Extravisual senses, always good.
Undead Appearance: An interesting way to get some resistances and immunities, but very expensive.


Ability Increase: Not intrinsically bad—you just have better options available at this price point.
Grab: Talk with your GM about whether they would allow the tongue attacks granted by Licker to be used here—if so, you could potentially consider it for a grappling build.


Alignment Smite: Calling this a smite is doing a disservice to the paladin. Weak damage, applies to only one type of natural attack, 1/day use. Blech.
Channel Resistance: Ew, no. When did you last fight a cleric channeling offensively?
Constrict: Bipedal forms are ineligible.
Extra Feat: Corpokineticists are ineligible.
Keen Scent: Bad prerequisites, bad evolution.
Minor Magic: Weak.
Poison: Play a toxikineticist if you’re so horny for poisons.
Rake: Bipedal forms are ineligible.
Rider Bond: Bipedal forms are ineligible.
Shadow Form: Dealing half damage to corporeal creatures is a deal-breaker.
Shared Slot: Corpokineticists are ineligible.
Sickening: Weak and affects allies.
Trample: Size restrictions are likely to be your undoing unless you’re using Kinetic Form liberally.
Trip: Nah, not for a 2-pointer.
Weapon Training: You are a weapon—you don’t need to wield them.


3-Point Evolutions


Deeper Darkness: Good, but the opportunity costs are high.


Blindsense: Tremorsense is better and cheaper for corpokineticists.
Burrow: Not for a 3-pointer. Become a geokineticist if you love earth glide so much.
Celestial Appearance / Fiendish Appearance: Too expensive for what you receive.
Damage Reduction: Both corpokineticists (Bone Armor, Advanced Exoskeleton) and geokineticists (Flesh of Stone) have better means of acquiring DR. Use one of them.
Frightful Presence: Enemies are almost guaranteed to be immune.
Major Magic: Weak, and too many prerequisites.
Sacrifice: Keep your own HP.
Swallow Whole: Nah. Leave this for monsters.
Web: Interesting, but it has a bad prerequisite and doesn’t do enough.
🤺 Feats & Traits
KIN 301: Feats


Kineticists are funny, because beyond a few essentials (Ability Focus, Point-Blank Shot, Precise Shot, maybe Weapon Finesse or Combat Casting) they don’t generally need a lot of feats. This compounds the “What do I spend my money on?” problem by making it difficult to figure out what you’re going to spend your feats on, too. Third-party materials fix this, generally speaking, but in some cases they fix it too well—a lot of the third-party feats reviewed in this guide I had to straight-up veto because they were just too powerful. Those that are left, however, are nicely balanced feats that will continue to allow the kineticist to grow in interesting directions without making them gods of the battlefield.




Combat Feats


Universal


Ability Focus: Kinetic blasts do in fact count for Ability Focus, so that’s the easiest +2 you’ll ever get on your infusions’ save DCs.


Additional Traits: You never know what more traits can buy you.
Combat Casting: Per the rules text in the universal monster rules on spell-like abilities, you can in fact cast spell-like abilities like utility talents defensively in order to avoid provoking an attack of opportunity, and you probably should if you intend to use utility talents often in combat. Ranged kinetic blasts will provoke anyway since they’re ranged attacks, and you can’t provoke twice for the same action.
Craft [Item Type]: The Kinetic Crafting feat stinks, but the Kinetic Crafter universal utility talent (third-party) is actually quite good. It’s your call whether to focus on any of this.
Elemental Focus / Greater Elemental Focus: If your kinetic blasts deal acid, cold, fire, or electricity damage, there’s absolutely no excuse not to take Elemental Focus and Greater Elemental Focus, which will give you an effective +2 to all your infusion save DCs.
Improved Initiative: Going first is an eternally important goal in Pathfinder, although remember that the Elemental Whispers utility talent can get you a familiar that grants a +4 bonus to initiative—that can either supplant or complement Improved Initiative.
Obedience Feats: Obedience feats are a huge, huge subsystem in Pathfinder, but they’re not often used by players. That’s a shame, because many are incredibly powerful. I’ll drop a link to my guide on the subject, On Bended Knee, here.


Acrobatic Spellcaster: As a high-Dexterity class that needs to do a lot of melee-range casting of spell-like abilities, there are lots of worse options to pick than Acrobatic Spellcaster.
Big Game Hunter: Not every GM will allow this, as it’s 3.5e content, but it’s a solid (if somewhat boring) passive bonus to hit and damage.
Weapon Focus: A decent option for kineticists with physical blasts, as they’ll generally need some help hitting, even at their highest BAB. Energy blasters don’t have to worry about this at all.
Spell Penetration / Greater Spell Penetration: Energy blasters have to deal with spell resistance, so they’ll likely need Spell Penetration and Greater Spell Penetration at higher levels to deal with that.


Aerial Roll: Only well-suited for aerokineticists, but it’ll help soak stray hits that your defensive talent doesn’t handle.
Auspicious Birth (Conjunction): Of very narrow use for neurokineticists using the Hivemind series of utility talents. If that’s you, though, watch out below.
Auspicious Birth (Sun Sign): I can’t find anything that mentions that spell-like ability wild talents from the light element actually have the light descriptor, but that would make a lot of sense. If the GM rules in your favor, photokineticists will get a kick out of Auspicious Birth (Sun Sign).
Flyby Attack: Good for aerokineticists, who are always zooming about.
Uncanny Concentration: Very narrowly applicable for cinderland adepts, who will want to ignore penalties for vigorous while riding their horses around.


Vital Strike: Remember that you can’t Vital Strike with kinetic blasts.




Ranged DPS


Precise Shot / Improved Precise Shot: The two most important feats for ranged kineticists not to stink at what they do.


Point-Blank Shot: It’s needed for Precise Shot, so you’ll darn well take it.


Bullseye Shot: Most ranged DPS builds can’t effectively take Bullseye Shot, as it locks them out of full-attack actions. Kineticists don’t particularly have to worry about making full-attacks, so they’re free to select Bullseye Shot at their leisure. It’s a nice bonus to attack if you’re not gathering power for anything.
Deadly Aim: Deadly Aim isn’t always the right move for ¾-BAB classes like the kineticist, and it applies only to physical blasts anyway—ranged touch attacks like energy blasts can’t benefit.


Clustered Shots: Only of interest to elemental annihilators who will actually make multiple attacks, or else to those using Flurry of Blasts.
Expert Sniper: Any element that can get at-will invisibility (aether and light, predominantly) should at least consider whether they want to snipe using Expert Sniper.
Far Shot / Lob Shot: If you could get Lob Shot without Far Shot, I’d say go for it, especially for physical blasts that need help skirting around cover, but Far Shot is a bad, bad prerequisite.


Acute Shot: Kinetic blasts can’t be shot outside of their first range increment at base.
Circuitous Shot: You have the Snake form infusion. Use that instead.
Rapid Shot / Manyshot: You can’t use Rapid Shot or Manyshot with kinetic blasts, which is part of the reason you have quicken metakinesis.
Snap Shot / Improved Snap Shot: From the kinetic blast rules text: “The kineticist is never considered to be wielding or gripping the kinetic blast (regardless of effects from form infusions).” From the Snap Shot rules text: “While wielding a ranged weapon with which you have Weapon Focus, you threaten squares within 5 feet of you.” If you’re unable to wield a kinetic blast, you’re unable to use Snap Shot. That simple.




Melee DPS


Lunge: Great for increasing your reach during your round, but remember that it goes away after your turn is over—no taking lunging attacks of opportunity with Kinetic Whip.
Step Up / Following Step / Step Up and Strike: I love these feats for helping maneuver around enemies that would rather five-foot step out of range. They still work well for kineticists.
Weapon Finesse: Kinetic blasts count as light weapons for the purposes of Weapon Finesse, and since you’ll probably want to conserve your point buy in Dexterity and Constitution, it’s a good idea to get this feat unless you’re a kinetic knight.


Blind-Fight: Remember that Blind-Fight only applies to melee attacks. It’s good, and will help you in situations where concealment is an issue.
Combat Reflexes: Kinetic blades won’t stay manifested in the off-round, but kinetic whips will, and they can be used to brutal effect to take attacks of opportunity against enemies. A good playstyle.
Multiattack: A decent feat for the viscera element, which gets a bazillion natural attacks.
Power Attack: As with Deadly Aim, kineticists don’t necessarily have the BAB to make full use of Power Attack. It can be good with physical blasts and Kinetic Blade, just use it on less heavily armored targets.


Agile Maneuvers: Look, I don’t think that kineticists should do combat maneuvers, but if you do, your Dexterity bonus is likely to be higher than your Strength bonus unless you’re a kinetic knight.
Catch Off-Guard: Of narrow interest for melee aetherkineticists, who may want to consistently target flat-footed AC with their blasts.
Improved / Greater [Maneuver]: Again, I don’t personally recommend these for melee kineticists, but they’re here if you want them.


Combat Expertise: Even if you wanted to use combat maneuvers as a kineticist, Dirty Fighting has long since become the primary way to do it—clears away more prereqs, cuts down on your multiple attribute dependency with Intelligence 13, and generally does a better job of doing what a kineticist needs done.




Tanking


Iron Will: Kineticist’s Will saves are prominently, aggressively bad. Patch ‘em.
Toughness: Having more HP increases the effective amount of burn you can take by one, which is a good thing for kineticists.


Armor Proficiency, Medium: Upgrading your armor to medium isn’t a terrible idea, but if you’re going with heavier armor, you’re probably also playing a kinetic knight, and they won’t need this feat.




Casting Feats


Debuffing


Bludgeoner / Enforcer: Although Intimidate isn’t the first skill I’d think of a kineticist using, they can do it decently, especially with Greater Skilled Kineticist and Kinetic Awe. You’d obviously want to select an element that’s capable of dealing bludgeoning damage if you were going down the Enforcer route.


Dazzling Display: Kinetic Awe is better.




Summoning


Charnel Soldiers: This one only applies to void kineticists who are using Kinetic Invocation with animate dead, but man, is it powerful for them. Excellent stuff.
Spell Focus (Conjuration) / Augment Summoning / Superior Summons / Evolved Summons / Summon [Alignment] Monster: Useful only for artistic summoners, who can cast summon monster as the summoner class.




Ancillary Feats


Anticaster


Dispel Focus / Greater Dispel Focus: Necessary feats if you intend to do a lot of dispel magic work. I’ll have at least one counterspelling build in the Sample Builds section.


Destructive Dispel: You should check with your GM about whether they’ll allow the various dispel magic utility talents (mostly introduced in third-party books) to work with Destructive Dispel. If they allow it, it’s a great option for shutting down casters.
Dispel Synergy: A great little feat. After you’ve dispelled an enemy caster, you jump in with a nasty substance infusion to lock them down.


Arcane Vendetta: A nice little damage boost against arcane casters.




Skills


Lector Priest / Servant in the House of Truth: Shabti-exclusive feats that make you absolutely bae at Spellcraft and Knowledge checks, respectively. The Professor build will use Servant in the House of Truth pretty extensively.
Spirit Ridden: A classic for gaining access to skills that you wouldn’t normally have.


Breadth of Experience: A nice bump to a bunch of Knowledge skills, if that’s your jam. Dwarf is probably your best bet, racially.




Kineticist-Exclusive Feats


Kinetic Invocation: Kinetic Invocation is a game-changing feat that many kineticists should have. It’s so big that it really merits its own section—and it’ll get one under each element’s heading—but suffice it for now to say that you probably want this feat unless there’s an extraordinarily compelling reason for your element not to have it (my rankings would probably go Void, Earth > Water > Aether > Air > Wood, Fire). Adding more flexible casting to a kineticist is just what the doctor ordered.


Extra Wild Talent: Never irrelevant. You’re going to have to pick lower-level stuff, but there are plenty of cheap infusions and utility wild talents that nearly every kineticist will have had to skip over in favor of more combat-relevant stuff. Use this opportunity to go back and get them! Note: the legendary kineticist rewrites this feat so that you can select wild talents at your level, instead of your level - 4. I really like this change, and don’t feel that it busts open the power curve, but I can also understand the rationale behind not implementing it. Your call, GMs.
Mobile Gathering: I quite like Mobile Gathering. Since the range of kinetic blasts can be quite finicky unless you’ve invested in Extended Range or Extreme Range and soft cover is a problem that won’t go away for a long time, it’s nice to be able to tweak your position by more than a 5-foot step. If you’re in someone’s threatened range, though, just use Acrobatics to get out of there. You’re almost guaranteed to fail the concentration check associated with this feat.


Elemental Overload: If you’ve made it to 15th level, you probably don’t have many other build priorities, and crit/sneak attack negation is an expensive luxury. Sure, I’ll give this a solid B.
Enduring Might: If you’re getting Gather Might, you should get Enduring Might. Just that simple. Bonuses to Dexterity and Constitution will always help you when it’s not your turn, whether that help comes in the form of boosted Reflex or Fortitude saves, bonus AC, bonus HP, and more. Gather Might still isn’t going to beat gather power for every build, but Enduring Might does make the Gather Might option significantly more appealing in combat. Once again, life is gravy if you’re a gathlain, but your GM wasn’t insane enough to let you play one of those, were they? Were they?
Improved Elemental Counterspell: Counterspelling is a weird, niche subsystem of Pathfinder mechanics, but since you’re not burning any resources to counterspell (unlike with dispel magic) and the selection of viable spells now becomes pretty wide, why not? 388 out of Pathfinder’s 3,039 spells have one of these descriptors, so you cover about ⅛ of all spells in the game (12.7%) with this feat.


Delay Blast: I have a hard time understanding how you would use this feat. You might be able to use it tactically, but timing the delay is tricky, and using a standard action to activate it prematurely essentially wastes one standard action. You could try to use it in intrigue scenarios, but gather power’s huge, flamboyant display of elemental energy and the inherently spectacular nature of kinetic blasts make it difficult to imagine how anybody would be fooled by the delayed blast. It’s like if someone runs around the corner holding a huge water balloon, shouts, “I’m going to hit you with this water balloon,” and then expects you to look around going, “Huh? Where’d all this water come from?”
Elemental Ki: I’m rating this feat yellow for the sole reason that monk/brawler-kineticist multiclasses will probably be somewhat rare. Elemental ascetic, the archetype that most closely aligns with the unarmed kineticist concept, isn’t all that great as an archetype, plus you don’t even technically qualify for Elemental Ki with the elemental flurry class feature—you only get to add an attack as if you had flurry of blows. Hard to know what to do with this feat.
Elemental Knowledge: Class skills gained from elemental focus range from “pretty bad” to “okay,” and your general lack of skill ranks or ability to focus point buy on mental stats will mean that you’re not getting a lot of mileage out of skill use even when you’re trying to be good at it. Kineticists don’t have a huge number of necessary feats beyond the essential ranged feats, though, so maybe?
Expanded Metakinesis: Disruptive Spell is probably the best argument for Expanded Metakinesis, but even then it’s a pretty thin argument. Furious deals a little more damage that isn’t worth the burn, Ectoplasmic isn’t particularly needed since you can already blow incorporeals to kingdom come, and you can get someone else to deal nonlethal damage instead of using Merciful Spell. Your job is to bring the boom, not take prisoners.
Flumefire Rage: A potential late-game pick for pyrokineticists, but the Charisma prerequisite is onerous and the Fortitude save DC is pretty high, even accounting for your Constitution score. Remember, your blasts scale in level with you, so the DC to save against fatigue will keep going up. Hard to keep pace.
Gather Might: I had to think about Gather Might for a while in all its technicolor nuance, and I’m still not entirely certain how to feel about it. On the one hand, it’s laughably, ridiculously good for gathlain, especially when paired with Enduring Might. Gathlain can eventually improve their burn reduction to -5 with a move action (it takes them until 18th level to be able to do it, but hey) which means you could flex into a +10 bonus to any physical ability score more or less at will. Attack rolls? Check. Reflex or Fortitude saves? Check. Save DCs for all your infusions or utility talents? Check. HP and AC? Check. That’s one hell of a package. It’s probably also reasonably good for aetherkineticists, chaokineticists, and phytokineticists, whose lack of access to a quality composite blast will force them to rely on their simple blasts for a lot longer. Bonuses to Constitution won’t ramp up the damage of Telekinetic Blast significantly, but it’s better than blowing 2 burn on Aetheric Boost. For just about everyone else, Gather Might remains situational, but can still be good for shoring up your saves, AC, HP, etc. if you want to spend the feat on Enduring Might.
Interweave Kinetic Blast: Although I initially scoffed at the idea of a teamwork feat that only functions with two kineticists on the same team, the idea actually does work in one instance that I can see: acquiring a chuspiki familiar with Interweave Kinetic Blast, then using their readied actions to create composite blasts every round. It takes some feat investment, but the payoff is pretty huge.
Kinetic Counter: The utility of Kinetic Counter is going to be almost entirely dependent on the type of campaign you’re playing in. Are you playing a pyrokineticist in Reign of Winter? Well, sorry to spoil that AP for you, but you fight a few enemies that deal cold damage in that campaign; good on you for investing in Kinetic Counter. If you’re in a normal campaign in which you fight varied types of enemies, this feat won’t see consistent enough use to merit its purchase.
Metamagic Invocation: I’m of the opinion that you don’t really need Metamagic Invocation. Kinetic Invocation spells are most commonly long-term self-buffs, battlefield control spells, utility spells, etc. that don’t particularly benefit from the metamagic feats listed here. Plus, the burn cost associated is often more than you’ll want to pay, especially since there’s no way to reduce burn on utility wild talents. Whatever burn you take when casting a Kinetic Invocation, you’re taking.


Kinetic Crafting: You can’t craft wands or scrolls with Kinetic Crafting, which downright stinks, and without any particular support for Spellcraft, you’re also not going to be great at ignoring the spell prerequisites for crafting wondrous items or magic arms and armor. The third-party Kinetic Crafter utility talent actually makes this playstyle possible.
Kinetic Leap: Accepting 1 point of burn for these bonuses is very much not worth it in the vast majority of scenarios, but neither does a 1/day Acrobatics bonus inspire confidence. Plus, a good number of elements (air, fire, aether, and void, at the very least) can achieve some form of flight without too much trouble, so how much long-term use is this feat going to be? Shout-out to the air element and its Air’s Leap utility talent, though, which can let you jump truly ridiculous distances with Kinetic Leap.
Parting Blast: Ha! You got me, Paizo. What a good joke. Surely you didn’t mean to write a feat that only activates once, immediately before you die, and then destroys your body. Surely you weren’t intending for this to be a real feat. You jokers, you.
Primal Kineticist: Name for me the last time you fought someone in an area of primal magic. I’ll wait. (Oh, and don’t think that the Primal Aether utility talent makes this any better. It’s still bad.)




[3PP] Kineticist-Exclusive Feats


TO-DO: Elemental Soothing, Enduring Remnants, Extra Tear, Kinetic Barbs, Impaling Crash, Kinetic Railgun, Mental Bastion, Metakinetic Trick, Overwhelming Defense, Overwhelming Prowess, Protean Alteration, Ranged Versatility, Soul Feast, Soul Tormentor, Spontaneous Chirurgery, Substantial Channeling


Accelerated Gathering: The action economy is Pathfinder combat. If you win at manipulating your own action economy, you win at combat, more often than not. Accelerated Gathering gives you more tools to do that.
Kinetic Remnants: Kinetic Bomb is already a really good infusion, and being able to create area denial caltrops that deal beefy damage is awesome. Watch out, though, because your kinetic remnants will also harm friends. Consider Precision Blast to deal with this.
Kinetic Sniper: Extended Range is one of the most commonly applied form infusions for kinetic blasts; applying it on top of another form infusion is just what the doctor ordered.
Precision Blast: How was this not a Paizo-official feat? Any kineticist worth their salt is going to be doling out AoE attacks on the regular. Wouldn’t you like to avoid cremating your friends?
Signature Infusion: Even a limited version of infusion specialization is still powerful. Obviously pick your best, most-used infusion.


Distance Blasting: The fact that Distance Blasting is always on with no burn cost is wonderful, but the Far Shot feat prerequisite is dumb, because Far Shot is bad. If you can handle that, sure, go for it.
Mutable Composition: Really helps composite blasts skirt around energy immunities, energy resistances, or DR. Got a fire/bludgeoning blast up against a fire-immune monster? Now you lose only a few points of damage, instead of a bunch.
Stealth Gathering: Gather power creates a loud, extremely visible display within 20 feet, which is terrible for stealth missions. Stealth Gathering at least prevents you from warning enemies in adjacent rooms.


Basic Kinetic Training: Most of the basic kinesis talents are questionably useful, at best, but there is a loophole where Basic Kinetic Training allows PCs to access more and more domains or domain spell-like abilities with the Advanced Kinesis and Greater Advanced Kinesis utility talents (also third-party). GMs should know that I vetoed Advanced Kinesis and Greater Advanced Kinesis (or, rather, I suggested that they be folded in with Kinetic Invocation somehow) so there’s no need to allow a player taking Basic Kinetic Training to get anything more than what it says on the box: a basic kinesis utility talent. Of note, Basic Kinetic Training cannot qualify you to take wild talents outside your element, even if they list a basic kinesis talent as a prerequisite, per this line in the wild talent rules text: “A kineticist can select only universal wild talents or those that match her element (see Elemental Focus above).” So don’t even think about it, min-maxers.
Burning Arcana: Not sure why you’d be making an arcanist/kineticist or magus/kineticist build, but it’s here if you want it?
Burning Ki: Same for kineticist/monk builds.
Enhanced Composite Boost: Only for elements like aether and void that get “boost” composite blasts when they expand to different elements. If that’s your case, this is probably green.
Extra Battle Burn: A must-have for war kineticists. An extra free point of burn in every encounter.
Extra Burn: What you want is for burn to impact you less, not necessarily to take on more of it. If you’re running at full burn on a base kineticist, you have 5 HP/HD. With Extra Burn, you only have 3 HP/HD, or perhaps even less if you take this feat multiple times. I mean, it’s here if you want it, but it’s not the right way to go about building a kineticist.
Extra Roper Talent: I’d recommend three full purchases of Extra Roper Talent for energy ropers. These abilities are usually very good, and it’s not as though high-level kineticists have many other feat priorities.
Extra Wild Talent Slot: Entropists should plan on getting as many instances of these feats as they reasonably can, as each instance allows them to essentially prepare two more “spells” (wild talents, that is) in their prepared slots. Imagine what it would be like if a wizard could take a feat to gain two more prepared spell slots every day! How awesome would that be!? Everyone would take that feat, and you should, too.
Flashy Finesse: Great for overwhelming souls, but bad for everyone else.
Improved Kinetic Channeling: As Kinetic Channeling.
Improved Stealth Gathering: Unfortunately, people are still going to see kinetic blasts emanating from your person, even if your power gathering is totally silent and invisible. Kineticists just...don’t make great intriguers.
Kinetic Channeling: Kinetic chirurgeon is a bad archetype, but Kinetic Channeling makes it slightly better.
Kinetic Disruption: With Countering Infusion, Spellturning Infusion, and Improved Elemental Counterspell, the kineticist counterspell build is already quite good; add on whole schools of magic, and it gets way, way better. Niche for anyone who’s not building to counterspell, though.
Kinetic Training / Talented Blaster: Taken together, these two feats enable up to four levels of multiclassing with relatively low penalties. This would normally smack of power creep to me, but kineticists are a weird class that can’t really multiclass well, so this enables some cool combos. The Aether Gorblin Evolved build will likely use these feats to get a four-level Filcher unchained rogue dip.
Mystical Corrosion: Unraveling Infusion is one of fire and light’s best, so you’ll want to get it if you’re a toxikineticist. Can make a pretty good Green Flame Blast dispel magic build this way!
Narrow Array / Wide Array: Two feats for entropists. Although I like the idea of being able to narrow or expand your shuffling focus options, doing so doesn’t net you much in practice. Your shuffling focus determines your simple blast, defense talent, basic utility talent, and save DC or caster level bonuses, without changing which infusions and utility talents you otherwise have access to. If you’re confident that you either don’t want one element in your array or want more elements available for the above gains, by all means, take these feats. I’m inclined to think that Wide Array is a bit less powerful; the save DC and caster level bonuses are the biggest draw of shuffling focus, but you can’t take wild talents outside of your array even with Wide Array. If you roll one of your two new elements, you’re penalizing nearly every wild talent you have while granting bonuses to an element that you won’t have any wild talents for.
Noxious Saliva: Marginally better than Komodo’s Bite, at least.
Planar Companion: Not truly necessary, even if you are focused on summoning.
Poisonous Foliage: Decent for a wood/poison build, but not for anyone else.
Spontaneous Talent: It’s martial flexibility, but for entropists. That’s as good as it sounds! You definitely want to be able to dynamically draw in infusions or utility talents as needed.
Stout Deterrent: CMD is ultimately pretty unimportant for kineticists, but there’s no doubt that this feat will make your score better.
Time Bomb / Precision Time Bomb: Implant Blast can have some interesting applications, but it takes creativity and the right kind of campaign to make it work.


Kinetic Clutches: Kineticists are a ¾-BAB class that typically won’t have a strong Strength score or a reason to take Improved [Maneuver] feats. I’d say leave these alone. Combat maneuvers are a trap for a lot of classes.
Kinetic Coils: As Kinetic Clutches.
Kinetic Combo: Most utility talents don’t have saving throws attached, and even if you’re playing with an element like mind that does, you want Adaptive Utility, not Kinetic Combo.
Kinetic Pin / Improved Kinetic Pin: As Kinetic Clutches.
Kinetic Proficiency / Defensive / Improved: Combat maneuvers and weapon qualities aren’t great things to spend feats on for kineticists.
Komodo’s Bite: Weak damage, weak condition.
Simple Composite: Helps you get around resistances, immunities, and DR in some scenarios, I suppose, but I don’t much see the point when other feats like Mutable Composition do it better.
Telekinetic Muscle: Let others perform Strength checks. You don’t particularly need Strength, and it’s not like you were going to make a lot of Climb or Swim checks—the elements that will (earth and water) have the means to do it well regardless.


Adaptive Utility: The fact that you can use utility wild talents at will is balanced in the core kineticist design by taking away scaling save DCs from enemy-targeted utility talents. Take that balance away, and you have chaos, at least in some elements. Virtually all of mind or time’s utility talents require saves, while other elements have only a handful.
Suggested Fix: Changing Adaptive Utility so that it applies to only a single utility talent is a reasonable change to make, although some GMs may still want to veto the whole thing.
Afterburn / Autobuffer: So powerful it’s basically required. Bad, remember?
        Suggested Fix: Veto.
Burn Resistance: Anything that reduces how crippling burn is to a player is immediately suspect. Another feat that really shouldn’t exist.
        Suggested Fix: None, really. Veto.
Brutal Blast: Kineticists don’t need to get any more single attribute-dependent than they already are.
        Suggested Fix: Veto like it’s going out of style.
Composite Blast Technique: Composite Blast Technique is good enough that there would really be no argument for any other feat at 7th level, especially for geokineticists, pyrokineticists, and others who get rewarded with awesome composite blasts for sticking with their primary element. This feat really shouldn’t exist—it takes away a meaningful choice that players have to make about whether to expand their element or not, and that’s bad.
        Suggested Fix: None are possible. It’s simply too powerful. Veto.
Divergent Focus: Limits on “number of effects you can have active at one time” are usually in place for a reason.
        Suggested Fix: Veto.
Energy Aim: Energy blasts targeting touch AC are already pretty OP, and they don’t need more damage to go with them. Another feat that’s so good that it would be all but required for energy blasts. Remember: if it’s so powerful that it’s a no-brainer, that’s the mark of a badly written feat.
        Suggested Fix: Veto already.
Extended Buffer: Anything that gives you free burn as a feat is too powerful.
        Suggested Fix: Veto.
Kinetic Acceleration: Haste is an incredibly common team buff, even accounting for the fact that elements like air or time get access to it in-class. These bonuses are too powerful, especially the gather power clause.
        Suggested Fix: Shouldn’t exist. Veto.
Kinetic Prodigy: Again, feats so powerful that you’d be an idiot not to take them should generally be scrapped.
        Suggested Fix: Veto.
Soul Tormentor: I’m not denying that dark elementalist needs the help, but increasing elemental overflow bonuses to this degree is way too large a boost in power.
        Suggested Fix: Veto.
Stout Maneuvers: Stout Maneuvers is on the margins of what’s acceptable. Check with your GM about whether they think this is appropriate. I mean, on the one hand, it makes a SAD class even SADder, but on the other hand, combat maneuvers aren’t massively overpowered even when they work.
        Suggested Fix: Might be fine as-is? I’m erring on the side of caution.
🏇 Archetypes
KIN 410: Archetypes


Y’all, I’m not going to lie: the kineticist is probably one of the worst classes in Pathfinder when it comes to archetypes. That’s not Paizo’s fault, at least not entirely: the occult classes were some of the last to be released in 1e’s publication cycle, and they just didn’t have as much time with these classes as others. Nevertheless, I know for a fact that the occultist, mesmerist, and spiritualist got some cool archetypes, so why not the kineticist? Arakineticist and kinetic knight are about the only two Paizo-official archetypes I’d ever want to play, and even then it’s a bit of a stretch. Now, third-party content does a much better job of coming up with cool concepts for the kineticist class, and once again I believe that you’re not getting the full kineticist experience unless you allow these at your table. I’ve tried to warn GMs when abilities are too powerful, though, and rewritten them where necessary.




Archetype References




Archetype (Wheat)
	Versatility
	Power
	Arakineticist
	+1
	+1
	Kinetic Knight
	-1
	+2
	Terrakineticist
	+0
	+2
	Cinderlands Adept
	-1
	+1
	Elemental Annihilator
	-2
	+2
	Elemental Purist
	+1
	+0
	Ioun Kineticist
	-1
	+1
	Psammokinetic
	-1
	+1
	



Archetype (Chaff)
	Versatility
	Power
	Aquakineticist
	-1
	-1
	Dark Elementalist
	+0
	-2
	Blightburner
	-2
	-1
	Blood Kineticist
	-2
	-1
	Elemental Ascetic
	-1
	-2
	Elysiokineticist
	-2
	-1
	Leshykineticist
	-2
	-1
	Blighted Defiler
	-2
	-2
	Kinetic Chirurgeon
	-2
	-2
	Overwhelming Soul
	-2
	-2
	Psychokineticist
	-2
	-2
	



________________
The Wheat




Arakineticist


The Basics


        The arakineticist archetype gets the ability to manipulate curses, both offensively and defensively. With no major losses and some fairly large gains in debuffing ability and anticaster defenses, there’s a strong argument for the archetype if you’re looking to play with the void element anyway.


        Versatility: +1
        Power: +1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Living Curse: Alters elemental focus. Void wouldn’t necessarily be my first choice of a primary element for a kineticist, but as long as you have a back-up plan for undead, you shouldn’t be in too much trouble here. At least you’re free to expand into other elements at 7th level.


        (4) Accursed Shadow (Sp): Replaces 4th-level utility talent. You know what? I actually like accursed shadow. Void kineticists are not known for their overabundance of good utility talents (Kinetic Invocations notwithstanding) and necromancy or death effects can be very nasty, especially for a class with a poor Will save. I’d take this even if it were optional, which is generally a leading indicator of a good archetype ability.


        (6/8) Curse Spinner (Sp): Replaces 6th- and 8th-level utility talents. I’m not sure why you weren’t given the Curse Breaker utility talent—it’s better than just having remove curse, it’s already written, and...well, it’s better. There doesn’t need to be a third reason. Without the bonuses to saves vs. curses and hexes that Curse Breaker confers, I’d give this portion of the ability a yellow. Bestow curse, on the other hand, is sky blue! It’s one of the hardest-hitting, most versatile debuffs in the game, and you should absolutely spend a few points of burn on it every day.


        (13) Accursed Infusion (Su): Replaces 13th-level infusion. (Note: even though Archives of Nethys lists this ability as replacing the 13th-level utility talent, kineticists don’t receive a utility talent at odd levels; they do, however, receive an infusion at 13th level. Go with RAI here and just replace your 13th-level infusion.) Accursed Infusion is pretty good, on balance. It’s incredibly expensive, but gather power, supercharge, and three tiers of infusion specialization will be helping you mitigate those burn costs at this level. I wish that there were more options for doling out this effect in a wider radius, but as Singularity is the only AoE form infusion that negative blast or void blast can receive, you’re staring down the barrel of 7 burn without even considering composite blast costs. If you want my suggestion, use negative blast (0 burn) + gather power/supercharge (-2 burn) + Extended Range (1 burn) + Accursed Infusion (4 burn, down to 1 from infusion specialization) to set yourself up with a sustainable cursing package. If you’re a gathlain, feel free to substitute void blast for negative blast (more damage) or Singularity for Extended Range (more AoE cursing). Either way, it makes for a good support/debuffing package—you’re almost a pocket witch.


Is It Worth It?


        Losing out on three sequential utility talents right in your mid-levels is a bitter pill to swallow, especially when Kinetic Invocations like animate dead exist. Accursed shadow and curse spinner are both better than most of void’s utility talents, though, so I’m not too mad; you’ll just have to spend some resources on Extra Utility Talent whenever you get the chance. Because void definitely benefits from some extra debuffing, I’d go so far as to say that the arakineticist archetype should actually be thought of as the “base” archetype for the void element—bestow curse is simply too powerful to be ignored.




________________






Cinderlands Adept


The Basics


        A full pyrokineticist that gets their own horse for mounted combat. It’s hard to justify giving away utility talents at fixed levels when you could get a mount using feats, but it’s here if you want it.


        Versatility: -1
        Power: +1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Class Skills: Alters class skills. Ride and Handle Animal are objectively worse than Stealth and UMD, but not necessarily for an archetype that’s built to be mounted all the time.
