        (1) Fire Focus (Su): Alters elemental focus and expanded element. Fire is one of the best elements that you can go all the way with; Blue Flame Blast is extraordinarily powerful, and has a number of great infusions available to. I’m fine with this.


        (1) Galloping Siphon (Su): Replaces 1st-level infusion. There are a bunch of good 1st-level infusions for fire (Fan of Flames, Burning Infusion, Extended Range, etc.) so the opportunity cost here is fairly steep in early levels. Too bad. The fire resistance that galloping siphon grants is meh, but you definitely need bonuses to your concentration checks—every blast you shoot while mounted will require such a check.


        (4) Mount: Replaces 4th-, 8th-, and 12th-level utility talents. No one would ever accuse fire of having an overabundance of useful utility talents, so you should be able to grab the bare essentials (Fire’s Fury, Searing Flame, Firesight, Smoke Storm, and the Flame Jet talents) without too much trouble. Even with restricted access and a lower effective level, an animal companion is still a good pick-up for the kineticist class, although I question whether you’re not better off purchasing a mount through feats instead of giving away utility talents. One of the best parts about mounted combat for kineticists is that their damage output is not at all dependent on full-attack actions, which means that you don’t have to wait for 14th level and the Mounted Skirmisher feat in order to deal full damage—just ride around using your standard actions as normal to blast. Being able to gather power while using your mount’s speed for movement is also pretty cool. Watch your concentration checks, though, as those can get gnarly while mounted.


Is It Worth It?


        If you were going to be playing an all-fire kineticist anyway, cinderlands adept isn’t the worst archetype you could get. The addition of mounted combat is a nice touch for the kineticist, and allows you to gather power and blast while your mount does the moving for you. Mounted Archery and Improved Mounted Archery probably won’t be too necessary for a pure pyrokineticist, since all of their attacks will target touch AC, but together they can build a mobile skirmisher who is constantly blasting enemies away with fireballs while riding pell-mell through the battlefield.




________________






Elemental Annihilator


The Basics


        An odd kineticist archetype that surrenders all utility talents for more offense. Strictly for those who want to bring an intense amount of boom at the cost of nearly everything else.


        Versatility: -2
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Devastating Infusion (Su): Replaces 1st-level infusion and basic kinesis talent. Devastating infusion is best thought of as a regular weapon that elemental annihilators can use. You’re treated as having full BAB when you use it, you can pair it with Vital Strike, its damage die is a static 1d8 instead of scaling like your normal blast, and you get to add your Constitution modifier to damage exactly as you would get to add your Strength modifier to damage if you were wielding a one- or two-handed melee weapon. You’ll definitely want to get Weapon Finesse so that you can continue to use Dexterity and Constitution exclusively when attacking, whether in melee or at range; remember also that devastating infusion will be more effective when you need to hit AC better (full BAB on DI vs. ¾ BAB on your regular physical blasts) but will suffer more against DR than your normal blast (multiple smaller hits on DI vs. one big hit on your regular physical blasts).


        (1) Dampened Versatility (Ex): The elemental annihilator’s “thing” is that it doesn’t get any utility talents. I’d argue that you’re not really a kineticist at that point; between your bonus feats and blast training class feature, you’re really more like a fighter, slayer, or ranger, not a kineticist.


        (2) Bonus Feats: Replaces 2nd-, 8th-, 10th-, 14th-, and 18th-level utility talents. There are some chestnuts here, particularly since you don’t need to meet feat prerequisites. Again, this feels reminiscent of the ranger/slayer combat style feats, though with less specialization based on your role.


        (3) Increased Range (Su): Replaces 3rd- and 9th-level infusions. I’d be more impressed with increased range if you got to apply it to your devastating infusion, but as they’re both form infusions, you can’t apply more than one. But isn’t the whole point of elemental annihilator that you’re meant to use devastating infusion to attack, rather than your normal blasts? Yes, it is. So why force Extended Range and Extreme Range on you? I really have no clue. They’re great infusions—don’t get me wrong—but I’d rather not be forced to take them, especially as they do literally nothing to help out devastating infusion.


        (4) Ever-Present Threat (Su): Replaces 4th-level utility talent. Just a function tax to let you threaten in melee range without taking something like Kinetic Whip.


        (5) Blast Training (Ex): Replaces 5th-level infusion. Something that every full-BAB class has in common (well, unchained monk excepted) is passive buffs to attack and damage, which are necessary to keep your iterative attacks at least somewhat competitive as levels roll by. From a design perspective, this class feature firmly plunks the elemental annihilator into the “non-magical full-BAB martial” category.


        (6) Flurry of Devastation (Su): Replaces 6th-, 12th-, and 16th-level utility talents. The elemental annihilator continues on its path to being a full-BAB martial. It’s fun that you can switch-hit pretty effectively with FoD—remember that devastating infusion doesn’t provoke in melee—but you’re otherwise not getting anything here that a normal full-BAB class wouldn’t be getting for free. The fact that you can’t use metakinesis or substance infusions with FoD is a major bummer, but probably a good decision from a game balance perspective.


        (20) Omnicide (Su): Replaces omnikinesis and 20th-level utility talent. Well, that seems...excessive. 50d6+50 damage nets you an average of 225 damage, not to mention that all of that could be empowered or maximized as usual with metakinesis. Useful if you want to one-shot the Tarrasque, but it’s a bit overkill.


Is It Worth It?


        As I’ve mentioned, the sooner you stop thinking of elemental annihilator as a kineticist and start of thinking of it as a bizarre full-BAB martial switch-hitter, the easier your life will be. You get no utility talents and no infusions of your own choosing until 11th level, but in exchange you get bonus feats, passive bonuses to hit and damage, and the ability to full-attack like a normal full-BABer. Since elemental annihilators don’t have much to spend burn on, you’re probably best off selecting elements with strong defensive talents, then blowing your burn on defense, or else not monkeying with burn much at all and simply enjoying all the extra HP. Anyway, it’s an unusual playstyle (and one that I’d personally get deathly bored of after about half a session) but there’s no denying that it’s potent offensively.




________________






Elemental Purist


The Basics


        A kineticist that only gets one element but steals infusions from other elements. Interesting for aether- or pyrokineticists, but likely underwhelming for any other element.


        Versatility: +1
        Power: +0


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (6) Limited Buffer (Su): Alters internal buffer. Internal buffer is ultimately one of the more skippable kineticist class features, so I’m not too mad about trading it away. It’s always nice to have, of course, but I think that the versatility of elemental impossibility more than makes up for its slightly reduced efficacy.


        (7) Elemental Impossibility (Su): Replaces expanded element and 11th- and 19th-level infusions. This class feature is incredibly cool in that it allows you to poach form and substance infusions from other elements and apply them to your single-element blasts. Some of these won’t apply—Burning Infusion, for example, is never going to work on an aerokineticist, no matter how hard you try—but many others will, and they let you cherrypick some of the very best infusions in the game for your personal use. You’re locked into a single element with elemental impossibility, so elemental purist is best used with elements that are already strong as single-element picks. Even though earth fits this bill, it probably doesn’t need any help when it comes to infusions, between Rare-Metal, Entangling, Impale, Fragmentation, Magnetic, etc.; fire is probably the best choice for kitting out Blue-Flame Blast, with aether coming in a close second to try to give Force Blast or Telekinetic Blast a little more combat viability. Careful around that burn—it’ll eat you up if you use your impossible infusions too often throughout the day.


        (20) Elemental Apocalypse (Su): Replaces omnikinesis. Overkill, most likely, and locks you out of alternative capstones. Good for terrorizing helpless villagers, though.


Is It Worth It?


        It can be, with the right element. Elemental impossibility is unappealing with its unavoidable burn cost, but can lend one-note elements like aether or fire some much-needed versatility. Your call, amici miei.




________________






Ioun Kineticist


The Basics


        A kineticist that uses the power of ioun stones to fuel their aetheric blasts. Gives away quite a bit of aether’s renowned utility, but gains in damage output.


        Versatility: -1
        Power: +1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Ioun Focus: Alters elemental focus. A lot of the infusions lost from aether are ultimately forgettable, but the utility talents lost are definitely not—Telekinetic Haul, Telekinetic Finesse, and Telekinetic Invisibility are, like, 90% of the cool utility that aetherkineticists can bring to bear on problems. I’m excited to pick up Fragmentation and Rare Metal Infusion, but Cyclone and Jagged Flesh can pound sand. On balance, though, you’re losing a lot of your utility through ioun focus in favor of (ultimately) more damage.


        (1) Ioun Cloud (Su): Even without knowing quite yet what the archetype is using these ioun stones for, I don’t like that they could theoretically be sundered pretty easily, and I don’t like the penalty to concentration checks that you take when some are lost, absent, or destroyed. I’ll reserve judgment until I know more.


        (1) Basic Iounkinesis (Su or Sp): Replaces basic telekinesis. Useful, I suppose, if you intend to have a huge menagerie of ioun stones and rotate them out frequently? Seems like a costly proposition for not a lot of benefit, but…


        (6) Ioun Buffer (Su): Alters internal buffer. You end the game with six ioun stones in your cloud, which is twice what internal buffer would have given you, so I suppose this is a net gain. I wouldn’t recommend destroying your ioun stones in all but truly life-threatening circumstances—even for very high-level kineticists, tossing away 4,000-20,000 gp on a whim is not a wise idea. Probably better if you have a dedicated crafter in your party who’s feeding you ioun stones for half cost—you could let loose with some pretty big blasts by burning out a stone when you really need it.


        (6) Personal Resonance (Su): Replaces elemental overflow ability score boosts. Really good ioun stones are costly, which is the main drag on personal resonance. How are you going to afford six good ioun stones with great resonant powers when each is costing you 20,000+ gp a pop? The size bonuses from elemental overflow may not be as flexible, but they’re free and stack with the enhancement bonuses you’ll get from your dual-attribute belt. I dunno, I’m just not super impressed.


        (7) Azlanti Blast (Su): It seems as though Azlanti Blast deals normal damage for a composite physical blast, which is probably better for aether in most scenarios than Force Blast. Paired with Rare-Metal Infusion and/or Fragmentation, and you can actually get a fairly good AoE routine going as an aetherkineticist. Too bad you lost so many of your good utility talents, eh?


Is It Worth It?


        I’d call this mostly a sidegrade to the aetherkineticist package. You lose out on some of your best utility talents, but in return you get better infusions and a better composite blast, plus the ability to draw down more firepower when you really need it through ioun buffer. Be prepared to spend just about everything you have on ioun stones, though.




________________






Kinetic Knight


The Basics


        A heavily armored melee kineticist that thrives in the heat of combat.


        Versatility: -1
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Courtly: Alters class skills. Even though this fits with the “knight errant” flavor of the kinetic knight, it won’t do you a lot of good, as Charisma is likely to be a dump stat and there’s no particular reason for a kineticist to invest in Sense Motive.


        (1) Elemental Blade (Su): Alters kinetic blast and infusions, and replaces metakinesis and metakinetic master. Elemental blade essentially eliminates your ability to function at range, instead granting you a bucket of free form infusions for melee play. This leaves your base infusion slots free to get whatever substance infusions seem best to you. Losing metakinesis is a blow, but to be honest, kinetic knights will have a much, much harder time gathering power than ranged kineticists, so it’s maybe good that you can’t pile as much burn on top of your blasts. I don’t think I’m going to rate this feature, as it’s not really stronger or weaker than the base kineticist—just intended to do something very different.


        (1) Kinetic Warrior (Ex): Yeah, I’ll take this! Combat Expertise is an onerous little feat tax that nearly nobody uses anyway, so skipping that and getting straight to the dozens of feats that require it is a great bonus. Improved [Maneuver] feats and style feats generally require Combat Expertise, so you can give those playstyles a little more consideration with kinetic warrior.


        (2) Elemental Bastion (Ex): Alters elemental defense. 1st level is going to be mighty awkward for kinetic knights, who will likely have boosted Strength at point buy but can’t yet wear medium or heavy armor. If you can groan through that first level, the wait is worth it. Heavy armor and a shield will make you tough indeed, and the fact that you can still gather power despite not having two hands free is gravy.


        (3) Knight’s Resolve (Ex): Replaces 3rd-level infusion and supercharge. Resolve is a great defensive class feature, good for shrugging off Will saves and status conditions that would otherwise cripple you. Kinetic knights don’t get the challenge class feature, so you’ll have to be a little more judicious about when you use resolve than a full samurai would.


Is It Worth It?


        The kinetic knight is one of the few Paizo-official archetypes that I don’t find either deathly dull or brokenly bad. If you’re looking to play a tanky melee warrior with a full complement of utility talents, this is the place to begin.




________________






Psammokinetic


The Basics


        A desert-themed kineticist that has some monk influences.


        Versatility: -1
        Power: +1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Alignment: Alignment restrictions are never particularly fun, but workable. At least a lawful alignment allows you to multiclass with monk.


        (1) Burning Winds (Su): Alters elemental focus. Woof. The changes to the kinetic blasts are window-dressing, more or less, but the changes to these utility talents definitely aren’t. For a refresher, I gave three out of the four utility talents you gain a red rating, while Chain, Magnetic Infusion, and Celerity are all either green or blue. I’m not a fan of these changes at all.


        (3) Ki Pool (Su): Replaces elemental overflow. Interesting stuff. With the passive bonuses to hit gone from elemental overflow, you’ll have to rely more on spending ki from your ki pool to actually hit stuff—well, those using Sand Blast will have to do that, at least. Sirocco Blast users will still be laughing all the way to the bank. Getting the ability to override DR/metals is interesting, as that’s usually reserved for Rare-Metal Infusion. The fact that you can stack monk or Ninja levels for the purposes of your ki pool probably makes it a cinch that this would be your go-to archetype for multiclassing. Yeah, I’ll give this a pass.


        (7) Sand Element (Su): Alters expanded element. Air and earth are both great elements, and they do well together. You’ll have Wings of Air to keep you at range, Flesh of Stone to keep you safe on defense, and Sandstorm Blast to blow enemies away. Constrained choices are never great, but in this instance it’s not at all detrimental to the class.


Is It Worth It?


        If you’re looking for a ki pool or a monk/kineticist multiclass, sure. The ki pool is probably a little more versatile than elemental overflow, but less powerful, and the restricted options for utility talents aren’t great. I’ll give it a narrow pass.




________________






Terrakineticist


The Basics


        A kineticist that gets access to different sets of blasts, infusions, defensive talents, and utility talents depending on the terrain that they’re in. It’s a cool concept.


        Versatility: +2
        Power: +0


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Terrakinesis: Alters elemental focus and kinetic blast. I’ll be honest, I love the flavor behind terrakinesis because it resembles one of my favorite occultist archetypes, the geomancer. The geomancer is cool because it almost always has a terrain-appropriate spell at the ready, just as the terrakineticist will almost always have a terrain-appropriate utility talent at the ready. Heat Adaptation is a pretty crappy utility talent, but if you only acquire it once you step onto the Plane of Fire (and can swap it out for something else automatically as you leave) it actually becomes quite good! Where terrakinesis is going to struggle, though, is on offense. Creatures that dwell in the Plane of Fire are 100% guaranteed to have fire immunity most of the time, and guess what? The fire element deals exclusively fire damage. Because enemies in elementally-associated terrains tend to have resistance or immunity to the kinetic blasts you’ll bring to the table, your best bet is probably to select physical blasts where you can, since DR will be less prevalent in elemental terrains than energy resistance or immunity. At any rate, a fun and versatile power, even if it’s offensively suboptimal.


        (2) Terrakinetic Defense: Alters elemental defense. The only trouble here is that different defensive talents require vastly different amounts of burn investment to work at full capacity. Water, for example, has a defensive talent that’s pretty light on burn, whereas aether and earth have much more burn-heavy defensive talents. You may not switch terrains often enough to need to consider which element’s defensive talent you’re investing burn for, but sometimes you may. In some instances, this will also have adverse effects on which wondrous items you use—everyone, for example, needs an amulet of natural armor, but what if you’re in a forest and Flesh of Wood’s bonus type doesn’t stack with your amulet? Wasted burn. It’s a fringe concern, but something to consider.


        (7) Expanded Terrakinesis: Replaces expanded element. Usually staying as a single-element build hampers your versatility, but not for the terrakineticist, who is treated as the master of any terrain they’re in. The problems with offensive match-ups will only grow more acute here, but the good news is that nearly all composite blasts are physical, so energy resistances and immunities will be problems only if you foresee yourself going adventuring in the desert or Plane of Fire often.


        (20) Omniterrakinesis (Su): Replaces omnikinesis. Thpbbb. What a stinker of a capstone. You can’t even trade it away for an alternative capstone, either. Blech.


Is It Worth It?


        If you can handle the total absence of choice about when your element shifts, certainly it is. Many elements have terrain-specific utility talents or infusions that are only going to be useful in the right circumstances, but the terrakineticist all but ensures that you’re going to get those wild talents handed to you in only the right circumstances. I like it a lot.












________________
The Chaff




Aquakineticist


The Basics


        As the name would imply, the aquakineticist archetype makes you better at deep-sea adventuring. The biggest loss is water’s normal defensive talent, which gets stripped away in favor of cold resistance, and the constraint that you must select only water for your expanded elements. It’s a downgrade in power, but might still be worth it if you’re playing an aquatic-heavy game like Ruins of Azlant.


        Versatility: -1
        Power: -1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Aquatic Focus: Alters expanded element. I’m on record as saying that I think all the way is the weakest way to play a hydrokineticist, so I’m not a super big fan of this class feature. Other elements have lots to offer, you know?


        (1) Basic Aquakinesis (Sp): Replaces basic hydrokinesis. Adjusting buoyancy isn’t really a huge feature unless you’re going to be more or less permanently underwater, and even there, a swim speed or freedom of movement will serve you just as well. I’m unimpressed.


        (1) Ocean’s Caress (Su): Replaces defensive talent. No, no, no. All wrong. Water’s defensive talent is among the best in the business, topped only by earth’s, and replacing that with cold resistance is no good at all. Shield bonuses to AC are far more important.


        (2) Call of the Deep: Replaces 2nd- and 6th-level utility talents. Call of the deep hands you these utility talents a little faster than you’d normally get them, and that’s a good thing, as they’re both great wild talents for seafaring kineticists. 


        (7) Ice Propagation (Su): Alters cold simple blast. Nothing but upside here, since you’re not losing anything. Will you ever use it? Eh, maybe.


Is It Worth It?


        Perhaps if you’re in an all-aquatic game, yes. For literally any other type of game, going aquakineticist takes away too much versatility and power to be under serious consideration.




________________






Blightburner


The Basics


        A kineticist that specializes in...radiation resistance? Okay.


        Versatility: -2
        Power: -1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Earth Focus: Alters elemental focus. Earth’s a good element. Not inherently bad.


        (1) Blightburn Manipulation (Su): Replaces basic geokinesis. So you can...cook food with your hands? Mkay. This doesn’t seem like a worthy replacement for basic geokinesis, which is actually a pretty good basic kinesis utility talent.


        (2) Radiation Absorption (Su): Replaces 2nd-level utility talent. Please list for me the number of times you have taken ability damage from radiation. I’ll wait.


        (2) Blightburn Aura (Su): Replaces ability score boosts from elemental overflow and defense utility talent. Earth’s defense talent is the best defense talent there is, full stop. Trading that away for a crappier version of fire’s defense talent is just plain wrong. Sure, enemies will be a little more wary of attacking you, but reach weapons, ranged weapons, and spells can all punch through with no consequences, and it’s not like fire resistance or immunity is uncommon. I gave Searing Flesh from fire a red, and I’ll do the same here—it’s even more of a kick in the groin because you’re giving up huge amounts of DR/adamantine in order to get this aura. Nuh-uh.


        (6) Radiation Resistance (Su): Alters gather power and internal buffer. Again: please list for me the number of times you have encountered radiation in a Pathfinder game. I’m still waiting.


Is It Worth It?


        Blightburner is hilariously underpowered. If you were playing a kineticist in a post-apocalyptic nuclear winter where radiation was incredibly common, sure, maybe it’d eke out a yellow rating, but mostly it just takes all the great things about the earth element and turns them into garbage.




________________






Blighted Defiler


The Basics


        The blighted defiler sucks life energy out of living creatures in order to fuel its wild talents, but it does so...poorly.


        Versatility: -2
        Power: -2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Alignment: Alters alignment. So you have to be evil-aligned, eh? I kind of wish there were a “Master Yoda” version of the blighted defiler that accessed the life force suffusing all of creation without sucking it dry, but it’s Paizo’s game, not mine. At any rate, we’re not starting off strong here.


        (1) Steal Power (Su): Alters gather power. I guess you’re not losing anything here, since the underlying gather power ability isn’t affected at all, but steal power itself is a hilariously limited ability. Consider the size of your average dungeon: couple hundred feet in size, right? Well, by 4th level, you’ll be stripping life out of plants, flies, and babies within 1000 feet of you on all sides. And if any portion of your enormous circles overlap—any portion at all—you waste the ability and nothing happens. Unless you’re flying at top speed across the forest moon of Endor, there’s not going to be enough access to fresh plant life to use this ability more than once or twice per day, and that much only if you’re lucky in your terrain choices.


        (1) Elemental Might (Ex): Alters key ability score and elemental overflow. Strength, huh? I suppose this would let you build a Kinetic Blade build a little easier, but what you’re giving up is a bunch of potential burn, and that’s no bueno. Think about it: if one 10th-level kineticist has 22 Constitution and another has 14 Constitution, the first kineticist can take 4 points of burn before reaching the effective number of HP that the second kineticist had at the beginning of the day. Not to mention bonuses to Fortitude saves and all that. The alterations to elemental overflow are interesting, but as I mentioned above, you’re not going to be able to steal power more than once or twice a day, and the reduction in your effective burn capacity will probably decrease your elemental overflow bonuses in the long run. Blech.


        (6) Life Buffer (Su): Replaces internal buffer. At least you’ve finally gotten the means of using steal power even when you’re in an area that you’ve already blighted, but again, how are you going to fill that buffer? At this level, you’re blighting a 3,000-foot diameter circle, and it stays blighted for an entire month after you use steal power. How are you going to make that work?


        (6) Stolen Strength (Su): Replaces elemental defense. No defense talents, and all the abilities are predicated on the idea that you’re using steal power more than three times per day. Three times!? Paizo, have you not been listening? One time is difficult enough, let alone three! You’ll almost never see these bonuses, and the opportunity cost is massive.


        (20) Steal Life (Su): Replaces omnikinesis. To top it all off, the blighted defiler can’t even get an alternate capstone ability, since they replace omnikinesis. No, you don’t want a crappy save-or-die effect keyed off of a Fortitude save.


Is It Worth It?


        Blighted defiler is an amazing character concept that is almost perfectly unplayable. Literally, the only time it stops shooting itself in the proverbial foot is when it’s pausing to reload. When your main archetype feature is this badly broken, you can’t really do much about the rest.




________________






Blood Kineticist


The Basics


        Do I taste pennies? Yes, it’s blood-bending from Avatar: The Last Airbender.


        Versatility: -2
        Power: -1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Blood Focus (Ex): Alters elemental focus. Water isn’t a bad element, and it’s important to remember that only the infusions granted by this archetype fail against constructs, undead, elementals, inevitables, etc., not all infusions. Nevertheless, you’re starting with more constraints than most hydrokineticists, and that’s not great.


        (1) Wrack (Su): Replaces 1st-level infusion. Simple physical blasts deal an average of 4.5 damage per two levels before applying their Constitution modifier, so I suppose this ability is decent when you’re facing DR 5 or 10/adamantine, alignment, or null in early levels before you’re equipped to handle that. Since you can’t apply metakinesis to wrack, though, you’re almost always going to get better damage by just using empower and a composite blast to blow someone up instead of using this finicky weapon. A save for quarter damage? Nah.


        (5) Bleeding Infusion (Su): Replaces 5th-level infusion. Target selection aside, this still isn’t a great infusion. Bleed damage doesn’t stack unless explicitly stated, so you’re taking on 2 point of burn in exchange for a few bleed damage that will be healed by any fast healing, regeneration, cure light wounds, etc. floating around. And a Fortitude save negates, too. I’m less than impressed.


        (6) Blood Tell (Sp): Replaces 6th-level utility talent. Oh, that’s cool. Blood biography is a great information-gathering spell, and you’ll be able to interrogate any number of dead creatures now that you can cast it for 0 burn. There’s no reason ever to increase the burn cost, so...well, don’t do that.


        (7) Blood Blast (Su): Alters expanded element. The biggest weakness of Blood Blast is that you’re not expanding your infusion selection or damage typing at all, which kind of defeats the purpose of getting into an expanded element. This is actually a bigger deal than you might think.


        (8) Blood Throw (Su): Replaces 8th-level utility talent. Foe Throw is a solidly good infusion from aether, so I’m happy to get it here. Pity you’re surrendering so many utility talents, though.


        (9) Gut-Wrenching Infusion (Su): Replaces 9th-level infusion. Gut-Wrenching Infusion at least has a longer duration than Toxic Infusion or Venom Infusion, the closest analogs from the regular infusion list, but it still doesn’t really rise above a yellow. This is a lot of burn for a relatively weak debuff, and a Fortitude save negates as usual.


        (11) Vampiric Infusion (Su): Replaces 11th-level infusion. I gave Vampiric Infusion from the void element a yellow rating, and that seems deserved here, as well. You’re just saving a standard action at the cost of 3 burn, which doesn’t seem terribly exciting to me.


        (20) Blood Mastery (Su): Replaces omnikinesis. An entirely underwhelming capstone that also locks you out of alternate capstones. Not worth it even if you make it this far.


Is It Worth It?


        When the best ability an archetype has to offer is an at-will blood biography, you know you’re in trouble. I want to like blood kineticist, but you’re ultimately being forced to give up a bunch of infusions and utility talents for some extremely mediocre replacements, many of which you can’t even use on a significant segment of the enemies! It’s so metal, and so underwhelming.




________________






Dark Elementalist


The Basics


        A kineticist that uses the liquified souls of her enemies as fuel. Evil, evil, evil.


        Versatility: +0
        Power: -2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Alignment: Alters alignment. As with blighted defiler, this alignment shift is just another set of constraints.


        (1) Dark Studies: Alters class skills, base saving throws, and key ability score. So you’re a...wizard now? Kind of? Dark studies is a complete overhaul of what makes a kineticist a kineticist. You lose your strong Reflex and Fortitude saves, gain a strong Will save, lose all your HP, and gain a bunch of skill ranks and class skills. As far as survivability goes, this is definitely a huge loss: Reflex and Fortitude saves will keep you alive, and eliminating your incentives for a high Constitution score means that you’ll have no more HP than any other d8 HD class while simultaneously being expected to take on as much burn as any kineticist. Dark elementalists have a way around that in the form of soul power, of course, but it ain’t foolproof. With a higher Intelligence modifier and all Knowledge skills, you now make a halfway decent skill monkey, but be real: was that really what you were looking for out of a kineticist? I mean, if so, good. Dark elementalists make better support characters than combat characters anyway.


        (1) Soul Power: Alters burn and elemental overflow, and replaces internal buffer. Soul power is another overhaul of the kineticist class, completely changing how burn works. Base kineticists have high Constitution scores, as we all know; the purpose of having high Constitution is that (among other things) Constitution acts as a sponge for all the nonlethal burn damage that kineticists will take throughout the day. If you need to absolutely cremate something as a base kineticist, you can probably afford to in the middle of battle, trusting your boatloads of HP to keep you relatively safe from all the burn damage. Dark elementalists, however, have high Intelligence scores, not Constitution scores. This changes the calculus completely. If a base kineticist with 22 Constitution and 10 Intelligence takes 6 burn in a single round, that leaves them with 8 * HD of HP left. There’s definitely cause for concern, but you’re not necessarily in imminent danger of falling unconscious. If a dark elementalist with 10 Constitution and 22 Intelligence takes 6 burn in a single round, that’s a five-alarm emergency—you have only 2 * HD of HP left. This makes dark elementalists exceptionally bad at handling large amounts of burn in the middle of battle, when there’s no action-efficient way for them to mitigate it.


        Now, it’s cool that soul power doesn’t care about where your burn came from. If you want to use a bunch of utility talents and Kinetic Invocations to buff yourself, control the battlefield, etc. without being able to reduce the burn cost with gather power, soul power doesn’t care! Just suck a bunch of corpses dry after the battle and you’re back to full HP. Effectively, though, base kineticists don’t care about where burn comes from, either: if we use the numbers in the paragraph above and you’re at full HP, taking 1 HP/level of burn damage still leaves the base kineticist with way more HP than the dark elementalist, even though the base kineticist has 1 burn and the dark elementalist has 0 burn. Feel me? Anyway, there’s no real benefit to soul power, especially as you lose internal buffer and most of your elemental overflow bonuses. Not good.


Is It Worth It?


        I really do understand what Paizo was going for here, and A+ for flavor. Both dark studies and soul power have serious, glaring weaknesses, though, that take away a lot of the kineticist’s functionality without necessarily adding much in return. I can’t recommend it unless you’re building with some incredibly specific backstory in mind.




________________






Elemental Ascetic


The Basics


        An attempt at a kineticist/monk hybrid that falls pretty flat on its face. Flurry of blows doesn’t solve every problem, especially not when you’re trading away some of the kineticist’s best class features to get it.


        Versatility: -1
        Power: -2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Elemental Flurry (Su): Alters kinetic blast and replaces elemental overflow. And with that, you’re a monk. Not an unchained monk, though, a chained monk—and those are very different ballgames. The biggest problem with the core monk’s flurry of blows class feature is accuracy, namely that monks get no accuracy bonuses, have ¾ BAB, and thus will almost never hit with any of their eight attacks. The same is true here: kineticists have ¾ BAB, elemental flurry trades away your only accuracy bonus in elemental overflow, and so you’re going to be left whiffing an uncomfortable percentage of the time. Not to mention, you’re giving away most if not all of your ranged combat prowess with elemental flurry—and it’s not even necessary! Base kineticists can do just fine as switch-hitters. Why restrict yourself like this?


        (1) Elemental Wisdom (Su): Alters key ability scores. Wouldn’t be a monk build without making it incredibly MAD, would it? Yes, your kinetic blast damage is still based on Constitution, but your other Constitution-based wild talents (substance infusions, most utility talents) now traffic in Wisdom. An unnecessary split that only makes life harder.


        (1) AC Bonus (Ex): Replaces elemental defense. Losing the ability to wear light armor is bad enough, but worse than that is the loss of your defense wild talents, some of which are incredibly strong. This class feature is in no way a compensation for what you give up.


        (5) Powerful Fist (Su): Replaces 5th-, 9th-, and 13th-level infusions. In addition to the opportunity cost of losing all these infusions (although, come to think of it, what do you have to spend those on, with so many locked away from elemental flurry?) powerful fist makes the same mistake as the core monk: it doesn’t matter how much damage you do if you can’t hit anything.


Is It Worth It?


        Almost certainly not. Kineticists can do just fine full-attacking in melee with Kinetic Fist—no elemental ascetic needed—while retaining elemental overflow, elemental defense, their SAD attribute set-up, and the ability to, I don’t know, engage in ranged combat at all. Sorry if I’m salty. I’m just hacked off that this archetype is so bad.




________________






Elysiokineticist


The Basics


        Kind of a paladin-esque kineticist. Too much emphasis is placed on Positive Blast, however, which has never served the kineticist class well.


        Versatility: -2
        Power: -1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Elysium’s Soul: Alters elemental focus and class skills, and replaces expanded element. Thpbbb. When asked the question, “What simple blast would you like to play the first six levels of the game with?” the answer should never be Positive Blast. I suppose this might, might be palatable in a Carrion Crown campaign where you’re bringing in a PC at a high level, but it’s a terrible way to start a character.


        (1) Basic Elysiokinesis (Sp): Replaces basic phytokinesis. Sure, stabilize is always at least somewhat useful, even if resistance and virtue are pointless after a few levels. It’s better than making gardens grow with your mind, at least, but it’s such a small improvement that I don’t really feel comfortable giving it even a green rating.


        (1) Elysian Infusion (Su): Replaces 1st-level infusion. Again, unless you’re exclusively battling undead and evil outsiders until 7th level, even Elysian Infusion is going to fall flat on its face. It makes Positive Blast marginally more useful, but how much lipstick can you slap on a pig?


        (2) Elysian Aura (Su): Replaces flesh of wood. Elysian aura is more powerful than flesh of wood, but correspondingly more limited in which threats it guards against. I think I prefer it to flesh of wood by a bit, but it doesn’t blow my mind. Again, works best in overwhelmingly evil campaigns—Wrath of the Righteous, Carrion Crown, etc.


        (15) Ghaelelight Blast (Su): You get the ability to bypass some forms of DR/alignment with Ghaelelight Blast, but the opportunity cost of not expanding into other elements hurts pretty badly. Nah.


Is It Worth It?


        No, it really isn’t. Elysian aura is the only decent class feature in this archetype, and being locked into wood exclusively for your expanded elements—with freaking Positive Blast as your first simple blast—makes it all but unplayable unless you’re bringing it in at 7th level or higher.




________________






Kinetic Chirurgeon


The Basics


        Paging Dr. Kim Etta Sist to the burn ward, Dr. Kim Etta Sist to the burn ward. Seriously, though, kineticists were not made to be healers, and kinetic chirurgeon doesn’t fix that. Among the worst archetypes for the class.


        Versatility: -2
        Power: -2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Kinetic Chirurgery (Su): Replaces infusions. With all your infusions gone, the kineticist becomes a single-target blaster, and a terrible one, at that. Mercies are fine (and you actually get them faster than paladins do!) but the Kinetic Healer utility talent will always be hindered by the fact that you’re dealing scaling damage either to yourself or to the target of your healing (!) by taking a point of burn. Your healing buffer will help, of course, but not much. Unless you plan to spend literally the entire campaign in a forest, my recommendation would be to pick water as your element—you don’t want wood.


        (5) Metahealer (Su): Replaces metakinesis and infusion specialization. Your healing gets stronger, yadda yadda yadda, but your blasting stinks worse than ever. At 20th level, you’ll be healing 13d6 (plus some extra if you have a kineticist’s diadem, etc.) for an average of about 45 damage, but once you subtract the 20 unavoidable points of nonlethal damage, metahealer starts to look mighty unappealing. Healing 25 damage in aggregate at 20th level is not a worthwhile use of your actions.


        (6) Healing Buffer (Su): Alters internal buffer. Too little, too late. If any archetype needs the changes that the legendary kineticist makes to burn, it’s kinetic chirurgeon. Can’t do much to help a doctor who burns you as she heals you.


Is It Worth It?


        It really, really isn’t. Losing metakinesis and all infusions tanks your damage output in exchange for about as much healing as a cleric or paladin who invested nothing in their healing abilities. Blergh.




________________






Leshykineticist


The Basics


        An archetype for vine leshys who want to cosplay Treebeard all day long.


        Versatility: -2
        Power: -1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Leshy Element: Alters elemental focus and expanded element, and replaces 6th- and 10th-level utility talents and metakinesis (maximize). You’re locked into wood for both your elemental focus and expanded element, which is hard to deal with. Not off to a good start.


        (1) Basic Leshykinesis (Su): Replaces basic photokinesis. Okay, Paizo, now you’re just making up words. At any rate, this ability only confers any real benefit if you’re trying to engage in combat while in your plant form, and...why would you do that?


        (6) Photosynthetic Buffer: Alters internal buffer. Fair is fair: I like being able to sunbathe to restore your internal buffer. It’s not enough to save this archetype, though.


        (20) Green Rebirth (Su): Replaces omnikinesis. It’s functionally unlimited resurrection, but who’s going to play at this level?


Is It Worth It?


        Being restricted to vine leshys as your race and to wood as both your primary and expanded element is enough to convince me that this archetype is a bad idea. Besides photosynthetic buffer, there’s nothing here that you couldn’t get by simply selecting wood twice as your element.




________________






Overwhelming Soul


The Basics


        A Charisma-based kineticist that can’t take burn. It’s as bad as it sounds.


        Versatility: -2
        Power: -2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Mind Over Matter (Ex): Alters class skills and key ability score. Constitution is objectively the better main casting stat—certainly it’s better than Charisma. Sorry. Facts are facts.


        (1) Mental Prowess (Su): Alters burn and replaces internal buffer. Mental buffer pretty radically narrows what the kineticist is capable of. For example, you can’t use one-burn utility talents unless you have a daily use of mental prowess remaining, not to mention you can’t fire off any huge blasts that would cause you to take multiple burn. Hell, you can’t even fire off a blast that would cause you to take 1 burn. Bad, bad, bad.


        (3) Overwhelming Power (Ex): Replaces elemental overflow. Overwhelming power is constant, which is a point in its favor, but doesn’t grant you a damage bonus comparable to elemental overflow, and doesn’t grant any size bonuses to physical stats, crit or sneak attack negation, etc. Why, overwhelming soul, why?


Is It Worth It?


        Overwhelming soul gets a lot of hate on the boards and, well, it’s unfortunately deserved. If you wanted to torpedo the class in two easy steps, changing your main casting stat from Constitution to Charisma and prohibiting you from voluntarily taking burn would do the trick.




________________






Psychokineticist


The Basics


        A kineticist that tries to make a Wisdom key ability score work...and fails pretty hard.


        Versatility: -2
        Power: -2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Emotional Intensity (Ex): Alters class skills and key ability score. There’s a lot to unpack here. Kineticists kinda suck at Will saves, which might make you excited to get Wisdom as your key ability score, but frankly, Constitution is still a lot better for a class that can expect to see some heavy combat fire. Crucially, though, you can’t use any wild talents while under the effects of a non-harmless emotion or fear effect. If an enemy so much as uses the Intimidate skill to give you the shaken condition, you can’t use kinetic blasts, infusions, defensive talents, or utility talents. This, combined with the monumentally large penalties to your Will save coming in mind burn, will basically turn you into bait for any caster or creature with a fear aura.


        (1) Mind Burn (Ex): Alters burn. Whereas regular burn might get you knocked out prematurely, mind burn will ensure that you fail every Will save that gets thrown your way—and I would remind you that Will saves are the worst possible saves to fail. I submit the following three logical propositions: 1) the mind burn class feature will cause you to fail most Will saves; 2) most non-harmless emotion or fear effects are negated by Will saves; 3) you cannot use any wild talents if you are under the effects of a non-harmless emotion or fear effect. Are you beginning to see the problem? Even if you’re not dominated, charmed, hit with suggestion, or otherwise turned into some caster’s meat puppet, mind burn will cause you to simply stop functioning for basically any combat that involves Will saves. This is even worse than overwhelming soul, and that’s saying something.


        (1) Mental Overflow (Ex): Alters elemental overflow. Even with emotional intensity changing your key ability score, Intelligence and Charisma do virtually nothing for the kineticist. At least elemental overflow gives you boosts to Constitution and Dexterity, both useful attributes for the class.


Is It Worth It?


        It most certainly is not. Psychokineticist is even more brokenly bad than overwhelming soul; that’s not a trophy that any archetype should want to put on its mantle.
🏇 Archetypes (3PP)
KIN 411: 3PP Archetypes


Y’all, I’m not going to lie: the kineticist is probably one of the worst classes in Pathfinder when it comes to archetypes. That’s not Paizo’s fault, at least not entirely: the occult classes were some of the last to be released in 1e’s publication cycle, and they just didn’t have as much time with these classes as others. Nevertheless, I know for a fact that the occultist, mesmerist, and spiritualist got some cool archetypes, so why not the kineticist? Arakineticist and kinetic knight are about the only two Paizo-official archetypes I’d ever want to play, and even then it’s a bit of a stretch. Now, third-party content does a much better job of coming up with cool concepts for the kineticist class, and once again I believe that you’re not getting the full kineticist experience unless you allow these at your table. I’ve tried to warn GMs when abilities are too powerful, though, and rewritten them where necessary.

________________
The Wheat


[3PP] Artistic Summoner


The Basics


        A kineticist that uses artistic flair to paint or origami-fold summoned creatures into life. Think Okami for the PS2, PS3, and Wii, if you remember that game.


        Versatility: +2
        Power: +1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Elemental Focus (Ex): Alters elemental focus. I’m fine with getting water as an elemental focus, and water should almost certainly be your first element: its defensive talent is better, its infusions are better, and its utility talents are better, whether you’re talking about first-party content only or first- and third-party content together.


        (1) Art Blast (Sp): Alters kinetic blast and replaces basic kinesis utility talent. This isn’t really a gain or a loss, just a little narrowing. Physical blasts are fine.


        (1) Oil Blast (Su): Replaces 1st-level infusion. Since the burn cost is reduced by one and it doesn’t count against your substance infusion limit, there’s really no need to ever leave Oil Infusion off your kinetic blast—at the very worst, it does nothing, and at the very best, someone attacks with a flaming weapon and lights them on fire. You’ll have a bit of trouble taking advantage of Oil Infusion unless you start summoning fire elementals or something, though.


        (1) Sculpting Talent (Ex): Yeah, I can get behind this. Just as a reminder, I rated the utility talents as follows: Fire Sculptor, Ice Sculptor, Shape Wood, Stone Sculptor, Gravity Sculptor. Those ratings still seem correct on further examination; I would obviously suggest taking only those talents that you feel will be useful in your game. Artistic summoners lose out on a lot of utility talents, so you might not actually be able to take most or even some of these.


        (2) Art Summoner (Sp): Replaces 2nd-, 8th-, and 14th-level utility talents and 7th- and 15th-level expanded elements. Now we’re talking! Summon monster is an awesome ability for bringing more back-up to any fight, and the fact that your summons are constructs instead of their normal creature type grants them a lot of immunities that they wouldn’t normally have had. As always, I’ll recommend RedRazors’ excellent Master Summoner app for easy summoned creature management in Pathfinder.


        (7) Creative Genius (Su): Replaces 7th-level infusion. Getting Wood Blast or Water Blast is fine, I guess, although if you took my suggestion and went with Water Blast at 1st level, Wood Blast isn’t really going to give you anything exciting. The bigger bonus here is that you can summon monsters directly next to creatures, including when you summon more than one. If you have Extended Range or Extreme Range, this dramatically increases where you can make summons appear. A classic combo is to hit someone with the Oil Infusion associated with creative genius, then use art summoner to summon a couple of fire elementals, lighting them on fire instantly.


        (10) Artistic Expanded Element (Su): Replaces 10th-level utility talent. So you do actually get an expanded element—it’s just delayed a bit. Another reason to select wood as your expanded element (and not your elemental focus) is that wood does actually start to get some worthwhile utility talents right around this level, at least if you’re playing with third-party content.


        (11) Artistic Composition (Ex): Finally, a composite blast at 11th level, just in time for supercharge. Simulacra aren’t necessarily going to be huge threats to targets, but they do add to an artistic summoner’s ability to harass foes with a boatload of summons, especially as creative genius and artistic composition can be used together to pop out a simulacrum and multiple summons on the same round. As far as special simulacrum types go, stone and gravity are once again the most powerful, so prioritize those utility talents if you have to prioritize.


Is It Worth It?


        The biggest things that the artistic summoner loses out on are 1) expanded elements, and 2) utility talents. Especially with support from third-party utility talents, though, both wood and water can do reasonably well for themselves, and the versatility of being able to summon whatever you want many times per day will help fill in a lot of the gaps that the lost utility talents left. It’s a great archetype with just the right balance of tradeoffs and rewards.




________________






[3PP] Corpse Puppeteer


The Basics


        The corpse puppeteer is a void or viscera kineticist that trades a bunch of infusions away in exchange for a permanent corpse companion capable of taking eidolon evolutions. You sacrifice a great deal of power yourself, but the pets more than compensate you, in my opinion. The Grotesquomancer Evolved build will use this archetype.


        Versatility: -1
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Elemental Focus (Su): Would you really expect an archetype with a title like “corpse puppeteer” to have any other elements as its focus?


        (1) Corpse Puppet (Su): Replaces 1st-, 5th-, 9th-, 13th-, and 17th-level infusions. With this many infusions gone, corpse puppeteers will never be phenomenal blasters; they’ll either have to take the Extra Wild Talent feat often or simply content themselves with a smaller repertoire of infusions. Don’t worry, though, because all of these lost infusions buy us something very important: an undead or construct corpse companion that scales and gains feats as a druid’s animal companion. With two claws and a good Strength score, Medium humanoids are generally the superior option here, although you can feel free to switch forms as often as you can gather together enough slaughtered humanoids. (This, by the way, is the best option for “healing” your companion if you can manage it—Kinetic/Void Healer is expensive, but just getting a new corpse companion is cheap if you have enough bodies.)


        (1) Share Utility Wild Talents (Su): Replaces link and share spells. Corpse puppets are likely to be closer to the action than you are, so share utility wild talents is a solid upgrade to your range.


        (4) Fleshcrafting (Ex): Replaces 4th-level utility talent. Eidolon evolutions are exactly what the doctor ordered for undead minion masters. Extra attacks, extra defenses, etc. are all possible here.


        (6) Preserved Puppet (Ex): Replaces devotion. I suppose this saves you the trouble of creating a new corpse puppet if your old starts stinking, but that’s not a huge upgrade.


        (7) Advanced Puppetry (Ex): Replaces expanded element. Whoops! No expanded element for you until 10th level. I hope you like what you picked. The ability to resurrect Large corpses is really good, and I would suggest that you do so. Large humanoids again win due to the supremacy of static damage bonuses getting applied twice for two claws (humanoids) rather than once for one bite (animals). Multiple smaller companions are generally not a good idea—their effective level starts to lag behind fairly quickly, and the swift actions needed to direct each individually will begin to eat at you.


        (10) Expanded Element (Su): Replaces 10th-level utility talent. Late access to expanded element and a lost utility talent. Woof. Well, at least you have improved fleshcrafting to console you.


        (10) Improved Fleshcrafting (Ex): Many of the two-point Unnatural Evolutions are quite good, including adding extra arms or heads for more natural attacks. I’m happy about this.


        (15) Fleshcrafting Master (Ex): Finally, Huge-sized corpses are viable! Huge humanoids are the way to go here, and while I’m okay with some of the three-point evolutions available to you through Greater Unnatural Evolution, I’m of the opinion that you’re better off using your evolution points for more natural attacks, flight, skills, etc., all of which can be found in the one- or two-point tiers.


        (20) Corpse Master (Ex): Replaces omnikinesis. More bonuses to your corpse puppets. Every little bit helps!


Is It Worth It?


        Yeah! Void is the obvious choice for a kineticist summoner thanks to their stellar Kinetic Invocation list, which features animate dead and command undead, and this simply dials that up to 11. I’m of the opinion that chaokineticists make better corpse puppeteers than corpokineticists for that reason, and also because Reconstruction Infusion can eventually let you heal your animated dead out of combat for free—it’s slow, of course, but free with infusion specialization. Be prepared to lose a lot of your combat functionality, though, because that many infusions gone can’t be easily replaced.




________________






[3PP] Dimensional Ripper


The Basics


        A kineticist crossed with a portal gun. It’s...very powerful.


        Versatility: +2
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Elemental Focus (Su): All of the utility talents you’ll need as a dimensional ripper are in these elements anyway, so it’s a good thing to be restricted here. Keeping you on the rails.


        (2) Dimensional Tear (Sp): Replaces 2nd-level utility talent. Sooo...it’s the gun from Portal, basically. That should tell you almost everything you need to know about its rating, but a few salient points:


   * They block line of sight. In a pinch, you can use one of these babies as cover for an ally to leave an enemy’s threatened range. It’s not the best use of your actions, but conjuration specialists occasionally summon monsters for the express purpose of providing soft cover. Why not?
   * They have to be placed vertically in empty squares on solid ground. No mid-air dimensional tears for you. What is this, Dr. Strange?
   * Huge or larger creatures can’t pass through. There are utility talents that change this, but for the most part PCs are well-served by not allowing big enemies through tears.
   * Enemies attacking through a tear have their attack exit a random tear, while you get to pick. This only applies if you have more than two tears open, naturally.
   * Enemies can move through tears, too. It’s only attacks that get scrambled, not movement.
   * You can maintain 4 tears at 2nd level, 6 tears at 7th, 8 tears at 11th, 10 tears at 15th, and 12 tears at 19th. Saved you that little bit of math.


        (7) Improved Tearing (Sp): Replaces expanded element. You lose your expanded element, which is a big, big hit. In return, though, you get an infusion, better action economy on your dimensional tears, more tears per action, and the ability to place tears in mid-air (although you can’t place them over “hazardous terrain,” however your GM interprets that). It should say a lot that you give up expanded element and I’m still rating this ability blue.


        (8) Dimensional Travel (Sp): Replaces 8th-level utility talent. Ostensibly, you’re giving up a utility talent, but you get Light Speed Travel (a utility talent), Hyper-Dimensional Blast (an infusion), and more options for your tears. Smells like power creep to me.


        (9) Dimensional Fury (Sp): Replaces metakinesis (maximize). Again, this just feels way too powerful. Giving melee kineticists pounce is particularly egregious, as you’re not supposed to be able to full-attack with Kinetic Blade.


        (10) Rapid Tearing (Sp): Replaces 10th-level utility talent. More action economy upgrades. Never hurts.


        (11) Mobile Tears (Su): Replaces 11th-level infusion. More Steven Strange shenanigans. It’s nice to be able to move tears, instead of having to create new ones.


        (15) Expanded Element (Su): Again, all the elements that you’d probably want as a dimensional ripper anyway.


        (17) Multi-Dimensional Fury (Su): Replaces metakinesis (twice). I don’t think you come out ahead in damage with MDF, but neither do I think you fall particularly behind. We’ll call it a green.


        (20) Spatial Collapse (Sp): Replaces omnikinesis. Feels similar to the elemental annihilator’s capstone ability, omnicide. It’ll deal some damage, that’s for sure. I’m unclear whether enemies take the full brunt of disintegrate if they fail their Reflex save, or if they need to fail a Reflex save to even get hit with disintegrate, then need to make a Fortitude save as usual for reduced damage.


Is It Worth It?


        For players, it sure is. I’ll go on record as thinking that dimensional ripper is simply too powerful, though. You lose your expanded element, yes, but being able to get infinite combat teleportation, the ability to shoot your blasts around corners, etc. is all just way more than an archetype should give you. I doubt that Paizo would have written something this powerful, and that’s your cue that it probably shouldn’t be in your game.




________________






[3PP] Divine Conduit


The Basics


        It’s a paladin/kineticist hybrid, complete with smite evil, a lay on hands-esque class feature, fear immunity, DR/alignment, and a mount. Pretty cool!


        Versatility: -1
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Alignment & Aura of Good (Ex): Thank God someone finally removed the lawful good requirement from a paladin-esque archetype.


        (1) Kinetic Smite (Sp): Replaces 1st-level infusion. Smite evil is generally weaker for the kineticist (who typically attacks only once per round) than for the paladin (who attacks multiple times per round, once iterative attacks are a thing), and the fact that you can’t add your entire Constitution bonus is...well, it’s probably justified, given how powerful Constitution is, but it’s less powerful than smite evil is normally. Still, though, the fact that you can smite multiple times per day at 1st level is a great bonus, and will help physical blasts hit reliably right from the start.


        (2) Divine Defense (Su): Replaces elemental defense. Adamant Faith is an upgrade to any defensive talent except earth’s Flesh of Stone—DR/alignment is slightly weaker than DR/adamantine because creatures with an alignment subtype automatically bypass the damage reduction. In most instances, though, this will simply make you better and tankier than you were at little to no cost.


        (2) Holy Healing (Sp): Replaces 2nd-level utility talent. If you’re playing an energy blaster, the fact that holy healing always treats your blast as physical for the purposes of how much healing you give is lovely, but physical blasters are stuck with the burn cost bill. Nice to be able to heal yourself as a swift action, but are you really healing yourself if you’re also taking burn? That’s a complex debate.


        (4) Fearless Defender (Su): Replaces 4th-level utility talent. Hmmm. Well, being immune to fear is something that the psychic classes would normally salivate over, but since kineticists don’t have any emotion components to fulfill, that’s kind of wasted on them. A nice perk, but not game-changing.


        (8) Glorious Steed (Sp): Thankfully, you replace nothing in order to get access to glorious steed, but I’d still give it a good rating even if it were compulsory. Phantom steeds can let you fly, remember, which makes it a great adjunct to normally land-bound elements like wood or sound.


        (10) Empowering Aura (Su): Replaces 10th-level utility talent. Whether empowering aura will really work in your favor depends almost entirely on what utility talents are available in your element, but because wild talents aren’t written like spells (and why didn’t you do that again, Paizo?) I can’t give you an easy list of wild talents with a range of personal and a duration longer than one round. You’ll just have to judge for yourselves. It can be very strong in the right circumstances, I’ll tell you that much for free, but is pretty narrowly applicable.


Is It Worth It?


        If you look at what you’re actually losing with divine conduit, it’s one infusion (1st), three utility talents (2nd, 4th, 10th), and your elemental defense. What elements have generally lackluster utility talents, a bad elemental defense talent, and generally limited mobility? Well, wood would be top of my list, along with sound and poison. Void probably fits the bill, too. The Minstrel build is going to have this archetype, for those interested in seeing how a frontline bard/paladin hybrid would work with a kineticist.




________________






[3PP] Dragon Pact Kineticist


The Basics


        A kineticist that gains access to either breath weapons or natural attacks from a draconic patron. The main components lost are expanded elements, so it makes a good choice for those who were planning on sticking with one element anyway.


        Versatility: -1
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Draconic Pact (Ex): This isn’t a mechanical ability so much as it is a narrative hook. Having an incredibly powerful entity like a dragon involved in some way with your character is great for weaving a story.


        (1) Draconic Aspect I (Su): Alters kinetic blast and replaces 1st-level infusion. Jeez, there’s a lot packed in here. So, in exchange for your 1st-level infusion pick and not being able to use your kinetic blast in anything except a breath weapon, you get either Kinetic Fist for free and three primary natural attacks (form aspect) or Draconic Breath for free (breath aspect). This is what we in the biz call “powerful.” I’m uncertain how dragon pact kineticist stacks with the natural attacks in the viscera element generally and Unnatural Evolution specifically—that might be something to talk to your GM about if you want to build a dragon pact kineticist natural attacker—but getting AoE options at 1st level for breath aspect is definitely powerful.


        (2) Draconic Defense (Su): Replaces elemental defense. Skin of the Dragon is most similar mechanically to Flesh of Wood, but with some added energy resistances tacked on. This might be a downgrade in some cases, but it’s good enough that bottom-tier defense talents will see a nice boost.


        (7) Draconic Aspect II (Su): Replaces 7th-level expanded element. Let’s play “How many class features can we cram into one class feature?” game! Dragon Pact, you go first. Anyway, being able to expand your breath weapon size is a nice option to have, especially for cone effects, although I’d personally recommend you use energy blasts with a breath aspect dragon pact kineticist so that you don’t have to use Brutal Blast on the regular—just too expensive. Draconic Fusion is okay for times when you need to skirt around energy resistance, but it won’t always be applicable and pushing your expanded element back is tough.


        (8) Draconic Flight (Su): Replaces 8th-level utility talent. A really nice fly speed and good maneuverability? Yeah, I’d take this even if it were optional.


        (10) Expanded Element (Su): Replaces 10th-level utility talent. So not only do you push back your expanded element 3-4 levels, but you also need to surrender a utility talent to get it? Not good.


        (15) Draconic Aspect III (Su): Replaces 15th-level expanded element. Bigger breath weapon size, better natural attacks, and a fear aura don’t quite compensate you for the loss of your expanded element, but they’re not terrible, either. Natural attack builds probably come out best here, with an effective +5 to your tail slap attack and a doubled crit range for all your other Dragon Pact natural attacks.


        (20) Pact Unbound (Su): Replaces omnikinesis. Pact unbound is fine, but if you chose breath weapons, you’re probably not built for natural attacks, and if you chose natural attacks, you’re probably not built for breath weapons. Plus there’s that small clause about your pact donor trying to murder you as you approach this level...


Is It Worth It?


        Dragon pact kineticist is a pretty foolproof way to build either a natural attack build or an AoE energy blaster, but the loss (or delay) of your expanded elements comes at a fairly high cost. Definitely a “lost versatility, gained power” archetype.




________________






[3PP] Dread Soul


The Basics


        An evil-aligned kineticist that actually gets quite a bit right, forcing burn onto enemies and trapping slain foes in soul gems for later use.


        Versatility: -1
        Power: +1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Alignment and Aura of Evil (Ex): You have to be non-good, but your aura then essentially makes you evil. If you can be detected by detect evil, smitten by a paladin’s smite evil class feature, destroyed by holy word, etc., was there any point in making you non-evil to begin with?


        (1) Damned Loyalty (Ex): “Loyalty” seems like a bit of a farce when you consider the nature of many evil outsiders. Anyway, this is mostly a flavor ability, but don’t underestimate how much it would stink to die, have your team pull together the resources to try to resurrect you, and then have that spell fail.


        (1) Evil Blast (Su): Replaces 1st-level infusion. Getting Aligned Infusion on every blast for free is good, but you are limited to attacking creatures with DR/evil, and how many of those are there floating around in Paizo APs or modules? Hell’s Vengeance is about the only campaign I could see this working in.


        (2) Fiendish Form (Su): Replaces elemental defense. I gave the dragon pact kineticist’s elemental defense a green, and these class features are nearly identical. Decent bonuses to natural AC (similar to wood’s Flesh of Wood defensive talent) and energy resistances are okay, but not game-changing. Still better than the defensive talents from void, mind, poison, sound, etc., so in general you’re coming out ahead.


        (5) Damning Drain (Sp): Replaces infusion specialization 1, 3, and 5. I’m inclined not to think too well of damning drain. It’s a cool idea, increasing both the risk and reward of what is normally a passive discount on infusions, and opens up the possibility of getting even one-burn utility talents for free by shunting the cost onto enemies...but if the enemy has an Intelligence score less than 3 or can’t take nonlethal damage (constructs, undead, etc.) then damning drain won’t work. Not to mention that if an enemy simply passes their Will save, you take the burn anyway and are staggered until the end of your next turn. Hard to get too confident about something that could leave you staggered. If you’re fighting mostly enemies with class levels, it should usually be easy to pick those with poor Will saves, but that’s not a given. Like I said, it’s a high-risk, high-reward ability; I prefer infusion specialization.


        (6) Evil Soul (Su): Replaces internal buffer. Since you’ll realistically be using damning drain much of the time, evil soul really affects how much burn enemies will be taking. Unlike internal buffer, however, evil soul doesn’t help you wind up for really big, flashy rounds of blasting, and falls a little flat because of that.


        (7) Blessing of the Beast (Ex): Replaces 7th-level expanded element. Neither the upgrade to Flesh of the Fallen nor Soulburning Infusion is a particularly worthy replacement for your expanded element. Intimidate checks? More energy resistances? Giving enemies small amounts of burn? Nah.


        (9) Sinful Soul (Su): Replaces metakinesis (maximize). Ah, now that’s more like it! Forcing enemies to make two saves against your damning drain increases the chances that they’ll fail one by a sizable margin, which makes it easier and easier to offload burn onto hapless foes. I can get behind this all the way.


        (10) Expanded Element (Su): Replaces 10th-level utility talent. It’s not totally lost in this archetype, but still…


        (15) Soul Ravage (Sp): Replaces 15th-level expanded element. Improved Soul Burning Infusion is pretty much garbage, but Soul Entrapment Infusion is way, way better, allowing you to use damning drain even if there aren’t any viable living targets on the battlefield. This is an easy candidate for the Signature Infusion feat, as you’ll need access to soul gems created with this infusion if you want to have smooth sailing as a dread soul.


        (16) Soul-Damning Specialization (Su): Replaces composite specialization. If you’ve made it to this level, you’re probably using Soul Entrapment Infusion extensively to create soul gems, and this class feature simply makes it easier to pull that off: lower burn cost, lower Will saves to resist—the whole nine yards.


        (20) Master of Damnation (Su): Replaces omnikinesis. Master of damnation allows you to use damning drain with essentially no cap on daily uses, and you’ll be able to afford to put sinful soul on every use that counts. Definitely a top-shelf capstone.


Is It Worth It?


        I know that I rated a lot of the dread soul’s abilities as being subpar—and they are, when you compare them with other A-grade kineticist archetypes. But they’re so cool. Forcing enemies to accept burn on your behalf, sucking enemies into soul gems as you kill them, wracking their souls with pain even after death...there’s just awesome flavor here. If you want to play an explicitly evil kineticist, this would be my vote—certainly aberrant kineticist, blighted defiler, and dark elementalist ain’t gonna cut it.




________________






[3PP] Elemental Avatar


The Basics


        But everything changed when the Fire Nation attacked! Yes, the elemental avatar is the recreation of a certain beloved Nickelodeon cartoon character, capable of using air, earth, fire, and water with equal facility. It’s a strong combat archetype that loses out on some utility talents, so it’s best in games that already have a number of spellcasters to handle the out-of-combat versatility that you won’t be as good at anymore.


        Versatility: +1
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Universal Focus (Su): Alters elemental focus and replaces 1st-level infusion. Mama pajama, that’s a spice meatball! Four simple blasts and four basic utility talents, all at 1st level. Instantly solves a lot of your DR and energy resistance problems, as well as problems of whether you’ll be targeting regular AC or touch AC. Love it. For reasons that will become apparent at 7th level, you’ll likely want to choose all physical blasts (earth, air, water, and fire, which you don’t get an option for) or all energy blasts (electric, cold, fire, and earth, which you don’t get an option for), then plan your infusions and feat choices for either physical or energy blasting.


        (2) Chosen Defense (Su): Alters elemental defense. Holy cow, elemental avatar, slow down! We’re going to 11 too fast. Chosen defense is awesome for giving you your pick of elemental defense talents, and for giving you what is essentially the Expanded Defense utility talent at 8th level for free. Earth and water would be my near-universal recommendations: water as your 2nd-level defensive talent because it doesn’t require much burn investment and because AC is still quite effective at low levels, and earth as your 8th-level defensive talent because you’ll want to start stacking elemental overflow by then and because DR is more relevant at higher levels.


        (2) Delayed Utility Wild Talent (Ex): Alters utility talents. Now, of course, comes Mephistopheles’ portion of the Faustian bargain that the elemental avatar makes—you get a lot fewer utility talents as an elemental avatar than you normally would. Make sure you cherrypick just the best stuff, and don’t try for any fancypants utility talent lines. Wings of Air, Searing Flame, Celerity, Firesight, Tremorsense, Stone Sculptor—just go with what’s already there.


        (3) Delayed Infusion (Ex): Alters infusion class feature. In exchange for more simple blasts and defensive talents, you have very few infusions. Even though I’m rating this red, I’m actually fairly okay with it—if you want infusions that you can apply to all your many blasts, you’ll have to go with mostly universal infusions, and there aren’t too many of those that are worth purchasing. Besides, you can always take Extra Wild Talent to buy into any infusions that you really want.


        (7) Elemental Fusion (Su): Replaces expanded element. You probably don’t need an expanded element with the elemental avatar, because you start out with four elements, no? Because you’re choosing between Avatar Blast (physical) and Spirit Blast (energy), it’s probably worthwhile to focus on one type of blast: either get feats and infusions for the physical blasts (water, earth, and air) or for the energy blasts (electric, fire, and cold), and treat that as your main tool. Spirit Blast users will still have Earth Blast for when they need to avoid energy resistances, and Avatar Blast users will still have Fire Blast for when they need to target touch AC. Once again, try to do double duty on infusions—you won’t get many, so it’s nice if you can make them work for multiple simple and composite blasts.


        (9) United Defense (Ex): Replaces metakinesis (maximize). A nice little burn-saver. I’d recommend earth as your primary and water as your secondary. Fire can be a very good early-double-digits pick as the burn you invest in Flesh of Stone starts to surpass what Shroud of Water can handle, and Searing Flesh is certainly great for 20th level in the apex state—enemies will think twice about attacking you if they take 40 fire damage every time they do. Enveloping Winds is still mostly worthless. On the downside, losing your maximize metakinesis ensures that you’ll never be able to burst-nova bosses in quite the same way as a normal kineticist; empower metakinesis is more useful for sustainable DPR, however, and your more versatile blast typing also ensures that you’ll hit (and deal full damage) more than a normal kineticist. It’s not an incredibly big loss.


        (10) Expanded Basics (Ex): With no lost class feature, sure, why not? There are only two simple blasts you might have missed in any given combo (air/electricity or water/cold), so don’t fret too much if you can’t fit it in. Not like you had enough infusions to make great use of it all anyway.


        (20) Apex State (Su): Replaces omnikinesis. Ohoho, are they skirting Avatar: The Last Airbender references here, eh? Once you enter into the Avatar Sta...whoops, Apex State, the clock is ticking, because the burn will come quickly. You’re nigh-indestructible, though, and being able to pump out an empowered composite blast every round without even gathering power is awesome.


Is It Worth It?


        Elemental avatar can best be thought of as a middle ground between elemental annihilator (all combat, no utility talents whatsoever) and the base kineticist (normal combat, normal utility talents).
