Here you’ve got more simple blasts, better composite blasts, and better defense talents, but fewer infusions and utility talents—the utility talent issue being the biggest downside. If you can deal with only a few utility talents, then this archetype is an incredibly tough and strong combat kineticist, equally at home in melee or at range.




________________






[3PP] Elemental Brethren


The Basics


        An archetype that caters exclusively to the geniefolk (ifrit, oread, suli, sylph, and undine) and allows them to use rounds of the suli’s elemental assault racial trait to reduce the burn cost of infusions. Great archetype for the geneifolk.


        Versatility: -1
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Elemental Focus (Su): Makes sense. Fine.


        (1) Blood of the Elements (Su): Replaces basic kinesis utility talent. Of course, blood of the elements is only the opening salvo in this archetype, so it’s not too crazy yet. But it’s nice to get Incremental Elemental Assault for free in case, you know, you actually want to use elemental assault to actually deal damage.


        (3) Elemental Fuel (Su): Elemental assault is way less valuable than free burn, so getting to fuel your kinetic blasts and infusions this way is a phenomenal gain.


        (6) Augmented Internal Buffer (Su): Alters internal buffer. Since it’s free? Sure, why not. Great if you have spare rounds at the end of the day.


        (7) Elemental Genius (Su): Replaces 7th-level expanded element. Getting access to one infusion earlier than you would have been able to is awesome, as is the bonus infusion and utility talent. Love.


        (9) Blast Burst (Su): Replaces 9th-level infusion. I’m not overly fond of blast burst—you have better uses of elemental assault than a minor damage bonus.


        (10) Improved Expanded Element (Su): Replaces 10th-level utility talent. Personally, I’d just stick with your base element—you’re getting more high-level infusions handed to you here, and that’s valuable. Or, heck, you can get higher-level utility talents if you broaden your horizons. I’m not sure why this class feature replaces your utility talent but also grants you a bonus utility talent. What?


        (15) Efficient Assault (Su): Replaces 15th-level expanded element. That elemental assault is starting to get stretched pretty far, no? Anyway, great stuff. Easily the best class feature you’ve gotten so far.


        (20) Efficient Omnikinesis (Su): Replaces omnikinesis. A little narrower in scope than omnikinesis, but correspondingly more powerful.


Is It Worth It?


        Why on earth one of the geniefolk would ever take another archetype is beyond me—elemental brethren is a straight upgrade for those races. Getting to use elemental assault rounds to fuel burn abilities is just too powerful to be ignored.




________________






[3PP] Elemental Scion


The Basics


        A single-element kineticist that gets a bunch of boosts to kinetic blasts, infusions, and utility talents. Easily one of the strongest archetypes in the third-party sphere if you were planning to play a single-element build anyway.


        Versatility: +1
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Elemental Heart (Su): Alters elemental focus and replaces 1st-level infusion. I think I’d almost always choose to have more versatility through infusions and different blast types, but this class feature certainly isn’t bad for earth, fire, light, poison, and any other element that has only one blast type. We’re already doing fantastic work for single-element builds, and it won’t stop here.


        (7) Focused Element (Su): Replaces 7th-level expanded element. So in addition to the infusion and composite blast you’d usually get for expanding into the same element, you also get to jump ahead in infusion and utility talent levels, not to mention increasing all of your save DCs by 1. Again, there’s absolutely no downside here for single-element kineticists.


        (15) Elemental Master (Su): Replaces 15th-level expanded element. More damage, more save DC boosts, and more infusions/utility talents. This archetype is great.


        (20) Elemental Embodiment (Su): Replaces omnikinesis. I’m not honestly sure that the writers of this archetype meant “wild talents,” because that would include things like defensive talents, which normally have wildly increased power anytime you accept burn. Anyway, elemental embodiment’s power will depend on what kinds of infusions and utility talents you have in your chosen archetype, but at the very least it’s likely to result in a lot of saved burn and upgraded effects.


Is It Worth It?


        Darn right it is! If you were planning on playing a single-element kineticist anyway (fire is the classic example, but other elements can benefit, too) then there’s zero reason not to play an elemental scion. Literally, zero. It’s all upside. Even though you would think this element would restrict your versatility, it actually adds to it: you get both types of simple blasts, you can select more and better infusions/utility talents, and your save DCs get bulked up enough to enable utility talent-heavy playstyles. It’s a very powerful archetype, to the point that I might consider vetoing it. It’s not that it’s too powerful, just that you surrender nearly nothing for what you get, and what you get is quite strong.




________________






[3PP] Energy Roper


The Basics


        A kineticist that piledrives all of its resources into short-distance grappling, much like the Umbral Court Agent prestige class. And you know what? It actually kinda works! I never thought I’d like a kineticist grappler, but it actually kinda works.


        Versatility: -1
        Power: +1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Kinetic Tendril (Su): Alters infusions and kinetic blast, and replaces basic kinesis utility talent and 1st-level infusion. So, here’s the good news: you’re actually pretty boss at grappling now. You get effective full BAB for the purposes of grapple checks, you can grapple people from range, you can still gather power while grappling, and you qualify for a bunch of third-party kineticist feats that improve grappling. Now for the bad news: you can’t use kinetic blasts except when you’re attempting to grapple someone or maintain a grapple, and you can learn no form infusions apart from Kinetic Tendril. This is sacrificing a huge amount of power and versatility for what is essentially a party trick, but if you want to be good at grappling, there’s no doubt that Kinetic Tendril is the best option available.


        (2) Grappling Utility (Ex): Alters 2nd-, 6th, 10th-, 14th-, and 18th-level utility talents. Since you’re not forced to take any of these bonus feats in place of utility talents, this class feature is really nothing but upside. A quick run-down of the feats on offer:


   * First-Party
   * Improved/Greater Grapple: Static bonuses are always good for combat maneuver builds, and in this case Greater Grapple also doubles your action economy, letting you grapple and inflict damage or pin in the same turn, maintain two grapples at a time, and more.
   * Dirty Fighting: Far superior to Combat Expertise for nearly all classes, thanks to its better bonuses and mountain of cleared prereqs. You’ll need it to progress to Improved and Greater Grapple anyway.
   * Rapid Grappler: The ultimate in grappling technology, Rapid Grappler + Greater Grapple lets you grapple (standard) → pin (move) → damage or restrain (swift), all at relatively little penalty. Unlocking more of the action economy is key for grapplers.
   * Body Shield: A little extra cover is nice, but the immediate action cost prevents it from being too abusable.
   * Chokehold: A good magehunting feat, and it’s nice to have the option of pinning larger opponents. You’ll probably want Kinetic Form at some point so that you can start growing larger for exactly this reason.
   * Kraken Style / Throttle / Wrack: Kraken Style is a good middle-of-the-road style feat for grapplers, giving you a bit more increased damage on every round you maintain a grapple. Your Wisdom modifier isn’t likely to be nearly as high as a monk’s, so don’t expect to do monster damage with these feats until you get to Kraken Wrack, which (per the grappling utility rules text) allows you to use your kinetic blast in place of an unarmed strike. Blowing up someone’s armor or weapon while you’re grappling them is a pretty cool move.
   * Pinning Knockout: Since you’re using your kinetic blast in place of an unarmed strike, double damage is really a lot of damage. But by the time you have someone pinned, they’re basically done for anyway, whether you’re tying them up, dealing damage
   * Bushwhack: A little too reliant on surprise rounds for my tastes, but might come in handy if you’re playing an aether or light kineticist who has access to at-will invisibility.
   * Pinning Rend: Less useful than Pinning Knockout because bleed damage doesn’t stack by default and a number of enemies are immune to bleed or critical hits.
   * Snapping Turtle Style / Clutch / Shell: Minor AC bonuses don’t really excite me, but Snapping Turtle Clutch’s ability to initiate a grapple maneuver as an immediate action is interesting.
   * Snoutgrip: The penalty to grapple is concerning, and there are some pretty steep prereqs.
   * Unfair Grip: An effective +1 to your CMB and CMD vs. grapples. Not terribly exciting.
   * Dirty Grapple: Since you use the lower of your two CMB scores to combine a dirty trick and grapple maneuver, trying to do both is just going to make you lousy at both. Stick with pure grappling.
   * Sleeper Hold: Sloooow. Way too slow for combat.
   * Third-Party
   * Stout Maneuvers: Either Agile Maneuvers or Stout Maneuvers is basically required for this archetype.
   * Stout Deterrent: A good feat if you’d rather monsters didn’t turn the grapple back on you.
   * Improved Kinetic Pin: The utility of Improved Kinetic Pin will depend a great deal on what substance infusions are available in your element. If you’re a phytokineticst? No way. If you’re a neurokineticist? Hell yeah.
   * Kinetic Coils: Kinetic Coils’ utility depends on how often you’re tying people up. If your go-to move is grapple (standard) → pin (move) → tie up (next round standard or move), then Kinetic Coils just really disincentivizes people from trying to escape. And bloodvine rope is actually quite strong—a DC 30 Strength check or a DC 35 Escape Artist check is the only thing that will get you out. Remember that you get to use 20 + your CMB for the escape DC, if that’s higher.
   * Improved Kinetic Proficiency: Only worthwhile if you’re trying for maneuvers other than grapples. Kinetic Tendril is already treated as a having the grapple weapon quality.
   * Kinetic Proficiency: Not needed at all unless you’re focusing on other maneuvers.
   * Defensive Kinetic Proficiency: I’ve got no information on this one, because it’s not on the Spheres of Power wiki.


        (3) Elemental Grip (Su): Alters elemental overflow. Wooow. That’s great. Big, big bonuses to your CMB, and enemies can’t effectively attack you or resist your substance infusions. Things are starting to perk up for the energy roper.


        (5) Powerful Tendrils (Ex): Replaces 5th-level infusion and infusion specialization 1. A lot of Kinetic Clutches is superseded by Kinetic Tendril, so I’m not entirely sure what the point is here. With the extended crit range of Kinetic Tendril (19-20) and this ability’s burn refund on a confirmed crit, you should probably be taking Improved Critical with just about every energy roper in order to give yourself the maximum chance of success.


        (9) Energy Roper Talents: Replaces metakinesis (maximize, quicken, and twice). Oh boy, another long list of abilities to sift through!


   * Tendril Revenge (Ex): Sounds like a bad Japanese por...well, we’ll get into that when you’re older. Tendril Revenge is, in my opinion, the best talent of them all. Your Kinetic Tendril’s reach will be at least 20 feet by 12th level, and the fact that you can initiate grapples as attacks of opportunity with no CMB penalty blows feats like Fast Grappler out of the water. Horrifyingly effective against casters, since no matter where they go, you’ve got them cornered.
   * Advanced Kinetic Pin (Sp): Requires 13th level. Advanced Kinetic Pin is the only way to grapple more than about two enemies at a time. Now, granted, the -10 penalty to CMB stinks, but it’s the only way to keep a horde of minions locked up. You might consider keeping the trash mobs locked together with an Advanced Kinetic Pin while reserving a single-target or Tendril Splice grapple for harder targets—that’ll improve your chances of maintaining all those grapples adequately, and is still possible to sustain in the action economy
   * Extended Reach (Su): Grappling creatures from farther and farther away is always a worthy endeavor.
   * Focused Kinetic Pin (Sp): A lot of fights pit you against single difficult targets, and Focused Kinetic Pin is a way to kill those targets with extreme prejudice while you’ve got them pinned.
   * Giant Wrangler (Su): If you’re an energy roper, you need to be able to grapple any size of enemy. Period.
   * Tendril Splice (Sp): Another strong contender for a purple rating, Tendril Splice basically doubles the number of foes you can have grappled at a time. Very strong.
   * Counterstriking Tendril (Ex): You’re buying back your own attacks of opportunity, essentially, and that’s well worth it, especially if you’re using Kinetic Form or similar abilities to grow big. You’ll basically have to take this power in order to get Tendril Revenge, though.
   * Empowered Grip (Sp): If you’re not planning to use a substance infusion on your grapple attempt, empowered Grip is a nice boost to your CMB that should be free with a few levels of infusion specialization.
   * Extra Grappling Utility (Ex): Extra combat feats, always nice.
   * Strongarm (Ex): Flat damage bonuses similar to Kraken Style. It’s good enough.
   * Tendril Utility (Su): Enough bonuses are packaged in here to make it interesting, and certainly very thematic. Performing tasks with your tendrils is the best part.
   * Around the World (Sp): Getting to reposition creatures with your trip attempts is fun, as is bull rushing them into other creatures. Be aware that your CMB is not going to be nearly as good for these other maneuvers as it is for grappling, though.
   * Collateral Damage (Su): Fairly minor damage, and the positioning would be difficult to get right.
   * Deadly Tendrils (Su): I mean, eh? It’s a little extra damage, on average, but not a whole lot. Good if you have no other priorities.
   * Natural Grappler (Su): I guess if you have natural attacks? Doesn’t seem like a huge priority for this archetype.
   * Planar Leash (Ex): Similar to Around the World in some respects, in that it lets you do more with a combo of a grapple plus some other maneuver. Hard to pull off when your CMB to drag will be quite a bit lower than your CMB to grapple, though.
   * Sweep (Ex): Not inherently bad, although trip CMDs are notoriously even harder to beat than grapple CMDs.
   * Tendril Rage (Ex): I guess I’m confused here. Kinetic blasts can always be used as parts of a full-attack, but you only get to replace one attack with a kinetic blast. If that’s the intent, why bother? If the intent is that you get to make a full-attack with a kinetic blast on every hit, that’s wildly overpowered and should be vetoed immediately.
   * Yank (Ex): As Sweep. Not inherently bad, just difficult.


        (11) Suregrip (Ex): Replaces infusion specialization 3 and 5. Suregrip isn’t a bad power, but the fact that it replaces two levels of infusion specialization (and you’ve already lost one!) is really bad for the archetype. This all just means that you can’t apply...well, really any substance infusions to your blasts. Granted, you don’t have a lot of metakinesis vying for your burn, but still, bad bad bad.


        (20) Tetherlord (Sp): Replace omnikinesis. This sounds like something a really pretentious grade schooler would call themselves after winning a few tetherball games. “Tetherlord” is apt, though, because there’s basically no one and nothing that you can’t grapple at this point.


Is It Worth It?


        There’s a lot to unpack in this archetype, and my brain starts to break as I think about grappling more and more targets. The loss of infusion specialization and any ranged capabilities are probably the biggest blows to the archetype, but as long as you’re positive that you want to grapple things for a living, there’s no kineticist better suited to the task. Keep in mind that you’re given no means of countering freedom of movement, so most high-level casters will just nope out of your grapples anyway.




________________






[3PP] Entropist


The Basics


        The entropist is, in essence, a prepared arcane caster disguised as a kineticist. You’re able to prepare infusions and utility talents in slots much like a prepared caster would, but your kinetic blasts, defense talents, save DCs, and caster levels will shift depending on where your shuffling focus (a little “elemental roulette” mechanic) takes your element that day. With some losses in metakinesis and defense talents, the archetype definitely doesn’t have more raw power than other archetypes, but it is far more flexible in what it can prepare for and do.


        Versatility: +2
        Power: -1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Alignment and Class Skills: Some minor limitations here, but nothing too bad.


        (1) Planar Array (Ex): Alters class skills. There’s a definite occultist feel here: you get a bunch more wild talents at 1st level, but they’re keyed to a special bonded object without which you cannot use those talents. The penalties for replacing this object are incredibly steep (one week and 500 gp/level!) so don’t let anything jeopardize it. Also note that the Wide Array and Narrow Array feats are specifically tailored to let entropists get more or fewer elements in their array.


        (1) Wild Talents: Alters and replaces infusions and utility talents. Wooow. Wow wow wow. I didn’t even know that I wanted a prepared caster version of the kineticist until I read this ability, and now I’m obsessed. The tl;dr version is that you’re now a prepared caster, except the “spells” you’re preparing are infusions and wild talents. Given your massive selection of elements and ability to learn new wild talents by spending money on them, there should be very few situations in which you’re not prepped for something. You can also heighten utility talents by preparing them in higher-level slots, which can single handedly make a lot of utility talent save-or-suck builds viable. A wand of keep watch is going to be your best friend in this archetype, as you’ll need to stay awake basically every night to keep learning new wild talents.


        (1) Shuffling Focus (Ex): Replaces elemental focus and alters kinetic blast. Shuffling focus is kind of like a wizard’s opposition school, except instead of a type of magic (abjuration, conjuration, illusion, etc.) you’re instead focusing on one of the four elements in your array—and you’re focusing on it randomly. Now, note that no matter what your shuffling focus gets assigned to, you can still use infusions and utility talents from any element in your array, but because they’ll take penalties to save DCs and caster level it won’t typically be in your best interest to prepare enemy-targeted wild talents from your “opposition elements.” Shuffling focus will require creative play, but thanks to favored focus and with some lateral thinking in your wild talent preparation, you should be okay.


        (2) Mutable Defense (Su): Alters elemental defense. I’m definitely not a fan of this class feature. The power of defense talents varies wildly, and you can’t reasonably plan to replace wondrous items (e.g., amulets of natural armor with Flesh of Wood, cloaks of resistance with Victorious Aria, etc.) if you can’t plan for what defense talent you’ll have on a given day. It would stink to sell your cloak of resistance because you were counting on Victorious Aria’s resistance bonus to saves, only to then get energy resistances from Reactive Skin the next day.


        (7) Favored Focus (Ex): Replaces expanded element. Favored focus eases the sting of shuffling focus by making at least one of your elements available to you at all times. Because you’ll get pretty consistent bonuses to save DCs and caster level in your favored focus, you should plan to make your favored focus a more combat-oriented element. I’d hesitate to choose aether, for example, because aether just doesn’t do a lot of blasting with infusions or enemy-targeted utility talent work, and therefore doesn’t benefit significantly from save DC or caster level boosts. You also have to remember that your choice of favored focus will determine your composite blast complement, which can be important depending on your array.


        (9) Bend Fate (Sp): Replaces metakinesis (maximize) and metakinesis (twice). Bend fate is an interesting little ability in that it gives you a chance to influence where your shuffling focus goes, but penalizes you quite steeply for not going with the flow of the d4. Additionally, losing your maximize and twice metakinesis, while not devastating to your sustainable DPR (which probably couldn’t accommodate that much burn anyway, unless you’re playing a high-level gathlain) significantly cuts down on your ability to blow things up in one massive round. This is very much a versatility for power tradeoff.


        (20) Full Array (Sp): Replaces omnikinesis. Finally, at 20th level, you can choose where your shuffling focus goes, although you still pay a penalty for it. I guess this is slightly better than omnikinesis, so I’ll rate it green.


Is It Worth It?


        Sure! Being able to prepare infusions and utility talents is an awesome addition to the kineticist chassis, and with so many wild talents floating around, you’ll mostly be able to work around the vicissitudes of your shuffling focus. A fun archetype.




________________






[3PP] Fusion Kineticist


The Basics


        A two-element kineticist that gets lots and lots of infusion cross-fertilization, bonus wild talents, and more. Definitely on the more powerful end, and the archetype gives up basically nothing if you were already planning to have two elements.


        Versatility: +1
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Unbalanced Elements (Su): Alters elemental focus and replaces 1st-level infusion and basic utility talent. Two blasts right from the get-go is awesome. You’ll probably want to diversify into one physical blast and one energy blast, just to have options. Keep in mind that you won’t be able to pick up wild talents for your sub-element at the same level as your main element until 15th level, though.


        (2) Defined Defense (Su): Alters elemental defense. This is the other thing to be aware of: your sub-element, not your main element, gets the defensive talent. As usual, earth and water are probably the strongest first-party mechanical contenders here, with light, time, and maybe viscera headlining the third-party options.


        (7) Elemental Balance (Su): Replaces 7th-level expanded element. Really nothing but upside here: you still get a composite blast that deals better mixed damage, you get a second defense talent and another infusion or utility talent for free, and your effective kineticist level for your sub-element (Level - 2) surpasses what the base kineticist gets (Level - 4). What’s not to love?


        (9) Cross Infusion (Sp): Replaces metakinesis (maximize). I love cross infusion. It seems innocuous, but the fact that you can cherrypick the absolute best infusions and then apply them to any of your blasts opens up a whole world of interesting combos. Chain on a fire blast? Magnetic Infusion on a telekinetic blast? Bloody Infusion on an acid blast? Cross infusion ensures that your best infusions can always be available, no matter what blasts you’re using.


        (15) Elemental Harmony (Su): Replaces 15th-level expanded element. More free wild talents, better effective kineticist levels for your sub-element, and you can even pare back the action economy for utility talents. Celerity + Pitfall in the same action? I’ll take two, thank you very much.


        (20) Perfect Fusion (Sp): Replaces omnikinesis. Getting two wild talents right at 20th level means you can pick up some extraordinarily powerful 9th-level utility talents (if you have any—only mind has several) at your leisure. It feels pretty tame for a capstone, but don’t sleep on the fact that you can now also apply basically any infusion to your composite blasts, as well.


Is It Worth It?


        Heck yeah! There’s basically no reason not to play a fusion kineticist if you’re already planning to go for two elements instead of one or three.




________________






[3PP] God-Touched




The Basics


        God-touched kineticists are a hybrid archetype with the oracle class, complete with a curse and revelations. As with many third-party archetypes, there are some minor changes that can help bring it in line with Paizo content, but it’s a well-balanced archetype overall that won’t bust the power curve. This would be a good time to mention that I’ve previously written an oracle guide called Bell, Book, and Candle, so jump over to that if you’re uncertain about where to find good revelations or curses.


        Versatility: -1
        Power: +1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Kineticist’s Curse (Ex): If you’re going to get revelations, getting a curse seems a reasonable trade—the only archetype that I know of that grants you revelations without one is the ravener hunter inquisitor archetype, and that’s a pretty brokenly powerful archetype. Since you can’t perform any dual-cursed oracle shenanigans where the strengths of one curse cancel out the weaknesses of another curse (deaf + wolf-scarred face is the classic combo) and can’t select a curse that grants bonus spells, your menu of available options will be limited. Tongues is probably the least restrictive curse that meets the rubric.


        (3) Revelation: Alters elemental focus and expanded element, and replaces 2nd-, 6th-, 10th-, 14th-, and 18th-level utility wild talents. Let’s get some obvious caveats (obveats) out of the way: first, some of the mysteries on this list are from third-party publishers, and as I haven’t rated them myself, I can’t speak to their power level. Cautious GMs should almost certainly restrict players to first-party mysteries and revelations unless they want to take the time to review content themselves. Second, there’s absolutely no reason why some kineticist elements should get to pick their revelations from several different mysteries (lookin’ at you, fire, void, and wood) while others are stuck with just one mystery. GMs, force your players to pick one mystery at 3rd level and stick with it until 20th—that’s what oracles have to do, after all. Third, although revelations are often quite powerful, so are utility wild talents. This feels like a pretty even-stevens trade to me.


Is It Worth It?


        Sure, I think so. With half of your utility talents gone, god-touched kineticists will be even more focused on getting cream-of-the-crop wild talents than other types of kineticists, but they’ll also have some pretty powerful revelations to compensate. Flight revelations, armor revelations, and initiative-modifying revelations are generally the most powerful effects available in the oracle mysteries, so prioritize those if they’re available to your element.




________________






[3PP] Hex Kineticist


The Basics


        A kineticist who trades out some of their utility talents and an expanded element in exchange for witch hexes and a familiar! A hugely potent debuffer, kind of like a souped-up arakineticist archetype, but for any element.


        Versatility: +2
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Devoted Servant (Ex): Replaces basic utility talent. A fully formed witch’s familiar is a way better option than your basic utility talent, so snap this up eagerly. Guard them with your life, though, because you’ll be abysmal at blasting if they ever die.


        (2) Kinetic Curse (Su): Replaces 2nd-level utility talent and alters utility talents. Talk to your GM about their interpretation of this rules text. Since you don’t actually get the hex class feature, I would assume you’re ineligible for the Extra Hex feat, but you may be able to select more hexes than what your level progression would give you with the Extra Wild Talent feat. That might be stretching it, though. Anyway, the fact that you can get even a limited number of hexes at no penalty to your level and using your Constitution modifier for save DCs is incredible. An easy way to make a kineticist a top-shelf debuffer.


        (3) Imbued Familiar (Sp): Replaces 3rd-level infusion and deliver touch spells. It’s nice that your familiar can now defend itself, but I can’t be the only one who thinks it would be a weird choice to have your familiar draw this much attention to itself when you blast gets tanked every time it dies.


        (7) Elemental Bond (Sp): Replaces 7th-level expanded element. Sure, okay, whatever, infusion specialization for your familiar. Let’s get to the good part: Hex Synthesis. In addition to vastly streamlining your action economy by letting you hex while you blast, you can attach hexes to form infusions. If you have a massive AoE form infusion, attach Evil Eye to it, and then just keep using Cackle, you can keep an entire battlefield full of enemies debuffed for entire fights. Hex Synthesis is super cheap at only 2 points of burn, too.


        (10) Expanded Element (Su): Replaces 10th-level utility talent, although I don’t see how it’s possible to both take a hex and replace the utility talent slot that hex was supposed to go into. Delaying your expanded element is obviously not great for kineticists, but I’d wager that the class features you get in this archetype are more than worth your while.


        (15) Greater Elemental Bond (Sp or Su): Replaces 15th-level expanded element. Again, familiar blasting, yadda yadda yadda. Let’s skip to the part where you get a major hex for free and can now start applying major hexes to your blasts for a measly 4 points of burn. Yeah, let’s get to that.


        (20) True Elemental Bond (Sp): Replaces omnikinesis. Better than omnikinesis, certainly, and your familiar can continue to do wackier and wackier stuff with its own blasting. Very nice if you want to let it shoot stuff while you focus on debuffing.


Is It Worth It?


        Woof. Yeah, it definitely is. Comboing witch hexes with kinetic blasts turns this archetype into a major force to be reckoned with, and hexes are flexible enough that you can choose to be a buffer, a debuffer, or anything else you might care for.




________________






[3PP] Kinetic Duelist


The Basics


        A more lightly armored version of the kinetic knight that also gives up fewer class features. Fun on the whole, if you want to play a kineticist assassin or something like that.


        Versatility: -1
        Power: +1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Weapon and Armor Proficiencies (Ex): You pick up medium armor and bucklers. Good gets.


        (1) Kinetic Blade Mastery (Su): Alters kinetic blast. Kinetic blade mastery and your Ranged Blast infusion essentially swap the melee/ranged DPS role for kineticists. Instead of getting ranged blasts for free and buying your way into melee through Kinetic Blade, you now get melee blasts for free and can buy your way into ranged blasts through Ranged Blast. Getting to take attacks of opportunity right at 1st level with no investment in Kinetic Whip is a big deal, and you should be very excited about it.


        (1) Ranged Blast (Ex): Every kinetic duelist should still buy back into the right to use ranged blasts, because you never know when they’ll be needed. It’s nice that Ranged Blast doesn’t count against the number of form infusions you use on a blast—there’s no functional loss over a base kineticist.


        (10) Kinetic Whip Mastery (Ex): That’s a lot of fun, too! Now you can get 10-foot reach permanently for only 1 point of burn.


        (11) Synchronous Charge (Su): Replaces supercharge. You won’t be able to use composite blasts or metakinesis as much as you want to, but the flexibility to use full-attack actions is good recompense. Remember that your Kinetic Blade hits for full kinetic blast damage (minus elemental overflow bonuses) on every iterative attack in a full-attack action.


        (13) Kinetic Assault (Su): Replaces metakinesis (quicken). Hmmm. The extra damage is nice and all, but keep in mind that a number of elements get at-will teleportation, and any element can grab Ride the Blast to essentially teleport and attack at the same time. I’m fine with it.


        (17) Dual Blades (Su): Replaces metakinesis (twice). Yeah, that’s fun. You’re essentially replacing your metakinesis with an infusion that does the same thing, but the infusion is better because infusion specialization can cut its cost down where there are fewer opportunities to reduce the cost of metakinesis.


Is It Worth It?


        If you’re put off by the kinetic knight’s overwhelming loss of class features and were hoping to play something on a nice middle ground between super tanky (kinetic knight) and only mildly tanky (base kineticist) then kinetic duelist is the archetype for you. It’s fun to get to take attacks of opportunity right away in your melee kineticist career.




________________






[3PP] Kinetic Lancer


The Basics


        A kineticist archetype that takes a bunch of cues from the iconic dragoon class in Final Fantasy. Think jumping a hundred feet into the air, then impaling someone with a spear of fire on the way down.


        Versatility: +0
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Energy Pounce (Ex): Replaces basic utility talent and 1st-level infusion. Oh, I’m so glad that there’s an archetype for the Kinetic Leap playstyle in this class. It just makes me so happy. Because you get Kinetic Blade for free, you’re not really losing an infusion, and you’ll want to accept 1 point of burn on Kinetic Leap at the start of every adventuring day so that you can get your Acrobatics bonuses and free Kinetic Blade infusion more or less permanently. It’s also incredibly nice that your movement when using Kinetic Leap doesn’t provoke any attacks of opportunity (!), and doubly nice that you take no damage from falling.
        Before going on, it’s worth reviewing Acrobatics and the jumping rules for a second. You’ll primarily be long jumping in this archetype, the DCs for which are easy to remember: whatever the result of the skill check, that’s how far you jumped. If you can’t get a running start of 10 feet, you only move half as far. If your base speed is over 30 feet (take note, those using Elemental Acceleration!) then you get a +4 racial bonus to jump for every 10 feet by which your base speed exceeds 30 feet, but you can never jump farther than your maximum movement (i.e., a double move) for the round. Keep track of those rules, because kinetic lancer is going to tamper with some of them in awesome ways.


        (1) Dragoon Dive (Ex): Replaces gather power. Before you do a spit take upon realizing that this archetype trades away gather power and supercharge, don’t worry! You still effectively have them, it’s just that they’re tied to jumping and attacking in full-round actions now, rather than being discrete elements. I mean, this is just cool. You’re permanently charging enemies and impaling them with your Kinetic Blade, Kinetic Spear, Blade Whirlwind, etc., and you can even tag Vital Blade onto the end—definitely not a feature of most charges. You won’t be able to get your burn reduction if you’re ever staggered, but then again, neither would a base kineticist.


        (2) Dragoon Leap (Ex): Replaces 2nd-level utility talent. Here’s where things start to get fun. Air’s Leap grants a bonus to your Acrobatics checks made to jump equal to your kineticist level, doubles the result of your final Acrobatics check (!), and you can accept a point of burn to double the results again. Then dragoon leap eliminates the restriction on how far you can jump in a round and treats you as always having a running start. At 2nd level, you should have roughly a +20 bonus to Acrobatics (10 Kinetic Leap + 2 Air’s Leap + 3 class skill + 2 ranks + 3 Dexterity), and the result of every roll gets doubled. Assuming an average roll of 10, you can jump 60 feet horizontally without taking burn, or 15 feet straight into the air. This will get even crazier.


        (5) Kinetic Spear (Su): Kinetic Spear is totally optional, but awesome. It’s got reach, you can do some mild crit-fishing with it (18-20/x2 is possible by endgame), and it accepts Vital Blade for even more damage. Plus, there’s no attack more archetypal for a Final Fantasy-esque dragoon, right!?


        (8) Dragoon Frenzy (Ex): Replaces 8th-level utility talent. So now you can freaking pounce with your Kinetic Blade, Spear, Whip, etc., doling out full-attacks to your target (with Vital Blade, no less!) and dealing some extra damage in a five-foot area around the target. I don’t need to tell veteran players how awesome this is.


        (9) Impaling Crash (Su): Replaces metakinesis (maximize). You probably weren’t going to be using maximize metakinesis that much in this archetype anyway, so good riddance. Instead, you get to freaking impale people with your kinetic weaponry. Doing this over multiple rounds is costly on the burn, but leaves enemies pincushioned with multiple weapons that they probably can’t remove, leading to some positively lethal damage over time.


        (11) Impossible Leap (Ex): Replaces supercharge, although remember, you’re not actually losing supercharge. So...at this point, just to take stock of where we are, let’s check back in on our Acrobatics check, which was a +20 at 2nd level, leading to an average of 60 feet jumped on a roll of a natural 10. Now, we should have (at a bare minimum) a +50 (20 Kinetic Leap + 11 Air’s Leap + 3 class skill + 11 ranks + 5 Dexterity), and every result gets multiplied by four. On that same natural roll of a 10, you can leap 240 feet horizontally, or 60 feet vertically. Um.


        (13) Furious Dragoon (Ex): Replaces 13th-level infusion. More attacks, more AoE damage. I’d take this infusion for the archetype even if it was optional.


        (17) Brutal Dragoon (Su): Replaces metakinesis (twice). I’ll take passive damage and save DC buffs over expensive metakinesis any day of the week.


Is It Worth It?


        Kinetic lancer is easily my favorite archetype for the kineticist, first-party or third-party. How fun is this!? By 15th level, the standard level at which I end most of my guides’ builds, you’d have a +75 to Acrobatics checks to jump (20 Kinetic Leap + 15 Air’s Leap + 3 class skill + 15 ranks + 6 Dexterity + 16 racial from Elemental Acceleration) and would be able to leap 340 feet horizontally or 85 feet vertically on a roll of a natural 10. Plus you get pounce, some cool damage over time abilities, and more. An incredibly fun, effective, and mobile melee combatant.




________________






[3PP] Metakinetic Savant (Veto)


The Basics


        An archetype that gives up absolutely nothing in exchange for wheelbarrows full of extra metakinetic power. One of the only archetypes that I feel obligated to veto outright.


        Versatility: +2
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (5) Metakinetic Feats: Replaces metakinesis. Even though this ability replaces metakinesis, it doesn’t really replace it—it only gives you the option of replacing it. If you want to hang onto empower, maximize, and quicken, go right ahead! But you have a bunch more options now. I won’t bother to rate all of these feats, because there are oodles of them, but I’m comfortable rating it blue since it’s nothing but more versatility to build the kineticist you want.


        (5) Expanded Metakinesis (Ex): Again, nothing but upside. You can choose to get Expanded Metakinesis earlier and pick up different things with it, but there’s no requirement that you do.


        (6) Metakinetic Buffer (Su): Alters internal buffer. More upside with very few consequences. Since infusion specialization will generally be used to reduce the cost of your infusions to 0 burn, gather power is all that’s left to handle metakinesis for base kineticists. Now, though, you can get more burn for your metakinesis for free. This is probably too powerful.


        (16) Advanced Metakinetic Master (Su): Replaces metakinetic master. All your metakinesis gets a one-burn discount, rather than one specific type of metakinesis. Blatant power creep.


        Special: Ha! And you can take the archetype with other archetypes that alter metakinesis, so there’s not even an opportunity cost. Literally every kineticist and legendary kineticist should be a metakinetic savant, by this logic.


Is It Worth It?


        It’s a third-party archetype that has no opportunity cost, stacks with almost everything, and vastly increases power and versatility. Remember what my criterion for overpowered content is: if it’s so good that you’d be a fool not to take it, it’s too good. That’s the case here. Veto this archetype ASAP, GMs.




________________






[3PP] Onslaught Blaster


The Basics


        A kineticist that just shoots a bunch of weak blasts instead of one big one. Mathematically, however, the archetype changes very little, and the opportunity cost of so many replaced class features is that you can’t select a better archetype in its place. I’d probably stay away from this one, on the whole.


        Versatility: +1
        Power: -1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Onslaught Blast (Sp): Alters kinetic blast. Onslaught blast has a couple of differences worth noting with kinetic blast. On the positive side, you can target more than one target in a turn right from 1st level. Each will get targeted at your full BAB, and each will take the static damage from your Constitution modifier. Assuming onslaught blasters can hit, this makes them very good at “AoE damage” in early levels, and the fact that you can choose how many blasts go to each target means that you can afford to be more granular in how you deal damage than a normal kineticist—avoiding hitting enemies with 1 HP left for 250 damage would be nice, no? Additionally, since attacks against a single target are counted as one attack, you don’t even have to worry about getting feats like Clustered Shots—you attack once, deal with spell resistance, DR, and energy resistances once, etc. On the negative side, onslaught blasters are exclusively ranged combatants, and can’t ever use melee or short-range form infusions, even to protect themselves when backed into a corner. On the whole, I think you pick up more here than you lose. Don’t worry about the damage bonuses—they’re just there to compensate you for the loss of damage from elemental overflow, and don’t contribute to power creep.


        (1) Focused Blaster (Ex): Replaces gather power, supercharge, and metakinesis master. A few more attacks for you here from your iteratives, but they won’t make an enormous difference. A net positive for the archetype, still.


        (3) Burned Blaster (Ex) and Weakened Overflow (Su): Alters elemental overflow. These abilities are really two halves of one ability, elemental overflow, so I’m rating them together. The bonus blasts just compensate you a bit more for the damage you lost in onslaught blast and elemental overflow. It’s not really better or worse.


        (5) Excessive Blaster (Su): Replaces metakinesis (empower). Quick napkin math would seem to say these abilities are about the same. Empowered simple physical blasts deal ((1d6+1) * (kineticist level / 2)) * 1.5, which is about 68 average damage at 20th level, plus your Constitution modifier and elemental overflow bonuses. Excessive blaster deals ((1d6+2) * (kineticist level / 2)) + ((1d6+2) * 5), which is about 83 average damage at 20th level, plus your Constitution modifier. Again, accounting for elemental overflow, these are almost exactly the same.


        (9) Targeted Strike (Su): Replaces metakinesis (maximize). An interesting ability in that targeted strike doesn’t do the same thing as maximize metakinesis, but focuses more on debuffing. The utility here depends on your party composition and where the enemy you’re targeting lies in initiative order. Since the debuff only lasts until the start of the enemy’s next turn, targeting someone who comes immediately after you in initiative will do literally nothing. I’m inclined to think this ability isn’t as good as maximize metakinesis.


        (13) Extreme Blaster (Su): Replaces metakinesis (quicken). Does the exact same thing as metakinesis (quicken), if you think about it. You get an additional 10 blasts at 20th level, which is the equivalent of shooting a second blast.


        (17) Omni Strike (Su): Replaces metakinesis (twice). Because you can’t apply your empower or maximize metakinesis replacements to omni strike, it’s actually a much worse ability than metakinesis (twice). The whole point of that metakinesis is that you get to carry over metakinesis to two blasts! Without that, it’s just bad.


Is It Worth It?


        Meh. Quick math on my part would seem to say that the changes to metakinesis and elemental overflow don’t actually, well, change anything from the base kineticist, which leaves you with only the ability to target multiple enemies at low levels without form infusions. That’s not terrible, but it’s also not an advantage that would make you want to run out and grab this archetype.




________________






[3PP] Soundweaver (Rewrite)


The Basics


        The soundweaver archetype is meant to turn you into a bard/kineticist hybrid, but it does so by giving you a bunch of upgrades to Cantikinesis, which is already a monstrously overpowered utility talent. In lieu of rating the class, I’m simply going to rewrite it below.


        Versatility: +1
        Power: -1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Minstrel Training (Ex): Alters kineticist class skills, base saving throws, and wild talent key ability score. (No changes need to made here. Balanced.)


So you’re a bard now in all but name: same skill ranks, same saves, same key ability score. If you want to play the normal kineticist blaster role, this is a demonstrable downgrade, and certainly any key ability score other than Constitution is about as welcome as a kick in the fruitstand. Nevertheless, soundweaver, we’ll play along, yes?


        (1) Burning Passion (Ex): Alters burn. A soundweaver’s power over the elements is not drawn from the elements themselves, but from the music created by the inner and outer spheres. A soundweaver may choose to ignore the detrimental effects of a number of points of burn per day equal to their Charisma modifier. Any points of burn taken in excess of the soundweaver’s Charisma modifier cause the soundweaver to take nonlethal damage, as per the burn ability. Points of burn negated by the burning passion ability still provide positive effects such as elemental overflow bonuses.


Really just an insurance policy against soundweavers prematurely destroying themselves with burn.


        (5) Kinetic Concert (Su): Replaces internal buffer and 5th-, 9th-, 13th-, and 17th-level infusions. At 5th level, a soundweaver can create bardic performances as a bard of their soundweaver level. Soundweavers need not use instruments to begin or maintain a kinetic concert, instead using manifestations of elemental power (e.g., raindrops drumming on rocks, the flickering light of fire, etc.) to create performances with visual or auditory components. This ability counts as bardic performance for the purpose of qualifying for feats and masterpieces and for effects which affect bardic performances.


There’s really no need to hit the soundweaver with an effective level penalty. Surrendering four infusions and internal buffer is enough to merit a fully scaling bardic performance, especially without all the Cantikinesis nonsense.


        (11) Kinetic Masterpiece (Su): At 11th and 19th levels, soundweavers may choose to learn a bardic masterpiece in place of an infusion wild talent, treating their soundweaver level as their effective bard level to determine its effects. Soundweavers cannot learn masterpieces in place of feats, although those with bard levels can qualify for and learn masterpieces as a bard.


We’ll make this optional, but the rest of it can be left as-is.


        (20) Harmonious Convergence (Su): Replaces omnikinesis. At 20th level, the soundweaver has mastered their ability to command the elements with their music. During a kinetic concert, they can use gather power as a swift action to reduce the cost of a kinetic blast by 1 (to a minimum of 0). This cannot be done on the same turn they use gather power as a move or full-round action.


No real changes except to eliminate the bardic masterpiece normally granted at 20th level.


Is It Worth It?


        I like the bard/kineticist hybrid idea, but Cantikinesis is an overwhelmingly powerful way to give these abilities to kineticists. If you’re looking for a good buffer with lower damage/debuffing potential, the rewritten soundweaver that I present here is a good option, and maintains the power balance that Paizo likely would have wanted for the class.




________________






[3PP] Telekinetic Bladeshifter


The Basics


        An elemental annihilator-esque, aether-only archetype that gives you some powerful combat abilities in exchange for nearly all of your infusion complement.


        Versatility: +0
        Power: +2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Weapon and Armor Proficiencies: Alters weapon and armor proficiencies. Sure, nothing but upshot here. Good to have greatswords and the like available, although with scaling damage as sacred weapon, you’re free to pick whatever you like.


        (1) Telekinetic Focus: Alters elemental focus. Alright, that’s not so bad. Telekinetic bladeshifter lets you hold on to all your utility talents while tossing your infusions, and you really couldn’t have a better element for that than aether.


        (1) Base Attack Bonus and Base Saves: Alters BAB and saves. Full BAB is obviously excellent, although you’re compensated somewhat by elemental overflow not providing any bonus to attack rolls. Losing strong Reflex saves is...acceptable. I’d rather have strong Fortitude saves anyway.


        (1) Telekinetic Blade (Sp): Alters elemental overflow, kinetic blast, and infusions, and replaces 1st-, 5th-, 9th-, 13th-, and 17th-level infusions. Woof. There’s a lot to unpack here. For starters, you’re not really a kineticist anymore, at least not in the traditional sense—with so many of your infusion slots gone and no way to use form infusions, your AoE damage potential (and much of your debuffing potential) is just gone. To make matters hairier, you also can’t effectively use any other blast than Telekinetic Blast. The upside to all this is that you now get to wield what is more or less a standard weapon using the same scaling damage dice as a warpriest, and you even get advanced weapon training (!) as a fighter at 9th level. This isn’t truly better or worse than a base kineticist, it’s just that the two are built for radically different things. This has more of a full-BAB fighter feel to it than a kineticist feel to it.


        (1) Bonus Feats: Alters wild talents. This is the real difference between this archetype and elemental annihilator. Whereas elemental annihilator forces you to lose all your utility talents, telekinetic bladeshifter just gives you the option to trade away as many as you like. Don’t have anything that fits your build? Go with something different! Hard to get mad at more options.


        (2) Forcewoven Shield (Su): Replaces elemental defense. Hmmm. I like better AC as much as the next person, and there’s certainly no shortage of ways to get a shield bonus, even without wielding a shield: a wand of shield, Shield of Swings, Two-Weapon Defense, etc. will all work. Still, the bonuses are finicky enough to acquire that I’m not quite as fond of them as I am of Force Ward.


        (3) Forcewoven Weapon (Su): Replaces 3rd-, 11th-, and 19th-level infusions. Ohhh, now that’s tasty. This is essentially a magus’ arcane pool packaged into a kineticist ability, and I’ll gladly take that over three infusions that you couldn’t really use anyway. The real kicker, though, is that if you have Force Blast, you can expend 2 points of burn (reduced as normal by infusion specialization!) to convert your entire attack to (1) target touch AC, (2) deal force damage,  and (3) deal normal weapon damage. That’s really good. You’ll now hit everything in the game easily, ignore DR, damage incorporeal creatures normally, and all for full damage. Sure, you’ll have to pick up Spell Penetration and Greater Spell Penetration, but it’s well worth the effort.


        (9) Bladeshifter’s Specialty (Ex): Replaces composite specialization, 5th-, 11th-, and 17th-level infusion specializations, and metakinesis (maximize and twice). I suppose it’s fine to lose most of your metakinesis and infusion specializations, since your kinetic blasts and infusions are mostly gone, and there’s no denying that some of these specialties are cool. Seems pretty late for some of them, though. Like...you’re really telling me that a telekinetic bladeshifter who wants to build a Two-Weapon Fighting build hasn’t been able to manifest a second weapon until now?


        (20) Telekinetic Weapon Mastery (Ex): Replaces omnikinesis. Oh, jeez, what a powerhouse. DR/— is beefy enough without adding the potential for x2/14-20 dual-wielded kukris and all the critical feats you can shake a stick at. And unlike rogues, who can’t really make great use of high crit modifiers, the fact that your weapon damage scales over time will mean that you’re dealing very respectable damage with all these crits. You’ll definitely be better at combat with this ability.


Is It Worth It?


        For me, telekinetic bladeshifter is what elemental annihilator should have been: you’ve got plenty of potential for building a more combat-focused kineticist here, but you don’t have to surrender all your utility to do so. Assuming you’re fine with being locked into aether and don’t want to do any AoE blasting, there aren’t a lot of downsides here.




________________






[3PP] True Psychic


The Basics


        A kineticist that perfectly blends the aether and mind elements in a beautiful union. It’s a lot of fun, and a kineticist I would probably want to try at some point.


        Versatility: +1
        Power: +1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Psionic Focus (Ex): Replaces 1st-level infusion. Heck yeah, two elements for the price of one infusion? I’ll take it. Mental Blast is almost certainly the right call here, as it deals untyped damage on an energy blast with a huge infusion selection. Can’t get much better than that.


        (2) Psionic Defense (Sp): Alters elemental defense. Force Ward would be my pick, but YMMV.


        (7) Psychic Residue (Su): Replaces 7th-level expanded element. Telekinetic Blast’s big failing is that it has nearly no good infusions to go with it. With all of mind’s many infusions applied, it suddenly becomes one of the better simple physical blasts out there. Another reason not to settle for Telekinetic Blast until you can get this power to go with it.


        (8) Mindscape Traveler (Su): Replaces 8th-level utility talent. As I’ve said before, I haven’t delved too deeply into the mindscape rules, but if you want to play a true psychic, you’d probably want this ability anyway. It’s cool to be able to invade someone’s mind and blow their brains to bits—with mind bullets! That’s telekinesis, Kyle.


        (9) Psychic Composites (Sp): Replaces metakinesis (maximize). I’m totally fine with surrendering maximize metakinesis—it doesn’t get used all that often anyway, unless you’re winding up for a truly epic round of damage. I don’t particularly know why you’d use Force Blast when Encephalon Blast is available (fighting incorporeals, maybe?) but the option is nice to have.


        (15) Mindscape Savant (Su): Replaces 15th-level expanded element. I’m bummed that you don’t get the improvements to save DCs and CL with your 15th-level expanded element, but it is what it is. More mindscape shenanigans for you.


        (20) Mindscape Master (Su): Replaces omnikinesis. Now that’s more like it. If you can get someone inside a mindscape, you own them. A perfect way for this archetype to show that it’s the master of the mental battlefield.


Is It Worth It?


        Yep! Being able to pick and choose basically everything from either mind or aether right from 1st level is something I didn’t even know I wanted until I got it. If you want to play, well, a true psychic, there’s no better archetype for you than this one: it shores up a lot of the weaknesses of both elements while taking away relatively little.




________________






[3PP] War Kineticist


The Basics


        An archetype that experiments with “battle burn,” a replenishing resource that can only fuel short-term utility talents.


        Versatility: +1
        Power: -1


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Burn (Ex): Alters burn. Hmmm. A lower burn cap means fewer permanent elemental overflow bonuses (not that this archetype really does elemental overflow anyway) and fewer big nova turns. I dunno. On its own, it seems like a relatively minor downgrade, but a downgrade nevertheless.


        (3) Battle Burn (Ex): Alters elemental overflow. Ironically enough given its name, battle burn actually makes you less effective in out-and-out combat—kineticists really need elemental overflow to compete with other classes, especially for accuracy. Most ¾-BAB classes can self-buff somehow, and full-BAB classes are rarely without attack and damage bonuses (weapon training, rage or bloodrage, studied target, etc.). With only half your normal elemental overflow bonuses, you’ll basically be locked into energy blasts in order to even hit enemies. And battle burn, well, it’s good for utility talents, since those can’t normally have their cost reduced through gather power, so the war kineticist makes a good battlefield controller, buffer, or other role that relies on heavy utility talent use. I don’t think I would call it an upgrade for the traditional kineticist playstyle, though. Note that legendary kineticists get this class feature for free, albeit not as early.


        (6) Battle Buffer (Su): Alters internal buffer. More “free” burn, but only for certain uses. This is actually an upgrade, but not much of one, especially since you can’t refill the buffer (or spend burn from it) any faster than normal.


Is It Worth It?


        Eh, maybe. I’d prefer just to play a legendary kineticist if I want some battle burn, though. The rest of the archetype seems unnecessary.




________________














________________
The Chaff


[3PP] Aberrant Kineticist


The Basics


        A kineticist that grants malign eldritch forces influence over their mind in exchange for power. It suffers from a lot of weaknesses and design flaws.


        Versatility: -2
        Power: -2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Class Skills: Knowledge (Dungeoneering) is fine, I guess, since you don’t lose anything for it. Not really worth a rating, though.


        (1) Mental Fortitude (Ex): Alters base saving throws. Together with the possessive burn class feature, aberrant kineticists should basically never have to fear Will saves in much the same way that base kineticists never have to fear Fortitude saves. It’s nice that you get to keep your Reflex save progression the same, though. Can’t really rate this one—Will saves and Fortitude saves are just good for different things.


        (1) Malign Manifestation (Su): Replaces basic kinesis utility talent and alters kinetic blast. This archetype has a very Sha’ir occultist feel to it, and that’s not a good thing. I don’t think you particularly gain a lot from being able to originate your blasts from your wysp familiar, and if it’s killed (whether by AoE attacks that its improved evasion doesn’t end up protecting against or by normal, targeted attacks) you’re basically boned. Sure, you can re-summon it, but it costs 3 points of burn and a full-round action, neither of which you want to deal with in the middle of a pitched combat.


        (1) Possessive Burn (Ex): Alters key ability scores and burn. Ha! At 5 points of burn, your character becomes an NPC under the control of the GM. Yes, let’s see how well that plays in Poughkeepsie. Seriously, though, losing control of your character is terrible, and even if you wanted to use the “convert influence to lethal damage” mechanic to avoid that ultimate outcome, you can’t, really, because your Constitution score isn’t very high! Whereas base kineticists have huge Constitution scores that help them mitigate burn, an aberrant kineticist’s Constitution score is likely to be no higher than 14, which doesn’t leave them with much HP leeway to start taking lethal burn. This is bad, bad, bad.


        (1) Alien Union (Su): Replaces gather power and supercharge. This is actually kinda cool: in exchange for one or 2 points of unavoidable burn, you get to reduce the burn cost of all kinetic blasts for the next minute without having to use gather power or supercharge. If you’re about to go into a big, protracted fight, this might be worthwhile—the action economy means a lot in Pathfinder—but it’s unfortunate that you have no way to mitigate burn in shorter fights. If a fight lasts only two rounds and you spent 2 points of burn to join your alien union for a full minute, eight of those rounds are going to be wasted. Like I said, it’s cool, but I don’t think it’s more powerful.


        (3) Tainted Manifestation (Ex): Alters elemental overflow. Okay, so you get freaky and scary when you’ve got burn on board. The bigger problem with tainted manifestation is that it doesn’t alter elemental overflow in such a way that it accommodates the lower amount of burn you’re able to carry around on a regular basis. The overwhelming soul, for example, just gives you constant bonuses to hit and damage, but the aberrant kineticist doesn’t. Kind of a design oversight, as it’s unlikely that this archetype will ever be able to carry enough burn to reach the highest tier of elemental overflow bonuses. That ain’t good.


Is It Worth It?


        Ugh. No. Like I said, the archetype that aberrant kineticist reminds me of most is the Sha’ir occultist archetype, which is widely regarded as one of the worst, most broken archetypes out there—heck, there was even a Max the Min Monday on the Sha’ir on Reddit a while back. If your malign manifestation gets killed, you’re in serious trouble, and even when it’s around the possessive burn and tainted manifestation class features are a lot worse than anything the base kineticist gets.




________________






[3PP] Cerebral Kineticist


The Basics


        An Intelligence-based kineticist that pays a lower cost for their first few points of burn in exchange for a correspondingly higher cost for their last few points of burn. It doesn’t fare well.


        Versatility: -2
        Power: -2


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Intellectual Control (Su): Alters class skills and key ability score. Intelligence is an objectively inferior stat for kineticists to focus on than Constitution, but this does allow kineticists to play a more skill monkey playstyle. I would almost certainly opt to play with the mind element (which gets a number of good skill monkey utility talents) before resorting to cerebral kineticist, though.


(1) Mental Barrier (Ex): Replaces 1st-level infusion. “Ignoring burn?” you think, “It’s too good to be true!” And, well, yes, it is too good to be true. Sure, you don’t take any penalties from your first few points of burn, but any burn beyond your mental barrier limit is absolutely crippling. You’ll see.


(1) Psychological Burn (Ex): Alters burn. There’s the whammy. In exchange for taking no penalties on your first few points of burn, you take massive penalties on your last few points. You might be able to eke out 2 points of burn on a regular basis if you’re using an energy blast that doesn’t rely heavily on attack rolls, but any more than 2 points leaves you destroyed. This is the class feature that sinks the archetype.


(3) Cerebral Overflow (Ex): Alters elemental overflow. Cool, I guess, except you’ll never be able to take enough burn to proc most of elemental overflow’s bonuses without also rendering yourself a drooling, comatose wreck. 


Is It Worth It?


        Gracious me, no. Psychological burn is an awful idea for any kineticist, and will likely lead to your group becoming so exasperated with your character that they coup de grace you in your sleep just to give you a chance to bring in a backup character. Blech.




________________






[3PP] Nihilicist


The Basics


        The nihilicist is kind of like the cipher investigator archetype. Both involve nothingness, oblivion, and people gradually forgetting that you exist, and both are very flavorful archetypes that can’t pack enough mechanical punch to make them actually worth taking in most cases. The nihilicist loses a huge swath of its infusions and utility talents, not to mention its expanded element, and the cool features like nihilistic curse just can’t quite compensate.


        Versatility: -2
        Power: +0


        Stacks With: 


The Abilities


        (1) Unfound Element: Where other kineticists have elements, you just don’t! And that’s alright. Your utility talent complement is going to be pretty drastically limited, but your infusions are okay, and you get some other powers to make up for it.


        (1) Null Element (Su): Replaces elemental focus, expanded element, and basic utility talent, and alters utility talents. The biggest advantages to Empty Blast and Zero Blast (at least as presented in this ability—there will be upgrades later) are that they deal nihil damage, bypassing DR, energy resistances, and energy immunities, and that they can be either energy or physical blasts, depending on your preference. Kinda like two elements in one, right from 1st level. Now, you’re still losing all element-based defense talents, utility talents, infusions, etc., and even though some of those infusions will get returned to you, it’s a big loss. Speaking of infusions, I’ll sum up their ratings here. As you can see, the form infusions are pretty serviceable, but the substance infusions are pretty bad.


Associated Infusions
   * Form
   * Torrent (3rd)
   * Cyclone (4th)
   * Singularity (4th)
   * Chain (5th)
   * Cloud (7th)
   * Explosion (7th)
   * Substance
   * Pulling / Pushing Infusion (1st)
   * Bowling Infusion (2nd)
   * Grappling Infusion (5th)
   * Unraveling Infusion (5th)
   * Disintegrating Infusion (6th)
   * Enervating Infusion (7th)


        (1) Nihil Damage (Ex): Nihil damage can get healed in parallel with lethal damage, since it functions (for the most part) as nonlethal damage, so you’ll have to watch out for divine casters, fast healing or regeneration, etc. It’s cool that nihil damage ignores hardness and can disintegrate objects—you could blast through stone, adamantine, and even walls of force as early as 1st level. Great for lateral progression through a dungeon or simply disintegrating enemies’ weapons.


        (1) Nihilistic Curse (Su): Replaces 1st-, 9th-, and 17th-level infusions. Wow. Pretty nasty. Because you can affect multiple creatures at once with your curse and there’s no stated duration, your GM may rule that you can blind entire crowds of enemies as early as 5th level. Sickened is the only good condition at the 5 damage price point, but pretty much everything after that (with the possible exception of exhausted) is well worth the lost damage.


        (2) Nullify Wounds (Sp): Replaces elemental defense. This would a bottom-of-the-barrel defense talent if I were rating things normally, so there’s no reason why it shouldn’t receive a red rating here. Nullify wounds will make your magical healing a tiny bit more efficient, but the HP and uses per day are so small that there’s no real argument for it, especially not as a replacement for your defense talent.


        (2) Reduced Existence (Ex): Replaces 2nd-, 6th-, and 12th-level utility talents. Without any real utility talent variety to pick from, I suppose it’s not too devastating to lose this many utility talents, but then again, the benefits you get from reduced existence aren’t exactly great, either. Permanent mind blank at 12th level is the absolute best argument for the ability, but everything else is fairly humdrum. If you want to commit serious crimes, that 12th-level power is also aces. Maybe a nihilicist serial killer that the PCs have to bring to justice?


        (3) Nihilistic Infusion (Su): Replaces 3rd-level infusion. Some general action economy upgrades for your nihilistic curse class feature. Decent, but not mind-blowing.


        (20) Terminus (Sp): Replaces omnikinesis. What a way to go, eh? Just blow yourself to literal oblivion and leave behind a gift basket for your team. Gone but never forgotten! Oh, wait. No, you are forgotten. I read that wrong. This is worse than omnikinesis, and omnikinesis already wasn’t fabulous.


Is It Worth It?


        It isn’t, really. Nihilistic curse is cool, as is permanent mind blank, but those are both things that a dedicated void kineticist could achieve with the arakineticist archetype and the Kinetic Invocation feat. There’s simply too much being given up here.




________________


💪 Dips, VMC, & Gestalt
KIN 605: Dips, Gestalt, and Prestige
Dips


        Both dipping from a kineticist into another class and dipping from another class into kineticist are not avenues I would generally recommend. Kinetic blasts, utility talents, and infusions all have effective spell levels on which you are dependent for absolutely everything: kinetic blast damage, infusion and utility talent save DCs, caster level for penetrating spell resistance with energy blasts, and on and on. The kineticist has no Paizo-official means of making up these lost levels, and only one or two ways in third-party material to do so. Dexterity and Constitution, the kineticist’s main attributes, don’t particularly stack with anything else in Pathfinder, and with so few necessary feats, you’re not even really helping to smooth away feat prerequisites. Variant multiclassing is almost always the superior option for kineticists.




Variant Multiclassing


        Variant multiclassing was introduced in Pathfinder Unchained, and essentially allows you to give up some of your feats in exchange for class features from another class—Pathfinder 2e would go on to adapt this system to build its own archetype and multiclassing architecture. Let’s run through the list quickly.


   * Barbarian: This...actually isn’t bad, at least for melee builds. Rage synergizes nicely with your Constitution modifier, uncanny dodge is good for just about any slower class, and DR/— will help all the non-earth melee kineticists out there. You could do a great deal worse.
   * Rogue: A very, very appealing dip for aether kineticists, who can make excellent use of trapfinding in conjunction with Telekinetic Finesse and sneak attack in conjunction with Hyper-Dimensional Blast. I’d give this one a try myself in an aether build.
   * Inquisitor: A good all-purpose variant multiclass, and stern gaze stacks nicely with Kinetic Awe, if you want to go that route.
   * Monk: There are some fun Kinetic Fist builds out there that would get a lot easier with scaling unarmed strike damage, and Paizo seems to really encourage the monk/kineticist crossover concept. I’m fine with this. Make sure you pick up wild talents like Armor of Nature if you intend to do away with manufactured armor.
   * Alchemist: These abilities seem like they would be mostly wasted on a kineticist.
   * Cavalier: There are some good cavalier orders out there, but none that grab me by the throat and make me pay attention.
   * Cleric: Your Charisma score isn’t likely to be high enough to do much with channeling, but domain abilities can be exceptionally useful in the right circumstances.
   * Fighter: It’s not terrible, but there are kineticist archetypes (telekinetic bladeshifter, notably) that allow you to get fighter feats. Just stick with those.
   * Gunslinger: This was the archetype combo that Paizo missed. Who doesn’t want to shoot void blasts out of a gun? But there’s not a lot of synergy here.
   * Oracle: The revelations mostly don’t benefit kineticists, which is too bad, because oracle revelations are awesome.
   * Paladin: Eh, again, not terrible, but not particularly great, either.
   * Bard: Nah. You’re not really meant to be a skill monkey, and Charisma will almost always be a dump stat.
   * Druid: The animal companion is the only worthwhile part, and you can pick that up for fewer feats than you surrender to this variant multiclass.
   * Magus: I don’t even think the magus variant multiclass is functional for the kineticist?
   * Ranger: None of these features are great.
   * Sorcerer: The phoenix bloodline hack won’t work on a pyrokineticist, so that scuppers about the only worthwhile crossover.
   * Summoner: Be an artistic summoner if you want to summon monsters so badly.
   * Witch: Most of the witch variant multiclass can be recreated better with the hex kineticist archetype and the Elemental Whispers utility talent.
   * Wizard: Kineticists can get a familiar just fine with Elemental Whispers. I wouldn’t bother with the rest.




Gestalt Builds


Gestalt character creation is a process that essentially combines all the class features and casting capabilities of two classes, and is typically done when PCs need to be extra powerful, as when an adventure path designed for a four-person party is only being run by two or three PCs. Complete rules can be found here. It’s not terribly difficult to pick a gestalt class, because anything you choose isn’t a trade-off so much as an addition. Picking something that grants better armor proficiencies, bonus feats, a strong Will save progression, or more skill proficiency is always a good bet, but the kineticist doesn’t synergize super well with most classes. It’s a bit of an odd duck.




Prestige Classes


        Apart from the Deific Obedience evangelist prestige class, which I mention here only because a full 10 levels don’t cause you to lose more than one level of base class progression, the only other prestige class that I would consider for a kineticist is the esoteric knight. In exchange for about five levels of base class progression, you can get full BAB, a strong Will save, five bonus combat feats, and some fun psychic abilities. It’s great for elements such as aether that peak kind of early in their progression.
⚔️ Equipment
KIN 660: Magic Items


As much as I’d love to give you a detailed run-down of every magical item in Pathfinder, I don’t have to, because I already did! I released The Armamentarium a while back that surveys every unique weapon, unique armor, and wondrous item available in the game, then adds content tags to them so that you can easily search for bonuses, ability boosts, and more. /u/SorteKanin also has a fabulous search engine that helps you look for wondrous items appropriate for your character. These are resources that will help you build the kineticist you want and need in your own game, instead of the one I could recommend to you from the outside. There are precious few wondrous items worth having for kineticists, but I’ll do my best to call them out as I go.
🪄 Wondrous Items
Wondrous Items


The Big Six: “The Big Six” is a name that Pathfinder players use to talk about the six most important wondrous items in the game: a cloak of resistance, an amulet of natural armor, a ring of protection, an attribute-enhancing headband or belt, magical armor, and a magical weapon. Normally, there are no ifs, ands, or buts about any of these pieces of equipment—if you don’t have them, you’re dead—but there are some exceptions for kineticists that are worth noting.


   * Belt of Physical Might (Dexterity or Strength + Constitution): What kineticists don’t spend on a magical weapon, they should spend on a belt of physical might, which will boost both your Constitution score and either your Dexterity or Strength score, whichever you use to determine your attack bonuses. Constitution is by far the most important attribute for a kineticist to have, as it determines everything from your save DCs to your kinetic blast damage to how much burn you can take on in a day. If you can afford it, though, a dual-stat belt is really what you want.
   * Cloak of Resistance: Kineticists have terminally poor Will saves, which is the main reason you’ll be wanting a cloak of resistance. Vibrokineticists who have Victorious Aria can skip the cloak of resistance, as their defense talent’s resistance bonuses won’t stack with the cloak’s.
   * Magical Armor: Thanks to size bonuses to Dexterity from elemental overflow, enhancement bonuses to Dexterity from a belt of physical might, and any starting investment in the attribute, kineticists should have very high Dexterity modifiers after a while, which is just as well given that the base class is proficient only with light armor. As you gain in levels, wealth, and power, plan to invest in celestial armor, which counts as light armor, grants a +9 armor bonus to AC with a maximum Dexterity rating of +8, and even allows you to cast fly 1/day. There’s really no other sensible magical armor choice for PCs proficient with light armor. For hydrokineticists considering using Shroud of Water as their armor: just don’t. The shield bonus to AC is much more valuable.
   * Amulet of Natural Armor: d8 classes aren’t necessarily meant to be the toughest crowd around, but kineticists often turn that expectation on its head with their mountain of HP, strong defense talents, and Dexterity focus. Creatures in the late game will still probably hit you much of the time; that’s no reason to ignore your AC, however. If you’re a phytokineticist who has Flesh of Wood or a dragon pact kineticist you can skip the amulet of natural armor, as, again, the bonus types won’t stack.
   * Ring of Protection: Another AC-boosting item. No defense talents duplicate the effects of a ring of protection, so you’ll have to purchase this item no matter what kind of kineticist you’re playing.
