   * Headband of Inspired Wisdom / Vast Intelligence: Mental stats aren’t particularly important for kineticists. I’d gladly take any spare headbands of inspired wisdom lying around for the sake of my Will save and Perception checks, but it’s not necessarily something I would rush out to purchase at the earliest opportunity. Intelligence is also an option if you’re perverse and want better skills.
   * Magical Weapon: The least important item of the Big Six for a kineticist to have. Who needs weapons when you are a weapon? The main issue here is that, even though you’re proficient with simple weapons, you can’t gather power as a base kineticist without both hands free. You need to gather power nearly every turn, ergo no weapons. If you’re a legendary kineticist who can gather power with only one hand free, you might consider something like a +1 training dagger to grant you a bonus combat feat and take the occasional attack of opportunity without Kinetic Blade.


Conduit Glove (1,000 gp, CL 6): A wondrous item so obvious that I’m amazed Paizo didn’t create it. It was always a bit stupid that kineticists had to have both hands free to gather power, so the conduit glove is just a cheap way to fix that if your GM isn’t okay with the legendary kineticist class. Grab an overflowing rod, a +1 training dagger, a buckler, etc., and go to town.
Crystal of Elemental Knowledge (Price varies; CL 15): A nice way to pick up more utility talents. Since utility talents acquired this way can’t be used as prerequisites for anything, use these crystals to go back and pick up low-level one-off utility talents that you weren’t able to fit into the build.
Overflowing Rod (25,000 gp, CL 10): Another item, like the kineticist’s diadem, that has to actually be created by a high-level kineticist. Sigh. I actually prefer the overflowing rod to other more expensive options, though, because elemental overflow grants very stable, steady improvements to both attack and damage rolls, and can help you access size increases and sneak attack/crit negation percentages much faster than you’d normally be able to. Definitely a must-have for legendary kineticists, who can gather power with only one hand.
Robe of Elemental Resistance (Price varies; CL 15): The robe of elemental resistance does functionally the same thing as a cloak of resistance, except it occupies a different wondrous item slot and is limited by your elemental overflow scaling. This isn’t necessarily better or worse than a cloak of resistance unless you’re playing at very high levels and can get a +6 to your saves, but the shoulder slot has about twice the number of wondrous items available as the body slot, so making the switch opens up a lot of interesting items for your shoulders.
Vest of Elemental Defense (Price varies; CL 15): Just like the robe of elemental resistance can stand in for a cloak of resistance, the vest of elemental defense can stand in for a ring of protection. There are 56 wondrous items in the chest slot and over 200 magical rings in the game (and none of them are to be used lightly) so you’re trading up to quite a bit more versatility for no extra cost.


Bracers of Elemental Conversion (12,000 gp; CL 10): This is an example of a very well-balanced third-party item. Altering energy blasts so that they deal different kinds of energy damage could easily be overpowered, but between the burn cost, the relatively limited duration, and the reduction in damage, there’s a good cost associated with using the item that would prevent players from just spamming it. It opens up options for energy blasters without making them incredibly deadly.
Focusing Glove (Price varies; CL 5): There are some nice options available here, but the fact that using them costs you even more burn (which isn’t mitigated by infusion specialization) is a major party foul. Unless you’re a gathlain, you won’t have the ability to regularly pay for these enchantments.
Hollow Rod (121,500 gp, CL 15): The hollow rod is ridiculously, ridiculously expensive, but it allows you to maximize kinetic blast damage or Kinetic Healer healing 3/day for 0 burn. You won’t get this until very late in your career even if you decide to shoot for it, so don’t give it much thought. Also remember that base kineticists can’t gather power with even one hand occupied.
Kineticist’s Diadem / Esoteric Diadem (Lesser, 18,000 gp; Normal, 50,000 gp; Greater, 98,000 gp; CL 10): The kineticist’s diadem series gives you a little extra damage (1d6/2d6/3d6) on kinetic blasts that specifically match the attuned element in the diadem. Kineticist’s diadems are for the main five elements released in Occult Adventures, and esoteric diadems are for void and wood. As written, there aren’t any options for the third-party elements, but a reasonable GM should handwave that. One of the main issues here is that you can’t simply buy these items—they have to be created by a kineticist of at least 10th level who has Craft Wondrous Item. How many of those do you suppose are running around? Additionally, the extra damage doesn’t apply to most melee form infusions (Kinetic Blade, Kinetic Whip, etc.) so melee builds can get right the heck out.


Body Wraps of Kinetic Fist (Normal, 4,000 gp; Layering, 6,000 gp; CL 6): Natural attack and unarmed strike builds will really appreciate the body wraps of kinetic fist, and the option to layer it over an existing body slot item is great. I feel comfortable not vetoing this item—it’s essentially one level of infusion specialization that applies only to a specific form infusion, so it’s not busting the game wide open.
Kinetic Blademaster Glove (Price varies; CL 6): Kinetic Blade builds will get quite a bit out of these gloves. I’d recommend the enduring option so that you can take melee attacks of opportunity before Kinetic Whip hits the scene.
Ring of Condensing Explosions (Price varies; CL 10): Kineticists would want to save up for the 13,000 gp kinetic version of this ring, but only if they’ve invested heavily in Kinetic Bomb. It’s a great way to deal more damage to single targets, especially because the Kinetic Bomb infusion stacks with empower metakinesis and is easily reduced to 0 burn by infusion specialization.
Wraps of Suppressed Size (Normal, 32,000 gp; Surging, 76,000 gp; CL 12): I suppose it’s nice that you can hold onto your increases in reach, CMB, etc. from Kinetic Form even when you’re Medium-sized, but is it really worth this much money? You’re essentially paying 32,000-76,000 gp for the ability to deal with doorways.


Leviathan Cross (12,335 gp; CL 12): A +1 conductive light crossbow would actually allow you to make full-attacks with your kinetic blast, but you have to remember that you can’t use a move action to gather power and full-attack, and your kinetic blast would apply to only one shot anyway. Plus, you’d need to get Rapid Reload in order to do anything useful with this crossbow, and it’s a wasted feat. Why not just get Snake as usual, gather power as usual, and blast as usual?


Burn Fragment / Burn Shard (Price varies; CL 1 / 8): Once again, kineticists should pay the price for their burn. That’s what the class is designed around, and there’s a reason Paizo didn’t create any items or feats that allow them to sidestep the effects of burn.
Crown of Focused Power (Normal, 7,000 gp; Greater, 20,000 gp; CL 9): Wow, does this item need to be vetoed. Better burn reduction when gathering power, huge bonuses to elemental overflow, and no cap on daily uses? Throw this out of your games right now.
Disc of Rainbow Tears (18,400 gp; CL 15): Way too powerful, and dependent on teardrop ioun stones, which I’m also vetoing.
Planar Gates Tattoo (1,300 gp; CL 3): Kineticists probably shouldn’t be able to completely counter grapples just by purchasing a cheap slotless item. My two cents.
Ring of Elemental Strength (Simple, 5,000 gp; Composite, 10,000 gp; CL 10): Flat damage bonuses to kinetic blasts are the first thing every powergamer is going to look for. This is one of those third-party features that is so good that you’d be a fool not to take it, which is a sign that it probably needs to be vetoed.
Robe of Burn Conversion (30,000 gp; CL 15): Even taking into account the fact that you’d need to be a 15th-level aetherkineticist in order to craft this item, it’s still too powerful. From an HP damage perspective, you’ve essentially halved the price of each point of burn. Can you imagine? Veto.
Teardrop Ioun Stones (Price varies; CL 12): Too powerful again. Veto.
Vambraces of Overflowing Power (Price varies; CL 6): I’m inclined to veto this item. The designers at Paizo had a specific game balance in mind when they designed how elemental overflow applies to infusions, and their clear design vision was that melee attacks wouldn’t get elemental overflow bonuses. If this item were allowed, any melee kineticist who didn’t take it would be hamstringing themselves, and that’s usually the mark of an overpowered item.
🗡️ Weapons
Weapon Enchantments


Training (+1): Training weapons grant one free combat feat, chosen at the time of item creation. If you’re a legendary kineticist or own a conduit glove and can gather power with one hand, there’s really no reason not to have something like a +1 training dagger, as you’re essentially paying a few thousand gold for a free feat. Feats granted by this enchantment can’t be used as prerequisites for anything else, so you’ll have to choose something innocuous, but it’s still a nice perk.
🛡️ Armor
Armor Enchantments


Slick / Improved Slick / Greater Slick (3,750 / 15,000 / 33,750 gp): One of the biggest things that kineticists have to watch out for is getting grappled. You won’t have the CMB to escape grapples naturally, but your high Dexterity scores, light armor, and general slipperiness should let you use Escape Artist to great effect. Slick armor just gives you big, passive bonuses to your Escape Artist checks. Simple and effective.


Shadow / Improved Shadow / Greater Shadow (3,750 / 15,000 / 33,750 gp): Kineticists can also get pretty sneaky if they want to! Shadow armor is to Stealth what slick armor is to Escape Artist. I think most kineticist elements that are good at scouting don’t particularly need phenomenal Stealth scores, but the +5 version is cheap and effective.


Restful (4,500 gp): Useful specifically for kineticists who are going down the road to crafting, restful armor will let you get some work done on wondrous items without causing you to be fatigued or exhausted the next day.


Energizing (10,000 gp): Energizing armor’s only purpose is to defend you against other kineticists, and I can’t think of many APs or GMs that feature enemy kineticists.


Burn Resistance (10,000 gp): Just as the Burn Resistance feat needs to be vetoed because of how powerful it is, so too does the burn resistance enchantment need to be vetoed. Between the two of them, you could essentially take 3 points of burn without any consequences, and that would break the game balance.
🍷 Mundane & Consumable


📑 Sample Builds
KIN 801: Sample Builds (Paizo)


        Since there’s such a lot of crossover (cross-contamination, some GMs would say) between third-party content and Paizo-official content in this guide, I’m going to be keeping the third- and first-party builds separate in this Sample Builds section. For Paizo-official builds, here are the assumptions I’m making: all builds assume the base, Paizo-official kineticist chassis and only Paizo-official elements, wild talents, feats, and items; likewise, all builds will treat your kineticist level as the standard -4 for the purposes of what wild talents you can purchase with Extra Wild Talent.




Build Name
	Role
	The Aether Gorblin
	The most wretched, annoying, difficult-to-pin-down puke you’ve ever had the misfortune of playing with. She’s invisible, she’s small, she’s crazy-good at Stealth, she’s got Roll With It, Force Ward, and Flesh of Stone to negate damage, and she’s stealing your stuff right now.
	The Angelfire
	An all-fire kineticist that burns away all forms of resistance and immunity and even brings some decent magehunting capabilities to the party.
	The Grotesquomancer
	An all-void kineticist that uses Kinetic Invocation to gain command undead and animate dead, becoming a pocket necromancer in the process. Powerful, if more than a little straightforward.
	The Hurricane Queen
	An all-air kineticist that flies and blasts enemies from a distance with bolts of lightning. Not the most inventive build, but with air’s good infusions and utility talents, quite serviceable.
	The Stonelord
	An all-earth geokineticist with the kinetic knight archetype who can really take a beating and is still plenty threatening in melee range.
	The Tidal Blaster
	An all-water hydrokineticist that’s pretty good at a lot of different combat roles. Not spectacular at any of them, but pretty good.
	



________________
The Aether Gorblin
CN Goblin Kineticist 15


Strength: 10 (-2)  Dexterity: 16 (+4)  Constitution: 16  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 8 (-2)
Elements: Aether → Earth → Air
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: HP 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (aether), gather power, kinetic blast (telekinetic)
2: Elemental defense (Force Ward)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: 
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, internal buffer +1
7: Expanded element (earth), kinetic blast (earth blast, aetheric boost) 
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (maximize)
10: 
11: Infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Infusion specialization 4
15: Expanded element (air), kinetic blast (air blast, sandstorm blast), elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Extended Range), Utility Talent (Basic Telekinesis), Point-Blank Shot
2: Utility Talent (Telekinetic Finesse)
3: Infusion (Telekinetic Boomerang), Precise Shot
4: Utility Talent (Telekinetic Haul)
5: Infusion (Kinetic Blade), Roll With It
6: Utility Talent (Telekinetic Invisibility)
7: Extra Wild Talent (Skilled Kineticist)
8: Utility Talent (Expanded Defense)
9: Infusion (Snake), Dirty Fighting
10: Utility Talent (Telekinetic Maneuvers)
11: Infusion (Wall), Improved Disarm or Steal
12: Utility Talent (Ride the Blast)
13: Infusion (Foe Throw), Greater Disarm or Steal
14: Utility Talent (Earth Glide)
15: Extra Wild Talent (Wings of Air)


Strategy


        The Aether Gorblin is designed to be the most frustrating, unkillable tank your GM has ever squared off against, though to be honest it’s difficult to build a really good aetherkineticist with only Paizo-official wild talents. At the core of the build are a few facts. First, goblins are Small size and have a +4 racial bonus to both Dexterity and Stealth, giving you a combined +10 to Stealth at 1st level and a big AC/initiative bonus. Second, goblins can take the Roll With It feat, which allows them to use this crazy-high Dexterity score to negate blows from enemies, flinging themselves away from melee range in the process. Third, the aether element gives you Telekinetic Invisibility at 6th level; while not foolproof, Telekinetic Invisibility guarantees that you can go invisible whenever you want, which makes you very difficult to even find, much less attack. Fourth, both aether’s defensive talent and earth’s defensive talent (you’ll expand into earth at 7th level and get Flesh of Stone at 8th) are no slouches, giving you a buffer of temporary HP and DR/adamantine. In sum: enemies can’t find you, because you’re invisible and have an awesome Stealth score. Even if they can find you, they’ll have a hard time hitting you, because you’ve got a 50% miss chance from invisibility and an excellent AC score from Small size and Dexterity bonuses. Even if they can hit you, they’ll have a hard time dealing significant damage, because you have temporary HP and DR/adamantine. Even if they can deal damage, they’ll have a hard time continuing to deal damage, because you can negate the damage of the first attack in a full-attack and knock yourself a bunch of feet with Roll With It, go invisible, and then start blasting again. Like I said, it’s the quirkiest ranged tank in Pathfinder.
        Let’s talk offense, then, shall we? Invisibility breaks in Pathfinder only when you take offensive actions, which in our case means attacking with a kinetic blast, rolling a combat maneuver check, or using a utility talent that directly affects an enemy. Know what doesn’t break it? Using Sleight of Hand to steal objects off an enemy’s person if they’re unaware of your presence (and hey, guess who’s mostly invisible?). How about moving large objects around as barricades or impediments to movement, dropping items on enemies heads, or altering the environment to your advantage with Telekinetic Haul? Totally legal, per the invisibility rules. Summoning a wall of roiling telekinetic energy with the Wall infusion? Yes, as long as you don’t target an enemy with it. You can attack with Telekinetic Blast, of course, but it will never deal a huge amount of damage and doesn’t have many appealing infusions to go with it. Aether works best when it’s working from the shadows anyway, so use this fact to your advantage and think about how to harm or impede enemies indirectly, rather than directly. It’s not like they’ll be able to kill you anyway. The last piece to come into focus (and this is completely optional) is the combat maneuvers portion of the build. I know, I know, I said no maneuvers. You can get Telekinetic Maneuvers to swap out your abysmal Strength modifier for your great Dexterity or Constitution modifier, though, and Improved/Greater Steal/Disarm can be fun tricks for wrenching weapons, spellbooks, amulets or necklaces, etc. away from enemies before they get a chance to react. Remember that if they’re unaware of your presence, you get to use Sleight of Hand to steal things, a task that should be significantly easier with Skilled Kineticist and items like the masterful gray gloves or prestidigitator’s cloak. We’ll return to this in our Aether Gorblin Evolved build to see how third-party materials bulk up this build concept.
________________


The Angelfire
NG Human Kineticist 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 8
Elements: Fire → Fire → Fire
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: Bonus wild talents 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (fire), gather power, kinetic blast (fire)
2: Elemental defense (Searing Flesh)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: 
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, internal buffer +1
7: Expanded element (fire), kinetic blast (blue flame)
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (maximize)
10: 
11: Infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Infusion specialization 4
15: Expanded element (fire), elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Burning Infusion), Utility Talent (Basic Pyrokinesis), Point-Blank Shot, Precise Shot
2: Utility Talent (Fire’s Fury)
3: Infusion (Fan of Flames), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
4: Utility Talent (Searing Flame)
5: Infusion (Extended Range), Elemental Focus (Fire)
6: Utility Talent (Firesight), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Torrent)
7: Expanded Element Bonus Wild Talent (Smoke Storm), Spell Penetration
8: Utility Talent (Flame Jet)
9: Infusion (Eruption), Dispel Focus
10: Utility Talent (Greater Flame Jet)
11: Infusion (Unraveling Infusion), Greater Dispel Focus
12: Utility Talent (Flame Shield), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Elemental Whispers)
13: Infusion (Snake), Greater Spell Penetration
14: Utility Talent (Purging Flame)
15: Greater Elemental Focus (Fire)


Strategy


        Yawn. Another boilerplate kineticist build. The Angelfire is designed to be a ranged combatant, pelting enemies with blasts of fire from afar and using Searing Flesh and Flame Shield as a deterrent against melee aggressors. Fire doesn’t have a huge bevy of useful first-party utility talents, so I just went with the cream of the crop: Fire’s Fury is obviously necessary to maximize your damage output, and Searing Flame + Burning Infusion is the necessary one-two punch to start eroding fire resistances on the regular. Smoke Storm + Firesight is a nice battlefield control combo, although you will have to beware that your team won’t be able to see through your smoke unless you specifically equip them to be able to do so. Your AoE damage will obviously excel with Fan of Flames and Eruption, and you’ll pick up Snake in later levels to help you deal with cover. Flame Jet + Greater Flame Jet is a nice mobility combo, both for in-combat and out-of-combat purposes. Finally, I tacked on a bit of magehunting ability with Dispel Focus + Greater Dispel Focus + Unraveling Infusion. It’s nice to be able to perform targeted dispels more or less at will, and the two Dispel Focus feats ensure that you’re at least halfway decent at doing so. Fire spells are very common, too, so you might even be able to counterspell certain builds effectively, although you’re more likely to waste readied standard actions trying to anticipate when an enemy caster is going to cast a fire spell.

Elements: Void → Void → Void
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: HP 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (void), gather power, kinetic blast (gravity)
2: Elemental defense (Emptiness)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: 
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, internal buffer +1
7: Expanded element (void), kinetic blast (negative, void)
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (maximize)
10: 
11: Infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Infusion specialization 4
15: Expanded element (void), elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Extended Range), Utility Talent (Basic Chaokinesis → Retrain: Void Healer), Point-Blank Shot, Precise Shot
2: Utility Talent (Elemental Whispers)
3: Infusion (Kinetic Blade), Kinetic Invocation
4: Utility Talent (Command undead)
5: Infusion (Weighing Infusion), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
6: Utility Talent (Animate dead)
7: Infusion (Snake), Outflank
8: Utility Talent (Eyes of the Void)
9: Infusion (Singularity), Charnel Soldiers
10: Utility Talent (Eyes of the Void, Greater)
11: Infusion (Wall), Spell Penetration
12: Utility Talent (Suffocate)
13: Infusion (Flurry of Blasts), Greater Spell Penetration
14: Utility Talent (Death ward)
15: Infusion (Darkness Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Absentia)


Strategy


        Listen, I’m going to be real with you: this is a boring build. Void barely has anything to do in Pathfinder, at least if you’re sticking with Paizo-official content. So...the Grotesquomancer is built to be a pocket necromancer, using the Kinetic Invocation feat to get access to command undead and animate dead. Going to throw up a link to Brewer’s Guide to Undeath again, because you’ll need to read it if you want to become a decent necromancer. Basically, summon up the biggest, baddest undead you can and then just let them go ape with Void Healer, Outflank, and Charnel Soldiers. If you feel like jumping in with blasts, you’re more than capable, with all the usual suspects for a ranged build (Point-Blank Shot, Precise Shot, Extended Range, Snake, etc.). Weighing Infusion, Singularity, and Ability Focus will make you at least halfway decent at debuffing later in your career, and Suffocate makes for a good magehunting tool. Absentia and mind blank (which I would have taken at 16th level, if I extended these builds that far) attempt to hide the fact that you’re a necromancer, although I didn’t make space for it earlier in the build. If you’re looking for my opinion, though, void does best when it has access to third-party wild talents, so skip over to the Grotesquomancer Evolved build if that’s interesting to you.

The Hurricane Queen
CG Hobgoblin Kineticist 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16 (+2)  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 8
Elements: Air → Air → Air
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: HP 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (air), gather power, kinetic blast (electric)
2: Elemental defense (Enveloping Winds)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: 
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, internal buffer +1
7: Expanded element (air), kinetic blast (air, thunderstorm)
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (maximize)
10: 
11: Infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Infusion specialization 4
15: Expanded element (air), elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Extended Range), Utility Talent (Basic Aerokinesis), Point-Blank Shot
2: Utility Talent (Air’s Reach)
3: Infusion (Thundering Infusion), Precise Shot
4: Utility Talent (Air Cushion)
5: Infusion (Penetrating Infusion), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
6: Utility Talent (Wings of Air)
7: Infusion (Magnetic Infusion)
8: Utility Talent (Celerity)
9: Infusion (Snake), Mobile Gathering
10: Utility Talent (Aerial Evasion)
11: Infusion (Chain), Extra Wild Talent (Windsight)
12: Utility Talent (Greater Windsight)
13: Infusion (Torrent), Spell Penetration
14: Utility Talent (Wind Manipulator)
15: Greater Spell Penetration


Strategy


If there’s one thing that writing all these Paizo-official kineticist builds makes me realize, it’s that Paizo-official kineticist builds are actually pretty dull. There’s no particular finesse or nuance to feat selection or wild talent selection—you just take whatever’s best and make it work. The Hurricane Queen is meant to be what it sounds like: a flying savant of air and ranged combat. The build relies primarily on Electric Blast, since that blast generally has better infusions available for air than Air Blast does. You’ll use Air’s Reach, Wings of Air, and Aerial Evasion to stay as far back (and as well protected) as possible, tweaking your position with Mobile Gathering and hitting enemies with Penetrating Infusion (when you need to punch through electricity resistance) or Magnetic Infusion (when you’re more interesting in buffing or debuffing). Windsight should let you ignore some common forms of battlefield control, and Chain + Torrent gives you good AoE capabilities in later levels. Like I said: nothing too fancy in Paizo-official builds.

The Stonelord
LG Dwarf Kineticist (Kinetic Knight) 15


Strength: 16  Dexterity: 12  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 10 (+2)  Charisma: 8 (-2)
Elements: Earth → Earth → Earth
Racial Traits: Fey Thoughts, Iron Citizen
Favored Class Bonuses: HP 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (earth), gather power, kinetic blast (earth), courtly, elemental blade, kinetic warrior
2: Elemental bastion
3: Elemental overflow +1, knight’s resolve
4: Elemental defense (Flesh of Stone)
5: Infusion specialization 1
6: Elemental overflow +2, internal buffer +1
7: Expanded element (earth), kinetic blast (metal)
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3
10: 
11: Infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, greater resolve
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: 
14: Infusion specialization 4
15: Expanded element (earth), elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Pushing Infusion), Infusion (Kinetic Blade: Kinetic Knight Bonus), Utility Talent (Basic Geokinesis), Iron Will
2: Utility Talent (Elemental Whispers)
3: Infusion (Blade Rush), Steel Soul
4: Utility Talent (Earth Climb)
5: Infusion (Entangling Infusion), Infusion (Kinetic Whip: Kinetic Knight Bonus), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
6: Utility Talent (Tremorsense)
7: Utility Talent (Clockwork Heart: Expanded Element Bonus), Mobile Gathering
8: Utility Talent (Shift Earth)
9: Infusion (Magnetic Infusion), Infusion (Blade Whirlwind: Kinetic Knight Bonus), Combat Reflexes
10: Utility Talent (Kinetic Form)
11: Infusion (Rare-Metal Infusion), Infusion (Whip Hurricane: Kinetic Knight Bonus), Toughness
12: Utility Talent (Stone Sculptor)
13: Infusion (Bowling Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Enduring Earth)
14: Utility Talent (Greater Shift Earth)
15: Extra Wild Talent (Earth Glide)


Strategy


        As with all the first-party builds in this guide, there’s not a whole lot to the Stonelord: I wanted a tough-as-nails dwarven geokineticist who could plow through anything, and I got it. You’ll have heavy armor, a shield, huge amounts of DR/adamantine, Toughness, Iron Will, Steel Soul, Lightning Reflexes via Clockwork Heart, a familiar bonus that you can use to get more bonuses to your Will save, and the samurai’s resolve class feature. If you can’t make that work in melee, I don’t know what else to do for you. On the offensive side, all the usual melee form infusions granted by kinetic knight are on display, and you’ve got some decent substance infusions (mostly Entangling, Magnetic, and Rare-Metal Infusions) to take advantage of with them. Since you don’t have any metakinesis to spend your burn on, start using Metal Blast as many rounds as you can spare it, applying Magnetic Infusion to grant yourself massive bonuses to hit. You’ve also got Combat Reflexes, Kinetic Form, and Kinetic Whip, so with a little investment into Dexterity or a friendly caster giving you cat’s grace you should be able to take plenty of attacks of opportunity every round.

7: Expanded element (water), kinetic blast (cold, ice)

Strategy


Oh boy, another humdrum vanilla kineticist build! There’s very little to say about the Tidal Blaster: it’s a jack of all trades, with decent mobility (innate swim speed, Waterdancer, Ice Path), battlefield control (Water Manipulator, Slick, Slick Infusion), debuffing (Entangling Infusion), defense (Toughness, Shroud of Water, Shimmering Mirage), offense (Penetrating Infusion, Spray, Torrent), and even intrigue (Veil of Mists). The build obviously functions most effectively when you’re around water much of the time, so consider it for Ruins of Azlant or something similar.
📑 Sample Builds (3PP)
KIN 802: Sample Builds (3PP)


        For third-party builds, here are the assumptions I’m making: all builds assume the legendary kineticist class from Legendary Kineticists, and allow any wild talents, feats, and items from Kineticists of Porphyra I-IV and Legendary Kineticists I-II; no reductions in effective kineticist level will be applied for the purposes of what wild talents you can purchase with Extra Wild Talent or through your expanded element. Although this is not explicitly called out in any build, I would once again suggest that GMs allowing the legendary kineticist at their tables veto the changes to burn (too powerful) and scaling utility talent save DCs (again, too powerful). Everything else, in my opinion, is fine as-is.




Build Name
	Role
	The Alghollthu
	A mind kineticist who dominates other creatures and bends them forcibly to her will using Smooth Negotiator, Psychic Persuasion, Psychic Enslavement, and the Telepuppet utility talent line. All your enemy are belong to us.
	The Aether Gorblin Evolved
	An upgraded version of the Paizo-official Aether Gorblin build that uses a four-level filcher unchained rogue dip to turn your Sleight of Hand score into something more interesting.
	The Angelfire Evolved
	An upgraded version of the Paizo-official Angelfire build. Now featuring even more burning, plus invisibility!
	The Druid
	A wood kineticist who focuses on battlefield control using the Kinetic Cover line of utility talents, the Verdant Aura line of talents, and others to create impassable walls or areas of difficult terrain.
	The Grotesquomancer Evolved
	An upgraded version of the Paizo-official Grotesquomancer build that uses the corpse puppeteer archetype to field an unreasonable number of undead servitors.
	The Gutwarper
	A viscera + aether kineticist who focuses on the Visceral Whip utility talent line to whip people with her own intestines.
	The Hurricane Queen Evolved
	An upgraded version of the Paizo-official Hurricane Queen build that focuses on using Storm Cloud to blast lightning down out of impenetrable banks of fog. Very difficult to pin down, and very difficult to hit.
	The Mesmer
	A mind kineticist who uses the Hivemind and Mesmerizing Suggestion utility talent lines to keep their team firing on all cylinders while also locking down enemy mobs with brutal debuffs and soft control.
	The Minstrel
	A sound kineticist who uses the Inspiration Amp utility talent line and a few divine conduit archetype goodies to buff allies and make sure they’re ready to rock. A definite “paladin of Shelyn” flavor.
	The Phantom
	A light + sound kineticist who focuses on using extremely realistic illusions to distract and foil enemies, scout out hostile locations, and generally bring creative solutions to bear on problems.
	The Professor
	A mind + viscera kineticist who uses cherrypicked utility talents in order to become (surprisingly!) one of the premiere skill monkeys and Knowledge jockeys in the game.
	The Prototype
	An Alex Mercer homage build that uses viscera + earth to gain a ludicrous number of natural attacks and disgusting, bladed  appendages.
	The Spellsieve
	An aether kineticist who focuses on counterspelling, dispel magic, and other anticaster tactics. Also the resident wondrous item crafter of the builds section.
	The Stonelord Evolved
	An upgraded version of the Paizo-official Stonelord build. More defense, more tanking abilities, more battlefield control—even flight!
	The Tidal Blaster Evolved
	An upgraded version of the Paizo-official Tidal Blaster build. It’s still a jack of all trades, but hits harder, moves faster, and utilizes burn more efficiently than the Paizo-official build.
	The Toxicant
	A poison kineticist who focuses on, well, poisons and constantly ticking ability damage. Why hit HP when you can hit that fighter’s low Charisma score?
	The Wendigo
	A water + void kineticist who focuses on battlefield control, auras, and intense damage over time, whether from bleed effects or the deep chill of winter itself.
	The Witchblade
	An aether build that pairs the telekinetic bladeshifter archetype with fighter feats and utility talents to become a top-tier magehunter.
	





________________
The Alghollthu
NG Human Legendary Kineticist 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 8
Elements: Mind → Mind → Mind
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: HP 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (mind), gather power, kinetic blast (mental)
2: Elemental defense (Numbness)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, expanded element (mind), kinetic blast (encephalon)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Infusion specialization 4, expanded element (mind)
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Reifying Infusion), Utility Talent (Basic Neurokinesis), Point-Blank Shot, Precise Shot
2: Utility Talent (Predicted Maneuvers)
3: Infusion (Empty Infusion), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
4: Utility Talent (Psychic Persuasion)
5: Infusion (Extended Range), Extra Wild Talent (Smooth Negotiator)
6: Utility Talent (Additional Allies), Expanded Element Bonus Wild Talent (Piercing Thoughts)
7: Infusion (Scrambling Infusion), Spell Penetration
8: Utility Talent (Enduring Control)
9: Infusion (Psychic Connection), Extra Wild Talent (Improved Smooth Negotiator)
10: Utility Talent (Passive Telepuppet)
11: Infusion (Lock Up Infusion), Greater Spell Penetration
12: Utility Talent (Psychic Enslavement)
13: Infusion (Disabling Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Active Telepuppet)
14: Utility Talent (Psychic Network)
15: Infusion (Unraveling Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Psychic Hotline)


Strategy


        The Alghollthu build was my attempt to create an enchantment-specialized wizard or sorcerer on a kineticist chassis: someone who can charm, dominate, and otherwise mind-control their way to absolute dominion over a horde of loyal (if drooling) thralls. What this build needs more than anything else is a means of increasing the save DCs of its utility talents. The legendary kineticist does this by default, but I recommended that you veto that class feature; the third-party Adaptive Utility also does this, but I also recommend that you veto that feat. Damn my integrity! But seriously, talk with your GM about whether you can buy your way back into one of these abilities by promising to take an additional point of burn on top of whatever you would normally take—that’s reasonably balanced, and much needed for Psychic Persuasion and Smooth Negotiator, which are the backbone of this build.
        This build needs to do about four things: (1) blast reasonably well, (2) have a few methods of acquiring mind-controlled minions, (3) increase save DCs to improve your chances of failed saves, and (4) have a few methods of maintaining said stable of mind-controlled minions. Starting with the first item, we have all the basics for a ranged combat build: PBS + Precise Shot, Spell Penetration + Greater Spell Penetration, Extended Range, blah blah blah. No AoE form infusions to speak of, but sometimes you have to let things go in a build. The second item is handled almost exclusively by three utility talent lines: Psychic Persuasion + Psychic Enslavement, Smooth Negotiator + Improved Smooth Negotiator, and Passive Telepuppet + Active Telepuppet. Combined, these utility talents will allow you to charm, dominate, inveigle, spy on, and outright possess any creature susceptible to mind-affecting effects (and quite a few who aren’t susceptible). To accomplish this, we turn to our third item, increasing save DCs. Absent the legendary kineticist class feature or feat mentioned above, you’ll have to make do with more primitive means of increasing save DCs—Predicted Maneuvers will do it on the cheap, provided you can actually predict an enemy’s actions, and Empty Infusion, Reifying Infusion, and Piercing Thoughts will ensure that you can break through mind-affecting immunities at least a little. Finally, we’ve got Additional Allies, Enduring Control, Psychic Connection + persistent metakinesis, Psychic Network, and Psychic Hotline. While none of these do anything huge on their own, when taken together they can help an enterprising neurokineticist to maintain up to four charmed or dominated allies, all of which can be managed in parallel. Think of these wild talents as administrative assistants: things just function better when they’re doing their job well.
        Outside of combat, you can mostly lean on your utility talents to charm people or monsters. If your GM will allow you to boost your save DCs, so much the better—like I said, this build more than any other in this guide relies on good utility talent save DCs. If not, well, you can always just keep hitting someone with an effect until they succumb! Spell-like abilities don’t have somatic or verbal components, so no one is really going to know that you’re doing anything, and you’ll always know the instant someone fails a save, thanks to Basic Neurokinesis. In combat, your go-to move should be to use Predicted Maneuvers plus a utility talent of your choice (or Empty Infusion + Psychic Connection) to start getting enemies on your side. Once you’ve secured a few “allies” (or if you had a few going into the combat) you can instead use Empty Infusion to start disabling single targets with persistent metakinesis and Scrambling Infusion, Lock Up Infusion, Disabling Infusion, etc. This build could theoretically deal some damage, but between the loss of maximize metakinesis on the legendary kineticist chassis and the lack of AoE form infusions, you’ll probably be a greater help to your team if you focus on debuffing and direct enemy control.
________________


The Aether Gorblin Evolved
CN Goblin Legendary Kineticist 6 / Filcher UnRogue 4 / Legendary Kineticist 5


Strength: 10 (-2)  Dexterity: 16 (+4)  Constitution: 16  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 8 (-2)
Elements: Aether → Earth
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: HP 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (aether), gather power, kinetic blast (telekinetic)
2: Elemental defense (Force Ward)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, expanded element (earth), kinetic blast (earth, aetheric boost)
7(1): Finesse training, sneak attack +1d6, trapfinding
8(2): Quicker than the eye
9(3): Finesse training (kinetic blast), sneak attack +2d6, rummage +1
10(4): Debilitating injury, filch
11(7): Internal buffer 1
12(8): Infusion specialization 2
13(9): Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
14(10): 
15(11): Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Extended Range), Utility Talent (Basic Telekinesis), Point-Blank Shot
2: Utility Talent (Telekinetic Finesse)
3: Infusion (Bloody Infusion), Precise Shot
4: Utility Talent (Telekinetic Haul)
5: Infusion (Kinetic Blade), Roll With It
6: Utility Talent (Telekinetic Invisibility)
7(1): Kinetic Training, Weapon Finesse (UnRogue Bonus)
8(2): Rogue Talent (Dampen Presence)
9(3): Extra Wild Talent (Telekinetic Maneuvers)
10(4): Rogue Talent (Superior Sniper), Improved Steal (Filcher Bonus)
11(7): Infusion (Ricochet), Extra Wild Talent (Aetheric Sniper)
12(8): Utility Talent (Expanded Defense)
13(9): Infusion (Hyper-Dimensional Blast), Talented Blaster
14(10): Utility Talent (Skilled Kineticist)
15(11): Infusion (Wall), Accomplished Sneak Attacker


Strategy


        Let’s check in on our aether gorblin, shall we? If you’ll recall our previous build, we focused on two main areas: defense (through Telekinetic Invisibility, Force Ward, Flesh of Stone, Roll With It, and the goblin’s naturally high Dexterity and Stealth scores) and combat maneuvers (through Telekinetic Maneuvers, Improved Steal, etc.). There were a few persistent, nagging problems, though. First, we could go invisible at will, but didn’t really have anything to do with that—no sneak attack damage, mainly. Second, we wanted to be able to snipe with our at-will invisibility, but had no tools for sniping, as sniping immediately broke our invisibility and left us vulnerable. Third, we had an incredible Sleight of Hand score for taking items off of unsuspecting foes, but that trick only worked if the foe was unaware of our presence—if they became aware, we had to switch to steal, and even with Improved Steal, Greater Steal, Telekinetic Maneuvers, etc., we just weren’t keeping up with the Joneses.
        We manage to kill a bunch of birds with one stone in this build by taking four levels of Filcher unchained rogue, a dip made possible through the Kinetic Training and Talented Blaster feats, which together allow us to treat our effective kineticist level as four higher for the purposes of our kinetic blast damage and utility talent selection. What does Filcher get us? Let’s start small with free Weapon Finesse—minor, true, but kinetic blasts count as light melee weapons when used with Kinetic Blade, and we want at least some options when we get up close and personal. We get trapfinding, which partners extremely well with Telekinetic Finesse to allow us to disable magical traps at range, plus a modest pool of sneak attack dice and debilitating injury for a little debuffing. Our two talents will go toward abilities that make us better at Stealth (Dampen Presence) and sniping (Superior Sniper); those will come into play later. The biggest gain, however, is the filch class feature, which allows us to use our Sleight of Hand skill bonus in place of CMB when performing steal maneuvers. This is huge, so let’s dive into it.
        It’s well-accepted in the Pathfinder meta that skills are easier to boost than attack rolls, and for good reason. Because of this, this build racks up big bonuses to Sleight of Hand checks from the masterful gray gloves (+10, untyped, to take items from creatures unnoticed), the prestidigitator’s cloak (+8, competence, to all SoH checks), silent blade vest (+5, competence, to all SoH checks), gloves of larceny (+5, competence, to all SoH checks), a +1 training dagger with the Greater Steal feat (remember that legendary kineticists can gather power with only one hand free!), Improved Steal (+2, untyped), and Skilled Kineticist (+½ kineticist level, untyped). By 15th level, you should be sporting about a +55 to Sleight of Hand (+10 masterful gray gloves, +8 prestidigitator’s cloak, +7 Skilled Kineticist, +2 Improved Steal, +2 Greater Steal, +3 class skill, +15 skill ranks, +8 Dexterity), and that’s enough to steal something from a CR 20 creature (median CMD 54) even on a natural 1. Heck, you can even throw in Skill Focus later, if you’re feeling like you want to flex even more. You should have no trouble stealing even items fastened to enemies with this build, so have fun stealing cloaks of resistance, amulets of natural armor, sheathed weapons, spell component pouches, spellbooks, holy symbols, and all the rest. Oh, and since Telekinetic Maneuvers functions as telekinesis, you can do all of this from long range—400 feet + 40 feet/kineticist level. Go nuts.
        This build leans even more heavily than its predecessor into the sniping rules, too, on the assumption that you want to deal sneak attack and remain invisible while doing so. The Superior Sniper rogue talent and Aetheric Sniper utility talent are together going to reduce our Stealth penalty from sniping to 0, as well as extending the duration of Telekinetic Invisibility by one round; Dampen Presence will ensure that no creature can spot us, even with blindsight or tremorsense, and shadow armor (or improved/greater shadow armor) in conjunction with our natural Stealth bonuses should keep our checks insanely high. With this combo in place, you can move and snipe an enemy without breaking invisibility (Round 1), move with Stealth while refreshing Telekinetic Invisibility (Round 2), and then be ready to rinse and repeat starting in Round 3. You’ll likely want sniper goggles so that you can make sneak attacks at any range. Even if you are ever caught without invisibility, Hyper-Dimensional Blast denies enemies their Dexterity bonuses to AC, so you can continue dealing sneak attack damage even while visible. All this, and you can do everything that the base Aether Gorblin was capable of. See why I said third-party stuff does good things for this class?
________________


The Angelfire Evolved
NG Human Legendary Kineticist (Fusion Kineticist) 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 8
Elements: Fire (Main) + Light (Sub)
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: Bonus wild talents 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, unbalanced elements (fire/light), gather power, kinetic blast (fire, light)
2: Defined defense (Illusory Duplicates)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, elemental balance (fire/light), defined defense (Searing Flesh), kinetic blast (solar)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, cross infusion
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Elemental harmony (fire/light), infusion specialization 4
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Point-Blank Shot, Precise Shot
2: Utility Talent (Fire’s Fury)
3: Infusion (Burning Infusion), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
4: Utility Talent (Searing Flame)
5: Infusion (Kinetic Bomb), Precision Blasting
6: Utility Talent (Firesight), Archetype Bonus Wild Talent (Extended Range), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Smoke Storm)
7: Infusion (Improved Burning Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Improved Kinetic Bomb)
8: Utility Talent (Optical Camouflage)
9: Infusion (Snake), Spell Penetration
10: Utility Talent (Fire’s Explosive Fury)
11: Infusion (Unraveling Infusion), Dispel Focus
12: Utility Talent (Light Speed Travel), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Flame Shield)
13: Infusion (Melting Infusion), Greater Spell Penetration
14: Utility Talent (Greater Optical Camouflage), Archetype Bonus Wild Talent (Long Distance Travel)
15: Infusion (Beacon Infusion), Greater Dispel Focus


Strategy


        As usual, the Angelfire Evolved does everything that the first-party build could do, but better, partly because of the changes that the fusion kineticist and legendary kineticist archetype/class make to the build, partly because of some of the great third-party additions to fire’s infusion and utility talent complement, and partly because light is such an awesome element to dip. Fusion kineticist, for a refresher, lets you focus on two elements, a primary element and a secondary element. You get the simple blasts of both elements, the defense talent of your secondary element (Illusory Duplicates, in our case, which is a better defense talent at low levels), but can only select wild talents in your secondary element at reduced levels until much later in the game. Honestly, though, this isn’t so bad, as we’re only in the light element for a few selected utility talents and the versatility of having both a physical and energy blast at low levels.
        Offensively, the build is better than ever. Searing Flame + Fire’s Fury + Burning Infusion is still boss, except now we also have Fire’s Explosive Fury and Improved Burning Infusion to really double down on the damage. Improved Burning Infusion in particular is crucial for melting away enemies’ fire resistance. Kinetic Bomb and Improved Kinetic Bomb are generally superior to a lot of other AoE blasts, and their one weakness (i.e., that you need to actually hit the main enemy to proc the AoE) is almost entirely negated by the fact that you’re using an energy blast. Plus, you’ve got Precision Blasting, so you can now exclude your allies from those blasts! Win, win, win. The usual control elements (Smoke Storm + Firesight) and anticaster elements (Unraveling Infusion + Dispel Focus + Greater Dispel Focus) are also there, alive and unspoiled.
        So let’s finally talk about what we’re getting from the light element. Primarily? Invisibility and teleportation. There’s nothing super stealthy about the fire element, and mobility has always been problematic, too. No longer! It takes a while to get there, but you can eventually give yourself greater invisibility while doling out your buckets of flame, dimensional hop across the battlefield as a move action, and even teleport for a modest burn cost. Enemies won’t be able to find you, and even if they can, they’ll have to break through your mirror images, and even if they do, Flame Shield + Searing Flesh will ensure that they pay a cost for every hit. Meanwhile, you can target flat-footed touch AC (or just flat-footed AC if you’re using Light Blast), apply all your fire infusions to Light Blast and vice versa, strip away all magical buffs, create battlefield control at will, and proc some nasty persistent damage.
