The Druid
N Human Legendary Kineticist 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 8
Elements: Wood → Wood → Wood
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: Bonus wild talents 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (wood), gather power, kinetic blast (wood)
2: Elemental defense (Flesh of Wood)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, expanded element (wood), kinetic blast (positive, verdant, great oak)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Expanded element (wood), infusion specialization 4
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Extended Range), Utility Talent (Basic Phytokinesis → Retrain: Woodland Step), Point-Blank Shot, Precise Shot
2: Utility Talent (Kinetic Cover)
3: Infusion (Kinetic Bomb), Precision Blast
4: Utility Talent (Verdant Aura)
5: Infusion (Entangling Infusion), Kinetic Remnants
6: Utility Talent (Improved Kinetic Cover), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Warp Wood), Expanded Element Bonus Wild Talent (Mobile Blast)
7: Infusion (Improved Kinetic Bomb), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
8: Utility Talent (Verdant Overgrowth)
9: Infusion (Snake), Weapon Focus (Kinetic Blast)
10: Utility Talent (Thorn Wall)
11: Infusion (Improved Mobile Blast), Extra Wild Talent (Briarthorn Overgrowth)
12: Utility Talent (Grasping Overgrowth), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Continuous Regrowth)
13: Infusion (Bloody Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Greater Woodland Step)
14: Utility Talent (Greater Kinetic Cover)
15: Infusion (Improved Bloody Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Rapid Regrowth)


Strategy


I felt that I owed you at least one wood build, so you’re getting it: one and only one wood build. There’s no point (ever, really) to building a first-party phytokineticist, and even with third-party talents, you’re mostly doing things that earth, water, or void could do as well or better, and with less hassle. Still, I suppose it’s worth showcasing what the element can do, no? The big draw of the Druid build is that it’s one of the premiere battlefield controllers that I have on the roster, right up there with the Wendigo. Difficult terrain, walls, vision obstruction, ticking environmental damage, you name it—the Druid is here to make enemies’ lives hell with it.
Let’s start simple with your aura. Verdant Aura, which you’ll pick up at 4th level, is going to grant you a radius in which flowers and grass grow supernaturally well. Although this is okay on its own, especially if you have someone who can benefit from that terrain (tell your ranger friends to bring favored terrain [forest] with them), the main use is opening you up to later iterations. Verdant Overgrowth at 8th makes everything within your radius difficult terrain, grants you concealment from outside attacks, and lets you dole out the entangled condition to enemies who fail their saves. At 11th and 12th levels, we add Briarthorn Overgrowth and Grasping Overgrowth, each of which adds more damage and more debuffing. Allies can hide quite effectively in your aura, too, thanks to Briarthorn Overgrowth’s greensight clause and Greater Woodland Step allowing allies to be unimpeded by forested terrain. It’s a great way to hide squishy casters from unfriendly eyes. Then, of course, you also have the walls: Kinetic Cover + Improved + Greater is a classic for kineticists, perfect for walling off narrow corridors, preventing enemies from taking attacks of opportunity because of cover, and partitioning the battlefield. Thorn Wall is, as always, bonkers.
Outside of the battlefield control elements, the build is quite simple. Normal ranged combat feats, of course. I went with Kinetic Bomb + Improved since wood doesn’t have any form infusions, much less AoE form infusions. Adding Kinetic Remnants + Precision Blast is a great way to deal AoE damage, create difficult terrain and caltrops, and control the battlefield. Improved Mobile Blast will eventually allow you to create a more-or-less autonomous Tumbleweed O’ Damage,™ which aids wood’s generally low offensive power. Finally, defense can mostly be handled at later levels by Continuous Regrowth and Rapid Regrowth—if any stray hits get through, you can just lay low for a few rounds and wait for your fast healing or regeneration to bring you back up.
________________
The Grotesquomancer Evolved
NE Human Legendary Kineticist (Corpse Puppeteer) 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 8
Elements: Void → Viscera
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: Bonus wild talents 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (void), gather power, kinetic blast (negative), corpse puppet
2: Elemental defense (Emptiness)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1, fleshcrafting
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, advanced puppetry
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
10: Expanded element (viscera), improved fleshcrafting, kinetic blast (bone)
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Expanded element (void), infusion specialization 4, kinetic blast (gravity, void)
15: Elemental overflow +5, fleshcrafting master


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Utility Talent (Basic Chaokinesis → Retrain: Atrophy Field), Point-Blank Shot, Precise Shot
2: Utility Talent (Void Healer)
3: Infusion (Pure Negative Infusion), Kinetic Invocation
4: 
5: Extra Wild Talent (Command undead)
6: Utility Talent (Animate dead), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Unnatural Evolution)
7: Infusion (Kinetic Bomb), Outflank
8: Utility Talent (Death ward)
9: Charnel Soldiers
10: Utility Talent (Improved Unnatural Evolution)
11: Infusion (Reconstruction Infusion), Spell Penetration
12: Utility Talent (Aura of Ash), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Undead Evolution)
13: Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast
14: Utility Talent (Oppressive Atmosphere)
15: Infusion (Improved Kinetic Bomb), Greater Spell Penetration


Strategy


        Ahhh, now that’s a little more grotesque, isn’t it? Much of the original Grotesquomancer chassis is left untouched, including Kinetic Invocation, command undead, animate dead, Void Healer, and the bare bones of a ranged build. We’ve doubled down on our minion creation capacities, though, and the extra effort pays off: the corpse puppeteer archetype is wickedly powerful, allowing us to grant feats and free eidolon evolutions to one of our undead servitors, not to mention being able to use utility talents through the puppet at will. We can eventually grant eidolon evolutions to all our animated dead (not just the corpse puppet) with Unnatural Evolution, Improved Unnatural Evolution, and Undead Evolution, and raising the dead becomes easier than ever with Aura of Ash. With the addition of Pure Negative Infusion, we have to worry less about selecting Negative Blast as our 1st level blast, so we do, eventually paving the way for functionally limitless (albeit slow) healing of our undead with Reconstruction Infusion. The last element of the build that I worked in was Kinetic Bomb and Improved Kinetic Bomb, which you can feel free to swap out as you like—I just enjoyed the idea of a necromancer blowing enemies up with negative energy, and not particularly caring whether they hit their undead minions or not. It’s not as though negative energy hurts them, right? Anyway, you’ll rarely (if ever) need to spend burn on blasts with this build—use burn to raise more undead whenever you need them, send your minions into melee to proc Outflank with each other, and then plug away with Void Healer, Reconstruction Infusion, or Kinetic Bomb + Negative Blast as needed to either keep your minions alive or turn your enemies dead (briefly, before making them undead).
________________


The Gutwarper
LE Hobgoblin Legendary Kineticist 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16 (+2)  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 8
Elements: Aether → Viscera → Aether
Racial Traits: Pit Boss
Favored Class Bonuses: HP 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, telekinetic focus (aether), gather power, kinetic blast (telekinetic)
2: Elemental defense (Force Ward)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, expanded element (viscera), kinetic blast (bone, aetheric boost)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Expanded element (aether), infusion specialization 4, kinetic blast (force)
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Kinetic Blade), Utility Talent (Basic Telekinesis), Weapon Finesse
2: Utility Talent (Aetheric Tempering)
3: Infusion (Extended Range), Combat Reflexes
4: Utility Talent (Telekinetic Haul)
5: Infusion (Ricochet), Extra Wild Talent (Distorted Area)
6: Utility Talent (Telekinetic Invisibility)
7: Infusion (Kinetic Whip), Extra Wild Talent (Petrified Innards)
8: Utility Talent (Unnatural Evolution)
9: Infusion (Hyper-Dimensional Blast), Extra Wild Talent (Visceral Whip)
10: Utility Talent (Improved Visceral Whip)
11: Infusion (Dismissing Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Improved Unnatural Evolution)
12: Utility Talent (Expanded Defense)
13: Infusion (Self-Destructive Infusion), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
14: Utility Talent (Greater Visceral Whip)
15: Infusion (Crippling Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Greater Unnatural Evolution)


Strategy


        I wanted to make a Visceral Whip build as part of this guide, just because it’s such a cool idea, and I kind of regret it in hindsight. Visceral Whip is a really tough playstyle to make work, largely because you don’t have the necessary combat tools for the playstyle until at least 10th level (when you can get Improved Visceral Whip) but also because the prereqs (both aether and viscera as your elements, Aetheric Tempering, Petrified Innards, Expanded Defense, etc.) are pretty hard to fill out. But let’s see what we ended up with, right? Since you need something to do until 10th level and I didn’t feel particularly like suggesting that you retrain all your feats, I started the build off with Kinetic Blade + Weapon Finesse and, later, Kinetic Whip + Combat Reflexes in order to prepare you for the playstyle you’ll use eventually. Distorted Area, Petrified Innards, Force Ward, and Reactive Skin will make you a little tougher in melee, but an aetherkineticist is never going to be the hardiest force on the battlefield. Get used to holding enemies at bay with Distorted Area and blasting them with close-range Telekinetic Blasts as needed. Once you do finally pick up Improved Visceral Whip, you’re ready to get down to business: just use your whip to take as many attacks of opportunity as Combat Reflexes will allow, and enjoy full BAB once you reach 14th level. You may not do a huge amount of damage, but your combat maneuvers will likely be pretty good, and you’ll threaten a huge area. I tossed in some late-game infusions (Self-Destructive, Crippling, Dismissing) so that you can take advantage of the substance infusion discounts conferred by the Visceral Whip wild talent line.
        Beyond that, I tried to stick to the basics of a good aether-/corpokineticist build: Telekinetic Haul, Telekinetic Invisibility, the three Unnatural Evolution wild talents. Pick whatever evolutions you want with those, by the way—more natural attacks can always be fun, of course, but don’t disregard Skilled, Wings, etc. if you’re looking for more utility. Finally, the build isn’t terrible at range, at least when the enemy isn’t in melee with allies. Hyperdimensional Blast will help a lot in ignoring cover bonuses to AC and targeting flat-footed AC (not that that usually gets you much in the endgame), so use it liberally whenever you’re worried about hitting foes.
________________


The Hurricane Queen Evolved
CG Hobgoblin Legendary Kineticist (Elemental Scion) 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16 (+2)  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 8
Elements: Air → Air → Air
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: HP 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (air), elemental heart, gather power, kinetic blast (d8 electric)
2: Elemental defense (Enveloping Winds)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, focused element (air), kinetic blast (thunderstorm)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Elemental master (air), infusion specialization 4
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Utility Talent (Basic Aerokinesis → Retrain: Elemental Acceleration), Point-Blank Shot
2: Utility Talent (Air’s Reach)
3: Infusion (Extended Range), Precise Shot
4: Utility Talent (Cloud Cover)
5: Infusion (Penetrating Infusion), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
6: Utility Talent (Wings of Air), Focused Element Utility Talent (Windsight)
7: Infusion (Magnetic Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Storm Cloud)
8: Utility Talent (Fade Into the Mist)
9: Infusion (Chain), Extra Wild Talent (Celerity)
10: Utility Talent (Fog Step)
11: Infusion (Snake), Spell Penetration
12: Utility Talent (Complete Circuit)
13: Infusion (Cloud), Mobile Gathering
14: Utility Talent (Aerial Evasion)
15: Infusion (Wall), Elemental Master Utility Talent (Magnetic Chain), Greater Spell Penetration


Strategy


The Hurricane Queen Evolved build, as with so many of the first-party builds that I then go in to upgrade into a third-party build, just feels right with third-party talents. Like, who knew that the Cloud Cover line of utility talents was what the air element was missing, right? But once you know about it, you’re like, yeah, that’s totally cool! Anyway, this build is all about setting up a bunch of clouds as high above enemies as you reasonably can, flying into those clouds, and then raining down lightning to destroy enemies while they’re trying to find you. Let’s break it down.
To start with, this build tacks on the elemental scion archetype onto a legendary kineticist chassis, so fair warning that it’s pretty heavy on the third-party content to begin with. Elemental scion treats your effective kineticist level as 2 higher for the purposes of wild talent access once you get your first expanded element, which is why you’ll see wild talents like Chain or Cloud taken earlier than they normally could be. Speaking of Chain, you’ll be focusing on Electric Blast, Chain, and their various upgrades (Complete Circuit, Magnetic Chain, etc.) to deal most of your damage in this build, so get used to using that form infusion all the time as you enter the double digits. You’ve got the two necessary feats for a ranged build (Point-Blank Shot and Precise Shot), the two necessary feats for an energy blast build (Spell Penetration and Greater Spell Penetration), and Ability Focus for the few infusions that actually require saves in this build. Penetrating Infusion can and should be used whenever you run up against electricity resistance, but otherwise you should be using Magnetic Infusion regularly to give your party those massive, massive buffs to attack rolls with metal weapons.
The other half of this build is the Cloud Cover + Storm Cloud + Fog Step + Windsight line of utility talents. Taken together, these wild talents allow you to create fog clouds at will above the battlefield as long as you have 30 feet of headroom. You can then fly directly into the clouds with Wings of Air, using Windsight to see out of them while enemies will take a 50% miss chance and be unable to identify where you are with normal vision. This, as it turns out, is a far better defensive tactic than using Enveloping Winds, as the miss chances will apply to all attacks, even ranged touch attacks or massive weapons, and most enemies simply won’t be able to target you at all unless they have blindsight, blindsense, lifesense, etc. with extremely long range. This frees up burn for other priorities, although you’ll want to accept it pretty quickly as usual to get your elemental overflow bonuses rolling.
So you’re up in the sky in a fog cloud. What next? Well, this is where things get really fun. Storm Cloud lets you shoot your blasts out of any square currently occupied by your fog, and Fog Step lets you teleport between fog clouds as a move or swift action. Elemental Acceleration + Mobile Gathering will together allow you to move 35-40 feet while gathering power. You’ve essentially become very, very hard to pin down. Say a caster just wants to target your fog cloud with gust of wind or fireball. How do they know you’re in there? They can’t see you, because it’s a fog cloud, and they can’t be sure that you’re in that cloud, even if your blasts are coming from there. You could have teleported away already—for all they know, you’re 100 feet away! Even if a caster does manage to lob an AoE spell into the correct cloud (about the only strategy that will work, since they can’t see you to target you directly) you have Aerial Evasion to let you soak up all the damage with your incredibly high Reflex save. By the time this build ends at 15th level, you can have up to four clouds active, which will make it maddening to try and find you. And if things ever go sour, you can use Fade Into the Mist to escape—I’d normally ding gaseous form for being slow and weak, but since Fade Into the Mist mimics your base land speed (and Elemental Acceleration will have increased that base speed by 40 feet at 15th level) you can actually fly 60-70 feet in a single move action, passing through cracks and keyholes as easily as mist.
The build’s only weakness, ironically enough, is ceiling height: the text of Cloud Cover specifies that your clouds need a minimum of 30 feet to work, so you’ll need to be wary of dungeons, underground caverns, etc. where things might get cramped. You’re not exactly crippled in these circumstances, as all of your offensive, buffing, debuffing, and mobility options remain online, but losing your cloud cover will make you a lot more vulnerable to spell attacks, which are probably the biggest danger to any kineticist—and not among the effects warded against by Enveloping Winds.
________________


The Mesmer
CN Human Legendary Kineticist 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 8
Elements: Mind → Mind → Mind
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: Bonus wild talents 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (mind), gather power, kinetic blast (mental)
2: Elemental defense (Numbness)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, expanded element (mind), kinetic blast (encephalon)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Expanded element (mind), infusion specialization 4
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Extended Range), Utility Talent (Basic Neurokinesis), Auspicious Birth (Conjunction), Point-Blank Shot
2: Utility Talent (Expanded Telepathy)
3: Infusion (Doubting Infusion), Precise Shot
4: Utility Talent (Thought Shield)
5: Infusion (Empty Infusion), Healer’s Hands
6: Utility Talent (Hivemind), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Kinetic Healer), Expanded Element Bonus Wild Talent (Mesmerizing Suggestion)
7: Infusion (Scrambling Infusion), Incredible Healer
8: Utility Talent (Improved Hivemind)
9: Infusion (Empathetic Surge), Spell Penetration
10: Utility Talent (Improved Mesmerizing Suggestion)
11: Infusion (Lock Up Infusion), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
12: Utility Talent (Greater Hivemind), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Psychological Healing)
13: Infusion (Improved Empathetic Surge), Greater Spell Penetration
14: Utility Talent (Greater Mesmerizing Suggestion)
15: Infusion (Greater Empathetic Surge), Heal Skill Unlock


Strategy


        Hoo boy! There’s a lot going on here. Whereas the Professor build is almost completely focused on skills and the Alghollthu build is almost completely focused on enemy control and debuffing, the Mesmer build is almost completely focused on support, support, support. You’ve got the feats and infusions you need to be able to blast and debuff effectively, at least against enemies that aren’t completely immune to mind-affecting effects, but that’s not the primary aim of the build. Instead, there are three complementary systems of buffing going on: 1) the Hivemind utility talent series and teamwork feat shenanigans, 2) the Mesmerizing Suggestion utility talent series and mesmerist trick shenanigans, and 3) the Kinetic Healing and Psychological Healing utility talent series and healing shenanigans. We’ll cover each as we go.
        By far the most effective thing that this build can do is hand out teamwork feats like they’re candy. You won’t have these abilities until 8th level—early iterations of Hivemind and Expanded Telepathy just give your teammates the ability to communicate with one another telepathically—but once you hit 8th, you can take any of the teamwork feats that either you or any other ally know and grant them to all your other allies for the cost of 1 point of burn. If you’re partnering with hunters, cavaliers, inquisitors, or other classes that receive bonus teamwork feats, this can be shockingly, horrifyingly effective. What’s more, the Auspicious Birth (Conjunction) feat that you’ll darn well take at 1st level increases the numeric value of any teamwork feat that you or your allies benefit from by 1. Outflank now provides a +5 bonus to attack rolls for flanking; Shake It Off now grants a minimum of a +2 to bonus to all saving throws; Allied Spellcaster now grants a +3 bonus to checks to overcome spell resistance, or a +5 and a +2 to all level-dependent variables for the upgraded version. At 12th level, you’ll even get the opportunity to steal teamwork feats from enemies, making fights with teamwork-heavy classes a walk in the park as you steamroll them with their own powers. Teamwork feats are poo-pooed by a lot of Pathfinder players, but take it from a guy who wrote his first guide on the inquisitor class: properly handled, teamwork feats can be brutally powerful.
        As if all that wasn’t good enough, neurokineticists can also pick up essentially the entirety of the mesmerist’s implanted tricks class feature. Tricks are complex enough to merit their own guide—and I’ll get there when I write a guide for the mesmerist next—but for now, suffice it to say that they can allow you and your allies to do some truly amazing things with almost zero action cost in the moment. Compel Alacrity, Astounding Avoidance, Mask Misery, and Mesmeric Mirror are favorites of mine. Finally, the Mesmer build has its own magical and non-magical healing capabilities, as exemplified by the Kinetic Healer + Psychological Healing utility talents and the Healer’s Hands + Incredible Healer + Heal Skill Unlock feats. (Feel free to swap out the non-magical healing feats for teamwork feats if no one in your party has any—Improved Hivemind is wasted without teamwork feats.) Even when your blasting isn’t working, you can still count on being able to support the party through non-magical healing and condition removal.
        Speaking of blasting and debuffing, this build isn’t exactly a slouch at those roles. I took a leaf from the Toxicant’s playbook, picked up Empty Infusion, and gave you a bunch of good form and substance infusions. Rather than dealing damage, it will mostly be to your benefit to activate Empty Infusion, activate Empathetic Surge, and then toss out something like Scrambling Infusion or Lock Up Infusion. Between tossing enemies into disarray and ensuring that your team is a telepathically charged, fully healed, tricked-out teamwork machine, the Mesmer build should have no trouble making mincemeat of encounters.
________________
The Minstrel
LG Archon-Blooded Aasimar Legendary Kineticist (Divine Conduit) 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12 (+2)  Charisma: 8
Elements: Sound → Sound → Sound
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: HP 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (sound), gather power, kinetic blast (sonic)
2: Elemental defense (Adamant Faith), holy healing
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1, fearless defender
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, expanded element (sound), kinetic blast (vibration, sonic boom)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
10: Empowering aura
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Expanded element (sound: +1 attack, damage, save DCs, CL), infusion specialization 4
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Utility Talent (Kinetic Smite), Utility Talent (Basic Vibrokinesis → Retrain: Elemental Whispers), Point-Blank Shot
2: Utility Talent (Kinetic Healer)
3: Infusion (Kinetic Blade), Weapon Finesse
4: 
5: Infusion (Extended Range), Precise Shot
6: Utility Talent (Inspiration Amp)
7: Infusion (Kinetic Whip), Infusion (Torrent), Combat Reflexes
8: Utility Talent (Glorious Steed)
9: Infusion (Disorientating Infusion), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
10: 
11: Infusion (Blade Whirlwind), Extra Wild Talent (Echolocation)
12: Utility Talent (Kinetic Form)
13: Infusion (Whip Hurricane), Spell Penetration
14: Utility Talent (Greater Echolocation)
15: Infusion (Cloud), Greater Spell Penetration


Strategy


        The Minstrel is kind of what I would imagine a paladin of Shelyn would look like: dexterous, musical, and unafraid of danger. The divine conduit archetype is a nice one for sound, because the element has a poor defensive talent, a relative scarcity of valuable utility talents, and no intrinsic mobility. Divine conduit solves all of that! Our defensive talent, Adamant Faith, will give us scaling DR/alignment right from 2nd level, our fearless defender class feature makes us immune to fear effects (which commonly attack our weak Will save), and we can heal ourselves with Kinetic Healing as a swift action thanks to our holy healing class feature. Having Sonic Blast (an energy blast) will not only ensure that we have an easy time hitting for most of our levels, but also that we maximize our Kinetic Healing—holy healing allows us to treat Sonic Blast as if it were a physical blast for the purposes of how much it heals. On offense, just the bare essentials for a nice switch-hitter: we’ve got PBS, Precise Shot, and Extended Range for when you need range, Weapon Finesse, Kinetic Blade, Blade Whirlwind, Kinetic Whip, and Whip Hurricane for when you need melee, and Torrent and Cloud for AoE damage. Kinetic Smite, a cornerstone of this build, will let you blow single targets to smithereens whenever you particularly feel like it, and Combat Reflexes will ensure that you can dish out attacks of opportunity at will with Kinetic Whip; Spell Penetration and Greater Spell Penetration get added in at later levels for enemies with spell resistance. Glorious Steed will give sound some much-needed mobility as you gallop across the sky on a horse made of pure music. If you feel like taking anything out of the build, removing the two ranged feats would allow you to take Sonic Reverberation and Building Reverberation with Extra Wild Talent, both of which could help you amp up your burst damage considerably in exchange for slightly less facility at ranged combat—with an energy blast like Sonic Blast, even Precise Shot isn’t that important, so it’s worth considering.
        The other thing that this build is designed to do is buff, buff, buff. Kinetic Healing is okay at healing, although it stinks that holy healing forces you to accept all burn from that wild talent yourself. The main attractions, however, are Inspiration Amp, Echolocation, and Greater Echolocation. “Wait,” I hear you asking, “did you really claim that this was a buffing build without Greater Inspiration Amp?” I did, in fact, and it all comes down to the empowering aura class feature that you receive at 10th level. Whenever the divine conduit uses a spell-like ability utility talent that affects itself for more than one round, it can choose to grant the utility talent to all allies within 30 ft. for rounds/level, too. So at 10th level, Inspiration Amp essentially becomes Greater Inspiration Amp—a full four levels early! Echolocation and Greater Echolocation, similarly, can grant your entire team blindsense or blindsight for rounds/level through empowering aura.
        Here’s what this looks like in combat: in your first full round, use a standard action and expend 1 point of burn to cast Inspiration Amp on all allies, including you, then either use your move action to grant blindsight through Greater Echolocation (if you have a suspicion that you’re facing darkness, concealment, illusions, etc.) or else move into position. You can hang back, blasting with Cloud, Torrent, or Extended Range if needed, but your real place is up close in melee, where your DR/alignment, excellent Constitution score, and elemental overflow bonuses should keep you safe. Use Kinetic Form to balloon up into Large or Huge size, smite someone, then pull out your Kinetic Whip and get to work. Enemies should provoke from nearly anything, as you should have anywhere between 20 and 25 ft. of reach—plus Combat Reflexes—in this build, and you can use Blade Whirlwind or Whip Hurricane when you want to just hit everything in a big radius around yourself. You only have one substance infusion, Disorientating Infusion, but as it will make all enemies you hit confused, you wouldn’t really want to use anything else. It’s expensive, though, so you’ll only really be able to use it with low-cost form infusions until you’ve gotten a few tiers of infusion specialization. Between massively buffed allies, confused enemies, and your own melee prowess, you should be all set.
________________


The Phantom
NE Human Legendary Kineticist 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 8
Elements: Light → Sound → Light
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: Bonus wild talents 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (light), gather power, kinetic blast (light)
2: Elemental defense (Illusory Duplicates)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, expanded element (sound), kinetic blast (sonic, flashbang)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Expanded element (light), infusion specialization 4, kinetic blast (glorious)
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Extended Range), Utility Talent (Basic Photokinesis → Retrain: Traceless Light), Point-Blank Shot, Precise Shot
2: Utility Talent (Phantom Illusion)
3: Infusion (Illuminating Infusion), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
4: Utility Talent (Photographic Transference)
5: Infusion (Beacon Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Blinding Flare)
6: Utility Talent (Optical Camouflage), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Inspiration Amp)
7: Infusion (Eruption), Stealth Gathering
8: Utility Talent (Additional Illusion)
9: Infusion (Snake), Improved Stealth Gathering
10: Utility Talent (Intelligent Illusion)
11: Infusion (Mobile Blast), Extra Wild Talent (Greater Blinding Flare)
12: Utility Talent (Hard Light Illusions), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Greater Inspiration Amp)
13: Infusion (Improved Mobile Blast), Extra Wild Talent (Light Speed Travel)
14: Utility Talent (Greater Optical Camouflage)
15: Infusion (Ricochet), Extra Wild Talent (Long-Distance Travel)


Strategy


        I’m so glad that at least one of the third-party elements leaned into illusion magic, because it’s a favorite of mine, and it’s not fair that kineticists should be left out of the fun. The light and sound elements work hand in hand on this front, though, because light can generally only provide visual illusions while sound can only provide auditory illusions. You need both to make it realistic, silly! We’ll start with Phantom Illusion at 2nd level; it will get upgraded to have auditory components at 6th level. Starting at 4th, you’ll be able to use your illusions to scout ahead—and remember, unless enemies interact with your figments, they don’t typically get a save to disbelieve them. Sending a bird, butterfly, mouse, etc. into a room won’t arouse much suspicion at all, especially since it won’t be clear that it’s an illusion. Once you hit 8th, you can sustain an additional illusion, controlling both with the same actions, and at 10th you’ll be able to pre-script your illusions. Hard Light Illusions at 12th level is necessary to keep your illusions believable to enemies that typically aren’t fooled by mere light and shadow.
        An illusion playstyle naturally synergizes with the at-will invisibility you receive through Optical Camouflage: since invisibility doesn’t break unless you actively attack an enemy, you can feel free to buff allies with Greater Inspiration Amp, toss out more illusions, or even direct your mobile blast to attack someone—it’s not you doing the attacking, after all! Moreover, Stealth Gathering + Improved + Traceless Light is an unbeatable combo for attacking from stealth, as there is literally no indication that you’re there at all, even as you blast enemies to smithereens. Blinding Flare + Greater will break stealth, but they’re strong enough that you really shouldn’t mind. And hey, once you reach 14th level and finally have access to at-will greater invisibility, you can do whatever you want with impunity. Overall, the Phantom is a phenomenal scout and support character. The damage you deal won’t be significantly higher than other kineticist builds, but your ability to problem-solve with at-will invisibility, teleportation, and illusion magic will make you more versatile than kineticists who are oriented purely toward combat.
________________


The Professor
LN Shabti Legendary Kineticist 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 12  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 7 (+2)
Elements: Mind → Viscera → Mind
Racial Traits: Blank Slate
Favored Class Bonuses: HP 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (mind), gather power, kinetic blast (mental)
2: Elemental defense (Numbness)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, expanded element (viscera), kinetic blast (bone, cerebral empowerment)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Expanded element (mind), infusion specialization 4, kinetic blast (encephalon)
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Extended Range), Utility Talent (Stimulated Intellect), Servant in the House of Truth
2: Utility Talent (Esoteric Knowledge 1)
3: Infusion (Reifying Infusion), Healer’s Hands
4: Utility Talent (Skilled Kineticist)
5: Infusion (Kinetic Bomb), Incredible Healer
6: Utility Talent (Unnatural Evolution: Skilled 1)
7: Infusion (Scrambling Infusion), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
8: Utility Talent (Improved Unnatural Evolution: Skilled 2, Skilled 3)
9: Infusion (Empty Infusion), Deific Obedience: Magdh
10: Utility Talent (Greater Skilled Kineticist: Heal)
11: Infusion (Lock-Up Infusion), Spell Penetration
12: Utility Talent (Esoteric Knowledge 2)
13: Infusion (Improved Kinetic Bomb), Greater Spell Penetration
14: Utility Talent (Greater Unnatural Evolution: Skilled 4, Skilled 5, Skilled 6)
15: Infusion (Pacifying Infusion), Heal Skill Unlock


Strategy


        I swore I was going to make a kineticist skill monkey, and I pulled it off! This build is definitely not the most combat-effective build, although you can eventually create quite a potent debuffer thanks to Empty Infusion, Ability Focus, Kinetic Bomb, mind’s many good substance infusions, and the persistent metakinesis option available to legendary kineticists. The real draw of this build, though, is the ability to absolutely spank bards and other skill monkeys around the battlefield. Hell, you could probably give investigators a run for their money!
        We’re going to start with a shabti for our race, which is notable for two reasons: first, they have good Constitution scores, and second, they give us access to the amazing Servant in the House of Truth origin feat, which allows us to roll any Knowledge check twice, as many times per day as we want. Let that sink in. We’ll swap out Basic Neurokinesis for Stimulated Intellect (naturally) to allow us to use our Constitution modifier in place of our Intelligence modifier on all Intelligence-based skill checks; even though the Blank Slate racial trait isn’t great (a triple rhyme) it does eliminate the redundant class skills we’d be getting from Stimulated Intellect and Past Life Knowledge. Esoteric Knowledge will let us functionally acquire a few more skill ranks and apportion them to unusual Knowledge skills that may come up, and Skilled Kineticist will make our Sense Motive and Knowledge (History) scores bae. Picking up Magdh’s Deific Obedience feat (I’m a relentless munchkin) gives us a +4 to all Intelligence-based skill checks, and with the three Unnatural Evolution utility talents from viscera once our elements expand, we have six +8 bonuses that we can assign anywhere—I’d choose Perception, perhaps UMD, and whatever Knowledge skills you want to be incredible at. Save your first Unnatural Evolution and your Greater Skilled Kineticist bonus for Heal, though.
        Why? Well, I stapled on a non-magical healing chassis to this build. Healer’s Hands + Incredible Healer and as many Knowledge (Planes) ranks as you can get is really all this path needs to get started, and should give you about a +18 Heal bonus (3 class skill + 1 Wisdom + 6 ranks + 8 Skilled evolution) by 6th level. If you can hit a 30 on that check—not impossible, even at 6th level—not only will you heal 30 damage through Incredible Healer, you’ll also add 6 HP from your ranks in Knowledge (Planes). By 10th level, you’ll have scaled this up to +27 (3 class skill + 1 Wisdom + 10 ranks + 8 Skilled evolution + 5 Greater Skilled Kineticist), which should give you around 50 damage healed as a full-round action, 10/day. Not bad, right? The Heal skill unlock at 15th level finally surpasses what Incredible Healer is capable of, and will instead grant you a flat 90 HP (plus 15 from Healer’s Hands), 15/day. Feel free to retrain Incredible Healer and Greater Skilled Kineticist if you make it this far, as you likely won’t need them to hit a DC 30 Heal check with any regularity (3 class skill + 1 Wisdom + 15 ranks + 8 Skilled = +27) and gain your Healer’s Hands bonus HP.
        Assuming you used most of your Skilled evolutions on Knowledge skills and have been selecting Esoteric Knowledges wisely, you should have bonuses somewhere in the ballpark of +36 by 14th level (3 class skill + 7 Constitution + 14 ranks + 8 Skilled evolution + 4 Deific Obedience) and will be able to roll every Knowledge check twice, taking the better result for an average check of 50. With the exact same feat and skill investment, investigators could get close to that number (3 class skill + 7 Intelligence + 14 ranks + 5 inspiration + 4 Deific Obedience) on more skills, and they might pull slightly ahead with Eidetic Recollection guaranteeing them the ability to take 10. Nevertheless, the fact that a kineticist can rival the premiere skill monkey in Pathfinder is...impressive.
________________


The Prototype
CE Human Legendary Kineticist 15


Strength: 16 (+2)  Dexterity: 12  Constitution: 16  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 10  Charisma: 8
Elements: Viscera → Earth → Wood
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: Bonus wild talents 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (viscera), gather power, kinetic blast (bone)
2: Elemental defense (Reactive Skin)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, expanded element (earth), kinetic blast (earth, fossilized)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Expanded element (wood), kinetic blast (wood, venus, autumn, ravaging nature), infusion specialization 4
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Kinetic Fist), Utility Talent (Basic Corpokinesis → Retrain: Unnatural Evolution: Claws), Medium Armor Proficiency, Extra Wild Talent (Bone Blades)
2: Utility Talent (Tongue Twister)
3: Infusion (Extended Range), Extra Wild Talent (Enhanced Fist)
4: Utility Talent (Licker)
5: Infusion (Ricochet), Toughness
6: Utility Talent (Natural Rush), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Metal Blades)
7: Infusion (Impale), Extra Wild Talent (Charged Attacks)
8: Utility Talent (Improved Unnatural Evolution: Limbs [Arms])
9: Infusion (Snake), Extra Wild Talent (Expanded Defense: Flesh of Stone)
10: Utility Talent (Explosive Evolution)
11: Infusion (Self-Destructive Infusion), Combat Reflexes
12: Utility Talent (Armored Form), Human Bonus Wild Talent (Kinetic Form)
13: Infusion (Aligned Infusion), Protean Alteration
14: Utility Talent (Greater Unnatural Evolution: Rend, Claws)
15: Infusion (Crippling Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Unnatural Evolution II)


Strategy


        The Prototype build is built for a single purpose: to grant you incredible amounts of natural attacks. It can do some other stuff, too, though, so let’s get to that before we move on to the main attraction. I didn’t have any particular goals for this build apart from “How many natural attacks can I get?” which left the infusion slots curiously free. Extended Range, Ricochet, Impale, and Snake ensure that you’re at least functional at range, although your lack of PBS and Precise Shot does hurt with all physical blasts. I did choose to expand into wood at 14th level (an odd choice, I know) if only to give you access to Ravaging Nature Blast, which can deal some fun ability damage and allows you to change which saves you want enemies to roll. Together with Self-Destructive Infusion and Crippling Infusion, you do at least have some minor debuffing capabilities, even if that’s definitely not what the build is for.
        So what’s it for? Like I said: natural attacks. The viscera element gives players a shocking number of ways to get more natural attacks, and I only wish you could swap out infusions for utility talents—I would have liked to get up to Greater Elemental Limb, which can grant two secondary tentacles, but didn’t have the space. We’ll start with the basics: Bone Blades can give you two primary claws and a primary bite for 1 point of burn at 1st level, which combined with Weapon Finesse, Kinetic Fist, and Unnatural Evolution (which you’ll retrain into at your first free utility talent retraining level) will ensure that you’re hitting for about 2d6 on each attack at full BAB. By 4th level, you’ll have added a free tongue attack from Tongue Twister + Licker, plus an additional 1d6 damage on each attack from Enhanced Fist. When Kinetic Fist is active, you should deal 1d4+2d6 (primary claw), 1d4+2d6 (primary claw), 3d6 (primary bite), 1d4+2d6 (secondary tongue, but using your kineticist level as your BAB) at 4th level, plus whatever Strength modifiers you’ve got. Not bad, hey?
        Things open up for you even more at 5th through 8th level. Natural Rush is the biggest pick-up, enabling you to perform a full-attack (claw, claw, bite, tongue) after every charge, as pounce. Metal Blades will start beefing up your damage with your Bone Blades, as well as allowing you to penetrate exotic DR. Charged Attacks will start stacking elemental overflow bonuses on this core of four natural attacks before your Unnatural Evolution pool starts kicking in at 8th level. Keen observers might have noticed that we took the Claws evolution at 1st level, which doesn’t stack with the claws you get from Bone Blades, and wondered why I chose that option—it’s because you can get an additional pair of arms at 8th level to accommodate those claws! These claws will be significantly weaker (no elemental overflow from Charged Attacks, no pounce from Natural Rush, no damage bonuses or DR penetration from Metal Blades) but still gain bonuses from an amulet of mighty fists, still gain bonuses from your Strength score, and will still get their damage dice upgraded through Kinetic Form later on.
        8th through 12th level are mostly about defense. Flesh of Stone is a phenomenal get, although you could easily stay in viscera for a full 15 levels and select Bone Armor and Advanced Exoskeleton to get DR—it’s largely a matter of preference. Either way, you’ll pick up some hefty DR/adamantine. Size increases are the name of the game with Kinetic Form, which increase your natural reach and amp up the damage dice of every single natural attack in your repertoire; Armored Form is a nice upgrade to Kinetic Form that, while not strictly necessary, will help you squeeze more out of your burn. Lastly, Explosive Evolution is a nice, versatile tool to have on hand for when you need something done fast. Need wings? Boom, Flight. Tremorsense? Boom, Tremorsense. A swim or climb speed? Boom, Swim or Climb. It can be used for more natural attacks, of course, and we’ll get there, but it’s so much more than that.
        13th through 15th level is the home stretch for this character, and the biggest pick-up is Greater Unnatural Evolution, which gives us Rend (for our four claw attacks) as well as another instance of Claws. Wait, but I thought we ran out of arms for those claws? We did, but Explosive Evolution can (in a pinch) give you yet another set of arms with the Limbs evolution, bringing the total number of claw attacks to six. I tossed in Combat Reflexes and Ability Focus at the end out of sheer boredom—Combat Reflexes is nice to take advantage of your huge reach, and Ability Focus is good for high-level debuffing with Self-Destructive Infusion or Crippling Infusion.
        One interesting thing to think about with this build is that you can’t really gather power if you want to deal optimal damage, so everything has to come from infusion specialization. Kinetic Fist is a given if you’re in melee, because it’s going to deal an extra 24d6 damage if all your attacks hit and the enemy has no DR, in addition to empowering any attacks of opportunity you may make when it’s not your turn. Apart from that, though, you can just feel free to add in a substance infusion and call it quits. Without gather power or supercharge, you can’t really use composite blasts (which would increase your Kinetic Fist damage from 3d6 to 6d6) or metakinesis without unavoidably taking burn, and that’s something you want to avoid if you can help it. Legendary kineticists can supercharge as a swift action at 11th level, though, which spares your full-attack action.
There’s a brief summary section below detailing exactly how many attacks you can get here, but I’ll save you the math: seven primary attacks (one bite and six claws), one secondary attack (one tongue), and the ability to rend.


Natural Attacks (Non-Unnatural Evolution)


Primary Bite, Huge: 2d6 (Base) + 3d6 (Kinetic Fist) + Elemental Overflow (Charged Attacks) + Amulet of Mighty Fists / Greater Magic Fang + 1 x Strength Modifier
        Source: Bone Blades
Primary 2x Claws, Huge: 1d8 (Base) + 3d6 (Kinetic Fist) + Elemental Overflow (Charged Attacks) + Amulet of Mighty Fists / Greater Magic Fang + 1 x Strength Modifier
        Source: Bone Blades
Secondary Tongue, Huge: 1d8 (Base) + 3d6 (Kinetic Fist) + Elemental Overflow (Charged Attacks) + Amulet of Mighty Fists / Greater Magic Fang + 0.5 x Strength Modifier
Source: Licker (Note: Remember that Licker treats your kineticist level as your BAB! Even though it’s a secondary attack, you shouldn’t have much trouble hitting with it.)


Natural Attacks (Unnatural Evolution)


Primary 2x Claws, Huge: 1d8 (Base) + 3d6 (Kinetic Fist) + Amulet of Mighty Fists / Greater Magic Fang + 1 x Strength Modifier
        Source: Unnatural Evolution (Claws) + Improved Unnatural Evolution (Limbs)
Primary Rend, Huge: 1d8 (Base) + 1.5 x Strength Modifier
        Source: Greater Unnatural Evolution (Rend)
Primary 2x Claws, Huge: 1d8 (Base) + 3d6 (Kinetic Fist) + Amulet of Mighty Fists / Greater Magic Fang + 1 x Strength Modifier
        Source: Greater Unnatural Evolution (Claws) + Explosive Evolution (Limbs)
________________


The Spellsieve
NG Shabti Legendary Kineticist 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 12  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 7 (+2)
Elements: Aether → Aether → Aether
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: HP 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (aether), gather power, kinetic blast (telekinetic)
2: Elemental defense (Force Ward)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, expanded element (aether), kinetic blast (force)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Expanded element (aether), infusion specialization 4
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Extended Range), Utility Talent (Basic Telekinesis), Point-Blank Shot
2: Utility Talent (Telekinetic Finesse)
3: Infusion (Kinetic Bomb), Precise Shot
4: Utility Talent (Kinetic Crafter)
5: Infusion (Empty Infusion), Lector Priest
6: Utility Talent (Unweave Magic), Expanded Element Bonus Wild Talent (Telekinetic Invisibility)
7: Infusion (Improved Kinetic Bomb), Dispel Focus
8: Utility Talent (Telekinetic Haul)
9: Infusion (Hyper-Dimensional Blast), Greater Dispel Focus
10: Utility Talent (Greater Unweave Magic)
11: Infusion (Dismissing Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Aetheric Sniper)
12: Utility Talent (Suffocate)
13: Infusion (Stitching Infusion), Improved Counterspell
14: Utility Talent (Touchsight)
15: Infusion (Foe Throw), Parry Spell


Strategy


        Arcanists are probably the undisputed champions of counterspelling, but I wanted to see whether I could make a kineticist build that did just as well with it. First off, we’re going to be a shabti for this build: they have the excellent Lector Priest origin feat that lets them take Spellcraft as a class skill and, more crucially, roll twice and take the better of any Spellcraft check they make. A big part of your magehunting potential comes from being able to identify any spell that an enemy casts, and your ability to craft magical items through Kinetic Crafter will also be helped greatly by Lector Priest. The key components to the anticaster role here are Extended Range (we want to be able to counterspell with Unweave Magic at ranges greater than just 30 feet), Unweave Magic and Greater Unweave Magic, the two Dispel Focus feats, Dismissing Infusion, Stitching Infusion, Empty Infusion, Parry Spell, and potentially Suffocate or Foe Throw. Let’s break it down bit by bit.
        Unweave Magic and Greater Unweave Magic are the most critical elements of this build. Aether is unique in that these wild talents allow your Telekinetic Blasts to be used as dispel magic, rather than another more specific spell. This vastly increases the versatility of what you can counterspell, since even feats like Improved Elemental Counterspell only allow you to counter spells with specific descriptors. Aether can just counter any spell. Dispel Focus and Greater Dispel Focus are present to ensure that you beat your caster level checks as often as possible; although dispel magic is far more versatile than other spells you could use to counterspell, it does require a caster level check instead of working automatically. Dismissing Infusion will handle summons that might get in your way, and Stitching Infusion is a powerful tool for denying enemy casters the opportunity to teleport away. (If you’re counterspelling aggressively enough, you might not even need Stitching Infusion!) Parry Spell, though a late addition with an unfortunately redundant prereq, is well worth its entry barriers, as it allows you to turn spells back on their casters on a whim. Don’t like the fireball headed your way? It just blew up in your enemy’s face. Don’t like that enervation? How about you take the negative levels, witch! Suffocate and Foe Throw, as utility talents that are keyed off of Fortitude saves, are just there to mess with casters, preventing verbal components in the former case and monkeying with battlefield positioning in the latter.
        Beyond that, we’ve got a halfway decent ranged build. Kinetic Bomb + Improved Kinetic Bomb and Hyper-Dimensional Blast give aether a lot of form infusions to play with that it would normally have, and with Aetheric Sniper at later levels you can even keep yourself relatively hidden while you blast people. I believe that counterspelling would break invisibility, since dispel magic technically targets a spell’s caster, not the spell itself. Telekinetic Finesse, Telekinetic Invisibility, and Telekinetic Haul are staples of an aetherkineticist’s diet, and are included just because they add so much versatility to the playstyle. This build is a caster’s worst nightmare, plain and simple, and can shut down pretty much any enemy that relies on spells or spell-like abilities.
        
________________


The Stonelord Evolved
LG Dwarf Legendary Kineticist (Kinetic Knight / Elemental Scion) 15


Strength: 16  Dexterity: 12  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 10 (+2)  Charisma: 8 (-2)
Elements: Earth → Earth → Earth
Racial Traits: Fey Thoughts, Iron Citizen
Favored Class Bonuses: HP 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (earth), gather power, kinetic blast (earth), elemental heart, courtly, elemental blade, kinetic warrior
2: Elemental bastion
3: Elemental overflow +1, knight’s resolve
4: Elemental defense (Flesh of Stone), battle burn 1
5: Infusion specialization 1
6: Elemental overflow +2, focused element, kinetic blast (metal)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, greater resolve
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: 
14: Infusion specialization 4, elemental master
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Bloody Infusion), Infusion (Kinetic Blade: Kinetic Knight Bonus), Utility Talent (Basic Geokinesis → Retrain: Kinetic Whispers), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
2: Utility Talent (Fist of Granite)
3: Infusion (Blade Rush), Steel Soul
4: Utility Talent (Magnetic Pull)
5: Infusion (Entangling Infusion), Infusion (Kinetic Whip: Kinetic Knight Bonus), Iron Will
6: Utility Talent (Clockwork Heart), Utility Talent (Tremorsense: Focused Element Bonus)
7: Infusion (Magnetic Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Earth Rider)
8: Utility Talent (Improved Magnetic Pull)
9: Infusion (Rare-Metal Infusion), Infusion (Blade Whirlwind: Kinetic Knight Bonus), Extra Wild Talent (Sinkhole)
10: Utility Talent (Kinetic Form)
11: Infusion (Incorporeal Infusion), Infusion (Whip Hurricane: Kinetic Knight Bonus), Combat Reflexes
12: Utility Talent (Greater Earth Rider)
13: Infusion (Aligned Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Durable Form)
14: Utility Talent (Pitfall), Utility Talent (Distorted Area: Elemental Master Bonus)
15: Infusion (Empty Infusion), Toughness


Strategy


        Oh, I like this one. Comboing kinetic knight with elemental scion is almost unfair, it’s so good, and thanks to the damage increases to Earth Blast and Metal Blast from elemental scion, you almost won’t miss metakinesis. Almost. Anyway, the third-party Stonelord Evolved does everything the first-party Stonelord did, but a bit better. We’ve held onto all of the good infusions, while throwing away garbage ones that we took because we didn’t have any other good choices at that level (Pushing or Bowling Infusion). In their place, we’ve gotten Bloody Infusion, a good, cheap substance infusion for dealing more damage, as well as Aligned Infusion and Incorporeal Infusion, which are excellent for punching through various creatures’ DR and which combo nicely with Rare-Metal Infusion. We’ve added flight through Earth Rider and Greater Earth Rider, tossed in some battlefield control with Pitfall and Sinkhole, and even added some nice natural attacks through Fist of Granite. Your normal attack routine should be to attack once with a blade or whip, then slam twice at a -5. It won’t work all the time, but it’s a nice little damage boost when it works for you.
        Probably the biggest additions to the build from a tanking perspective are four relatively innocuous utility talents: Magnetic Pull, Improved Magnetic Pull, Distorted Area, and Durable Form. Durable Form is the simplest, giving you a big stack of temporary HP that you can replenish throughout the day, increasing your elemental overflow bonuses and making you harder to take down all the while. Magnetic Pull and Improved Magnetic Pull are what’s going to make you into a true tank, though: they give enemies within a 15- to 25-foot radius scaling penalties to hit your allies (a -7 by the time you hit 15th level!) and scaling bonuses to hit you. Now, foes won’t want to try to hit you: you have DR/adamantine, great AC and HP, great saves against just about everything, resolve + greater resolve, and any buffs you might have gotten from your team. But foes won’t really have a choice, will they? What are they going to do, take that -7 and try to hit one of your allies? That’s a hard no for most monsters. You’ll get hit plenty, but this lets your team focus buffs on you to handle the heat, and you have plenty of tools for dealing with concentrated fire on your own. So go to work.
________________

Strategy


        The evolved version of the Tidal Blaster is a solid upgrade, thanks in no small part to the generally strong elemental brethren archetype, which grants the elemental native outsiders the opportunity to trade out elemental assault rounds for better burn capabilities, more kinetic blast damage, and more. You can take these builds in any direction you want, but I chose to lean out of battlefield control a little bit in favor of some healing. Even against the generally weak Reflex save, the Slick utility talent that I focused on in the vanilla build won’t be useful against monsters forever, while buffing and support are always relevant. The build is also faster (thanks, Elemental Acceleration), stronger (thanks, oodles of new infusions), and more efficient (thanks, Incremental Elemental Assault) than the previous build. You’re still a jack of all trades, but you’re better at it here than you were with first-party content alone.

The Toxicant
NG Half-Elf Legendary Kineticist 15


Strength: 10  Dexterity: 16  Constitution: 16 (+2)  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 12  Charisma: 8
Elements: Poison → Poison → Poison
Racial Traits: Dual-Minded
Favored Class Bonuses: Bonus wild talents 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (poison), gather power, kinetic blast (acid)
2: Elemental defense (Corrosive Miasma)
3: Elemental overflow +1
4: Battle burn 1
5: Infusion specialization 1, metakinesis (empower)
6: Elemental overflow +2, expanded element (poison), kinetic blast (virulent)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Expanded element (poison), infusion specialization 4
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Laced Infusion), Utility Talent (Basic Toxikinesis), Point-Blank Shot
2: Utility Talent (Master Poisoner)
3: Infusion (Empty Infusion), Precise Shot
4: Utility Talent (Biological Toxin)
5: Infusion (Extended Range), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
6: Utility Talent (Poison Amplification), Half-Elf Bonus Wild Talent (Flurry of Blasts), Expanded Element Bonus Wild Talent (Neutralizing Infusion)
7: Infusion (Kinetic Bomb), Precision Blast
8: Utility Talent (Personalized Venom)
9: Infusion (Improved Kinetic Bomb), Extra Wild Talent (Poison Immunity)
10: Utility Talent (Haze of Acid)
11: Infusion (Psychotropic Infusion), Spell Penetration
12: Utility Talent (Greater Biological Toxin), Half-Elf Bonus Wild Talent (Improved Flurry of Blasts)
13: Infusion (Melting Infusion), Greater Spell Penetration
14: Utility Talent (Deadly Vapors)
15: Infusion (Cloud), Extra Wild Talent (Venom Vector)


Strategy


        Poison doesn’t have a huge variety of playstyles open to it: you either poison things professionally, or you stay the heck out of this element. To that end, we need a few components to create a viable poison build. First, we need multiple methods of poison creation. Second, we need multiple methods of poison delivery. Third, we need a means of boosting our save DCs. Fourth, we need a means of ignoring poison immunity. Let’s go through the list point by point.
        I have to hand it to N. Jolly—poison is shockingly comprehensive in the avenues it gives you to create poisons. Perhaps our most boring avenue is Master Poisoner at 2nd level, which lets you craft poisons as you normally would but at an accelerated rate. Biological Toxin + Greater at 4th and 12th levels are by far our most consistent, powerful avenue for poison creation. For the low, low price of 1 point of burn, you can create a fistful of poisons at no material cost. This is a phenomenal benefit, as material costs are one of the primary barriers to entry in a poison playstyle. The mediocre save DCs of your biological toxins will later get patched by Poison Amplification, so don’t worry about that. If you ever feel like you’d rather hand out status conditions than ability damage, at 8th level you can take a stroll down our final avenue, Personalized Venom, which grants you the toxicant alchemist’s toxic secretion class feature. It’s fantastic for debuffing in more conventional ways.
        Once we’ve got several modes of creating poisons, though, we need a way to deliver them to enemies. Typically this involves poisoning weapons and then hitting someone with them, touching someone with a contact poison, throwing an inhaled poison, etc., but in the poison element we only really need one tool: Laced Infusion. This gem lets you deliver all those poisons through your kinetic blast, neatly sidestepping the hassle of poisoning weapons or poisoning yourself. You can even deliver poisons through AoE form infusions! The save DC penalty is annoyingly well-balanced, but I’ve baked in enough save DC boosts that you shouldn’t have much trouble poisoning multiple enemies with Kinetic Bomb, Improved Kinetic Bomb, or Cloud. Venom Vector is an interesting complement to Laced Infusion, but since this build isn’t meant to do melee combat, I left it alone until the very end of the build.
        After generating and delivering poisons, you need a way to increase the save DCs to the point that enemies can’t effectively guard against you. I’ve picked up low-hanging save DC increases like Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast), but the first real cornerstone of the Toxicant build is Empty Infusion. In exchange for the damage you normally would have dealt with your kinetic blast, you can instead increase the save DC of all your substance infusions (Laced Infusion included) by 1, +1 for every five levels. I would highly recommend doing this. Ability damage, when it works, can often lead to very quick debility, unconsciousness, and even death—commodities that are typically much more valuable than garden-variety HP damage. Starting each battle with Empty Infusion + Laced Infusion + Improved Kinetic Bomb + a poison created by Biological Toxin will immediately place enemies in the unenviable position of either having to take nasty ticking ability damage, growing weaker and more vulnerable as time goes on, or else burn actions and resources to cure or delay the poison. It’s a bad choice to have to make. If you’re attacking a single target, you can amp up the pain even more by using Flurry of Blasts + Improved Flurry of Blasts, both of which either impose save penalties on enemies or else force them to reroll saves entirely. Empty Infusion + Laced Infusion + Improved Flurry of Blasts is a nasty combo indeed. Since I assume in the third-party builds that you’re playing a legendary kineticist, I should also mention that persistent metakinesis is boss for pummelling enemies’ saves. Can you imagine all the bonuses you have, all the penalties that enemies have, and on top of that enemies have to roll their saves twice? Doesn’t bear thinking about.
        The final necessary element in this build is the ability to bypass poison immunity, which we can do simply through Neutralizing Infusion at 6th level. Note that even enemies who are immune to acid damage are not immune to the effects of Neutralizing Infusion—the only two requirements are 1) that you hit the enemy with your kinetic blast, and 2) that you penetrate spell resistance. You have Spell Penetration + Greater Spell Penetration, so this should be fairly simple to do. Again, a save negates here, so if your team’s monster identification reveals poison immunities, I’d highly recommend that you open with Empty Infusion + Kinetic Bomb (for AoE crowds) or Flurry of Blasts (for single targets) + Neutralizing Infusion in order to give yourself the best chance of nullifying those immunities before advancing to any other steps.
________________


The Wendigo
CE Hobgoblin Legendary Kineticist 15

Elements: Water → Void → Water
Racial Traits: N/A

Abilities


1: Burn, elemental focus (water), gather power, kinetic blast (cold)

4: Battle burn 1
