6: Elemental overflow +2, expanded element (void), kinetic blast (gravity)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 2
9: Elemental overflow +3, metakinesis (persistent)
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, infusion specialization 3, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken)
14: Expanded element (water), infusion specialization 4, kinetic blast (water, ice)
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Infusion (Kinetic Blade), Utility Talent (Basic Hydrokinesis → Retrain: Hydro Cutter), Weapon Finesse
2: Utility Talent (Elemental Acceleration)
3: Infusion (Extended Range), Ability Focus (Kinetic Blast)
4: Utility Talent (Cold Adaptation)
5: Infusion (Entangling Infusion), Spell Penetration
6: Utility Talent (Shroud of Shadows)
7: Infusion (Darkness Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Chilling Void)
8: Utility Talent (Frigid Terrain)
9: Infusion (Kinetic Whip), Extra Wild Talent (Atrophy Field)
10: Utility Talent (Grasp of the Void)
11: Infusion (Blade Whirlwind), Extra Wild Talent (Plains of Weeping Wounds)
12: Utility Talent (Improved Shroud of Shadows)
13: Infusion (Whip Hurricane), Greater Spell Penetration
14: Utility Talent (Gravity Sculptor)
15: Infusion (Bloody Infusion), Extra Wild Talent (Improved Bloody Infusion)


Strategy


        The concept for the Wendigo build was to create a creature of such vile negative energies and biting, wintry cold that no creature could stand to be in melee range with it for very long. Mechanically, the name of the game here is damage over time, but we’ll get to that in a bit. Let’s map out the defensive and mobility situation before getting to the main course. Fundamentally, the Wendigo is a melee build, with the usual suspects (Weapon Finesse, Kinetic Blade/Whip, Blade Whirlwind, Whip Hurricane, etc.) set up to make that work. Shroud of Water is nice to start the game with, since the shield bonuses to AC that it confers are very, very handy early on. I’ve given us Elemental Acceleration to boost land speed—always nice when you have to charge into battle—as well as Cold Adaptation, which, despite my distaste for it, is a prerequisite for Frigid Terrain, which is going to grant you even more speed and a lot of mobility debuffing down the line.
        To this solid melee chassis, we’re going to add some debuffing next. Ability Focus ensures that our save DCs are up to snuff, and Entangling Infusion is about all we should need to ensure that enemies can’t effectively escape us once we set our sights on them. At 8th level, we add Frigid Terrain as the first of several auras we’re going to end up having; although the rules text doesn’t specify what exactly “icy terrain” does mechanically, based on the rules for tactical movement in snow, sleet, hail, etc. it might include forcing enemies to spend anywhere between 5 and 20 feet of movement to move through one square of your icy terrain, and might also include Acrobatics checks not to fall down, similar to sleet storm. Either way, not much will be able to approach you easily while you’re under the effects of Frigid Terrain. At 6th, 7th, 10th, and 12th levels, our expansion into the void element also starts to pay off by granting us Shroud of Shadows, Darkness Infusion, Grasp of the Void, and Improved Shroud of Shadows, which together ensure that you can coat any area you want in darkness: Improved Shroud of Shadows gives you a 15-foot darkness aura, Darkness Infusion can be added to any kinetic blast you’d like, and Grasp of the Void lays down black tentacles in combination with darkness in a 20-foot radius spread. This isn’t supernatural darkness, so most enemies will be able to see through it, but the darkness itself is not the main event…
        ...the damage is. The linchpin of the build is Chilling Void, which is guaranteed to deal at least your level (and possibly twice your level) in cold damage to every enemy that ends their turn within an area of darkness that you created. The really cruel thing about pairing this guaranteed damage with all your battlefield control (Frigid Terrain, Entangling Infusion, Grasp of the Void, etc.) is that enemies simply won’t be able to escape those areas much of the time, forcing them to sit in one place and take ticking damage. This is amped up in the final levels of the build by the Atrophy Field → Plains of Weeping Wounds → Hydro Cutter + Bloody Infusion → Improved Bloody Infusion wild talent line, which permits you to deal slashing damage with the water blast you acquire at 14th level. With this final piece in place, you truly become an avatar of winter and blood. Whip Hurricane + Bloody Infusion + Plains of Weeping Wounds will dole out about 11-12 bleed damage per turn to everything you manage to hit, and thanks to Atrophy Field + Plains of Weeping Wounds, nothing but magical healing (which is greatly weakened in your Atrophy Field) will be able to heal it up. Add that 11-12 bleed damage to the 15-30 cold damage you’ll deal from Chilling Void + Improved Shroud of Shadows, and you’re dealing anywhere between 25 and 40 damage per round to every creature within a 15-foot radius at no real action cost. Springing for Improved Bloody Infusion (though expensive) can then stack up more bleed damage. Wendigo indeed.
________________


The Witchblade
NG Human Legendary Kineticist (Telekinetic Bladeshifter) 15


Strength: 16 (+2)  Dexterity: 12  Constitution: 16  Intelligence: 10  Wisdom: 10  Charisma: 8
Elements: Aether → Time → Void
Racial Traits: N/A
Favored Class Bonuses: Bonus wild talents 1-20


Abilities


1: Burn, telekinetic focus (aether), gather power, kinetic blast (telekinetic), telekinetic blade (longsword)
2: Elemental defense (Forcewoven Shield)
3: Elemental overflow +1, forcewoven weapon
4: Battle burn 1
5: Metakinesis (empower), telekinetic blade (dagger)
6: Elemental overflow +2, expanded element (time), kinetic blast (chrono, aetheric boost, alteration amplification)
7: Internal buffer 1
8: Infusion specialization 1
9: Elemental overflow +3, bladeshifter’s specialization (two-handed specialty), telekinetic blade (defensive weapon training)
10: 
11: Battle burn 2, internal buffer 2, supercharge
12: Elemental overflow +4
13: Metakinesis (quicken), telekinetic blade (Fighter’s Reflexes)
14: Expanded element (void), infusion specialization 2, kinetic blast (gravity, gravitic boost, esoteric modification)
15: Elemental overflow +5


Feats and Wild Talents


1: Utility Talent (Basic Telekinesis), Medium Armor Proficiency, Power Attack
2: Utility Talent (Elemental Whispers)
3: Step Up
4: Bonus Combat Feat (Following Step)
5: Cut from the Air
6: Combat Reflexes, Human Bonus Wild Talent (Celerity)
7: Infusion (Bloody Infusion), Disruptive
8: Utility Talent (Telekinetic Invisibility)
9: Smash from the Air
10: Utility Talent (Time Skip)
11: Spellbreaker
12: Toughness, Human Bonus Wild Talent (Dimensional Traveler)
13: Spellcut
14: Utility Talent (Improved Dimensional Traveler)
15: Step Up and Strike


Strategy


        The Witchblade build was my take on a dedicated kineticist magehunter. It’s actually not too hard to hunt mages as a kineticist anyway, since you can ready actions to blast casters as they wind up. With your huge damage numbers, the concentration checks to complete the casting then become more or less impossible to hit. What if you wanted to do things the old-fashioned way, though? You know, grab a sword, follow some five-foot steps, and cut your enemies down as God intended? Although first-party content has no real answer for this kineticist role, third-party content very much does in the telekinetic bladeshifter archetype. Let’s get into it.
        To begin with, telekinetic bladeshifters are full-BAB characters that have the usual weaknesses the fighter class has, notably poor Reflex and Will saves. Where fighters often lack a lot of the magical tools needed to make a magehunting build work, however, bladeshifters are packed with all the versatility inherent to the aether element, plus whatever versatility you pick up from your expanded elements. Being counted as a fighter for the purposes of feat prerequisites is enormous, as is the ability to trade out utility talents for bonus combat feats. Sure, I’d love to get my hands on dozens of utility talents, but ultimately this build won’t focus on its save DCs enough to make anything other than self-buffs worthwhile. We’ll eventually build toward a two-handed greatsword and medium armor setup, letting us tank Dexterity fairly safely—it’s not as though you have any form infusions that need Dexterity to fuel their save DCs.
        From a fighting standpoint, we’ve got two separate streams going: general feats and fighter-exclusive feats. Step Up + Following Step + Step Up and Strike is a classic combo that’s deadly to archers and spellcasters alike; Following Step in particular is great for maneuvering around enormous enemies without provoking attacks of opportunity. The fighter levels can offer much more, however: Cut from the Air + Smash from the Air make you mostly immune to ray attacks like enervation and scorching ray, Disruptive + Spellbreaker are phenomenal for punishing spellcasters even further as they try to escape you or cast their way out of trouble, and Spellcut is a potent defensive ability that will allow you to easily shrug off effects targeting your weak Will save. I’d recommend investing in Spellcraft for this build, because you’ll want to know which spells an enemy is targeting you with in order to prioritize which you’re going to use Spellcut on. To sum up: you can eliminate rays with Smash from the Air as many times as you have attacks of opportunity, using Spellcut to eliminate pure save-or-die spells. Spellcasters will provoke attacks of opportunity anytime they five-foot step away (Step Up and Strike) or fail to cast defensively (Spellbreaker), which they will do at a hefty disadvantage, especially for their highest-level spells (Disruptive).
        Beyond that, we’ve got some excellent at-will spell-like abilities. At-will haste from Celerity, at-will invisibility from Telekinetic Invisibility, and at-will dimensional hop from Time Skip are all phenomenal. The very last levels also grant you Dimensional Assault, Dimensional Dervish, Dimensional Maneuvers, and Dimensional Savant, making it a cinch to work in your full-attack routine while you teleport around the battlefield. Oh, and the advanced weapon trainings! Fighter’s Reflexes patches your poor Reflex save, and Defensive Weapon Training ensures that you benefit from your Forcewoven Shield defensive power constantly while still being able to wield a two-handed weapon. So, all in all, you’ve got a highly mobile, highly versatile hexblade-type character who can assassinate casters and archers, teleport into advantageous positions, and protect itself from a staggering number of attack modes. I’m a fan of this build.
🎓 Valedictions & Back Matter
Back Matter


        Well, my friends, after a little more than 350 pages, I think you’ve probably heard enough from me, and after having written these guides for so long (my 5-year anniversary is coming up soon!) I feel as though valedictions are less and less important between us. You’re always right around the corner, and you know that you can always reach me on Reddit, Ko-Fi, and anywhere else you might wish to. I hope you’ve enjoyed the guide, and I’ll see you all back at the tabletop very soon! Until then, may all your swords be sharp, and all your rolls be Nat 20s.


All my best for a good game,
Chris (/u/Allerseelen)
________________
[1] For the sake of argument, let’s go with Crushing Blast for a second, assuming a theoretical 1st-level kineticist that deals 1d6 + 1 + 4 damage on a blast and confirms every crit they threaten. At a x2 crit modifier, you deal (0.95 * 8.5) + (0.05 * 17) damage, for an average of 8.925 damage. If you take (0.95 * 8.5) + (0.05 * 34), the damage calculation with your x4 crit modifier factored in, you get an average of 9.775 damage; you spent 5 burn for an extra 0.85 damage above what a normal crit would have gotten you.


Well, okay, but what if we scale it up? A maxed-out 20th-level kineticist will deal 20d6 + 20 + 15 on a composite blast, average 105; we’ll further assume we’re empowered and maximized, for a total of about 225 damage ([120 + 20]*1.5 + 15). At a x2 crit modifier: (0.95 * 225) + (0.05 * 450) = 236.25. At a x4 crit modifier: (0.95 * 225) + (0.05 * 900) = 258.75. We’ll call that 5 burn for about 30 extra damage at 20th level, when the median HP of CR 20 creatures is 370. If you crit, they’re dead no matter what—the extra damage was overkill.


[2] Second verse, same as the first, a little bit louder and a little bit worse. Same idealized kineticist for the sake of experimentation. At 1st level, (0.95 * 8.5) + (0.05 * 17) = 8.925 damage, vs. (0.85 * 8.5) + (0.15 * 17) = 9.775 damage. At 20th level, (0.95 * 225) + (0.05 * 450) = 236.25 damage, vs. (0.85 * 225) + (0.15 * 450) = 258.75 damage. Same exact damage figures, except Keen Blast costs 1 point less burn. Neither is terribly effective, as I’m sure you’ll agree.
